1.1: bereshit bara elohim (bĕrēʾšît bārāʾ ʾĕlōhîm): 'In the beginning God created.' Three initial philological observations: (1) bereshit (in the beginning) is in construct state — the absolute beginning, not 'in a beginning of'; (2) bara (created) is used in the OT exclusively with God as subject and without specification of material — implying creation from nothing (ex nihilo), not merely formation from pre-existing chaos; (3) Elohim is grammatically plural but takes a singular verb (bara) — the plural of majesty, or early evidence of the complexity that later theology would develop as Trinity. The first word of Scripture establishes the fundamental theological axiom: God, not chaos, not a pantheon, not nature, is the origin of all things.
1.26: naase adam betsalmenu kidmutenu (naʿăśeh ʾādām bĕṣalmēnû kidmûtēnû): 'Let us make humanity in our image, after our likeness.' The divine plural (naase, let us; tsalmenu, our image) has generated three interpretive traditions: (1) a plural of majesty; (2) God consulting the heavenly court of angels (cf. Job 1:6; Isa 6:8); (3) an early intra-divine address (Trinitarian foreshadowing, as the Church Fathers read it). Tselem (image) is the word used for cult-statues representing a deity — in ancient Near Eastern thought, the king was the tselem of the gods, their representative and regent. By applying this to all humanity, Genesis democratizes the royal-priestly image: every human, not just the king, represents the divine presence on earth and exercises dominion on behalf of the divine.
1.2:
תֹהוּ וָבֹהוּ (tōhû wābōhû) — an alliterative, rhyming word-pair meaning "formless and empty" or "unformed and uninhabited." Neither word denotes chaos or destruction in itself; together they describe a state prior to ordered, inhabited creation. The phrase recurs in Jer 4:23 applied to desolated land — showing the pair describes the undoing of ordered creation, not primordial evil.

רוּחַ אֱלֹהִים (rûaḥ ʾĕlōhîm) — rûaḥ spans wind, breath, and spirit; the context tips toward the personal divine agent because of mĕraḥepet (hovering/brooding), a Piel participle from a root used of a bird caring for her young (Deut 32:11). Wind does not hover in this deliberate, nurturing sense; the personal Spirit who will animate creation is already present over the unformed deep.

תְּהוֹם (tĕhôm) — "the deep," cognate with Babylonian Tiamat. Unlike Babylonian cosmogony, the deep here is not a monster to be slain but a neutral condition over which God acts freely. The absence of a creation-combat myth is itself a theological statement.
1.3:
וַיֹּאמֶר אֱלֹהִים — the waw-consecutive imperfect (wayyiqtol) inaugurates the creation narrative's sequence. Hebrew narrative past tense uses this form throughout ch 1: each divine speech and act is rendered sequentially by waw + imperfect, not by simple perfects. The effect is a cascade of completed actions in real time.

יְהִי אוֹר (yĕhî ʾôr) — the jussive "let there be light." Light is created before the luminaries of day 4 — the light of day 1 is not solar light. The separation of the light-source from the light-bearing objects prevents any identification of אוֹר with the sun, important given Babylonian sun-worship. The Hebrew makes no further comment; the gap is theologically loaded.
1.4:
כִּי-טוֹב (kî-ṭôb) — "that it was good." The kî introduces a direct object clause: God saw that. ṭôb is an aesthetic-moral-functional term — good in the sense of fitting, right, beautiful, and working as intended. Each evaluation unit closes with this phrase; the cumulative effect is a God who evaluates rather than merely produces.

וַיַּבְדֵּל (wayyabdēl) — "and he separated." The verb bādal (to divide, distinguish) is used in Levitical purity law for holy-versus-common distinctions. God's creative work is structuring by distinction; the same verb will describe the Levites' separating role (Num 16:9) and Israel's call to distinguish between clean and unclean.
1.5:
וַיִּקְרָא (wayyiqrāʾ) — "and he called/named." In the ANE, naming is an act of authority and definition — to name is to establish the nature and role of a thing. God names the light "Day" and darkness "Night"; Adam will later name the animals (2:19-20), exercising derivative dominion through the same act.

יוֹם אֶחָד (yôm ʾeḥāḏ) — "one day" (cardinal, not "first day" as in the ordinal forms used for days 2–6). The distinction may signal that the first day stands apart as the foundation; alternatively, the cardinal signals the day is complete in itself. The evening-morning sequence (evening first) follows the Hebrew reckoning of a day starting at sundown.
1.6: רָקִיעַ (rāqîaʿ) — from rāqaʿ (to beat out, spread thin — used of hammering metal into sheets, Exod 39:3). The term describes a stretched-out expanse rather than a solid vault; the cognate Akkadian concept does imply a firmament, but the Hebrew verbal root emphasizes extension and spreading rather than rigid solidity. The MKT renders "expanse" rather than "firmament" to avoid reading in a cosmological theory the text does not require.
1.7: The distinction between bārāʾ (create, v. 1, 21, 27) and ʿāśâ (make, v. 7, 16, 25) is not absolute — both are used of God's creative acts — but bārāʾ appears at key junctures (creation itself, sea creatures and birds, humanity) while ʿāśâ covers the making of the expanse, the luminaries, and land animals. The pattern may signal that bārāʾ marks the truly novel; ʿāśâ covers formation and arrangement of existing material.
1.8: The repeated wayyĕhî-ʿereb wayyĕhî-bōqer ("and there was evening and there was morning") functions as a boundary marker closing each creation day. The waw-consecutive forms (wayyĕhî) keep the narrative moving — these are not isolated static descriptions but links in a temporal chain. The formula's regularity creates an architectural rhythm that the seventh day (2:1-3) will deliberately break.
1.9:
יִקָּווּ (yiqqāwû) — Niphal imperfect of qāwāh (to collect, wait). The waters gather themselves by divine command — the passive (Niphal) voice emphasizes divine causation without direct physical intervention; God speaks and reality conforms.

תֵּרָאֶה (tērāʾeh) — Niphal imperfect, "let the dry ground appear" — again the passive of appearance by command. The land does not so much emerge as become visible as the waters comply. The same verb will describe theophany: the LORD appears (nîrāʾ) to the patriarchs.
1.10: God names the dry ground אֶרֶץ (ʾereṣ, "land/earth") and the gathered waters יַמִּים (yammîm, "seas"). The naming authority exercised in v. 5 continues. The verb wayyiqrāʾ is the same throughout — naming as the royal act of defining and governing what one names.
1.11: לְמִינֵהוּ (lĕmînēhû) — "according to its kind." The phrase appears ten times in ch 1 and establishes the boundaries of reproducible kinds. The Hebrew mîn does not map exactly onto any modern biological category; it describes reproducibility within a kind. The phrase is not a statement about species boundaries but about the orderliness of creation — vegetation reproduces what it is.
1.12: The land "brought forth" (waṯṣēʾ hāʾāreṣ) in response to God's word — the ground is an agent executing the divine command, not merely a passive substrate. This mediated creation (God speaks → earth produces) is the pattern for the sea creatures (v. 20), land animals (v. 24), and eventually humanity (2:7, where God acts more directly). Each creative domain has its own mode of response.
1.13: The evening-morning formula closes the third day. Note that days 1-3 (separation: light/dark, waters/waters, water/land) are structurally paired with days 4-6 (filling: luminaries, birds/fish, land animals/humanity). Day 7 stands outside the pair. This literary architecture is a feature of the Hebrew, not translatable; it signals the text's compositional intentionality.
1.14: אֹתֹת וּמוֹעֲדִים (ʾōtōt ûmôʿăḏîm) — "signs and appointed times." Môʿēd (pl. môʿăḏîm) is the word used for Israel's appointed feasts (Lev 23:2-4) — the sacred calendar. The luminaries are set in the sky to govern the calendar, including the religious calendar; the creation of timekeeping is intrinsically tied to the ordering of worship. This verse is the theological basis for Sabbath and festival reckoning from the creation order.
1.15: The purpose clause — lĕhāʾîr ʿal-hāʾāreṣ ("to give light on the earth") — specifies function over essence. The text is not interested in the physics of luminaries but in their teleological role as servants of God's ordered creation. They are made (ʿāśâ) and placed (nātan) in service of the earth below.
1.16: שְׁנֵי הַמְּאֹרֹת הַגְּדֹלִים (šĕnê hammĕʾōrōt haggĕḏōlîm) — "the two great lights." Notably, the sun and moon are not named in the Hebrew text. The surrounding cultures had names — Shamash (sun), Sin (moon) — that carried divine status. By calling them only "the greater light" and "the lesser light," the text demotes them from deities to instruments. The stars are added almost as an afterthought ("and the stars also"), further reducing their cultic significance.
1.17: God "set them" (wayyittēn) in the expanse — the verb nātan (give, set, place) is used here as a functional placement. The luminaries are placed in a location for a purpose; they have no independent will or divine status. The same word describes giving gifts, placing objects, and assigning roles — here the luminaries are assigned servants, not worshipped lords.
1.18: The evaluation kî-ṭôb follows the luminaries' commission. Three functions are named: governing day and night, separating light from darkness. The threefold statement of purpose before the evaluation recalls the pattern of each creation unit — the goodness is tied to the fitness of the thing for its appointed role.
1.19: The evening-morning formula (fourth day). The structural pair with day 1 (light/dark separation) is now complete: day 1 created the condition, day 4 created the governors of that condition. The Hebrew architecture is clearer than any translation: days 1-3 create realms; days 4-6 fill them in corresponding order.
1.20:
יִשְׁרְצוּ (yišrĕṣû) — "let them swarm/teem." The verb šāraṣ denotes swarming, teeming life — used of small creatures in masses (insects, fish, frogs; cf. Exod 8:3; Lev 11:29). The same root gives šereṣ (swarming things), a major category of Levitical impurity. The use of the verb here is simply about abundance; the later purity connotations are applied differently.

עוֹף (ʿôp) — "flying creatures," not strictly "birds." The root ʿûp means to fly; the category includes all that flies and is broader than modern "birds." The MKT's "birds" approximates; the Hebrew does not exclude flying insects.
1.21:
וַיִּבְרָא — bārāʾ reappears here (day 5) after being absent since v. 1. The reuse marks sea creatures and birds as a category requiring a special creative act — not just forming or making but bārāʾ. The pattern will repeat at v. 27 for humanity. These three bārāʾ uses may mark the three most significant creative boundaries.

הַתַּנִּינִם הַגְּדֹלִים (hattannînim haggĕḏōlîm) — "the great sea creatures." The same word (tannîn) is used elsewhere for the sea-monster Leviathan (Ps 74:13; Isa 27:1). Here it is demythologized: the great sea creatures are made things, not divine adversaries. They are created by God and evaluated as ṭôb.
1.22: וַיְבָרֶךְ אֹתָם אֱלֹהִים — the first divine blessing in Genesis. The blessing formula (bārak) confers fruitfulness and multiplication. The same blessing will be given to humanity (1:28) and later to Noah (9:1). The blessing is functional — it enables and capacitates the creatures to fill their domains. The sea creatures and birds are blessed before any command is given to them beyond reproduction.
1.23: Fifth day formula closes. The architecture of the creation narrative requires that sea/sky (day 5) correspond to the separation of waters/sky (day 2). The filling of the sea and sky is now complete; day 6 will fill the land corresponding to day 3's land/sea distinction.
1.24: נֶפֶשׁ חַיָּה (nepeš ḥayyâ) — "living creatures/souls." The same phrase will describe the human being at 2:7. Nepeš is the animated, living being — not an immaterial soul but the whole creature in its vitality. Land animals and humanity share this description; the distinction comes in how they receive it (ground-production for animals; divine formation and breath for humanity).
1.25: The kî-ṭôb evaluation for land animals. All three categories — ḥayyat hāʾāreṣ (wild beasts), bĕhēmāh (livestock), and remeś (creeping things) — are evaluated good individually in the Hebrew. The MKT's "livestock" for bĕhēmāh captures the domesticated-animal sense (cattle, sheep, donkeys).
1.27:
וַיִּבְרָא אֱלֹהִים אֶת-הָאָדָם בְּצַלְמוֹ — bārāʾ appears three times in this verse: "God created mankind... in his image he created him; male and female he created them." The threefold repetition is poetic (this is one of the few poetic couplets/triplets in the creation narrative) and emphatic. The third bārāʾ expands from the individual to the pair: zākār ûnĕqēbāh (male and female), both are the ṣelem of God, both are created (bārāʾ) — not derived sequentially as in ch 2 but co-created as image-bearers together.

אָדָם (ʾāḏām) — in ch 1 this is the generic term for humankind (collective), not the proper name Adam. The semantic range covers: mankind collectively, a human being, and the proper name Adam (used in ch 5). The MKT renders "mankind" in ch 1 to preserve the collective sense; the shift to the proper name occurs at 2:20 and especially 3:17.
1.28:
וַיְבָרֶךְ אֹתָם — the blessing precedes the mandate: God blesses before commanding. The fertility command (pĕrû ûrĕbû, be fruitful and multiply) is not a law but the activation of a capacity already given in the blessing. The same formula appears at Noah's re-commissioning (9:1, 7) and the Abrahamic promises — the creation mandate is renewed after each catastrophic disruption.

כִּבְשֻׁהָ (kibbĕšûhā) — "subdue it." Kābaš is a strong term (tread down, subdue, bring into subjection — used of subduing enemies, Num 32:22, 29; Josh 18:1). Its use for humanity's relationship to the earth is intentionally robust — the mandate is not gentle management but active, purposeful ordering. This is royal-administrative language, not merely gardening.
1.29: זֹרֵעַ זֶרַע (zōrēaʿ zeraʿ) — "seed-bearing," a participial phrase modifying plants. The preoccupation with seed-bearing points forward to the seed as the mechanism of continuity — the same word zeraʿ will carry the promise of Gen 3:15. In ch 1 it is simply agricultural; its recurrence in 3:15, 12:7, 13:15, 15:3-5, 22:17-18 shows the seed motif gathering theological weight.
1.30: The extension of plant-food to all living creatures (nepeš ḥayyâ) in the original creation establishes a pre-Fall order without predation. The absence of meat-eating in the original mandate (reversed at 9:3 after the Flood) is a textual fact that the Hebrew presents without explicit comment. The kî kēn ("and it was so") formula closes with simple compliance.
1.31: מְאֹד (mĕʾōḏ) — "very." The single adverb elevates the cumulative evaluation beyond each day's ṭôb: all that he had made is not just good but emphatically, thoroughly good. This is the only use of mĕʾōḏ with ṭôb in the creation account — the final assessment is heightened over all partial ones. The sixth day closes the pairing with day 3 and completes the filling of creation's realms.

2.7: wayyitser YHWH Elohim et-haadam afar min-haadam wayyipach beapav nishmat hayyim wayyehi haadam lenefesh hayyah: 'YHWH God formed the man of dust from the ground and breathed into his nostrils the breath of life, and the man became a living creature (nefesh hayyah).' Two observations: (1) wayyitser (formed) uses the imagery of the potter (yatsar) — human embodiment is the result of divine craftsmanship, not accident; (2) nishmat hayyim (breath of life) — the identical phrase is used for all animals in 7:22; what distinguishes humanity is not the breath but the preceding formation in the divine image (1:26-27) and the naming task (2:19-20). Nefesh hayyah (living being/soul) is not a Platonic immortal soul but the whole animated creature — biblical anthropology is holistic: the person is the ensouled body, not a soul housed in a body.
2.24: al-ken yaazov ish et-aviv veimmo vedavak beishto vehayyu levasar echad (ʿal-kēn yaʿăzŏv ʾîš ʾet-ʾābîw wĕʾimmô wĕdābaq bĕʾištô wĕhāyû lĕbāśār ʾeḥād): 'Therefore a man shall leave his father and his mother and hold fast to his wife, and they shall become one flesh.' The narrative aside (al-ken, therefore/for this reason) signals a foundational principle being derived from the narrative. Davak (hold fast/cleave) is the same word used for Israel's covenant-commitment to YHWH (Deut 10:20; Josh 23:8) — marital union is described in covenant-fidelity language. Basar echad (one flesh): not merely physical union but a comprehensive sharing of life, identity, and destiny — which is why Paul calls it a 'profound mystery' (mysterion mega) of the Christ-church relationship.
2.1:
וַיְכֻלּוּ (wayĕkullû) — Pual imperfect of kālāh (to be complete, finished, brought to an end). The passive voice: the heavens and earth were completed — they brought themselves to completion under God's creative word. The same root kālāh will describe the completion of the tabernacle (Exod 40:33) and Solomon's temple (1 Kgs 6:9; 7:51) — a structural echo suggesting that creation itself is a cosmic sanctuary.

צְבָאָם (ṣĕbāʾām) — "their host/array." Ṣābāʾ (army, host) is used of the stars (Deut 4:19; Isa 45:12) and of the angels (1 Kgs 22:19). The term frames creation as an ordered host under the supreme commander — God as the LORD of Hosts (YHWH ṣĕbāʾôt) is already implicit in the creational completion.
2.2:
וַיִּשְׁבֹּת (wayyišbōt) — "and he rested/ceased." The root is šābat, from which "Sabbath" (šabbāt) derives. The word means to stop, cease — not to recover from fatigue (Exod 31:17 explicitly says God did not become weary). God's rest is the completion-rest of a creator whose work is finished, not the rest of a laborer. The Sabbath commandment (Exod 20:11; Deut 5:14-15) points back to this verse as its foundation — Israel rests because God rested.

מְלַאכְתּוֹ (mĕlaʾktô) — "his work." The same word is used of the skilled craftwork of the tabernacle artisans (Exod 35-36). The creation of the cosmos and the construction of the sanctuary are described in parallel vocabulary — the priestly authors of ch 1-2 may have intentionally structured the comparison.
2.3:
וַיְקַדֵּשׁ אֹתוֹ (wayĕqaddēš ʾōtô) — "and he sanctified/made it holy." Qādaš (Piel) means to set apart as holy, consecrate. The seventh day is the first holy thing in Genesis — before the tabernacle, before Sinai, God establishes holiness in time. The Sabbath is a sanctuary in time rather than space, and its sanctification precedes all human worship structures.

The seventh day notably lacks the evening-morning closing formula used for days 1-6. The rabbis observed this absence and interpreted the seventh day as having no end — open-ended, still continuing. Hebrews 4:9-10 builds on this: there remains a Sabbath-rest for the people of God.
2.4:
אֵלֶּה תוֹלְדוֹת (ʾēlleh tôlĕḏôt) — "these are the generations/account of." The formula tôlĕḏôt (from yālad, to bear/beget) recurs ten times in Genesis as a structural marker (2:4; 5:1; 6:9; 10:1; 11:10; 11:27; 25:12; 25:19; 36:1; 37:2). It introduces either a genealogy or a narrative account of what something or someone generated/produced. Here it looks backward: the account of how heavens and earth came to be.

יְהוָה אֱלֹהִים (YHWH ʾĕlōhîm) — the divine name YHWH (the LORD, the personal covenantal name) first appears at 2:4. From here through ch 3, the compound "the LORD God" (YHWH ʾĕlōhîm) is used consistently. The shift from ʾĕlōhîm alone (ch 1) to the compound name (ch 2-3) has long been noted — it introduces the personal, relational, covenant-making God who enters into direct relationship with humanity.
2.5: The verse uses Hebrew circumstantial construction to set the scene before the narrative: "when no shrub... had yet sprung up — for the LORD God had not sent rain... and no man was there to work the ground." This pluperfect-style background clause (the Qal perfect with waw is often pluperfect in context) establishes that ch 2:4ff. is not a sequential continuation of ch 1's day 6 but a close-up on the forming of man before vegetation was established. The agricultural focus (ʿāḇad hāʾăḏāmāh, work the ground) is deliberate: the ground needs a worker, and God provides one.
2.6: אֵד (ʾēḏ) — "mist" or "spring/stream." The term is rare (only here and Job 36:27); its precise meaning is contested. Cognate Akkadian edu means underground water/flood. Most modern scholars favor "springs" or "underground water" rising to water the surface — setting the agricultural scene before rain. The MKT's "streams" reflects this.
2.8: גַּן-בְּעֵדֶן מִקֶּדֶם (gan-bĕʿēḏen miqqeḏem) — "a garden in Eden, in the east." ʿĒḏen may derive from the Sumerian/Akkadian edinu (steppe, plain) or from the Hebrew ʿēḏen (pleasure, luxury — Ps 36:8). The geographic "east" places Eden in the mythological/primordial east — the direction of sunrise and of origins. The garden is a subset within Eden: God planted a garden in Eden, not identified with all of Eden.
2.9:
עֵץ הַחַיִּים (ʿēṣ haḥayyîm) — "the tree of life." Life is a gift mediated through this tree; access to it sustains ongoing life (3:22 confirms that eating from it would grant immortality). The tree recurs in Proverbs (3:18; 11:30; 13:12; 15:4) as a wisdom metaphor, and in Revelation (2:7; 22:2, 14, 19) as the restored eschatological gift. The loss of access to the tree of life (3:24) is the fundamental consequence of the Fall; restoration to it is the fundamental promise of the new creation.

עֵץ הַדַּעַת טוֹב וָרָע (ʿēṣ haddaʿat ṭôb wārāʿ) — "the tree of the knowledge of good and evil." Daʿat is experiential knowledge — not mere information but the knowing that comes from engagement. Ṭôb wārāʿ (good and evil) as a merism may mean comprehensive moral knowledge, or the ability to determine for oneself what is good and evil (the usurpation of divine prerogative). The serpent's promise (3:5) uses exactly this phrase.
2.10: The river description establishes the garden as the source of the world's water supply — creation's life flows outward from Eden. The fourfold division of rivers from a single source is geographic-theological: Eden is the fountainhead. Ezekiel 47 and Revelation 22 both invoke the same image of a river flowing from God's presence to sustain creation.
2.11: הַפִּישׁוֹן — the Pishon is unidentified with certainty; some locate it in Arabia (associated with gold and bdellium). The detail of Havilah's gold (zāhāb) is the only other geographic anchor. The geographic specificity grounds the narrative in real geography without allowing exact modern identification — intentional or simply reflecting ancient knowledge the author assumed.
2.12: בְּדֹלַח (bĕḏōlaḥ) — "bdellium," an aromatic resin (cf. Num 11:7 where manna is compared to it). שֹׁהַם (šōham) — "onyx" or "carnelian" (the exact stone is debated); it appears on the high priest's ephod (Exod 28:9, 20). The precious materials of the land around Eden foreshadow the materials used in the tabernacle and temple — Eden as the original sanctuary, the temple as Eden-recreation.
2.13: The Gihon is the name of the spring of Jerusalem (1 Kgs 1:33, 38, 45), associated with the city of David. Whether the Genesis Gihon is the same location or a symbolic naming, the connection links Eden's water-source with Jerusalem's central spring — the city of God as the restored Eden-center of creation.
2.14: חִדֶּקֶל (ḥiddeqel) — the Tigris, consistently identified (Dan 10:4; Assyrian Idiqlat). פְּרָת (pĕrāt) — the Euphrates. Two of the four rivers are the great rivers of Mesopotamia — the historical-geographic frame of ANE civilization. The Eden narrative is set in recoverable geography even if the garden itself is inaccessible (3:24). The two known rivers anchor the narrative in history.
2.15: לְעָבְדָהּ וּלְשָׁמְרָהּ (lĕʿāḇdāh ûlĕšāmĕrāh) — "to work it and to keep/guard it." ʿĀḇaḏ is the word for service, work, and also worship (ʿăbōḏāh = service/liturgy); šāmar is to guard, keep, watch (used of keeping commandments and keeping the sanctuary). Both terms are used together for the Levitical service of the tabernacle (Num 3:7-8; 8:26; 18:5-6). The man is placed in the garden as a priest-king: his mandate is both cultivation and guardianship, both priestly service and royal oversight.
2.16: כֹּל עֵץ הַגָּן אָכֹל תֹּאכֵל (kōl ʿēṣ haggan ʾākōl tōʾkēl) — the permission is stated with the emphatic infinitive absolute + finite verb (ʾākōl tōʾkēl) — "you may freely eat." The scope of freedom is maximized before the limit is stated. The command in v. 17 comes as restriction within a broadly generous gift, not as a primary prohibition.
2.17: מוֹת תָּמוּת (môt tāmût) — infinitive absolute + finite verb: "you will certainly die" or "dying you will die." The emphatic construction underscores the certainty and totality of the consequence. The serpent's counter (lōʾ mōt tĕmutûn, 3:4) uses the same construction negated — the debate is over the certainty of this very phrase. The death threatened may encompass separation from God, expulsion from the garden, spiritual death, and eventual physical death — all of which follow in ch 3. That the man does not physically die immediately (he lives to 930 years, 5:5) has suggested to many interpreters that the death is first relational/spiritual, with physical death as the ultimate expression.
2.18:
לֹא-טוֹב (lōʾ-ṭôb) — "it is not good." The first lōʾ-ṭôb in the creation narrative — the sevenfold good of ch 1 is here interrupted for the first time. The deficit is not in creation itself but in the incompleteness of the human situation: alone, without a corresponding partner.

עֵזֶר כְּנֶגְדּוֹ (ʿēzer kĕneḡdô) — "a helper corresponding to him" or "a helper as his counterpart." ʿĒzer (helper) is used of God himself as helper to Israel (Ps 121:2; 124:8; Exod 18:4) — the word carries no connotation of inferiority; it describes the role of the one whose strength supplies what is lacking in the other. Kĕneḡdô means "as before him," "face-to-face," "his match" — the preposition negeḏ indicates correspondence and facing, not subordination. The woman is the man's equal counterpart who faces him, not an assistant beneath him.
2.19: The Qal perfect yāṣar ("had formed") here is likely a pluperfect — the animals had already been formed; now they are brought to the man. The narrative interest is not the sequence of creation but the human act of naming. God brings the animals to the man to see "what he would call them" (mah-yiqrāʾ-lô) — God observes with interest the man's exercise of dominion through naming.
2.20: The man names all the animals — exercising the image-bearing dominion of 1:26-28 in the most concrete form. וּלְאָדָם לֹא מָצָא (ûlĕʾāḏām lōʾ māṣāʾ) — "but for the man no helper corresponding to him was found." The naming of animals establishes by negative evidence the uniqueness of humanity's need: no animal corresponds to the man. This sets up the creation of woman not as an afterthought but as the necessary completion that the animal parade has demonstrated to be missing.
2.21:
תַּרְדֵּמָה (tarḏēmāh) — "deep sleep." The same word is used of Abraham's deep sleep during the covenant ceremony (15:12) and of Saul's sleep in the camp (1 Sam 26:12). It is a special, often divinely-induced sleep that accompanies supernatural divine action. The man is passive and unconscious while God acts — the woman's creation is entirely God's work.

אַחַת מִצַּלְעֹתָיו (ʾaḥat miṣṣalʿōtāyw) — "one of his ribs/sides." Ṣēlāʿ is used elsewhere for the side of the ark (Exod 25:12; 37:3) and the side chamber of the temple (1 Kgs 6:34; Ezek 41:5-11). It can mean "rib" (the narrow sense) or "side" (the broader construction sense). Some interpreters read "side" here — God takes one side of the man to form the woman, resulting in two beings from one original. The MKT's "rib" follows the standard English tradition.
2.22: וַיִּבֶן יְהוָה אֱלֹהִים אֶת-הַצֵּלָע (wayyiḇen YHWH ʾĕlōhîm ʾet-haṣṣēlāʿ) — "and the LORD God built/fashioned the rib." The verb bānāh (build) is used here rather than yāṣar (form/shape) or bārāʾ (create). Bānāh is the builder's verb — used of building houses, cities, walls, and the temple. Woman is built/constructed, not merely shaped. The result is then brought (wayyĕbīʾehā) to the man — God as the one who presents the bride, a motif with a long trajectory.
2.23:
זֹאת הַפַּעַם (zōʾt happaʿam) — "this at last" or "this one, now!" The phrase is exclamatory and immediate — Adam's first recorded speech is a recognition and celebration. עֶצֶם מֵעֲצָמַי וּבָשָׂר מִבְּשָׂרִי — "bone of my bones and flesh of my flesh" — a covenant kinship formula (used in 2 Sam 5:1; 19:12-13 for tribal solidarity). The man recognizes the woman as belonging to him in the deepest way — not possessively but kinship-relationally.

לְזֹאת יִקָּרֵא אִשָּׁה כִּי מֵאִישׁ לֻקֳחָה זֹּאת — the ʾîš/ʾiššāh wordplay is explicit: "she shall be called ʾiššāh (woman) because from ʾîš (man) she was taken." The Hebrew pun is the man's own discovery — the naming arises from recognition of origin and correspondence. English "woman/man" or "she-man/man" approximates the wordplay; no translation fully preserves it.
2.25:
עֲרוּמִּים (ʿărûmmîm) — "naked." The word sets up the deliberate wordplay with the serpent's ʿārûm (crafty/shrewd, 3:1) — both words share the same consonantal root in Hebrew. The man and woman are ʿārûmmîm (naked, without concealment) while the serpent is ʿārûm (crafty, without revealing his intent). The innocence of nakedness without shame is the condition the serpent's craftiness will undo.

וְלֹא יִתְבֹּשָׁשׁוּ (wĕlōʾ yitbōšāšû) — "and they were not ashamed." The verb bûš (ashamed, put to shame) denotes the social experience of being exposed and found deficient. Their nakedness produced no shame because there was no covering needed — no moral deficit, no rupture in relationship to God or each other. The contrast with 3:7-10 (shame, hiding, covering) makes the transition from innocence to guilt viscerally apparent.

3.15: veevah asit beincha uvein haisha uvein zaracha uvein zarah tshuv yeshuphenu rosh veata teshuphenu akev: 'I will put enmity between you and the woman, and between your offspring and her offspring; he shall bruise your head, and you shall bruise his heel.' The protoevangelium (first gospel). Key philological issues: (1) The singular zarah (offspring) combined with the masculine singular pronoun hu (he) in the LXX translated as masculine singular autos — a masculine seed of the woman — though the Hebrew zera is collectively singular and the pronoun could be collective. Jewish midrash and Church Fathers both recognized the Messianic potential; (2) shuph (bruise/strike): used for both the heel-strike and the head-strike with the same verb — the same action, different targets, different outcomes. The serpent's strike is temporary (heel); the seed's strike is fatal (head).
3.1:
עָרוּם (ʿārûm) — "shrewd/crafty." The same adjective used positively in Proverbs (12:16, 23; 13:16; 14:8, 15, 18; 22:3; 27:12) for prudent wisdom — here it is the craft that circumvents rather than serves wisdom. The deliberate wordplay with 2:25 (ʿărûmmîm, naked) is a literary signal: the shrewdness of the serpent exploits the openness/nakedness of the human pair.

אַף כִּי-אָמַר אֱלֹהִים — "Did God really say...?" The serpent's opening is not a direct denial but a question that introduces doubt about the scope of the command. Note: the serpent uses ʾĕlōhîm (not YHWH ʾĕlōhîm of ch 2-3), subtly distancing the covenant name. The question misrepresents the command — God had permitted all trees; the serpent asks whether God had prohibited all trees.
3.2: The woman correctly identifies the scope of the permission ("we may eat from the trees in the garden") but her response already engages the serpent's framing. She corrects the misrepresentation but will in v. 3 add to the prohibition — "you must not touch it" — a slight expansion of the original command (2:17 mentions only eating, not touching). Whether this is her memory, her interpretation, or her own addition, it begins the process of misrepresenting the divine word.
3.3: לֹא תֹאכְלוּ מִמֶּנּוּ וְלֹא תִגְּעוּ בּוֹ — "you must not eat from it, and you must not touch it." The addition of "touch" (nāgaʿ) is the woman's addition to the original command (2:17). The prohibition of touching as well as eating represents either a traditional fence around the law (expanding the prohibition to prevent accidental violation), a memory error, or a deliberate elaboration. In any case, the text does not present it as God's word — and the serpent will shortly deny the truth of the word as the woman has stated it.
3.4: לֹא-מוֹת תְּמֻתוּן (lōʾ-môt tĕmutûn) — "you will not certainly die." The serpent uses the same emphatic construction God used in 2:17 (môt tāmût) but negated. It is a direct contradiction of divine speech. The three-part structure of the temptation follows: (1) question/doubt (v. 1), (2) direct denial (v. 4), (3) alternative appeal to self-interest (v. 5).
3.5: כֵּאלֹהִים יֹדְעֵי טוֹב וָרָע (kēʾlōhîm yōḏĕʿê ṭôb wārāʿ) — "like God [or: like gods], knowing good and evil." ʾĔlōhîm is grammatically ambiguous — it could be "like God" (singular referent) or "like the divine beings/gods" (plural). The serpent's claim is that eating will elevate the humans to divine-level autonomous knowledge. The irony: after the Fall, God himself says "the man has become like one of us, knowing good and evil" (3:22) — the serpent's claim is partially confirmed but the cost and mode were not disclosed.
3.6:
כִּי טוֹב הָעֵץ לְמַאֲכָל וְכִי תַאֲוָה-הוּא לָעֵינַיִם — the threefold evaluation mirrors but corrupts the creation evaluations. The woman sees (1) good for food (ṭôb lĕmaʾăkāl), (2) desirable to the eye (taʾăwāh hûʾ lāʿênāyim), (3) desirable for gaining wisdom (neḥmāḏ hāʿēṣ lĕhaśkîl). The three appeals correspond to what 1 John 2:16 calls "the desire of the flesh, the desire of the eyes, and the pride of life." Taʾăwāh (desire, craving) is the same word used of the Israelites' craving in the wilderness (Num 11:4; Ps 106:14).

וַתִּתֵּן גַּם-לְאִישָׁהּ עִמָּהּ וַיֹּאכַל — "and she also gave some to her husband who was with her (ʿimmāh), and he ate." The final phrase is shocking in its brevity: he was with her. Adam was present; he does not argue, question, or resist. His sin is not deception but passive acquiescence — which Paul distinguishes (1 Tim 2:14: "Adam was not deceived, but the woman was deceived and became a transgressor").
3.7:
וַתִּפָּקַחְנָה עֵינֵי שְׁנֵיהֶם (wattippāqaḥnāh ʿênê šĕnêhem) — "and the eyes of both of them were opened." The verb pāqaḥ (to open eyes, to have sight restored) is used of Hagar's eyes being opened to see a well (21:19), of Elisha's prayer that Gehazi's eyes be opened (2 Kgs 6:17), of the blind receiving sight. The irony is that the eyes opened to knowledge that shamed rather than illuminated — they see themselves for the first time as exposed and deficient.

וַיֵּדְעוּ כִּי עֵירֻמִּם הֵם (wayyēdĕʿû kî ʿêrummim hēm) — "and they knew that they were naked." The first thing the newly awakened moral knowledge produces is not wisdom but shame. ʿEirummim is a variant spelling of 2:25's ʿărûmmîm — the connection is deliberate. They were naked before and unashamed; now they are naked and ashamed. The knowledge they gained is first of their own inadequacy before God.
3.8: אֶת-קוֹל יְהוָה אֱלֹהִים מִתְהַלֵּךְ בַּגָּן לְרוּחַ הַיּוֹם — "the sound/voice of the LORD God walking in the garden in the cool of the day." Qôl is "voice" or "sound" — the humans hide from the approaching qôl of God, not necessarily a visible form. Lĕrûaḥ hayyôm — "in the cool of the day" — literally "in the wind/spirit of the day," the late afternoon breeze. The walking suggests a regular pattern: God's presence in the garden was not unusual. Hiding was.
3.9: אַיֶּכָּה (ʾayyekāh) — "Where are you?" The rhetorical question is not informational — God knows. It is an invitation to confess, to come out, to be found. The same form appears in Lam 1:1's "Alas!" (ʾêkāh) over desolated Jerusalem — a connection the rabbis made explicitly. God's "Where are you?" to Adam is echoed in the lament over fallen Israel. The question opens the judicial process that follows.
3.10: אֶת-קֹלְךָ שָׁמַעְתִּי בַּגָּן וָאִירָא — "I heard your voice in the garden and I was afraid." The first instance of fear in Genesis — and of humans fearing God. Before the Fall, God walked in the garden and the humans were unashamed; now the same divine presence produces fear. The disruption of the relationship is total: shame toward each other (v. 7), fear toward God (v. 10). Fear of God will later become a virtue in Israel (the fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom, Prov 9:10) — but this first fear is guilt-driven hiding, not reverent awe.
3.11: God's interrogation moves from location ("Where are you?") to action ("Who told you... Have you eaten...?"). The questions are forensic — establishing facts in the manner of a judicial proceeding. The Hebrew word order in "Have you eaten from the tree from which I commanded you not to eat?" puts the tree first for emphasis: the tree was the prohibited object; eating from it is the charge.
3.12: הָאִשָּׁה אֲשֶׁר נָתַתָּה עִמָּדִי — "The woman whom you gave to be with me." Adam's response implicates both the woman and God: you gave points upward, she gave me points sideways. The man who identified the woman as "bone of my bones" (2:23) now distances himself from her with a relative clause. The relational solidarity of 2:23-24 is instantly fractured. The syntax is revealing: "the woman — whom you gave — she gave me" — the blame-chain is grammatically stacked.
3.13: הַנָּחָשׁ הִשִּׁיאַנִי (hannāḥāš hiššîʾanî) — "the serpent deceived me." Nāšāʾ (Hiphil) means to lead astray, deceive. The woman does not deny the act but names the deceiver. Paul distinguishes Eve's deception from Adam's choice (2 Cor 11:3; 1 Tim 2:14) — both sinned, but by different mechanisms. The woman's response is more honest than the man's (she confesses "and I ate" directly); the man's response contained no explicit confession of eating.
3.14: כִּי עָשִׂיתָ זֹּאת — "because you did this." The serpent's curse is unconditional — no interrogation, no opportunity to respond. The serpent's curse has two parts: (1) lowest among animals, crawling and eating dust; (2) enmity with the woman. The crawling on its belly is a cursed abasement — whatever the serpent's original form, it is now permanently lowered. The dust-eating is likely a metaphor for utter defeat (Ps 72:9; Isa 49:23; Mic 7:17) rather than literal dietary prescription.
3.16:
הַרְבָּה אַרְבֶּה עִצְּבוֹנֵךְ וְהֵרֹנֵךְ — "I will greatly increase your pain in childbearing." The emphatic infinitive absolute construction (harbāh ʾarbbeh, doubling the verbal root) stresses the intensification. ʿIṣṣābôn (pain/toil) is the same word used of the man's agricultural toil in v. 17 — both curses are expressed in the same vocabulary, suggesting a structural parallel between the woman's domain (childbearing) and the man's (ground-working), both now marked by painful labor.

וְאֶל-אִישֵׁךְ תְּשׁוּקָתֵךְ וְהוּא יִמְשָׁל-בָּךְ — "your desire (tĕšûqāh) will be for your husband, and he will rule (māšal) over you." Tĕšûqāh (desire/longing) appears three times in the OT: here, in Song of Songs 7:10 (the husband's desire for the wife — reciprocal), and Gen 4:7 (sin's desire to control Cain — context is mastery/control). The word's connotation in 4:7 has led some to interpret the woman's desire here as a desire to control or dominate, with the man's ruling as the counteracting consequence. Others read it as a positive longing rendered unequal by the man's rule. Either way, the ordered partnership of 2:18-24 is now marked by tension and asymmetry — a description of the fallen state, not a prescription for the ideal order.
3.17: אֲרוּרָה הָאֲדָמָה בַּעֲבוּרֶךָ (ʾărûrāh hāʾăḏāmāh baʿăḇûrekā) — "cursed is the ground because of you." Note: the man is not cursed directly (as the serpent was, v. 14) but the ʾăḏāmāh (ground) is cursed because of him. The wordplay between ʾāḏām (man) and ʾăḏāmāh (ground) — established at 2:7 — now carries its full weight: the man is locked in a cursed relationship with the very source from which he was formed. ʿIṣṣābôn (toil/pain) — the same word as the woman's pain in v. 16: both curses produce ʿiṣṣābôn, labor transformed into painful striving.
3.18: קוֹץ וְדַרְדַּר (qôṣ wĕdardar) — "thorns and thistles." The earth produces what opposes cultivation rather than facilitating it. The same pairing appears in Hos 10:8 as an image of divine judgment. The thorn/thistle combination signals that the ground is no longer a willing partner in the man's vocation — it resists, frustrates, and competes with the cultivated crop.
3.19: בְּזֵעַת אַפֶּיךָ תֹּאכַל לֶחֶם (bĕzēʿat ʾappêkā tōʾkal leḥem) — "by the sweat of your face you will eat bread." The preposition phrase establishes the new mode: by painful exertion, not the easy abundance of Eden. עַד שׁוּבְךָ אֶל-הָאֲדָמָה כִּי מִמֶּנָּה לֻקָּחְתָּ — "until you return to the ground, for from it you were taken." The Pual perfect luqqāḥtā (you were taken) connects back to 2:7 and 2:23 — the same verb describes the rib taken from Adam to make Eve. Now the man is taken from the ground and will be returned to it. כִּי-עָפָר אַתָּה וְאֶל-עָפָר תָּשׁוּב — "for dust you are, and to dust you shall return." The inclusio with 2:7 (ʿāpār min-hāʾăḏāmāh) frames the human story: dust-sourced, dust-destined.
3.20: חַוָּה (ḥawwāh) — "Eve." The name is connected to ḥay (living) — she is "the mother of all living" (ʾēm kol-ḥāy). The naming of his wife after the curse is itself a statement of faith: in the moment of the death-sentence, the man names the woman "Life-giver." The act anticipates the zeraʿ promise of v. 15 — through the woman, life will continue and the promised offspring will come.
3.21: כָּתְנוֹת עוֹר (kotnôt ʿôr) — "garments of skin." God himself clothes the human pair — the first act of divine provision after judgment. The skin garments require the death of an animal; whether this implies sacrifice is implied but not stated. The contrast with the fig-leaf coverings (ḥăgorāh, 3:7) is significant: human covering is insufficient and temporary; divine covering is permanent and adequate. The theological tradition reads this as the first atonement-picture.
3.22:
הֵן הָאָדָם הָיָה כְּאַחַד מִמֶּנּוּ לָדַעַת טוֹב וָרָע — "Behold, the man has become like one of us, knowing good and evil." The serpent's promise (3:5) was not entirely false — the knowledge was gained. But what the serpent concealed was the cost and the mode: knowledge through disobedience, not through maturation under God's guidance. The divine "us" recurs (as in 1:26) — still unexplained in the text itself.

וְעַתָּה פֶּן-יִשְׁלַח יָדוֹ — "and now, lest he reach out his hand." The concern is not that immortality would be too good for the fallen human but that immortality in the condition of moral ruin would be unremitting misery — a fallen being locked into endless life. The expulsion from Eden is thus an act of mercy as well as judgment: the way to the tree of life is blocked until the condition that makes the tree deadly can be reversed.
3.23: וַיְשַׁלְּחֵהוּ יְהוָה אֱלֹהִים מִגַּן-עֵדֶן (wayĕšallĕḥēhû YHWH ʾĕlōhîm miggan-ʿēḏen) — "and the LORD God sent him out from the Garden of Eden." Šāllaḥ (Piel, intensive) means to send out forcefully — used of divorce (Deut 24:1-3) and of expulsion. The same verb will appear when Abraham sends out Hagar (21:14) and when the Egyptians expel Israel (Exod 12:33). The divine expulsion is an irrevocable change of status.
3.24:
אֶת-הַכְּרֻבִים (ʾet-hakkĕrubîm) — "the cherubim." The cherubim appear here for the first time — divine guardians who prevent unauthorized access to the holy. They will appear as the guardians of the ark of the covenant (Exod 25:18-22; 37:7-9), woven into the tabernacle curtains (Exod 26:1), carved into Solomon's temple (1 Kgs 6:23-29), and in Ezekiel's throne-chariot visions (Ezek 1:5-25; 10). They are the guardians of the divine throne and presence; here they guard the tree of life.

אֵת לַהַט הַחֶרֶב הַמִּתְהַפֶּכֶת (ʾêt lahaṭ haḥereḇ hammithappe|ket) — "the flaming/turning sword." Lahaṭ (flame) + the Hitpael participle of hāpak (to turn, overturn) — the sword turns in every direction, covering every angle of approach. The garden is sealed: the cherubim guard the eastern entrance, the sword covers all other approaches. The way back to Eden is completely blocked — until Revelation 22 shows the tree of life in the New Jerusalem, accessible again.

15.6: vehemin baYHWH wayyachsheveha lo tsedaqah (wĕheʾĕmīn bY̌HWH wayyaḥšĕbehā lô ṣĕdāqāh): 'And he believed YHWH, and he counted it to him as righteousness.' The most theologically dense sentence in Genesis. Heamin (believed/trusted): Hiphil of aman — not merely intellectual assent but the full posture of reliable trust, the same root as amen. Wayachsheveha (counted/reckoned/credited): the accounting verb used in financial and legal contexts — credit assigned. Tsedaqah (righteousness): the right-relational standing before God that Abraham receives not by doing but by trusting. Paul (Rom 4:3; Gal 3:6) and James (2:23) both cite this verse as definitional for faith-righteousness; their different emphases (Paul: before circumcision, therefore not by works; James: completed by the Aqedah of ch. 22, therefore enacted by obedience) are both grounded in the canonical shape of Genesis.
15.1:
אַחַר הַדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה (ʾaḥar haddĕḇārîm hāʾēlleh) — "after these things." ʾAḥar haddĕḇārîm hāʾēlleh is a recurring narrative connective in Genesis (22:1, 20; 39:7; 40:1; 48:1) that signals a significant event following a major one — the Melchizedek episode and the Sodom-king's offer are the preceding context; the covenant ceremony follows.

אַל-תִּירָא אַבְרָם אָנֹכִי מָגֵן לָךְ — "do not be afraid, Abram; I am your shield." The divine "do not fear" (ʾal-tîrāʾ) formula is the standard angelic and prophetic reassurance (cf. 26:24; Isa 41:10, 13, 14; 43:1, 5; Dan 10:12, 19). Māgēn (shield) — God as military protector; the same root as māgan in 14:20 ("delivered your enemies"). The shield image is explicitly military: having just refused Sodom's goods and made enemies of the Elamite coalition, Abram may fear reprisal. שְׂכָרְךָ הַרְבֵּה מְאֹד — "your reward/wages very great." Śāḵār (reward, wages, compensation) — God himself is the reward; the doubled intensifier harbēh mĕʾōḏ is emphatic.
15.2: אֲדֹנָי יֱהֹוִה מַה-תִּתֶּן-לִי — "Sovereign LORD, what will you give me?" The double divine address ʾAḏōnāy YHWH (Lord YHWH) is the form of heightened supplication used in lament and petition (Deut 3:24; Josh 7:7; Judg 6:22; Ezek 2:4; 4:14; Amos 7:2, 5). Abram complains to God — a bold but covenant-legitimate speech act. וְאָנֹכִי הוֹלֵךְ עֲרִירִי — "and I am going childless." ʿArîrî (childless, stripped) — from ʿārar (to strip bare, to lay waste); the LXX reads ametatithetos; the word implies desolate barrenness, not merely the absence of children. The heir by default is Eliezer of Damascus — a household servant who would inherit by ANE custom if the master died childless (attested in Nuzi adoption contracts).
15.3: בֶּן-בֵּיתִי יוֹרֵשׁ אֹתִי — "a son of my house will be my heir." Ben-bêtî (son of my house) = household servant; yôrēš (heir, one who inherits) from yāraš (to possess, inherit, dispossess). Abram states the problem plainly: without a biological heir, his estate and the covenant promise will pass through a servant. The word of YHWH must address this directly — which it does in v. 4.
15.4: לֹא יִירָשְׁךָ זֶה כִּי-אִם אֲשֶׁר יֵצֵא מִמֵּעֶיךָ — "this one will not be your heir, but one who comes from your own body." ʾĂšer yēṣēʾ mimmēʿêḵā (who comes forth from your inward parts/loins) — meʿeh (inward parts, bowels, the seat of emotion and reproduction) is the most intimate bodily term possible for biological descent. The promise eliminates the servant-heir category entirely: the covenant passes only through biological offspring.
15.5: הַשָּׁמַיְמָה וּסְפֹר הַכּוֹכָבִים — "look toward the heavens and count the stars." The locative haššāmaymāh (toward-the-heavens, with directional heh) and the imperative sĕpōr (count!) stage a visual-rhetorical challenge: "if indeed you can count them." The night-sky is then announced as the metaphor for the offspring (kōkāḇîm, stars) — the most sublime natural image of innumerability. The star metaphor here complements the dust metaphor of 13:16 and the sand metaphor of 22:17. The LXX follows the MT closely; Paul cites this verse in its LXX form in Rom 4:18.
15.7: אֲנִי יְהוָה אֲשֶׁר הוֹצֵאתִיךָ מֵאוּר כַּשְׂדִּים — "I am the LORD who brought you out of Ur of the Chaldeans." The divine self-identification formula ʾănî YHWH (I am the LORD) is the same as the Sinai declaration of Exod 20:2 ("I am the LORD your God who brought you out of Egypt") — the Ur/Exodus parallelism is structural: YHWH defined himself to Moses by the Exodus just as he defines himself to Abram by Ur. The verb hôṣēʾtî (Hiphil perfect: I brought out, I caused to go out) marks the completed past act of divine election — the calling is already accomplished.
15.8: בַּמָּה אֵדַע כִּי אִירָשֶׁנָּה — "how shall I know that I will inherit it?" Abram asks for a sign — immediately after faith has been credited to him (v. 6). This is not a contradiction but a feature of OT faith: asking for confirmation does not negate trust, it specifies its object. Compare Gideon's signs (Judg 6:36-40), Hezekiah's sign (2 Kgs 20:8), and Mary's question to Gabriel (Luke 1:34) — all are signs requested within the context of genuine faith. God answers not with rebuke but with a covenant ceremony.
15.9: קְחָה לִי עֶגְלָה מְשֻׁלֶּשֶׁת — "take for me a heifer three years old." Mĕšulleššet (three-year-old, tripled) — the animals specified (heifer, goat, ram each three years old, plus a turtledove and young pigeon) represent a combination of large and small animals used in various sacrificial contexts. Three-year-old animals are mature, prime animals. The dove and pigeon pair appears in Leviticus purity offerings (Lev 1:14; 5:7-11) — the entire list suggests a complete sacrifice, not a specific rite. The cutting of the animals (v. 10) is the covenant ceremony.
15.10: וַיְבַתֵּר אֹתָם בַּתָּוֶךְ (waybatter ʾōtām battāweḵ) — "he cut them in two down the middle." Bāttar (to cut in pieces) — a rare verb; the noun bĕtārîm (pieces cut in two) in v. 17 is related. This cutting-in-two is the act that enables the covenant ceremony: the parties pass between the halves, invoking upon themselves the fate of the cut animals if they violate the covenant (cf. Jer 34:18-20: "I will make the men who violated my covenant like the calf they cut in two and then walked between its pieces"). The birds are not cut — a deliberate distinction that has been explained variously (they are too small; the dove/pigeon pair serves a different sacrificial function).
15.11: וַיֵּרֶד הָעַיִט עַל-הַפְּגָרִים וַיַּשֵּׁב אֹתָם אַבְרָם — "birds of prey came down on the carcasses, and Abram drove them away." ʿAyiṭ (birds of prey, raptors) — the vulture/raptor image in the OT often denotes menace and desolation (Job 28:7; Isa 46:11 where the "ravenous bird from the east" = Cyrus; Matt 24:28 / Luke 17:37). Abram actively defends the covenant-sacrifice from defilement — he maintains the integrity of the ceremony even in the gap before God arrives. Some read the birds as a prophetic symbol of the foreign nations that will oppress Israel during the 400-year sojourn.
15.12: תַּרְדֵּמָה נָפְלָה עַל-אַבְרָם (tardēmāh nāplāh ʿal-ʾaḇrām) — "a deep sleep fell upon Abram." Tardēmāh (deep sleep, torpor) — the same word as Adam's deep sleep during the creation of the woman (2:21). The word appears in contexts of divine initiative: God acts while the human is unconscious; the sleep signals that the covenant's establishment will be entirely divine. וְאֵימָה חֲשֵׁכָה גְדֹלָה נֹפֶלֶת עָלָיו — "a great dark terror was falling on him." ʾÊmāh (terror, dread) + ḥăšēḵāh (darkness) + gĕḏōlāh (great) — the triple intensifiers describe the numinous dread of theophanic nearness (cf. Exod 20:18-21; the disciples' fear at the transfiguration, Matt 17:6; Ezekiel's face-down prostration, Ezek 1:28).
15.13: יָדֹעַ תֵּדַע כִּי-גֵר יִהְיֶה זַרְעֲךָ — "know with certainty that your offspring will be sojourners." The infinitive absolute yādōaʿ tēḏaʿ (know truly, surely know) — emphatic double of the same root, conveying absolute certainty. What follows is direct prophecy: 400 years of sojourning in a foreign land, slavery, and oppression. Gēr (sojourner, resident alien) — the status Abram himself inhabits in Canaan (17:8; 23:4; Ps 105:12). The offspring will be sojourners in someone else's land — Egypt — as Abram is a sojourner in Canaan. The parallel is intentional.
15.14: וְגַם אֶת-הַגּוֹי אֲשֶׁר יַעֲבֹדוּ דָּן אָנֹכִי — "but also the nation whom they serve I will judge." Dān ʾānōḵî (I will judge) — the same verb root as the name Dan (Judg 5:10). God promises to judge the enslaving nation — the Exodus will be an act of divine justice against Egypt, not merely rescue of Israel. וְאַחֲרֵי-כֵן יֵצְאוּ בִּרְכֻשׁ גָּדוֹל — "afterward they will come out with great possessions." The Exodus plunder of Egypt (Exod 12:35-36) is prophesied here, 400 years before it happens — the vocabulary (reḵûš, goods) echoes the same word used for Abram's wealth in 13:2 and the Sodom spoils in 14:11-12.
15.15: וְאַתָּה תָּבוֹא אֶל-אֲבֹתֶיךָ בְּשָׁלוֹם — "but you, you will go to your fathers in peace." The personal promise to Abram contrasts with the collective fate of his offspring: they will suffer for 400 years; he will die in peace. ʾEl-ʾăḇōteḵā (to your fathers) is an expression for dying and being gathered to the ancestors — the clan-death formula that implies connection beyond death. תִּקָּבֵר בְּשֵׂיבָה טוֹבָה — "you will be buried at a good old age." Śêḇāh ṭôḇāh (good old age, a ripe old age) — the covenant life fulfillment formula; Abram dies at 175 (25:7-8) "at a ripe old age, an old man and satisfied with years" — the precise fulfillment of this promise.
15.16: וְדוֹר רְבִיעִי יָשׁוּבוּ הֵנָּה — "and in the fourth generation they will return here." The "400 years" of v. 13 and the "fourth generation" here have generated considerable discussion: a generation of 100 years would make four generations = 400 years (and the Exodus genealogy for Moses in Exod 6:16-20 is compressed to four generations from Levi). כִּי לֹא-שָׁלֵם עֲוֹן הָאֱמֹרִי עַד-הֵנָּה — "for the sin of the Amorites is not yet full." Šālēm (complete, full) — moral completion as the trigger for judgment. God delays Israel's possession of Canaan by 400 years because the Canaanites' sin has not yet "ripened." The divine patience extends even to the wicked: judgment awaits the full measure of guilt. This principle also implies a moral dimension to the conquest: Israel displaces Canaan not arbitrarily but as a judgment on specific, fully-developed wickedness.
15.17: תַּנּוּר עָשָׁן וְלַפִּיד אֵשׁ אֲשֶׁר עָבַר בֵּין הַגְּזָרִים הָאֵלֶּה — "a smoking furnace and a blazing torch that passed between these pieces." The theophanic fire symbols — tannûr ʿāšān (smoking furnace/brazier) and lappîḏ ʾēš (torch of fire) — represent the divine presence. The divine fire is also the wilderness pillar (Exod 13:21-22), the Sinai theophany (Exod 19:18; Deut 4:11-12), and the Shekinah (1 Kgs 8:10-11). Critically, only the fire-symbols pass between the pieces — Abram is asleep (v. 12). The covenant is entirely divine in initiation, execution, and guarantee: YHWH alone walks between the animal halves, placing the covenant obligation entirely on himself. This unilateral structure is the grammar of grace — the Abrahamic covenant's unconditional character rests on God's self-commitment alone.
15.18: כָּרַת יְהוָה אֶת-אַבְרָם בְּרִית (kārat YHWH ʾet-ʾaḇrām bĕrît) — "the LORD cut a covenant with Abram." Kārat bĕrît (to cut a covenant) — the idiom derives from the cutting of the animals in the ceremony (vv. 9-10): to cut a covenant is to perform the sacrifice that ratifies it. The land boundaries follow: from naḥal miṣrāyim (the brook/wadi of Egypt, likely the Wadi el-Arish on the Sinai border) to the Euphrates — the full extent of the Davidic-Solomonic empire (1 Kgs 4:21; 8:65). The covenant land exceeds what Israel ever controlled in history, pointing the promise toward an eschatological fulfillment.
15.19: The קֵינִי (Kenites), קְנִזִּי (Kenizzites), and קַדְמֹנִי (Kadmonites) are listed first in the ten-nation land-promise list. The Kenites appear later as allies of Israel (Judg 1:16; 4:11, 17; 5:24; 1 Sam 15:6). The Kenizzites are associated with Caleb's clan (Num 32:12; Josh 14:6, 14) — Caleb "the Kenizzite" receives Hebron as his inheritance, the very city of Abram's altar (13:18). The list of ten peoples is broader than the standard seven-nation list of the Conquest period (Deut 7:1; Josh 3:10), suggesting an original land promise of greater scope than what the Conquest actually reached.
15.20: The חִתִּי (Hittites), פְּרִזִּי (Perizzites), and רְפָאִים (Rephaim) continue the land-promise list. The Hittites appear in Abram's own lifetime as the sellers of the Machpelah cave (ch. 23 — Ephron the Hittite); the Perizzites are among the Canaanite peoples whose territory will be Israelite territory (Exod 3:8, 17). The Rephaim appear already as a conquered people in ch. 14 (v. 5) — the covenant land includes territory that even the eastern coalition had swept through.
15.21: The final group in the land-promise list: אֱמֹרִי (Amorites, the highland Canaanites whose sin is "not yet full" in v. 16), כְּנַעֲנִי (Canaanites), גִּרְגָּשִׁי (Girgashites), and יְבוּסִי (Jebusites — the pre-Israelite inhabitants of Jerusalem). The ten-nation list frames the promised land as the entire territory of the pre-existing peoples — the Abrahamic covenant is simultaneously a land-grant and a judgment oracle. The moral framework of v. 16 (the Amorites' sin not yet full) prevents the land-promise from being mere territorial ambition: the transfer of the land is grounded in divine moral justice, not ethnic favoritism.

22.14: YHWH yireh asher yeamer hayom behar YHWH yeraeh (Yhwh yirʾeh ... ʾăšer yēʾāmēr hayyôm bĕhar Yhwh yērāʾeh): 'YHWH will provide / YHWH will be seen.' The name YHWH-Yireh (YHWH will see/provide) is a word-play: raah means both 'see' and 'provide' (to see to it = to provide). The place-name combines both meanings: on this mountain YHWH sees and is seen. The site is identified with Jerusalem and eventually Mount Moriah (2 Chr 3:1), where Solomon built the temple. The Aqedah's theology: the provision of the ram in place of Isaac establishes the substitution-pattern that the entire sacrificial system develops and that Christ fulfills finally.
22.1: וַיְהִי אַחַר הַדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה (wayhî ʾaḥar haddĕḇārîm hāʾēlleh) — "after these things." The narrative connective following the treaty and Beersheba period. וְהָאֱלֹהִים נִסָּה אֶת-אַבְרָהָם (wĕhāʾĔlōhîm nissāh ʾet-ʾaḇrāhām) — "and God tested Abraham." Nissāh (Piel of nāsāh, to test, try, prove) — the narrator reveals the test's nature immediately, so the reader knows what Abraham does not. The same verb is used for the wilderness testing of Israel (Exod 15:25; 16:4; 20:20; Deut 8:2, 16). Testing (nissāyôn) in the OT is not divine cruelty but divine disclosure: the test reveals what is already in the heart. Hinnēnî (here I am, ready, present) — Abraham's response; the formula of radical availability to God's call (used also in Exod 3:4; Isa 6:8).
22.2: קַח-נָא אֶת-בִּנְךָ אֶת-יְחִידְךָ אֲשֶׁר-אָהַבְתָּ אֶת-יִצְחָק — "take now your son, your only son, whom you love, Isaac." The five-fold specification is deliberately cumulative: (1) bĕnḵā (your son); (2) yĕḥîḏĕḵā (your only son — from yāḥîḏ, only, unique, alone); (3) ʾăšer-ʾāhaḇtā (whom you love — the first use of the verb ʾāhab, love, in the OT); (4) ʾet-yiṣḥāq (Isaac — named last, after the emotional buildup). John 3:16 echoes this structure: "his one and only Son" (monogenē) and "loved" (ēgápēsen). לֶךְ-לְךָ — the second great lēḵ-lĕḵā command, matching 12:1; the two commands bracket the Abraham narrative.
22.3: וַיַּשְׁכֵּם אַבְרָהָם בַּבֹּקֶר — "Abraham rose early in the morning." No recorded hesitation, no dialogue with Sarah, no delay — the narrative's silence on the night between the command and the morning is as significant as any speech could be. Wayyaḥăbōš ʾet-ḥămōrô (he saddled his donkey) — the patriarch himself saddles the animal, not his servants; the personal involvement signals the seriousness and directness of the response. וַיְבַקַּע עֲצֵי עֹלָה (he cut the wood for the burnt offering) — the preparatory work is complete before the journey begins; Abraham takes the wood, the fire (ʾēš), and the knife (maʾăḵelet, cleaver, knife for slaughter).
22.4: בַּיּוֹם הַשְּׁלִישִׁי (bayyôm haššĕlîšî, on the third day) — "the third day" in the OT carries the weight of divine action following a period of apparent inactivity: deliverance at the Red Sea (Exod 14-15 measured from departure), resurrection (Hos 6:2; Jon 1:17; 1 Cor 15:4), the giving of the Torah (Exod 19:11, 16). Hebrews 11:19 explicitly reads the Akedah through the resurrection lens: Abraham "considered that God was able even to raise him from the dead, from which, figuratively speaking, he did receive him back." The three-day journey places the Akedah in the theological register of death-and-restoration. וַיַּשָּׂא אַבְרָהָם אֶת-עֵינָיו — "Abraham lifted up his eyes" — the seeing-command formula of 13:14 now fulfilled by Abraham's own initiative.
22.5: שְׁבוּ-לָכֶם פֹּה עִם-הַחֲמוֹר — "stay here with the donkey." The two servants are left behind; father and son go alone. אֲנִי וְהַנַּעַר נֵלְכָה עַד-כֹּה (ʾănî wĕhannāʿar nēlĕḵāh ʿaḏ-kōh) — "I and the boy will go over there." וְנִשְׁתַּחֲוֶה וְנָשׁוּבָה אֲלֵיכֶם (wĕništaḥăweh wĕnāšûḇāh ʾălêḵem) — "we will worship and we will return to you." The first-person plural "we" is either (1) Abraham's faith statement: he believed both of them would return (Heb 11:19); or (2) a social euphemism to prevent the servants from interfering. The grammar permits both readings; the theology of Heb 11:19 takes the first seriously.
22.6: וַיַּשֶׂם אַבְרָהָם אֶת-עֲצֵי הָעֹלָה עַל-יִצְחָק בְּנוֹ — "Abraham placed the wood of the burnt offering on his son Isaac." The wood is placed on the son — a detail noted in patristic reading as a type of the cross-bearing (John 19:17: Jesus "carrying his own cross"). Whether intended by the narrator or not, the structural parallel is precise: the son carries the instrument of his own sacrifice. וַיִּקַּח בְּיָדוֹ אֶת-הָאֵשׁ וְאֶת-הַמַּאֲכֶלֶת (he took in his hand the fire and the knife) — Abraham carries the dangerous items himself, protecting Isaac from the fire and the knife.
22.7: הִנֶּה הָאֵשׁ וְהָעֵצִים וְאַיֵּה הַשֶּׂה לְעֹלָה — "the fire and the wood are here, but where is the lamb for the burnt offering?" Isaac's question is innocent and devastating: he sees the preparations, notes the missing element, asks. Śeh (lamb, sheep/goat for offering) — the specific sacrificial animal vocabulary. The question hangs in the air as the central tension of the narrative: where is the offering? The answer of v. 8 is at once evasive and prophetic.
22.8: אֱלֹהִים יִרְאֶה-לּוֹ הַשֶּׂה לְעֹלָה בְּנִי — "God will provide for himself the lamb for the burnt offering, my son." Yirʾeh-llô — the reflexive dative (lô, for himself) with the verb rāʾāh (to see): "God will see/provide for himself." This is the etymological basis of the name YHWH-Yireh in v. 14 (YHWH yirʾeh): where God sees, God provides. The expression is ambiguous in the moment — Abraham may be speaking a genuine theological principle without knowing how it will apply; the narrative reveals that the principle was literally and immediately true. וַיֵּלְכוּ שְׁנֵיהֶם יַחְדָּו (the two of them went together) — the phrase is repeated from v. 6 and will occur again after Isaac's question: "together" (yaḥdāw) at every stage, despite the terrible knowledge Abraham carries.
22.9: וַיַּעֲקֹד אֶת-יִצְחָק בְּנוֹ (wayyaʿăqōḏ ʾet-yiṣḥāq bĕnô) — "he bound his son Isaac." ʿĀqaḏ (to bind, tie — specifically the binding of feet or limbs; this verb appears only here in the OT) — the Akedah takes its name from this word. The binding of Isaac is the technical term for the preparatory restraint of a sacrifice before slaughter. וַיָּשֶׂם אֹתוֹ עַל-הַמִּזְבֵּחַ מִמַּעַל לָעֵצִים (he laid him on the altar, on top of the wood) — the altar, the wood, the bound son: the preparation is complete. There is no recorded resistance from Isaac, no recorded cry from Abraham.
22.10: וַיִּשְׁלַח אַבְרָהָם אֶת-יָדוֹ וַיִּקַּח אֶת-הַמַּאֲכֶלֶת לִשְׁחֹט אֶת-בְּנוֹ — "Abraham stretched out his hand and took the knife to slaughter his son." The verb šāḥaṭ (to slaughter, kill in the ritual sense used for sacrificial animals — cf. Lev 1:5; 3:2, 8, 13) confirms that the act Abraham was about to perform was not murder but sacrifice: he was treating his son as a sacrificial animal, as God had commanded. The hand is stretched out (šālaḥ yāḏ) — the same phrase as when God stretched out his hand in judgment (Exod 7:19; 9:15).
22.11: וַיִּקְרָא אֵלָיו מַלְאַךְ יְהוָה מִן-הַשָּׁמַיִם — "the angel of the LORD called to him from heaven." The intervention is from heaven — divine authority from above; in ch. 16 and 21 the angel appeared in the desert; here the heavenly call matches the elevated location (the mountain) and the elevation of the stakes. אַבְרָהָם אַבְרָהָם — the doubled name is the divine address of urgent importance: Moses! Moses! (Exod 3:4); Samuel! Samuel! (1 Sam 3:10); Martha, Martha! (Luke 10:41); Saul, Saul! (Acts 9:4). The repetition halts the action at the critical moment. Hinnēnî — "here I am" — the same response as v. 1; Abraham remains in readiness even at the moment of the knife.
22.12: כִּי עַתָּה יָדַעְתִּי כִּי-יְרֵא אֱלֹהִים אַתָּה (kî ʿattāh yāḏaʿtî kî-yĕrēʾ ʾĔlōhîm ʾattāh) — "for now I know that you are a God-fearer." Yāḏaʿtî (I have come to know, I know) — the divine statement of knowing after the test does not imply prior ignorance; it is a forensic declaration: the test has produced observable evidence that the claim of faith is real. לֹא חָשַׂכְתָּ אֶת-בִּנְךָ אֶת-יְחִידְךָ מִמֶּנִּי — "you have not withheld your son, your only son, from me." Ḥāśaḵ (to withhold, spare, hold back) — Paul cites the parallel in Rom 8:32: "He who did not spare (ouk epheisato) his own Son but gave him up for us all" — the language of the Akedah is applied to the Father's giving of the Son.
22.13: אַיִל אַחַר נֶאֱחַז בַּסְּבַךְ בְּקַרְנָיו — "a ram caught in a thicket by its horns." ʾAyil (ram, adult male sheep — specifically the animal used in burnt offerings, Lev 8:18-21; Num 29:2) — the animal is a ram, not the śeh (lamb) that Isaac asked about. The ram appears caught — God's provision arrives already prepared, already there; the ram was caught in the thicket while Abraham was raising the knife. Wayyiqaḥ ʾet-hāʾayil wayyaʿălēhû lĕʿōlāh taḥat bĕnô (he took the ram and offered it as a burnt offering instead of his son) — taḥat (instead of, in place of, as a substitute for) — substitutionary sacrifice: the ram dies in the place of the son.
22.15: וַיִּקְרָא מַלְאַךְ יְהוָה אֶל-אַבְרָהָם שֵׁנִית מִן-הַשָּׁמָיִם — "the angel of the LORD called to Abraham a second time from heaven." The second heavenly word (vv. 16-18) is the divine oath — the highest level of divine commitment possible. The first call (vv. 11-12) halted the sacrifice; the second call confirms the covenant in the most solemn form. The two calls frame the Akedah's resolution: rescue (first call) and covenant renewal (second call).
22.16: בִּי נִשְׁבַּעְתִּי (bî nišbaʿtî, by myself I have sworn) — the divine self-oath. God swears by himself because there is nothing greater to swear by — this is the theological argument of Heb 6:13-17: "since he had no one greater by whom to swear, he swore by himself." The self-oath (bî nišbaʿtî = literally "by me I have sworn") elevates the covenant to its highest possible register. יַעַן אֲשֶׁר עָשִׂיתָ אֶת-הַדָּבָר הַזֶּה וְלֹא חָשַׂכְתָּ אֶת-בִּנְךָ אֶת-יְחִידֶךָ — "because you have done this and have not withheld your son, your only son" — the covenant renewal is explicitly conditional on Abraham's obedience, but the oath makes the fulfillment unconditional once given.
22.17: כִּי-בָרֵךְ אֲבָרֶכְךָ וְהַרְבָּה אַרְבֶּה אֶת-זַרְעֲךָ (kî-ḇārēḵ ʾăḇārĕḵĕḵā wĕharbbāh ʾarbbeh ʾet-zarʿĕḵā) — "I will surely bless you and greatly multiply your offspring." Two emphatic infinitive absolutes (bārēḵ ʾăḇārĕḵĕḵā; harbāh ʾarbbeh) — the most emphatic Hebrew grammar for absolute certainty. כְּכוֹכְבֵי הַשָּׁמַיִם וְכַחוֹל אֲשֶׁר עַל-שְׂפַת הַיָּם — "as the stars of the sky and as the sand on the seashore" — both innumerability images combined in a single verse, restating and expanding 15:5 (stars) and 13:16 (dust). The offspring promise restated after the test with maximum rhetorical force. וְיִרַשׁ זַרְעֲךָ אֵת שַׁעַר אֹיְבָיו — "your offspring will possess the gate of their enemies" — the gate (šaʿar) = the city; possessing the gate = conquering the city; the promise anticipates the conquest and establishment of the Davidic kingdom.
22.18: וְהִתְבָּרֲכוּ בְזַרְעֲךָ כֹּל גּוֹיֵי הָאָרֶץ — "all nations of the earth will be blessed through your offspring." Wĕhitbārăḵû — Hithpael (reflexive-passive: they will bless themselves through / they will be blessed through). The form here (Hithpael) creates ambiguity between reflexive ("all nations will bless themselves by your offspring") and passive ("all nations will be blessed through your offspring") — Gal 3:8 and Acts 3:25 quote this verse as passive (echoing 12:3 where the Niphal form appears). The promise ends with the most universal scope: kol gôyê hāʾāreṣ (all nations of the earth), reaching far beyond the land of Canaan to embrace the entire human community.
22.19: וַיָּשָׁב אַבְרָהָם אֶל-נְעָרָיו — "Abraham returned to his young men." The return is recorded briefly — no dialogue, no emotional commentary. The narrative lacunism is itself a feature: what passed between Abraham and Isaac on the descent is not told. Wayyāqom wayyēlĕḵû yaḥdāw ʾel-bĕʾēr šāḇaʿ (they rose and went together to Beersheba) — "together" (yaḥdāw) again: they went up together (v. 6), came down together (implied), and return together. The relationship survives the test intact.
22.20: The genealogical notice of Nahor's family (vv. 20-24) is positioned here for a narrative purpose: Rebekah appears as Bethuel's daughter (v. 23). The next major action in the Abraham narrative will be finding Isaac a wife (ch. 24), and this genealogy introduces Rebekah into the story before the betrothal narrative begins. The notice also signals that the covenant line (which narrowed through Isaac) will be continued by a wife from the family of Nahor — the broader Abraham family, not a Canaanite woman.
22.21: Nahor's eight sons through Milcah: עוּץ (ʿûṣ, Uz) is the firstborn — the land of Uz is Job's homeland (Job 1:1); בּוּז (bûz, Buz) gives his name to the Buzite clan (Job 32:2 — Elihu the Buzite). The Nahorite genealogy thus connects to the Job tradition: Job's world is the world of the broader Semitic family descended from Nahor. The connections between the patriarchal and wisdom literature traditions run through these genealogical notices.
22.22: Kesed, Hazo, Pildash, Jidlaph, and Bethuel — these Nahorite names map onto the Aramean/northern Mesopotamian tribal geography. בְּתוּאֵל (Bĕtûʾēl, Bethuel) — "man of God" or possibly "daughter of God" (the name is unusual; some read it as "house of El") — the father of Rebekah and father-in-law of Isaac; he appears again in 24:15, 24, 47, 50; 25:20; 28:2, 5. The genealogy's descent to Bethuel is the narrative point of the list: it establishes Rebekah's family pedigree within the Nahorite-Semitic clan structure.
22.23: וּבְתוּאֵל יָלַד אֶת-רִבְקָה (ûḇĕtûʾēl yālaḏ ʾet-riḇqāh) — "Bethuel fathered Rebekah." Riḇqāh (Rebekah) — the name is introduced without commentary; she is "born" into the narrative at the moment the story needs her. The positioning of Rebekah's introduction immediately after the Akedah (where Isaac's existence is confirmed and the covenant line secured) and before the wife-search of ch. 24 is structurally elegant: the Akedah tests and secures Isaac; the genealogy introduces his future wife; ch. 24 brings them together.
22.24: וּפִילַגְשׁוֹ וּשְׁמָהּ רְאוּמָה (ûpîlaḡšô ûšĕmāh rĕʾûmāh) — "his concubine, whose name was Reumah." Pîleḡeš (concubine) — a secondary wife without the full legal status of the primary wife; a recognized category of marriage in the ANE. Nahor's four sons by Reumah complete his twelve sons total (eight through Milcah, four through Reumah) — a number that may deliberately parallel the twelve tribes of Israel who descend from Abraham's twelve-grandson structure (Ishmael also has twelve princes, 17:20; 25:12-16). The narrative geometry of twelve descendants is a recurring organizational principle.

49.10: lo yasur shevet miYehudah umechokek mibein raglav ad ki yavo shiloh velo yiqqehat amim: 'The scepter shall not depart from Judah, nor the ruler's staff from between his feet, until tribute comes to him; and to him shall be the obedience of the peoples.' Shiloh: the most debated word in Jacob's blessing. Major interpretations: (1) a place name (Shiloh in Ephraim — problematic geographically); (2) a form of sheloh = 'whose it is' (Ezek 21:27 uses similar language); (3) a Messianic title derived from shalom (peace). The LXX reads 'until what is reserved for him comes' — pointing toward a coming ruler. Christian and early Jewish messianic readings consistently identified this as a promise of a coming king who would gather the nations.
49.1: bĕʾaḥărît hayyāmîm — "in the latter days" (H319 ʾaḥărît + H3117 yôm). ʾAḥărît hayyāmîm is the eschatological formula that introduces prophetic vision throughout the canonical prophets: Isa 2:2, Mic 4:1, Hos 3:5, Dan 10:14. Here it frames the tribal blessings as prophetic-historical vision, not merely patriarchal testimony — Jacob sees the destiny of the tribes across time. The phrase carries forward into the NT as "the last days" (Heb 1:2; Acts 2:17), establishing the typological horizon of the passage.
49.2: hāʾăzînû... šimʿû — "give ear… listen" — the dual hearing-imperative that opens formal poetry and prophetic address (cf. Isa 1:2; Deut 32:1). The doubled imperative transitions the prose narrative into the elevated register of Hebrew poetry; ch49 is the first sustained piece of tribal poetry in the Pentateuch. Hāʾăzên (H238, to give ear/hearken) is more formal than šāmaʿ (H8085, hear) — together they call for attentive, receptive listening to a word of weight.
49.3: rēʾšît ʾônî — "the firstfruits of my strength/vigor." ʾÔn (H205) carries a productive ambiguity: it means both vigor/virile strength (used positively in Deut 21:17 for the firstborn's "firstfruits of his strength") and iniquity/trouble/wickedness (used negatively in Ps 66:18; Hos 12:11). The double meaning shadows the verse: Jacob praises Reuben as the firstfruits of his vigor while the word carries the seed of its own negation — the trouble (ʾôn) Reuben brought by defiling his father's bed (v4; 35:22). Rēʾšît (H7225, firstfruits/beginning) — the same word as in 1:1 ("in the beginning").
49.4: paḥaz kammayim lōʾ tôtar — "turbulent as the waters, you will not excel." Paḥaz (H6349, recklessness/instability/wantonness) — a rare word appearing only here, Judg 9:4 ("reckless men"), and Jer 23:32 ("reckless lying"). The water-simile: water that is paḥaz overflows its banks, cannot be contained; it cannot "excel" (tôtar, H3498, to remain/excel/have advantage). Reuben's character is the character of uncontrollable overflow. The sin named: kî ʿālîtā mišĕḵāḇ ʾāḇîḵā (you went up to your father's bed) — the Qal perfect with the ascending preposition ʿālāh applied to what should have been avoidance. The same sexual violation is recorded without comment in 35:22.
49.5: kĕlê ḥāmās mĕḵērōtêhem — "their swords are weapons of violence." Ḥāmās (H2555, violence/injustice/wrong) — the word used for the earth's violence that provoked the flood (6:11,13); the violence of oppression and assault. The word mĕḵērōtêhem is debated: MT reads a rare noun sometimes understood as "dwelling/kinship" or possibly "swords/weapons." The phrase frames Simeon and Levi as a pair whose instrument is violence — pointing back to the Shechem massacre (34:25-29), which Jacob explicitly recalls in v6.
49.6: bĕsōḏām ʾal-tāḇōʾ napšî — "let my soul not enter their council." Sôḏ (H5475, council/secret counsel/intimate circle) — the word for the intimate circle of the divine council (Ps 25:14; Jer 23:18; Amos 3:7: "God does nothing without revealing his sôḏ to his servants the prophets"). Jacob invokes it negatively: he wants his nepeš excluded from their deliberating-circle. Hōrĕgû ʾîš (they killed a man) and ʿiqqĕrû-šôr (they hamstrung oxen) recall the Shechem massacre; the violence extended from persons to property.
49.7: ʾarrûr ʾappām kî ʿāz wĕʿeḇrātām kî qāšātāh — "Cursed be their anger, so fierce, and their fury, so cruel!" The curse falls not on the men themselves but on their anger and fury — a precision in blessing/cursing that distinguishes disposition from person. ʾAppām (their anger, H639, literally "their nose/nostrils" — the nose flares with anger) + ʿeḇrāh (H5678, fury/overflow of anger — from ʿāḇar, to overflow). The sentence: ʾăḥalleqēm bĕyaʿăqōḇ wăʾăpîṣēm bĕyiśrāʾēl (I will divide them in Jacob and scatter them in Israel) — the future of Simeon (absorbed by Judah, lacking territory) and Levi (given scattered cities; Num 35) is traced to this moral pronouncement.
49.8: yĕhûḏāh ʾattāh yôḏûḵā ʾaḥeḵā — "Judah, your brothers will praise you." The wordplay governing the entire Judah-blessing: yĕhûḏāh (Judah) derives from yāḏāh (H3034, to praise/give thanks/confess) — Leah named him "Judah" because "this time I will praise YHWH" (29:35). Here the name and function align: "Judah = praise; your brothers will praise (yôḏûḵā) you." Yāḏĕḵā bĕʿōrep ʾōyĕḇeḵā (your hand on the neck of your enemies) — the posture of victory over a prostrated enemy; ʿōrep (H6203, back of neck) as the place one's hand goes in triumph (Josh 10:24). Yiššĕtaḥăwû lĕḵā bĕnê ʾāḇîḵā — "your father's sons will bow down to you" — the echo of Joseph's dream (37:7-9) now repurposed for the dynastic future of Judah.
49.9: gûr ʾaryēh yĕhûḏāh — "Judah is a lion's cub." Three lion-words accumulate: gûr (H1482, young lion/cub — a lion in its prime developmental stage), ʾaryēh (H738, the adult male lion — the standard term), lābîʾ (H3833, a great lion/lioness — an archaic poetic term for the mightiest lion, used in Job 4:11; Isa 30:6). The triple progression — cub, lion, great lion — traces Judah's dynasty from its origin through its mature power to its supreme dominion. Mî yĕqîmennû (who will rouse him?) — the lion at rest challenges all comers; the rhetorical question implies "no one." Rev 5:5 ("the Lion of the tribe of Judah has prevailed") picks up this exact imagery for Christ.
