Lĕmišpĕḥōtêhem — "according to their families/clans" — a kinship-organizing term used in genealogical and census lists; the ark-exit is framed as the beginning of the new order of families and nations that ch. 10 will elaborate.
8.20: מִזְבֵּחַ (mizbēaḥ) — "altar" — Noah builds the first explicitly named altar in the biblical narrative (the altars of Cain and Abel, 4:3-4, are implied but not named). מִכֹּל הַבְּהֵמָה הַטְּהֹרָה וּמִכֹּל הָעוֹף הַטָּהוֹר — "from every clean animal and every clean bird" — the clean/unclean distinction justified by the seven pairs of clean animals (7:2-3) is here revealed: the surplus was for sacrifice. Noah's first act upon leaving the ark is worship — the building of the altar and the offering of burnt offerings (ʿōlōt) establishes the post-flood world on the basis of sacrifice and worship rather than personal achievement.
8.21:
וַיָּרַח יְהוָה אֶת-רֵיחַ הַנִּיחֹחַ (wayyāraḥ YHWH ʾet-rêaḥ hannîḥōaḥ) — "and the LORD smelled the pleasing aroma." The anthropomorphic language is frank: God smells and is pleased. The term rêaḥ hannîḥōaḥ (aroma of rest/satisfaction) is the technical phrase used for the pleasing sacrifice smell throughout Leviticus and Numbers (Lev 1:9, 13, 17; 2:2, 9; 3:5; Num 15:3-24, etc.) — the Noahic sacrifice uses Levitical sacrificial vocabulary, grounding Sinai's cultic system in the post-flood covenant.

יֵצֶר לֵב הָאָדָם רַע מִנְּעֻרָיו (yēṣer lēb hāʾādām raʿ minĕʿūrāyw) — "the inclination/impulse of the human heart is evil from his youth." Yēṣer (formation, inclination — from yāṣar, the potter's verb of 2:7) is the word used in 6:5 for the pre-flood judgment: "every yēṣer of the thoughts of his heart was only evil continually." The situation is unchanged: God promises never again to curse the ground because of humanity despite the same evil inclination that triggered the flood. The post-flood grace is explicitly given against the background of unchanged human nature — the covenant is not based on moral improvement but on divine commitment.
8.22: עֹד כָּל-יְמֵי הָאָרֶץ — "while all the days of the earth remain." The temporal phrase frames the covenant of seedtime/harvest, cold/heat, summer/winter, day/night as conditional on the earth's endurance — not eternal in an absolute sense, but as long as the present world-order lasts. The six pairs (seed/harvest, cold/heat, summer/winter, day/night — plus implicitly the day/night pair) form the natural ordering of life: God commits to maintain the cosmic regularity that makes agriculture, seasons, and human life possible. This is not the Noahic covenant of ch. 9 (which involves blood and the rainbow sign) but a natural-order commitment that underlies it.

9.1: וַיְבָרֶךְ אֱלֹהִים אֶת-נֹחַ וְאֶת-בָּנָיו — "And God blessed Noah and his sons." The blessing formula repeats 1:28 exactly: pĕrû ûrĕbû ûmilĕʾû ʾet-hāʾāreṣ (be fruitful, multiply, fill the earth). The post-flood world begins with the same creation-blessing as the first world — Noah and his family are the second Adam, receiving the same mandate to multiply and fill. The repetition of the original blessing emphasizes continuity across the flood's discontinuity: creation's mandate stands.
9.2: וּמוֹרַאֲכֶם וְחִתְּכֶם (ûmôrăʾăḵem wĕḥittĕḵem) — "your fear and your dread." The noun pair môrāʾ (fear, terror) and ḥittāh (dread, terror) describes the changed relationship between humanity and the animal kingdom. In 1:28 and 2:19-20 (the original creation mandate and the naming of animals), there is no mention of animal fear — the relationship was harmonious dominion. Now, after the Fall and Flood, the relationship is maintained by fear rather than natural co-operation. This is the realistic acknowledgment that the post-Fall world operates on power rather than harmony.
9.3: כָּל-רֶמֶשׂ אֲשֶׁר הוּא-חַי לָכֶם יִהְיֶה לְאָכְלָה — "every moving thing that lives shall be food for you." The original food grant (1:29-30) limited humans to seed-bearing plants and fruit — meat was not permitted. Now God explicitly grants meat-eating: "just as I gave you the green plants, I give you everything." This is a new permission, not a clarification of an original permission. The grant is universal within the animal kingdom subject only to the blood restriction of v. 4.
9.4: אַךְ-בָּשָׂר בְּנַפְשׁוֹ דָמוֹ לֹא תֹאכֵלוּ — "but flesh with its nepeš — its blood — you shall not eat." The contrastive particle ʾaḵ (but, however, only) marks the single exception to the universal food grant. The syntax is compressed: flesh + bĕnaphšô (in its life/soul) = dāmô (its blood). The equation nepeš = dām (life = blood) is spelled out explicitly in Lev 17:11 ("the life of the flesh is in the blood") and 17:14 ("the life of every creature is its blood"). The blood prohibition grounds the Levitical blood-atonement system (Lev 17:11: "I have given it to you on the altar to make atonement for your lives; it is the blood that makes atonement by the life") in the Noahic covenant — the blood belongs to God because life belongs to God.
9.5: וְאַךְ אֶת-דִּמְכֶם לְנַפְשֹׁתֵיכֶם אֶדְרֹשׁ — "and surely for your blood I will require a reckoning, for your lives." Dāraš (to seek out, require, demand) is the legal-investigative term — God as the investigator of bloodshed. The accountability is from two sources: (1) from every animal (miḵkol-ḥayyāh) that kills a human — an extension of human accountability into the animal kingdom (cf. Exod 21:28-29, the goring ox); (2) from every human who kills another human. The structure expands the reach of divine justice beyond human-to-human violence.
9.6: שֹׁפֵךְ דַּם הָאָדָם בָּאָדָם דָּמוֹ יִשָּׁפֵךְ — "whoever sheds the blood of a human (hāʾādām), by a human (bāʾādām) shall his blood be shed." The chiastic structure in Hebrew is formally perfect: šōpēḵ dām hāʾādām / bāʾādām / dāmô yiššāpēḵ. The three elements (blood + the human + blood) frame the middle term (by a human) — the punishment mirrors the crime in both substance (blood for blood) and agent (human for human). כִּי בְּצֶלֶם אֱלֹהִים עָשָׂה אֶת-הָאָדָם — "for in the image of God he made humankind." The kî (because, for) grounds the capital sanction in the image-of-God status: to murder a human is to assault God's image-representative on earth. Crucially, the image-of-God status is affirmed as still operative after the Fall and after the Flood — it was not destroyed by sin, only damaged or distorted.
9.7: The fruitfulness mandate of v. 1 is repeated verbatim — the divine speech brackets the blood laws (vv. 2-6) with the creation blessing (v. 1) and its restatement (v. 7). The structure: blessing → permitted food (v. 3) → forbidden food/blood (v. 4) → blood accountability (vv. 5-6) → blessing repeated. The repeating blessing frames the new laws as operating within the context of divine generosity and life-promotion, not mere restriction.
9.8: The divine speech of covenant-establishment begins with the double address: "God said to Noah and to his sons with him" — the covenant is addressed to both the immediate recipients (Noah) and the next generation (his sons). The covenant is dynastic and generational from its first formulation.
9.9: אֲנִי הִנְנִי מֵקִים אֶת-בְּרִיתִי אִתְּכֶם — "I, behold, I am establishing my covenant with you." The emphatic independent pronoun ʾănî (I) + the demonstrative particle hinnĕnî (behold me/I am here) + Hiphil participle mĕqîm (establishing, making to stand) — the construction stresses both the divine person ("I myself") and the ongoing act (participle = continuous action). Bĕrît (covenant): this is the first time God explicitly calls his commitment a bĕrît; earlier commitments were present but the formal covenant terminology now appears. The covenant is unilateral in its establishment — God alone speaks and commits; Noah makes no oath.
9.10: וְאֶת כָּל-נֶפֶשׁ הַחַיָּה — "and with every living creature (nepeš) that is with you." The Noahic covenant is unique among biblical covenants in being explicitly covenanted with non-human creatures — birds, livestock, and wild animals are all named as covenant parties (vv. 10, 12, 15, 16, 17). This is a cosmic covenant, not merely a human one; God's commitment is to all animate life bearing the nepeš. The Noahic covenant encompasses the entire creational order.
9.11: לֹא-יִכָּרֵת כָּל-בָּשָׂר עוֹד מִמֵּי הַמַּבּוּל — "never again shall all flesh be cut off by the waters of a flood." The Niphal of kārat (to cut off, cut down — used for cutting covenants [kārat bĕrît, to cut a covenant] and for cutting off violators from the community) — ironically, the word for "cut off in judgment" is here used for the divine pledge that the ultimate judgment will never recur: never again shall flesh be "cut off" by flood. The double negative (lōʾ... ʿôd) emphasizes permanence: no more, not again.
9.12: זֹאת אוֹת-הַבְּרִית (zōʾt ʾôt-habbĕrît) — "this is the sign of the covenant." ʾÔt (sign, mark) — the same word used for the Sabbath as a sign of the covenant (Exod 31:13, 17), circumcision as a sign (Gen 17:11), and the signs given through Moses (Exod 4:8-9; 10:1-2). Every major covenant has a sign; the Noahic sign is universal and cosmic — visible to all people, not requiring circumcision or Sabbath-keeping to observe.
9.13: אֶת-קַשְׁתִּי נָתַתִּי בֶּעָנָן (ʾet-qašti nātattî beʿānān) — "I have set my bow in the clouds." Qešet means a bow — specifically, a warrior's bow used for shooting arrows. The same word is used of Nimrod's hunting prowess (10:9) and of the divine warrior's bow in Ps 7:12; Hab 3:9-11. By "setting the bow in the clouds," God hangs up his weapon of war — the bow is the ANE military symbol of divine judgment; a god who lays down the bow signals the cessation of war. God hangs his weapon in the sky as a visible reminder that the war of judgment is over. The rainbow is God's war-bow, decommissioned and displayed in the sky as a memorial of covenant peace.
9.14: The conditional clause structure — "when I bring clouds over the earth and the bow appears in the clouds" — makes the rainbow a periodic, repeated reminder rather than a one-time sign. The clouds (ʿānān) were associated with divine presence and theophany (Exod 13:21-22; 19:16-19; 1 Kgs 8:10-11; Ezek 1:4) — God's presence in the storm-cloud that might have brought flood is now the occasion for the bow's appearance, which interposes the covenant-reminder between the threatening cloud and the earth below.
9.15: וְזָכַרְתִּי אֶת-בְּרִיתִי (wĕzāḵartî ʾet-bĕrîtî) — "and I will remember my covenant." Zākar again in the covenant-faithfulness sense (cf. 8:1). God does not forget the covenant, but the remembering-language places the covenant on the side of divine faithfulness rather than human performance — the rainbow reminds God (from the human narrative perspective) and humanity simultaneously. The waters will never again become a flood to destroy all flesh — kol-bāśār (all flesh): the comprehensive formula of vv. 11, 15, 16, 17 reinforces totality.
9.16: בְּרִית עוֹלָם (bĕrît ʿôlām) — "an everlasting covenant." ʿÔlām (eternity, perpetuity, from of old and into the far future) qualifies the covenant as without temporal limit. The same phrase is used for the Abrahamic covenant (17:7, 13, 19), the Mosaic covenant in its application (Exod 31:16; Lev 24:8), and the Davidic covenant (2 Sam 23:5; Ps 89:28). The Noahic covenant is the most universally inclusive of all biblical covenants — it encompasses all living creatures and all generations.
9.17: God reiterates the sign-identification for clarity: "This is the sign of the covenant I have established between me and all flesh that is on the earth." The verse closes the covenant-establishment speech with a summary statement, a standard narrative technique (the "colophon" that closes a section by restating its content). The triple use of bĕrît (covenant) in this verse (in the Hebrew: haqqeṣet... bĕrît... bêynî ûbêyn kol-bāśār) reinforces the covenant's centrality to this entire section.
9.18: חָם הוּא אֲבִי כְנָעַן (ḥām hûʾ ʾăbî kĕnaʿan) — "Ham was the father of Canaan." The parenthetical identification of Ham as Canaan's father anticipates the curse of v. 25 — the reader is being prepared by noting the connection between Ham and Canaan before the incident that will curse Canaan. The three sons of Noah (Shem, Ham, Japheth) are the ancestors of the nations in ch. 10, giving the Ham/Canaan/curse narrative its geopolitical implications for Israel's later relationship with Canaan.
9.19: מֵאֵלֶּה נָפְצָה כָל-הָאָרֶץ (mēʾēlleh nāpĕṣāh kol-hāʾāreṣ) — "from these the whole earth was scattered/spread out." Nāpaṣ (to scatter, spread) — the same verb as in the Babel narrative (11:4, 8, 9), where God scatters the nations. The "spreading out" here is the positive peopling of the earth fulfilling the creation mandate; the scattering of Babel will be its divinely-imposed intensification. The bridge between the Noahic genealogical promise and the Babel dispersion runs through this verse.
9.20: וַיָּחֶל נֹחַ אִישׁ הָאֲדָמָה (wayyāḥel nōaḥ ʾîš hāʾăḏāmāh) — "Noah, a man of the soil, began." Hēḥēl is the Hiphil of ḥālal — "began" or "profaned/desecrated." The same form can mean either "began" or "made common/profane" (cf. 4:26: hûḥal, "at that time people began to call on the name of the LORD"). The ambiguity may be intentional: Noah began as a man of the ground, or Noah, a man of the ground, profaned himself. ʾÎš hāʾăḏāmāh (man of the soil) is the same construction as ʾādām min-hāʾăḏāmāh (Adam from the ground, 2:7; 3:19) — Noah is the new Adam, returning to work the ground.
9.21: וַיִּשְׁתְּ מִן-הַיַּיִן וַיִּשְׁכָּר — "and he drank from the wine and became drunk." The verb šākar (to become drunk) is used neutrally here — no explicit moral judgment on the drunkenness itself. וַיִּתְגַּל (wayyitgal) — "and he uncovered himself" — Hithpael of gālāh (to uncover, reveal). The reflexive (Hithpael) makes this self-exposure: Noah uncovered himself within his tent. The nakedness (ʿerwāh implied) connects back to 2:25 and 3:7 — nakedness in the biblical world is associated with vulnerability, shame, and violation.
9.22: וַיַּרְא חָם אֲבִי כְנָעַן אֵת עֶרְוַת אָבִיו — "Ham, the father of Canaan, saw the nakedness of his father." Rāʾāh ʿerwāh (to see nakedness) is the legal phrase in Lev 20:17-19 for forbidden sexual viewing/violation — "if a man takes his sister... and sees her nakedness (rāʾāh ʾet-ʿerwātāh)... it is a disgrace." The phrase in Leviticus consistently connotes sexual transgression, not accidental observation. The mildest interpretation (Ham accidentally saw, then gossiped about it to his brothers) does not fully account for the severity of the curse on Canaan or the use of this specific technical phrase. At minimum, Ham violated his father's dignity by observing and reporting rather than covering; at maximum, the phrase implies a more serious violation. The text leaves the specific act deliberately ambiguous.
9.23: וַיִּקַּח שֵׁם וָיֶפֶת אֶת-הַשִּׂמְלָה — "Shem and Japheth took a garment." The contrast is explicit: Ham saw and told; Shem and Japheth took a garment and covered. עַל-שְׁכֶם שְׁנֵיהֶם (ʿal-šĕkem šĕnêhem) — "on the shoulders of both of them" — both carry the garment together, walking backward (wayyĕlĕḵû ʾăḥōranît) so as not to see their father's nakedness. The backward-walking detail is not merely practical but honor-protective: they refuse even accidental sight. Their act of covering reverses Ham's act of exposing — shame covered vs. shame revealed.
9.24: מַה-עָּשָׂה לוֹ בְּנוֹ הַקָּטָן — "what his youngest son had done to him." The narrative describes Ham as the "youngest son" — but in the standard genealogical list, Ham is the middle son (Shem, Ham, Japheth in 5:32; 6:10; 7:13; 9:18; 10:1). Either "youngest" (qāṭōn) refers to relative status or honor-position in this context rather than birth order, or there is a tradition that Ham was in fact the youngest. Noah's "finding out what his son had done" implies that what Ham did was a discovered act, not merely observed — the detail that Shem and Japheth specifically did not see their father (v. 23) implies that what there was to see was something shameful that Ham saw (and possibly did).
9.25: אָרוּר כְּנָעַן (ʾārûr kĕnaʿan) — "cursed be Canaan." The curse falls on Canaan, Ham's son, rather than on Ham directly. The explanation commonly offered: cursing the son rather than the father may reflect the ancient Near Eastern legal-rhetorical convention in which the consequences of a father's sin fall on his lineage (cf. Exod 20:5), or may reflect that Ham's sin was done through Canaan (the text is silent on this). Theologically significant: the curse does not fall on Shem or Japheth's descendants, shaping the later Israelite understanding of Canaan's relationship to Israel. ʿEbed ʿăbādîm — "servant of servants" — the superlative construction (like "holy of holies" = most holy, "king of kings" = supreme king) means "the lowest possible servant," the ultimate subordinate.
9.26: בָּרוּךְ יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵי שֵׁם — "Blessed be the LORD, the God of Shem." The blessing of Shem is structured as a blessing of God who is Shem's God — the covenantal name YHWH appears here, connecting Shem's line with the covenant name. The Messianic line running through Shem (to Abraham, to Israel, to Christ) makes this blessing the covenantal promise: the LORD-covenanted God is the God of Shem's descendants. Canaan is servant to Shem — implying Israel's eventual conquest of Canaan under Joshua.
9.27: יַפְתְּ אֱלֹהִים לְיֶפֶת (yapet ʾĕlōhîm lĕyepet) — "May God enlarge Japheth." The wordplay is explicit: yāpat (enlarge, make wide) on Japheth (yepet). The blessing of Japheth is territorial expansion (pāṭāh = to be wide, open). וְיִשְׁכֹּן בְּאָהֳלֵי-שֵׁם — "and let him dwell in the tents of Shem." The subject of "dwell" is ambiguous: either Japheth dwells in Shem's tents (a sign of blessing — sharing the covenant space), or God dwells in Shem's tents (God's presence among Shem's descendants). The ambiguity is interpretively productive: both readings are theologically rich.
9.28: The genealogical framing verse closes the Noahic narrative. וַיְחִי-נֹחַ אַחַר הַמַּבּוּל — "Noah lived after the flood." The wayyiqtol marks the narrative continuation into the genealogical register. Noah lives 350 more years after the flood — his total of 950 years (v. 29) makes him the second-longest-lived of the antediluvian patriarchs (only Methuselah at 969 years exceeds him). The post-flood lifespans begin declining in ch. 11, marking the transition from the antediluvian world-era to the patriarchal era.
9.29: וַיָּמֹת (wayyāmōt) — "and he died." The genealogical death formula closes the Noahic narrative. The structure of ch. 5 (so-and-so lived X years, fathered Y, lived Z more years, and he died) is here abbreviated to the death notice only. Noah stands at the pivot of the flood narrative: the last of the antediluvian patriarchs, the first man of the post-flood world, the one through whom all humanity descends after the flood. His death closes the flood narrative and opens the space for the Table of Nations (ch. 10) and Babel (ch. 11).

10.1: אֵלֶּה תּוֹלְדֹת (ʾēlleh tôlĕḏōt) — "these are the generations/account of." The tôlĕḏōt formula (cf. 2:4; 5:1; 6:9; 11:10; 11:27; 25:12, 19; 36:1; 37:2) opens a new section of Genesis. Here it introduces the Table of Nations — not a genealogy in the strict biological sense but a political-ethnic mapping of the known world from Noah's three sons. The organizing principle is threefold: clans (mišpĕḥōtām), languages (bilšōnōtām), and lands/nations (bĕʾarṣōtām lĕgôyêhem) — the same formula closes each of the three son-sections (vv. 5, 20, 31) and the chapter itself (v. 32).
10.2: Japheth's seven sons are broadly associated with the northern and western peoples of the ancient Near East: גֹּמֶר (Gomer) = Cimmerians (Gimirrai in Assyrian); מָגוֹג (Māgōg) = a northern people, associated with eschatological enemies in Ezek 38-39; מָדַי (Māḏay) = Medes; יָוָן (Yāwān) = Ionia/Greeks (Yavan in Sanskrit and cognate languages); תֻּבָל (Tûbāl) = Tabal (Anatolia); מֶשֶׁךְ (Mešeḵ) = Mushki (Cappadocia); תִּירָס (Tîrās) = Tyrrhenians/Thracians (identification uncertain). The list is not intended to be exhaustive but representative of the Japhethite sphere.
10.3: Gomer's sons: אַשְׁכְּנַז (ʾAšḵĕnaz) = Scythians (Aškuzai in Assyrian sources); רִיפַת (Rîpaṯ) = possibly Paphlagonians or Celts; תֹּגַרְמָה (Tôḡarmāh) = Til-garimmu, a region in southeastern Anatolia, associated with horse-trading (Ezek 27:14; 38:6). These second-generation descendants of Japheth begin to fill in the northwestern tier of the ancient world known to the author.
10.4: Javan's sons represent the Greek world and its periphery: אֱלִישָׁה (ʾĔlîšāh) = Alashiya (Cyprus) or possibly Elis; תַּרְשִׁישׁ (Taršîš) = Tartessos in Spain or possibly Sardinia — a far-western trading destination (cf. Jonah 1:3; 1 Kgs 10:22); כִּתִּים (Kittîm) = Kition in Cyprus, later used broadly for western maritime powers including Rome (Dan 11:30; Num 24:24); דֹּדָנִים (Dōḏānîm) = possibly Dodaneans/Rhodians, or (with textual variant) Rodanites.
10.5: מֵאֵלֶּה נִפְרְדוּ אִיֵּי הַגּוֹיִם — "from these the coastland peoples of the nations were separated/spread out." Nāpraḏ (Niphal of pāraḏ, to be separated, divided) — the same verb root as Peleg's name (10:25) and implicit in the Babel scattering. The coastland peoples (ʾiyyê haggôyim = islands/coastlands of the nations) identifies the Japhethites as the maritime-western peoples. The threefold formula closes this section: lĕmišpĕḥōtām bĕlišōnōtām bĕʾarṣōtām bĕgôyêhem — by their clans, by their languages, in their lands, within their nations.
10.6: Ham's sons map onto Africa and the ancient Near East: כּוּשׁ (Kûš) = Ethiopia/Nubia; מִצְרַיִם (Miṣrāyim) = Egypt (the dual form suggests Upper and Lower Egypt); פּוּט (Pûṭ) = Libya or Somalia (identification debated; Nah 3:9 places Put near Egypt); כְּנָעַן (Kĕnaʿan) = Canaan. The listing of Canaan last, after three African/Nilotic peoples, places Canaan in a subordinate position — and given the curse of 9:25, this positioning is significant. Ham's line will include both the great imperial powers (Egypt, Babylon through Nimrod) and the promised-land occupants.
10.7: Cush's sons extend southward and eastward: שְׁבָא (Šĕbāʾ) = Saba/Sheba (Arabia or East Africa — the Queen of Sheba, 1 Kgs 10:1); דְּדָן (Dĕḏān) = an Arabian trading center (Jer 25:23; Ezek 27:15-20). These Cushite-descended peoples in Arabia represent the commercial network of the ancient incense trade routes.
10.8: וְנִמְרֹד הֵחֵל לִהְיוֹת גִּבֹּר בָּאָרֶץ — "Nimrod began to be a mighty warrior/hero in the earth." Gîbbōr (mighty warrior, hero — from gābar, to be strong, prevail) is used of warriors (2 Sam 23:8-39), Saul and Jonathan (2 Sam 1:19, 25, 27), and God himself as the divine warrior (Ps 24:8; Isa 42:13; Zeph 3:17). The Hiphil hēḥēl (began) echoes 4:26 (people began to call on the LORD's name) and 6:1 (people began to multiply) — the verb marks significant new beginnings, not always positive. Nimrod is the first named human "mighty one" — a political-military category that sets him apart from the genealogical norm.
10.9: גִּבֹּר-צַיִד לִפְנֵי יְהוָה (gibbōr-ṣayiḏ lipnê YHWH) — "a mighty hunter before the LORD." The expression lipnê YHWH (before the LORD, in YHWH's sight) can mean: (1) in God's presence/sight (a positive superlative — supremely great); (2) before/in front of God (as a rival or opponent — the Targum Onqelos adds "in defiance of"). The proverb attached ("like Nimrod a mighty hunter before the LORD") preserves an ancient saying. Given Nimrod's connection to Babel (v. 10), the "defiance" reading has textual support — the great-city builder and the one who hunted before God fit the Babel narrative's spirit of rival-to-divine ambition.
10.10: רֵאשִׁית מַמְלַכְתּוֹ בָּבֶל (rēʾšît mamlaktô bābel) — "the beginning of his kingdom was Babylon." Bābel appears here first in Genesis — immediately before the Babel narrative of ch. 11. Nimrod's kingdom (the first named human kingdom in the OT) begins at Babel and extends to Nineveh — the two great enemy cities of the OT prophets. שִׁנְעָר (Šinʿār) = Sumer/Babylonia — the same region as the Babel event (11:2). Nimrod's Babel is the historical/political root of the Babel tower-city project.
10.11: מִן-הָאָרֶץ הַהִוא יָצָא אַשּׁוּר — either "from that land he [Nimrod] went to Assyria" or "from that land Asshur went out" (subject ambiguous). If Nimrod, he extends his empire northward to Assyria — consistent with a Hamite building the great Assyrian cities. If Asshur, the text may simply trace the geographical spread of Assyria. The cities named — נִינְוֵה (Nînĕwēh, Nineveh), the great Assyrian capital — are historically attested from at least the 3rd millennium BCE.
10.12: הִוא הָעִיר הַגְּדֹלָה — "that is the great city." The designation "great city" (hāʿîr haggĕḏōlāh) is used in Jonah 1:2; 3:2-3; 4:11 for Nineveh — the city of 120,000 people that Jonah was sent to preach in. This parenthetical label connects the Table of Nations genealogy to the Jonah narrative — Nineveh's greatness originates in Nimrod's founding, and its significance will appear in the prophetic literature of 2 Kings and Jonah.
10.13: מִצְרַיִם (Miṣrāyim, Egypt) as father of peoples — the sons of Egypt are the peoples inhabiting the Nile Delta and surrounding regions. לוּדִים (Lûḏîm) = Lydians or Libyans; פְּלִשְׁתִּים (Pĕlištîm, Philistines) are traced in v. 14 to the Kasluhites through Egypt — placing Israel's great adversaries within the Hamitic genealogical framework. The Philistines originating from Egyptian stock is consistent with Egyptian administrative presence in Canaan during the Late Bronze Age.
10.14: כַּסְלֻחִים אֲשֶׁר יָצְאוּ מִשָּׁם פְּלִשְׁתִּים — "the Kasluhites, from whom the Philistines came." The parenthetical identification of the Philistines' Egyptian origin is textually noted but geographically the Philistines are most closely associated with the Aegean Sea Peoples (the Peleset of Egyptian records, 12th century BCE) who settled in Canaan's coastal plain. כַּפְתֹּרִים (Kaptōrîm) = Cretans — Amos 9:7 explicitly identifies the Philistines' origin as Caphtor (Crete), making the Kasluhite parenthetical and the Caphtorite line different traditional strands.
10.15: כְּנַעַן יָלַד אֶת-צִידֹן בְּכֹרוֹ — "Canaan fathered Sidon his firstborn." Ṣîḏōn (Sidon) is named the firstborn son of Canaan — the great Phoenician coastal city, Israel's northern neighbor and the source of the cedars of Lebanon. The list that follows (Hittites, Jebusites, Amorites, Girgashites, Hivites, etc.) is the standard Canaanite nations list of the Pentateuch (cf. Exod 3:8, 17; 23:23; 33:2; Deut 7:1; 20:17) — the Table of Nations is also a land-grant document, mapping the territory that Israel will later claim as Abrahamic promise fulfilled.
10.16: יְבוּסִי (Yĕbûsî, Jebusites) — the pre-Israelite inhabitants of Jerusalem (2 Sam 5:6-9; Josh 15:63). אֱמֹרִי (ʾĔmōrî, Amorites) — a broad term for highland Canaanites (cf. Num 13:29; Josh 10:5-6; Amos 2:9-10). These peoples are genealogically located as Canaanites descended from Ham — the Abrahamic covenant will involve displacing Ham's cursed line (9:25-27) in fulfillment of the blessing on Shem.
10.17: חִוִּי (Ḥiwwî, Hivites) — inhabitants of Gibeon (Josh 9:7; 11:19) and Shechem (Gen 34:2). עַרְקִי (ʿArqî, Arkites) — from Tell Arqa in northern Lebanon. סִינִי (Sînî, Sinites) — possibly from the region around Tell Kazel in northern Lebanon (the Siyannu of Ugaritic texts). These third-tier names in the Canaanite list represent specific city-states and clans rather than major ethnic groups.
10.18: וְאַחַר נָפֹצוּ מִשְׁפְּחוֹת הַכְּנַעֲנִי — "afterward the clans of the Canaanites spread abroad." Nāpōṣ (Niphal of pûṣ, to scatter, be dispersed) — the same verb as the Babel scattering (11:4: pen-nāpûṣ) and its fulfillment (11:8-9). The Canaanite dispersion anticipates the Babel narrative's scattering and the later displacement of Canaan by Israel as fulfillment of 9:25-27.
10.19: The geographical boundaries of Canaan are given: from Sidon southward to Gaza (Mediterranean coast), then inland toward Sodom, Gomorrah, Admah, Zeboiim to Lasha. The southern boundary cities (Sodom, Gomorrah, Admah, Zeboiim) are exactly the cities of the plain that will be destroyed in ch. 19 — the Table of Nations implicitly marks the doomed cities within the Canaanite territorial definition. Canaan's eastern boundary reaches toward the Dead Sea region.
10.20: The closing formula for Ham's descendants: ʾēlleh bĕnê-ḥām lĕmišpĕḥōtām bilšōnōtām bĕʾarṣōtām bĕgôyêhem — "these are the sons of Ham by their clans, by their languages, in their lands, in their nations." The fourfold formula (clans / languages / lands / nations) is identical for all three sons (vv. 5, 20, 31) — the same structural grid organizes all three genealogical lists, suggesting intentional literary organization of the total human family.
10.21: וּלְשֵׁם יֻלַּד גַּם-הוּא — "and to Shem also children were born." The narrative interest tips toward Shem at this point — he is identified as ʾăbî kol-bĕnê-ʿēber (ancestor of all the sons of Eber). עֵבֶר (ʿĒber) — the eponymous ancestor of the Hebrews (ʿibrî). The same consonants spell "crossing over" (ʿābēr) — possibly signifying those who crossed the Euphrates, those from "the other side" (Josh 24:2-3). Shem's genealogical line will narrow through Eber to Peleg to Terah to Abram — the Table of Nations is also a road map to the covenant line.
10.22: Shem's five sons: עֵילָם (ʿÊlām) = Elam (southwestern Iran); אַשּׁוּר (ʾAššûr) = Assyria; אַרְפַּכְשַׁד (ʾArpaḵšaḏ) = uncertain identification (possibly the Chaldean homeland or Arrapkha in northern Iraq) — the ancestor of Eber and thus of Abram; לוּד (Lûḏ) = Lydia (western Anatolia); אֲרָם (ʾĂrām) = Aram/Syria. Through Arphaxad runs the Abrahamic covenant line.
10.23: Aram's sons represent the Aramaic-speaking peoples: עוּץ (ʿÛṣ) = the land of Uz, home of Job (Job 1:1; Lam 4:21); חוּל (Ḥûl), גֶּתֶר (Geter), מַשׁ (Maš or Meshech, variant reading) — these represent the Aramaean city-states that will be significant in Israel's political history (Syria, 2 Sam 8:6; 1 Kgs 20:34; 2 Kgs 16:5-9).
10.24: וְאַרְפַּכְשַׁד יָלַד אֶת-שָׁלַח וְשֶׁלַח יָלַד אֶת-עֵבֶר — "Arphaxad fathered Shelah, and Shelah fathered Eber." The genealogical line is narrowing: from Shem's five sons, through Arphaxad alone, to Eber. This line will continue in the post-Babel genealogy of ch. 11 with even greater specificity, leading to Terah and Abram. The Table of Nations is simultaneously a full-world ethnography and a progressive narrowing toward the covenant recipient.
10.25: וּלְעֵבֶר יֻלַּד שְׁנֵי בָנִים שֵׁם הָאֶחָד פֶּלֶג כִּי בְיָמָיו נִפְלְגָה הָאָרֶץ — "Two sons were born to Eber: the name of the one was Peleg, for in his days the earth was divided." Peleḡ from pālaḡ (to divide, split) — the wordplay is explicit. "In his days the earth was divided" — most naturally referring to the Babel event of ch. 11, where God divides/scatters humanity. Peleg's name thus timestamps the Babel dispersion: it happened within his generation. Alternatively, some read "divided" as a reference to continental geography (less likely given the context). The genealogical note connects the Table of Nations to the Babel narrative chronologically.
10.26: Joktan's thirteen sons (vv. 26-29) represent the various tribes and regions of southern Arabia — Almodad, Sheleph, Hazarmaveth (= Hadramawt in Yemen), Jerah (lunar-deity name), and others. These are among the most obscure names in the Table of Nations; several can be tentatively connected to known South Arabian place names and peoples through archaeology and comparative Semitic studies.
10.27: Hadoram, Uzal, Diklah — these Joktanite names continue the South Arabian tribal geography. עוּזָל (ʿûzāl) may correspond to the ancient city Uzal (= Sanaa in Yemen, according to Arabic tradition). The density of names in the Joktanite list without additional narrative material signals that these peoples were known to the author's audience but peripheral to the biblical narrative's primary interests.
10.28: Obal, Abimael, Sheba — שְׁבָא (Šĕbāʾ) appears both as a Cushite son (v. 7, through Ham) and here as a Joktanite son (through Shem). The double occurrence may reflect the two known Sheba/Saba regions — one East African, one South Arabian — or the fact that the same tribal name existed in two different genealogical traditions. The Queen of Sheba (1 Kgs 10:1-13) is associated with the South Arabian Saba.
10.29: אוֹפִר (ʾÔpîr, Ophir) — the famous source of gold sought by Solomon's navy (1 Kgs 9:28; 10:11; 22:48; Job 22:24; 28:16; Ps 45:9; Isa 13:12). Ophir's precise location is debated (India, Arabia, or East Africa), but its genealogical placement among the Joktanite South Arabians tips toward an Arabian or East African identification. יוֹבָב (Yôbāb) — possibly connected etymologically to the name Job (ʾiyyôb), though the identification is uncertain.
10.30: The geographical extent of Joktan's descendants — from Mesha to Sephar in the eastern hill country — is another pair of location-markers that resist confident modern identification. Meša and Sĕpar likely represent the western and eastern limits of the South Arabian region where the Joktanite tribes settled.
10.31: The Shem-section closing formula mirrors v. 5 and v. 20: lĕmišpĕḥōtām bilšōnōtām bĕʾarṣōtām lĕgôyêhem — "by their clans, by their languages, in their lands, by their nations." The Semitic peoples, including the ancestors of both the Arabians (through Joktan) and the Hebrews (through Peleg to Eber to Arphaxad to Shem), are mapped out before the Babel narrative that explains why they are now distributed.
10.32: מֵאֵלֶּה נִפְרְדוּ הַגּוֹיִם בָּאָרֶץ אַחַר הַמַּבּוּל — "from these the nations were separated/scattered in the earth after the flood." The verb nāpraḏ (Niphal of pāraḏ, to separate, be scattered) provides the theological bracket: the diversity of nations was not an accident but a separation. The Table of Nations implicitly anticipates the Babel narrative's explanation of how this separation happened. The phrase "after the flood" grounds the entire human diversification in the post-Noah world — the whole table is a post-diluvian re-population.

11.1: שָׂפָה אַחַת וּדְבָרִים אֲחָדִים (śāpāh ʾaḥat ûḏĕbārîm ʾăḥāḏîm) — "one language and common/unified speech." Two terms for language: śāpāh (lip, language — lit. the speech-producing organ, used as a metonymy for language) and ḏĕbārîm ʾăḥāḏîm (common/unified words — the same speech content, not just the same language). The pre-Babel condition was linguistic unity — a single system of meaning shared by all humanity. This will be the condition restored eschatologically (Zeph 3:9: "I will give peoples pure speech/lips").
11.2: בְּנָסְעָם מִקֶּדֶם (bĕnāsĕʿām miqqeḏem) — "as they journeyed eastward/from the east." The direction is ambiguous: either they moved east (Cain also went east, 4:16; Lot goes east toward Sodom, 13:11 — east = away from God in Genesis) or they came from the east. בִּקְעַת שִׁנְעָר (biqʿat šinʿār) — "the plain of Shinar," the same location as Nimrod's kingdom (10:10). The deliberate settling (wayyēšĕbû šām) reverses the command to fill the earth (1:28; 9:1) — they settle, concentrate, and build rather than scatter and fill.
11.3: הָבָה נִלְבְּנָה לְבֵנִים (hābāh nilbĕnāh lĕbēnîm) — "come, let us make bricks." The cohortative plural (three cohortatives in vv. 3-4: nilbĕnāh, nibĕneh, naʿăśeh) echoes and parodies the divine cohortative of 1:26 ("let us make mankind") — humans now say "let us make" in deliberate mimicry of the divine creative "let us." The building materials are noted: lĕbēnāh (brick, kiln-fired) instead of ʾeḇen (stone), and ḥēmār (bitumen/asphalt) instead of śîḏ (lime/mortar). The parenthetical observation contrasts Mesopotamian construction (brick and bitumen — the standard materials in the alluvial plain of Shinar, where stone is unavailable) with Palestinian/Canaanite construction (stone and lime). The narrator implicitly critiques the substitute materials: synthetic brick instead of natural stone, petroleum tar instead of lime.
11.4:
הָבָה נִבְנֶה-לָּנוּ עִיר וּמִגְדָּל — "come, let us build for ourselves a city and a tower." Lānû (for ourselves) — the self-referential purpose is emphasized: the construction is for their own benefit, consolidation, and glory. Migdāl (tower) — from gāḏal (to be great) — a great tower. וְרֹאשׁוֹ בַשָּׁמַיִם — "with its top in the heavens" — the expression is idiomatic for extraordinary height (cf. Deut 1:28: "cities great and fortified up to heaven"; Dan 4:11: the tree reaching to heaven). It need not be taken literally as an attempt to climb to the divine realm.

וְנַעֲשֶׂה-לָּנוּ שֵׁם (wĕnaʿăśeh-lānû šēm) — "and let us make a name for ourselves." Šēm (name, reputation, fame) — this is the exact reversal of what God will do for Abram (12:2: "I will make your name great"). The builders seek to establish their own šēm through construction; God's plan is to establish the šēm of his chosen one through covenant. פֶּן-נָפוּץ (pen-nāpûṣ) — "lest we be scattered" — the very scattering they feared (and tried to prevent by city-building) is precisely what God brings about (v. 8). They resist the mandate to fill the earth (1:28; 9:1) by deliberately concentrating.
11.5: וַיֵּרֶד יְהוָה לִרְאוֹת אֶת-הָעִיר וְאֶת-הַמִּגְדָּל — "and the LORD came down to see the city and the tower." The anthropomorphic irony is sharp: the tower built to reach the heavens requires God to "come down" to see it. The verb yāraḏ (to come down, descend) undercuts the tower's pretension — what the builders thought was heaven-reaching, the deity can only see by descending from his actual dwelling. The narrative's irony is literary theology: the human project is small enough that God must stoop to see it.
11.6: הֵן עַם אֶחָד וְשָׂפָה אַחַת לְכֻלָּם — "behold, one people and one language for all of them." God's assessment begins with what he sees: unity of language and people. וְזֶה הַחִלָּם לַעֲשׂוֹת — "and this is only the beginning of what they will do" — ḥillam (their beginning) = infinitive of ḥālal, to begin. לֹא-יִבָּצֵר מֵהֶם כֹּל אֲשֶׁר יָזְמוּ לַעֲשׂוֹת — "nothing will be withheld from them that they purpose to do" — bāṣar (Niphal, to be withheld, cut off, inaccessible) — their unified capability poses a theological challenge: not that humans can actually reach God's heaven, but that human technological and political unity can amplify sin enormously. The divine response is not punishment for the tower itself but dispersal to limit the scope of unified human rebellion.
11.7: הָבָה נֵרְדָה וְנָבְלָה שָׁם שְׂפָתָם — "come, let us go down and confuse their language there." The divine cohortative hābāh nērĕḏāh (come, let us go down) directly mirrors and reverses the human cohortatives of vv. 3-4 (hābāh nilbĕnāh, hābāh nibĕneh-lānû): as the humans said "let us make" and "let us build," God says "let us go down." The divine response is structured as a counter-action to the human initiative, step by step. The "us" (nērĕḏāh, first-person plural cohortative) is the same plural as 1:26 — the divine council or the Trinitarian "us" — unexplained but deliberate.
11.8: וַיָּפֶץ יְהוָה אֹתָם מִשָּׁם עַל-פְּנֵי כָל-הָאָרֶץ — "so the LORD scattered them from there over the face of all the earth." The verb pûṣ (Hiphil: to cause to be scattered, disperse) fulfills exactly what they feared (pen-nāpûṣ, v. 4). What they built the city to prevent, God brings about. The scattering is also the fulfillment of the original mandate: milĕʾû ʾet-hāʾāreṣ (fill the earth, 1:28; 9:1) — God scatters them so the earth is filled as originally commanded, if not as they originally intended.
11.9: עַל-כֵּן קָרָא שְׁמָהּ בָּבֶל — "therefore its name is called Babel." The narrative etymology: bābel from Hebrew bālal (to confuse, mix — the verb used in v. 7: nābĕlāh śĕpātām, we will confuse their language). But the Akkadian name Bab-ilim means "gate of the god(s)" — the Babylonians understood their great city as the gateway between earth and heaven. The Hebrew narrator deconstructs this self-glorifying name: what the Babylonians called the "gate of God," the Bible calls "confusion." The wordplay subverts Babylon's entire self-understanding — its foundational claim to cosmic status is a pun on "confusion."
11.10: אֵלֶּה תּוֹלְדֹת שֵׁם — "these are the generations/account of Shem." The tôlĕḏōt formula opens the post-Babel genealogy. This second Shem genealogy (compare 10:21-31) narrows from the broad Table of Nations to a single focused line: Shem → Arphaxad → Shelah → Eber → Peleg → Reu → Serug → Nahor → Terah → Abram. The precision of the genealogy (age at fathering, years remaining, total lifespan) matches the pre-flood genealogy of ch. 5, but lifespans decline sharply.
11.11: Shem's total lifespan: 100 years before Arphaxad + 500 years after = 600 years. Shem is the longest-lived of the post-flood patriarchs and, remarkably, outlives Abraham (who dies at 175, 25:7) — Shem was still alive during Abraham's lifetime by the genealogical arithmetic. The genealogy from Noah to Abraham spans approximately 10 generations, mirroring the antediluvian 10 generations from Adam to Noah (ch. 5).
11.12: Arphaxad's 35 years to fathering Shelah marks the beginning of the post-Babel genealogical line. The form of each entry (wayyiḥî X ben-N šānāh wayyôleḏ ʾet-Y, "X lived N years and fathered Y") uses the same waw-consecutive structure as ch. 5, creating a formal parallel between the antediluvian and post-Babel genealogies — two symmetrical spans between catastrophes (Flood; Babel) leading to the Patriarchal era.
11.13: Arphaxad's remaining 403 years after Shelah: total lifespan 438 years. The lifespan is already dramatically shorter than Shem's 600 — the post-Babel genealogy shows a systematic, generation-by-generation decline in longevity: Shem (600) → Arphaxad (438) → Shelah (433) → Eber (464) → Peleg (239) → Reu (239) → Serug (230) → Nahor (148) → Terah (205). The pattern is not perfectly linear but the trend is unmistakable: the antediluvian world (Adam to Noah: 930, 912, 905, 910, 895, 962, 365, 969, 777, 950 years) is giving way to the patriarchal world.
11.14: Shelah after 30 years fathers Eber — the eponymous ancestor of the Hebrews. The brevity of the genealogical entries in vv. 12-25 reflects their structural-transitional function rather than narrative interest. Each entry confirms the unbroken chain from Shem to Abram, establishing the genealogical continuity of the covenant line across the post-Babel dispersion.
11.15: Shelah's additional 403 years: total 433. The regularity of the numerical pattern (30-35 years before fathering, then 200-430 years remaining) may reflect a schematic presentation rather than precise chronological record — as in ch. 5, the genealogy's theological function (continuity of the promise line) is primary; the numbers establish the bridge between the flood and the patriarchs.
11.16: Eber after 34 years fathers Peleg. עֵבֶר (ʿĒber) — the eponymous ancestor whose name gives "Hebrew" (ʿibrî). Eber is unusual in having the longest lifespan of the post-Babel patriarchs (464 years, v. 17), longer even than Shem (600 is longer, but Eber's 464 exceeds his grandson Peleg's 239 and all descendants through Terah). The longevity of the promise-line's most significant eponym may be deliberate emphasis.
11.17: Eber's additional 430 years: total 464. Eber is the last of the post-Babel patriarchs to live past 400 years. After Eber, the lifespans drop precipitously: Peleg (239), Reu (239), Serug (230), Nahor (148), Terah (205). This sudden drop in Peleg's generation corresponds to Peleg's name signifying the earth's division (10:25) — in the generation of the Babel dispersion, lifespans halve and the era of the patriarchs begins.
11.18: Peleg after 30 years fathers Reu. פֶּלֶג (Peleḡ) = "division" — his name marks the Babel generation. The genealogical line continues through Peleg (not Joktan) to reach Abram — the covenant line runs through the divided, dispersed world, not through the pre-division unity of Babel.
11.19: Peleg's remaining 209 years: total 239 years. The abrupt halving of the lifespan in Peleg's generation (from Eber's 464 to Peleg's 239) is the most dramatic transition in the post-Babel genealogy. Reu's lifespan (239 years, v. 21) matches Peleg's exactly — the two generations of Peleg and Reu mark the floor of the new lifespan era.
11.20: Reu after 32 years fathers Serug. The steady march of the genealogy moves the narrative toward Terah and Abram with increasing momentum — each generation brings the reader closer to the opening of the Abrahamic narrative in ch. 12. The genealogical bridge between the post-Babel world and the Patriarchal promises is being carefully constructed.
11.21: Reu's remaining 207 years: total 239 (matching Peleg's lifespan exactly). The genealogical precision continues without narrative commentary — the emphasis is on the unbroken chain of fatherhood from Shem to Abram, not on the individuals in the chain.
11.22: Serug after 30 years fathers Nahor. שְׂרוּג (Sĕrûḡ) — the name may be connected to the city Sarhugi/Serug in the Harran region (northwestern Mesopotamia), where Abraham's family will later settle (11:31-32). The geographical connection suggests the genealogy is also mapping the patriarchal family's homeland geography.
11.23: Serug's remaining 200 years: total 230. The progressive shortening continues: Serug (230) is shorter-lived than Reu (239) who is the same as Peleg (239). Nahor will live only 148 years (v. 25), the shortest lifespan in the post-Babel genealogy until Terah (205 = longer than Nahor but still in the new short-lifespan era).
11.24: Nahor after 29 years fathers Terah. נָחוֹר (Nāḥôr) — both the grandfather of Abram (here) and the brother of Abram (11:26-29) bear this name; the family naming pattern repeats names across generations, which is common in ANE genealogical practice. The Nahor of 11:22-25 is Abram's grandfather; Nahor in 11:26-29 is Abram's brother.
11.25: Nahor's remaining 119 years: total 148 — the shortest lifespan in the post-Babel genealogy. The dramatic decline from Shem's 600 to Nahor's 148 tracks across nine generations. The lifespan decline corresponds to the widening distance from the Flood (God's protective restraint of death) and the approaching era of the patriarchs, whose lifespans (Abraham 175, Isaac 180, Jacob 147, Joseph 110) are entirely within the human realm familiar to the ancient world.
11.26: וַיְחִי-תֶרַח שִׁבְעִים שָׁנָה וַיּוֹלֶד אֶת-אַבְרָם אֶת-נָחוֹר וְאֶת-הָרָן — "Terah lived seventy years and fathered Abram, Nahor, and Haran." The narrative is on the verge of the Abrahamic promises. Note: Abram is listed first, though the chronology of 11:32 and 12:4 suggests he may not be the oldest son (if Terah was 70 when he fathered his first son, and Abram left Haran at 75 after Terah died at 205, Terah would have been 130 when Abram was born — meaning either Abram is the youngest of the three, or Abram is listed first by prominence rather than birth order, following the genealogical pattern of listing the significant son first).
11.27: אֵלֶּה תּוֹלְדֹת תֶּרַח — "these are the generations/account of Terah." The final tôlĕḏōt formula before the Abrahamic narrative introduces Terah's family. The narrative now zooms from genealogical overview to dramatic close-up. הָרָן מֵת עַל-פְּנֵי תֶּרַח אָבִיו — "Haran died before/in the presence of (ʿal-pĕnê) his father Terah" — lit. "on the face of" = before the eyes of = while still alive. The premature death of Haran in Ur introduces the mortality theme that will haunt the Abrahamic narrative.
11.28: בְּאֶרֶץ מוֹלַדְתּוֹ בְּאוּר כַּשְׂדִּים — "in his land of birth, in Ur of the Chaldeans." The specification of Ur as the family origin grounds the patriarchal narrative geographically. Ur was one of the great Sumerian city-states of Mesopotamia (modern Tell el-Muqayyar in southern Iraq), flourishing c. 2600-2000 BCE — a center of sophisticated urban civilization, astronomy, and the moon-god Nanna worship. The patriarchal family's origin in this cosmopolitan pagan city makes God's call to "leave your land" (12:1) a call out of the very center of the ancient world's civilized, urbanized order.
11.29: וַיִּקַּח אַבְרָם וְנָחוֹר לָהֶם נָשִׁים — "Abram and Nahor took wives." שָׁרַי (Śārāy) — Sarai's name is the variant form of śārāh (princess/noblewoman, which God will rename her in 17:15); the exact relationship between the two forms is uncertain — possibly a dialect or archaic variant. מִלְכָּה (Milkāh) — "queen" (from meleḵ, king) — the sister of Lot, Haran's daughter. The immediate family circle is tight: Abram marries Sarai (his half-sister, 20:12); Nahor marries Milcah his niece.
11.30: וַתְּהִי שָׂרַי עֲקָרָה אֵין לָהּ וָלָד — "now Sarai was barren; she had no child." ʿĂqārāh (barren) — from ʿāqar (to be barren, uprootedness). This single sentence creates the central tension of the entire Abrahamic narrative: the man God is about to promise "a great nation" (12:2) has a barren wife. The promise and the impossibility are placed in immediate juxtaposition — the covenant's fulfillment will require divine action beyond natural means. The matriarchal barrenness pattern (Sarai, Rebekah, Rachel; and Hannah, Manoah's wife in the later narrative) is introduced here as its inaugural instance.
11.31: וַיֵּצְאוּ אִתָּם מֵאוּר כַּשְׂדִּים לָלֶכֶת אַרְצָה כְּנַעַן — "they went out with them from Ur of the Chaldeans to go to the land of Canaan." The journey to Canaan was Terah's initiative — he set out from Ur toward Canaan. But וַיָּבֹאוּ עַד-חָרָן וַיֵּשְׁבוּ שָׁם — "they came as far as Haran and settled there." The journey stopped at Haran (Ḥārrān in Akkadian, northwestern Mesopotamia, modern Harran in Turkey) — a city also associated with the moon-god worship and with the trade routes of the ANE. The incomplete journey creates the narrative gap that God's call of 12:1 will complete.
11.32: וַיָּמָת תֶּרַח בְּחָרָן — "and Terah died in Haran." The death of Terah closes the transitional genealogy and creates the narrative space for Abram's call. Terah's 205 years (unexpectedly longer than his son Nahor's 148) makes him an outlier in the declining lifespan pattern — possibly reflecting the narrator's theological interest in having Abram leave during his father's lifetime, or a traditional variant in the patriarchal genealogy. Stephen's account in Acts 7:4 states that Abraham left Haran after his father died — aligning with the sequence: Terah dies (11:32), then God calls Abram (12:1).

12.1:
וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל-אַבְרָם לֶךְ-לְךָ — "and the LORD said to Abram, 'go for yourself.'" Lēḵ-lĕḵā — the doubled imperative with the reflexive/emphatic dative suffix (-lĕḵā, "for you/yourself") carries both a command to go and an implication that the going is for Abram's own ultimate benefit and fulfillment. The same construction appears at 22:2 (go to Moriah) — the two lēḵ-lĕḵā commands bracket the Abraham narrative as its opening and climactic test.

The three objects Abram must leave are expressed in three increasingly intimate concentric circles: מֵאַרְצְךָ (mēʾarṣĕḵā, from your land — largest, most general), וּמִמּוֹלַדְתְּךָ (ûmimmôlaḏtĕḵā, from your kindred/relatives), וּמִבֵּית אָבִיךָ (ûmibbet ʾāḇîḵā, from your father's household — smallest, most intimate). The movement is from general to particular — from geography to bloodline to immediate family. God demands the severance of all three layers of identity before naming the destination.
12.2:
וְאֶעֶשְׂךָ לְגוֹי גָּדוֹל (wĕʾeʿeśĕḵā lĕḡôy gāḏôl) — "and I will make you into a great nation." Gôy (nation, political entity) is the word used for all the nations in 10:5, 20, 31-32 — all the gôyim from the Table of Nations are here promised to be matched and exceeded by the one gôy that will come from Abram. Seven divine promises follow (counted differently by scholars, but conventionally seven): (1) I will make you a great nation; (2) I will bless you; (3) I will make your name great; (4) you will be a blessing; (5) I will bless those who bless you; (6) whoever curses you I will curse; (7) in you all the families of the earth will be blessed. The sevenfold structure mirrors the seven days of creation — a new creative act is beginning.

וַאֲבָרֶכְךָ — "and I will bless you." Bārak (Piel, to bless) — the blessing formula that appeared in creation (1:22, 28), in Noah (9:1), and now in Abram. שְׁמְךָ גָּדוֹל — "your name great" — the reversal of Babel (11:4: "let us make a name for ourselves"). What humanity tried to achieve by city-building and tower-construction, God gives freely to the one who obeys the call to leave.
12.3:
וַאֲבָרְכָה מְבָרְכֶיךָ — "I will bless those who bless you." The reciprocal blessing/curse principle: those who bless Abram will be blessed; וּמְקַלֶּלְךָ אָאֹר — "whoever curses you I will curse" — the singular form (mĕqallĕlĕḵā is singular) for the cursing vs. the plural for blessing (mĕbārĕḵeḵā), possibly emphasizing that blessing is expected to be widespread but cursing rare.

וְנִבְרְכוּ בְךָ כֹּל מִשְׁפְּחֹת הָאֲדָמָה — "and in you all the families of the earth will be blessed." Wĕnibrĕḵû — the key form: Niphal (passive) third-person plural imperfect. The Niphal reading (passive: they will be blessed) is supported by the MT vocalization and is the reading Paul uses in Gal 3:8 ("in you shall all the nations be blessed"). An alternative vocalization gives Hitpael (reflexive: they will bless themselves by you/call themselves blessed by you, as in Gen 18:18 and 22:18 where the form is different). The MT passive reading supports Paul's Christological application: the blessing flows through Abram to all nations, not merely that nations will invoke Abram's name in self-blessing.
12.4: וַיֵּלֶךְ אַבְרָם כַּאֲשֶׁר דִּבֶּר אֵלָיו יְהוָה — "so Abram went, as the LORD had spoken to him." The obedience formula — as with Noah (6:22; 7:5; 7:9) — signals total and immediate compliance. Hebrews 11:8 comments: "By faith Abraham obeyed when he was called to go out to a place that he was to receive as an inheritance. And he went out, not knowing where he was going" — the narrative does not tell Abram his destination before he leaves, only that God will show him the land (12:1: "the land I will show you"). The going precedes the knowing.
12.5: אֶת-כָּל-רְכוּשָׁם אֲשֶׁר רָכָשׁוּ וְאֶת-הַנֶּפֶשׁ אֲשֶׁר-עָשׂוּ בְחָרָן — "all the possessions they had accumulated and the people (nepeš) they had acquired in Haran." Nepeš here in the sense of persons/slaves — "the souls they had made/acquired." ʿĀśû (they made/acquired) applied to persons: either the slaves and servants accumulated as wealth (standard ANE household servants), or possibly (rabbinic reading) converts/proselytes made in Haran. The word nepeš keeps the human-dignity dimension: these are living persons, not mere property, even if legally held as servants.
12.6:
אֵלוֹן מוֹרֶה (ʾēlôn môreh) — "the oak/terebinth of Moreh." Môreh = teacher, oracle-giver (from yārāh, to teach, to direct). The oak of Moreh at Shechem (Šĕḵem) was likely a divination site — a sacred tree associated with the receiving of oracles or instruction. Abram's first stop in Canaan is at a pagan sacred site, where YHWH appears to him (v. 7). God meets Abram at the very location where the Canaanites sought their gods — the LORD's theophany at a Canaanite holy site is itself a territorial claim.

וְהַכְּנַעֲנִי אָז בָּאָרֶץ — "and the Canaanite was then in the land." The parenthetical note acknowledges the obvious difficulty: the land God is promising to Abram's descendants is occupied. The note is not an explanation but an observation — the promise is made in full knowledge of the existing inhabitants.
12.7: וַיֵּרָא יְהוָה אֶל-אַבְרָם (wayyērāʾ YHWH ʾel-ʾabrām) — "and the LORD appeared to Abram." Nîrāʾ (Niphal perfect, lit. "was seen" or "appeared") — the theophany verb used for all patriarchal appearances of God (17:1; 18:1; 26:2, 24; 35:1, 9; 46:29; 48:3). The form indicates that God made himself visible to Abram — the nature of the appearance is not described, but it is an objective visible event, not merely an inner impression. לְזַרְעֲךָ אֶתֵּן אֶת-הָאָרֶץ הַזֹּאת — "to your offspring (zeraʿ) I will give this land." The land-promise is to the zeraʿ (seed/offspring) — the same collective-or-singular word that carried the promise in 3:15. The land promise is to Abram's descendants, not yet to Abram personally.
12.8: וַיִּבֶן שָׁם מִזְבֵּחַ לַיהוָה — "and he built there an altar to the LORD." Altar-building punctuates Abram's journey through the land: 12:7 (at Shechem, after the first theophany), 12:8 (at Bethel), 13:18 (at Mamre/Hebron), 22:9 (at Moriah). Each altar marks a site of divine encounter and Abrahamic worship — Abram is marking the land with worship of YHWH, establishing a counter-presence to the Canaanite cult sites. וַיִּקְרָא בְּשֵׁם יְהוָה — "and he called on the name of the LORD" — the worship formula introduced at 4:26 (Enosh's generation began calling on the LORD's name). Abram continues this line of YHWH-worshippers in Canaan.
12.9: וַיִּסַּע אַבְרָם הָלוֹךְ וְנָסוֹעַ הַנֶּגְבָּה — "Abram journeyed on, going and setting out toward the Negev." The infinitive absolute construction hālôḵ wĕnāsôaʿ (going and journeying = journeying progressively, continually moving) describes a steady southward migration. The Negev (hanneḡeḇ, the dry south) is the semi-arid region between Judah and the Sinai — Abram is moving from central Canaan toward the southern borderlands before the Egyptian episode.
12.10: וַיְהִי רָעָב בָּאָרֶץ — "now there was a famine in the land." The famine (rāʿāb) is the narrative pressure that drives Abram into Egypt — the land of promise cannot yet sustain its future heir. The same pressure (famine → Egypt) will drive Jacob's family to Egypt in 42:5; 43:1 — the Abram/Egypt episode is a structural anticipation of the Exodus narrative. לָגוּר שָׁם — "to sojourn (gûr) there" — gûr is the word for resident-alien sojourning, the status of someone living outside their native land without full citizen rights (cf. 17:8; 20:1; 21:23, 34; 26:3 — Abram consistently sojourns rather than owning the land).
12.11: הִנֵּה-נָא יָדַעְתִּי כִּי אִשָּׁה יְפַת-מַרְאֶה אָתְּ — "behold, I know that you are a beautiful woman in appearance." Yĕpat-marʾeh (beautiful of appearance/sight) — the standard biblical beauty idiom; cf. 26:7 (Rebekah) and 29:17 (Rachel). Abram's speech to Sarai reveals his fear before the fact: he plans the deception before it is necessary. Yāḏaʿtî (I know) — the knowing is intimate and certain; Abram is under no illusion about the danger his wife's beauty creates.
12.12: וְהָרְגוּ אֹתִי וְאֹתָךְ יְחַיּוּ — "they will kill me and let you live." The calculus is stark: Abram's life vs. Sarai's freedom. The wife-sister ruse (repeated in 20:1-18 with Abimelech and in 26:6-11 with Isaac and Rebekah) is Abram's default survival strategy — one that places Sarai in danger to preserve himself. The text presents this without direct authorial condemnation, but the narrative consequence (Pharaoh's righteous indignation in vv. 18-19; the divine plagues in v. 17) functions as implicit moral assessment.
12.13: אִמְרִי-נָא אֲחֹתִי אָתְּ — "say, please, that you are my sister." The request uses the particle nāʾ (please, I beg you) — indicating deference/request rather than command. In 20:12, Abram acknowledges that Sarai is indeed his half-sister (daughter of the same father, different mother), so the statement is technically true while functionally deceptive. The use of a partial truth to create a false impression raises the question of whether Abram's action is acceptable self-protection or cowardly deception — the narrative's non-resolution of this question is itself a literary feature.
12.14: כִּי-יָפָה הִוא מְאֹד — "for she was very beautiful." Mĕʾōḏ (very, greatly) — the same intensifier as the creation's "very good" (1:31). Sarai's beauty is extreme enough to be noticed by Egyptians immediately. The Egyptians' observation (wayirĕʾû) leading to official commendation (v. 15) follows quickly — the plan Abram feared comes true within verses of his warning to Sarai.
12.15: וַיְהַלְלוּ אֹתָהּ אֶל-פַּרְעֹה (wayĕhallĕlû ʾōtāh ʾel-parʿōh) — "and they praised her to Pharaoh." Hālal (Piel, to praise, commend, boast of) — the root of hallĕlûyāh (praise YHWH). Officials who see Sarai perform the same verb as worship: they extol her beauty to the king. Parʿōh (Pharaoh) — the Hebrew rendering of the Egyptian royal title pr-ʿȝ (great house), used for all Egyptian kings in the OT regardless of dynasty. Sarai is taken into the royal harem — the word bêt parʿōh (Pharaoh's house) is a term for the royal household/palace.
12.16: וּלְאַבְרָם הֵיטִיב בַּעֲבוּרָהּ — "and he dealt well with Abram for her sake." The material benefit Abram sought (v. 13: "that it may go well with me for your sake") is realized: sheep, cattle, donkeys, servants (ʿăbāḏîm wəšipḥōt), female donkeys, and camels. The material enrichment of Abram from the Egyptian sojourn (continued in 13:2: "Abram was very rich") parallels and anticipates the Exodus: Israel will be enriched by the Egyptians as they leave (Exod 12:35-36). The Egypt-sojourn typology runs deep.
12.17: וַיְנַגַּע יְהוָה אֶת-פַּרְעֹה נְגָעִים גְּדֹלִים — "and the LORD afflicted Pharaoh with great plagues." Nāgaʿ (Piel, to strike, afflict) + nĕgāʿîm (plagues, afflictions) — the same root for the Exodus plagues (nāgaʿ in Exod 11:1; the noun nega appears throughout Leviticus for skin diseases/impurities). The word-pair nāgaʿ/nĕgāʿîm creates an explicit etymological link between this mini-Exodus and the actual Exodus — Pharaoh afflicts Israel, God afflicts Pharaoh, in both the proto-Exodus (ch. 12) and the Exodus proper. The divine protection of Sarai (and thus the promise-line) is enacted without Abram's initiative or moral worthiness.
12.18: מַה-זֹּאת עָשִׂיתָ לִּי — "what is this you have done to me?" Pharaoh's rhetorical question exactly mirrors God's question to the woman after the Fall (3:13: "what is this you have done?") and to Cain after the murder (4:10: "what have you done?"). The pagan ruler's moral indignation uses the same formula as divine judicial inquiry — the irony is rich: the one who should be the least morally sophisticated is asking the question most associated with divine judgment. Pharaoh's rhetorical position is ethically superior to Abram's in this moment.
12.19: לָמָּה אָמַרְתָּ אֲחֹתִי הִוא — "why did you say 'she is my sister'?" The threefold rhetorical accusation (mah-zōʾt ʿāśîtā lî, v. 18; lāmmāh ʾāmartā, v. 19a; lāmmāh lōʾ higgaḏtā lî, v. 18) builds through repetition. Pharaoh's "now then, here is your wife" (wĕʿattāh hinnēh ʾištĕḵā) restores Sarai with a formal directive — the pagan king acts with more honor than the patriarch. The narrative does not record Abram's response to this indictment — his silence is as theologically significant as Pharaoh's words.
12.20: וַיְצַו עָלָיו פַּרְעֹה אֲנָשִׁים — "Pharaoh gave orders about him to his men." The expulsion of Abram from Egypt with his wife and possessions intact parallels and anticipates the Exodus: Israel leaves Egypt with Egyptian goods (Exod 12:35-36), escorted out by Pharaoh's command. The proto-Exodus that began with Abram's descent to Egypt in famine (12:10), the affliction of Pharaoh's household (12:17), and the departure with wealth (12:20; 13:2) provides the narrative template for the full Exodus of Israel's descendants centuries later. Abram is Israel in miniature.

13.1: וַיַּעַל אַבְרָם מִמִּצְרַיִם (wayyaʿal ʾaḇrām mimmitsrāyim) — "Abram went up from Egypt." ʿĀlāh (Qal waw-consecutive, to go up, ascend) — directional language that is topographically precise: Canaan is higher than the Nile Delta, so one always "goes up" from Egypt to Canaan. But in the OT the verb carries canonical weight beyond geography: going up to Canaan and going up to Jerusalem become theological movements (Ezra 1:3; 2:1; the post-exilic returns are all described with ʿālāh). Abram's return from Egypt with his wife and wealth complete (unlike the Egypt episode in ch. 12) signals restoration of the narrative that the famine had temporarily suspended.
13.2: כָּבֵד מְאֹד בַּמִּקְנֶה בַּכֶּסֶף וּבַזָּהָב (kāḇēḏ mĕʾōḏ bammiqneh bakkeseḵ ûḇazzāhāḇ) — "very heavy/weighty in livestock and in silver and gold." Kāḇēḏ (heavy, weighty) is the standard word for being wealthy or laden — the same root as kāḇôḏ (glory, honor, weightiness). The semantic field connects material wealth and social honor: to be heavy with goods is to be "glorious" in the eyes of the ancient world. The enumeration of livestock, silver, and gold will recur in the patriarchal wealth-formulas (24:35; 26:14) — the MKT reflects the material texture of ANE pastoral economics.
13.3: לְמַסָּעָיו (lĕmasāʿāyw, by his stages/journeys) — from nāsaʿ (to pull up tent stakes and set out; to journey by stages). The noun massāʿ (journey stage, stopping place) appears most densely in Numbers 33, where Israel's wilderness journey is recorded as a list of massāʾôt (stopping-places). Abram's return to Bethel stage by stage echoes the wilderness wandering — the patriarchal sojourn and the Sinai sojourn are structurally parallel. בֵּין בֵּית-אֵל וּבֵין הָעַי — the specific geography (between Bethel and Ai) marks the exact altar site from 12:8, completing the journey circle.
13.4: שֵׁם יְהוָה (šēm YHWH, the name of the LORD) — calling on the name of YHWH is the worship formula introduced in 4:26 (Enosh's generation "began to call on the name of the LORD") and repeated at each of Abram's altar sites (12:8; here; 21:33). The act of calling on the name is covenantal and public — it marks a site as belonging to YHWH's sphere. Abram returns to the Bethel altar after the Egypt episode: the lapse of ch. 12 is not the end of the story; the worship resumes. The Hebrew idiom qārāʾ bĕšēm YHWH includes the idea of proclaiming the name — invoking YHWH publicly over the landscape.
13.5: גַּם-לְלוֹט (gam-lĕlôṭ, also to Lot) — Lot is syntactically included within Abram's orbit: "also to Lot, who was going with Abram." Hahōlēḵ ʾet-ʾaḇrām (the one going with Abram) — the Qal participle presents Lot's accompanying as continuous. Lot has flocks, herds, and tents of his own — wealth accumulated during the Egypt sojourn (sharing Abram's blessing by proximity). The abundance of both men is what creates the conflict in v. 6.
13.6: לֹא-נָשָׂא אֹתָם הָאָרֶץ (lōʾ-nāśāʾ ʾōtām hāʾāreṣ) — "the land was not able to support them." Nāśāʾ (to carry, bear, lift) — the land literally "could not carry/bear them together." The same idiom appears in 36:7 for Esau and Jacob. The conflict of abundance is a land-carrying issue — the pastoral economy of the ancient Near East required vast territory for grazing herds; the land's limit forces the partition that the next verses describe.
13.7: וַיְהִי-רִיב (wayhî-rîḇ, and there was strife) — rîḇ is the legal/adversarial dispute word: a case, a lawsuit, a controversy (cf. Micah 6:1: "the LORD has a rîḇ with his people"). Even though here it is between herdsmen, the term elevates a pastoral dispute to a quasi-legal conflict. הַכְּנַעֲנִי וְהַפְּרִזִּי אָז יֹשֵׁב בָּאָרֶץ — the parenthetical note about Canaanites and Perizzites in the land reminds the reader that Abram and Lot are resident aliens (gērîm) — their conflict risks dishonoring YHWH's name before the eyes of the land's actual occupants.
13.8: אַל-נָא תְהִי מְרִיבָה (ʾal-nāʾ tĕhî mĕrîḇāh) — "let there not be strife." ʾAl (negative jussive) + nāʾ (please, I pray) — gentle entreaty rather than command; Abram defers to his nephew out of love rather than asserting the senior's claim. Mĕrîḇāh (strife, contention) — the same word as the water-strife site in the wilderness (Exod 17:7; Num 20:13). כִּי-אֲנָשִׁים אַחִים אֲנָחְנוּ — "for we are brothers/kinsmen." ʾAḥîm means kinsmen broadly (uncle-nephew, cousins — not just sibling brothers). The kinship claim is the moral ground for peace: family should not fight.
13.9: הֲלֹא כָל-הָאָרֶץ לְפָנֶיךָ (hălōʾ ḵol-hāʾāreṣ lĕpānêḵā) — "Is not the whole land before you?" The rhetorical question gives Lot the choice — Abram, the elder, cedes his priority right. אִם-הַשְּׂמֹאל וְאֵימִנָה (if left, I will go right; if right, I will go left) — the ABBA chiastic structure of the direction-offer encodes Abram's complete openness. The generosity is remarkable: Abram, the covenant recipient, gives up the right of first selection to preserve family peace. This deference contrasts with the grasping mentality of Babel and Lot's subsequent choice.
13.10:
כִּכַּר הַיַּרְדֵּן כִּי כֻלָּהּ מַשְׁקֶה (kikkar hayyardēn kî ḵullāh mašqeh) — "the whole plain of the Jordan, for all of it was well-watered." Kikkar (the round/circular area, the plain) — the Jordan Valley. Mašqeh (well-watered, irrigated) from šāqāh (to water, give to drink).

כְּגַן-יְהוָה כְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם — "like the garden of the LORD, like the land of Egypt." Two comparisons: Eden (the garden of the LORD, cf. 2:8-10) and Egypt (the Nile irrigation, 12:10). Both carry ominous resonance: the garden is where humanity was expelled from; Egypt is where Abram nearly lost Sarai. The most desirable territory in Lot's eyes is associated with exile and the foreign. The narrator's proleptic "before the LORD destroyed Sodom and Gomorrah" signals the fatal irony: Lot is choosing what looks like paradise, which will become destruction.
13.11: וַיִּסַּע לוֹט מִקֶּדֶם (wayyissaʿ lôṭ miqqeḏem) — "Lot set out eastward." Qeḏem (east, the direction of the rising sun) carries the narrative weight of departure throughout Genesis: the expulsion from Eden is eastward (3:24); Cain goes east (4:16); Babel is in the east (11:2). Lot's eastward movement falls into this pattern — east marks movement away from the presence of God and toward the region of judgment. The text's structural use of qeḏem is not coincidental.
13.12: אַבְרָם יָשַׁב בְּאֶרֶץ-כְּנָעַן וְלוֹט יָשַׁב בְּעָרֵי הַכִּכָּר — the two men's destinations are contrasted in a single verse: Abram in the land of Canaan (the promise); Lot among the cities of the plain. וַיֶּאֱהַל עַד-סְדֹם — "and pitched his tents as far as Sodom." ʾĀhal (to tent, to pitch a tent) — Lot gradually approaches Sodom; in 14:12 he is "living in Sodom"; in 19:1 he is "sitting at the gate of Sodom" (a position of civic leadership). The progressive enculturation into Sodom happens in stages, tracked by these verbal notices.
13.13: וְאַנְשֵׁי סְדֹם רָעִים וְחַטָּאִים לַיהוָה מְאֹד — "now the men of Sodom were wicked and sinners against the LORD exceedingly." Two near-synonyms pile up: rāʿîm (wicked, evil — the general moral category) and ḥaṭṭāʾîm (sinners — those who miss the mark, ḥāṭāʾ). The intensifier mĕʾōḏ (greatly, exceedingly) at the end is emphatic. The phrase laYHWH (against the LORD) frames their wickedness as transgression against the covenant God specifically, not merely against social norms. This verse sets the stage for the divine inspection and judgment of chs. 18-19.
13.14: שָׂא עֵינֶיךָ וּרְאֵה מִן-הַמָּקוֹם אֲשֶׁר-אַתָּה שָׁם — "lift up your eyes and look from the place where you are." The command śāʾ ʿênêḵā (lift your eyes) is the standard prelude to a divinely directed seeing-event throughout the patriarchal narratives (13:14; 18:2; 22:4, 13; 24:63; 31:10, 12; 33:1). Immediately after Lot departs, YHWH speaks to Abram — the covenant promise restated. The land promise is now given not merely for offspring (12:7) but directly to Abram's sight: "all the land that you see I will give to you." The land is visualized before it is possessed.
13.15: כִּי אֶת-כָּל-הָאָרֶץ אֲשֶׁר-אַתָּה רֹאֶה לְךָ אֶתְּנֶנָּה וּלְזַרְעֲךָ עַד-עוֹלָם — "for all the land that you see, to you I will give it and to your offspring forever." ʿAḏ-ʿôlām (forever, unto eternity) — this is the perpetual/eternal form of the land covenant: the promised possession is not temporary. ʿÔlām (long duration, age, perpetuity) — when applied to the covenant (17:7, 8, 13, 19; Exod 31:16-17), it carries the force of an irrevocable divine commitment that transcends any single generation. Remarkably, this unconditional promise comes immediately after Lot has chosen the best land — YHWH gives Abram what Lot visually appropriated.
13.16: וְשַׂמְתִּי אֶת-זַרְעֲךָ כַּעֲפַר הָאָרֶץ — "I will make your offspring like the dust of the earth." The dust metaphor for innumerability (here) complements the star metaphor of 15:5 and the sand metaphor of 22:17. Each image emphasizes a different dimension: dust = ubiquitous, earth-covering (geographic spread); stars = sublime, heaven-reaching (transcendence); sand = borderline, coastal (the seashore of promise). ʿĀpār hāʾāreṣ (dust of the earth) echoes Adam's creation from the dust (2:7) and Adam's return to dust (3:19) — the offspring who will be as dust are those formed from the dust, a subtle human-solidarity image.
13.17: קוּם הִתְהַלֵּךְ בָּאָרֶץ לְאָרְכָּהּ וּלְרָחְבָּהּ — "arise, walk through the land in its length and in its breadth." Hitchallēḵ (Hithpael of hālak, walk about through) — the Hithpael denotes reciprocal or intensive walking, traversing completely. This same form appears in 17:1 (God's command to "walk before me") and 5:22, 24 (Enoch "walked with God"). Walking through the land is a symbolic act of possession — traversing the territory claims it (cf. Josh 1:3: "every place your foot treads I will give to you"). The command anticipates Joshua's reconnaissance of the land; Abram is to survey what is promised.
13.18: אֵלֹנֵי מַמְרֵא אֲשֶׁר בְּחֶבְרוֹן — "the oaks of Mamre which are at Hebron." ʾĒlônê mamrēʾ (oaks/terebinths of Mamre) — the great trees site at Hebron; Mamre is an Amorite chieftain allied with Abram (14:13, 24). Abram settles at the site of his Amorite ally, near sacred trees. Ḥeḇrôn (Hebron) — the name may be connected to the root ḥāḇar (to join, to be allied/confederate) — "fellowship" — apt for a place where Abram lives among allied Amorites. Hebron will be the site of Sarah's burial (23:19), Abram's burial (25:9), and Israel's first royal capital under David (2 Sam 2:3-4). מִזְבֵּחַ לַיהוָה — another altar, the fourth in Abram's worship-marking of the land (12:7, 8; 13:4; here).

14.1: The narrative opens with a list of four kings who form the imperial coalition: אַמְרָפֶל (Amrāpel, Amraphel) king of Shinar (= Babylonia, cf. 10:10; 11:2); אַרְיוֹךְ (ʾAryôḵ, Arioch) king of Ellasar; כְּדָרְלָעֹמֶר (Kĕḏarlāʿomer, Kedorlaomer) king of Elam — an Elamite compound name meaning "servant of [the god] Lagamar"; תִּדְעָל (Tiḏʿāl, Tidal) king of nations (gôyim). The historical identification of these kings with specific ancient rulers is debated; the narrative does not depend on the identifications. What matters is the geopolitical scope: the eastern imperial coalition spans from Babylonia to Elam.
14.2: The five Canaanite kings of the plain include בֶּרַע (Beraʿ, Bera) king of Sodom and בִּרְשַׁע (Biršaʿ, Birsha) king of Gomorrah — both names may pun on Hebrew moral terms: beraʿ ("in evil") and biršaʿ ("in wickedness") — the narrator may be encoding the moral character of Sodom and Gomorrah's kings in their names, though this is debated as a wordplay vs. actual Semitic names. The city list (Sodom, Gomorrah, Admah, Zeboiim, Bela/Zoar) is the pentapolis of the plain, the same cities mentioned in 10:19 and whose destruction is narrated in 19:24-25.
14.3: עֵמֶק הַשִּׂדִּים הוּא יָם הַמֶּלַח — "the valley of Siddim, that is, the Salt Sea." The parenthetical identification equates the Valley of Siddim with the Dead Sea — the narrator writes from a post-destruction perspective, when the valley (once fertile, 13:10) has become the Salt Sea. The five city-plain is now underwater. This geographic parenthetical functions as a proleptic epitaph: even before the battle is described, the reader knows the valley ceased to exist.
14.4: שְׁתֵּים עֶשְׂרֵה שָׁנָה עָבְדוּ אֶת-כְּדָרְלָעֹמֶר — "twelve years they served Kedorlaomer, and in the thirteenth year they rebelled." The twelve-year submission and thirteenth-year rebellion provides the political context: the Canaanite pentapolis had been vassals of the Elamite king. The fourteenth year brings the punitive campaign. The numbers (12/13/14) track the years precisely — unusual specificity that signals either archival source material or deliberate literary framing of the event as a genuine historical incident within the patriarchal period.
14.5: הָרְפָאִים (hārĕpāʾîm, the Rephaim) — "the ancient ones/mighty ones"; these pre-Israelite giants appear throughout the OT (Deut 2:10-11, 20-21; 3:11; Josh 12:4; 13:12; 17:15; 1 Chron 20:4-8). The Rephaim were associated with both ancient pre-Canaanite peoples and with the shades of the dead in Sheol (Isa 14:9; 26:14; Ps 88:10; Job 26:5). The campaign sweeps through the entire Transjordanian territory, clearing out ancient peoples — the coalition's military reach is comprehensive and the narrative emphasizes the scope of their conquest before Abram enters the story.
14.6: הַחֹרִי בְּהַרֵרָם שֵׂעִיר — "the Horites in their mountain territory of Seir." The Horites (ḥōrî, cave-dwellers?) are the pre-Edomite inhabitants of the Seir region (Deut 2:12, 22), displaced by Esau's descendants. The campaign route sweeps south through the Transjordan, then curves back north — a military encirclement strategy that allows the eastern coalition to strike the southern Canaanite cities from the rear after isolating them geographically.
14.7: עֵין מִשְׁפָּט הִוא קָדֵשׁ — "En-mishpat, that is, Kadesh." The parenthetical identification links the obscure battle-site name with the well-known Kadesh-barnea of the wilderness narratives (Num 13:26; 20:1; Deut 1:2) — where Israel camped before the conquest. The coalition's route presages the later route of the Exodus-Conquest. אֵת כָּל-שְׂדֵה הָעֲמָלֵקִי — "the whole territory of the Amalekites" — Amalek appears as a people long before Esau's grandson Amalek of 36:12; the name here may be ethnic rather than eponymous, identifying the pre-Israelite inhabitants of the Negev.
14.8: The five Canaanite kings march out to meet the eastern coalition in battle in the Valley of Siddim. The narrative structure is deliberate: after tracing the eastern coalition's comprehensive sweep through the Transjordan (vv. 5-7), the five kings emerge to fight — the setup makes clear that the Canaanite armies have been watching a vastly superior force that has already routed every regional power in its path. The battle is practically decided before it begins.
14.9: The battle is framed numerically: four kings against five. Despite outnumbering the coalition, the five Canaanite kings lose. The numerical contrast (four vs. five, which should favor the Canaanites) heightens the drama of the defeat and sets up Abram's counterintuitive victory with only 318 men (v. 14). The pattern of the few defeating the many — a motif running from Gideon (Judg 7) to David (1 Sam 17) — is anticipated here in embryonic form.
14.10: בְּאֵרֹת חֵמָר עֵמֶק הַשִּׂדִּים — "the Valley of Siddim was full of tar pits." Ḥēmār (bitumen, tar, asphalt) — the same ḥēmār used as the mortar of Babel's tower (11:3) and for Moses's basket (Exod 2:3). The bitumen pits of the Dead Sea region are archaeologically attested; the valley's terrain was treacherous for retreating armies. The fleeing kings of Sodom and Gomorrah fall into the pits — a military disaster that explains how Lot and Sodom's goods are taken by the coalition without further resistance.
14.11: וַיִּקְחוּ אֶת-כָּל-רְכֻשׁ סְדֹם וַעֲמֹרָה — "they took all the goods of Sodom and Gomorrah and all their food."
