The language of zāraʿ preservation is the language of the Abrahamic covenant (17:7-10), here inverted: rather than waiting for divine provision (as Sarah was told to do, and as Abraham would later do on Moriah), the daughters take matters into their own hands. The parallel to the Sarai/Hagar episode (ch 16 — taking a shortcut to secure zāraʿ) is deliberate.
19.33: wĕlōʾ-yāḏaʿ bĕšiḵḇāh ûbĕqûmāh — "and he did not know when she lay down or when she rose." The phrase yāḏaʿ — "he did not know" — brings the sexual idiom of the chapter full circle. Sodom's men demanded to "know" the visitors (v5); Lot's daughters were described as "not known by a man" (v8); now Lot "does not know" (lōʾ yāḏaʿ) what happened — a moral oblivion enabled by wine. The use of yāḏaʿ throughout this chapter brackets the entire Sodom narrative: the word that should signify covenantal relationship is used for assault (v5), for innocence (v8), and for violated consciousness (v33,35). The chapter ends on the same term it began with.
19.34: wayĕhî mimmāḥǎrāt wayyōʾmer habbĕḵîrāh ʾel-haṣṣĕʿîrāh hēn-šāḵaḇtî ʾemeš ʾet-ʾāḇî — "The next day the older said to the younger, 'Last night I lay with my father.'" ʾEmeš (yesterday, last night) — an adverb used in contexts of recent time. The older daughter reports the event factually, without expressed regret or moral evaluation. The narrative maintains the same factual, evaluative silence — it does not insert a narrator's condemnation but the consequences (Moab, Ammon) will speak through the rest of the biblical story.
19.35: wattāqom haṣṣĕʿîrāh gam-hîʾ wattiškaḇ ʿimmô wĕlōʾ-yāḏaʿ bĕšiḵḇāh ûbĕqûmāh — "The younger also arose and lay with him, and he did not know when she lay down or when she rose." The same formula repeated verbatim — wĕlōʾ-yāḏaʿ bĕšiḵḥāh ûbĕqûmāh — for the second night emphasizes the pattern. The doubled formula (vv33,35) has the effect of insisting on the parallelism: the act is recorded twice in the same words, closing off interpretive escape routes. Lot's unknowing is complete — wine and darkness create a moral vacancy that the text refuses to mitigate.
19.36: wattaharên šĕtê ḇĕnôt-lôṭ mēʾăḇîhen — "So both of Lot's daughters became pregnant by their father." The bare statement — without embellishment, without divine comment — records the outcome. The word harah (to conceive, become pregnant) — the same word used for Sarah (18:14 — "shall Sarah conceive?") and for the Exodus generation's births. Here it is stripped of covenantal framing: the pregnancy is the product of calculation and deception, not divine promise. The contrast with Sarah's divinely promised conception (18:14) and the miraculous births of the covenant line is stark.
19.37: wattēled habbĕḵîrāh bēn wattigrāʾ šĕmô môʾāḇ hûʾ ʾăḇî môʾāḇ ʿad-hayyôm — "The older bore a son and called his name Moab; he is the father of the Moabites to this day." Môʾāḇ — the folk etymology here implies "from father" (mēʾāḇ), making the name a transparent reference to the incestuous origin. The Moabites and Israelites will be in perpetual tension in the subsequent narrative — but the genealogy of Ruth will bring a Moabite woman into the Davidic line (Ruth 4:13-22; Matt 1:5), and the God of Israel will declare himself the God of Moab's redemption through that lineage.
19.38: wĕhaṣṣĕʿîrāh gam-hîʾ yālaḏāh bēn wattiqrāʾ šĕmô ben-ʿammî hûʾ ʾăḇî ḇĕnê-ʿammôn ʿad-hayyôm — "The younger also bore a son and called his name Ben-Ammi; he is the father of the Ammonites to this day." Ben-ʿammî = "son of my people/kin" — again the name encodes the incestuous relation (ʿam = kinsman/people). Both Moab and Ammon will be thorns to Israel (Deut 23:3-6 excludes them from the assembly to the tenth generation), yet in the canon Ammon also has a place — Naamah the Ammonite is Solomon's wife and mother of Rehoboam (1 Kgs 14:21,31), weaving the descendants of Lot's cave into the royal line. The chapter ends not with closure but with "to this day" — these nations exist, and the narrative of their origins is the explanation of both their kinship with Israel and their antagonism.

20.1: וַיָּגָר בִּגְרָר (wayyāḡār biḡrār) — "he sojourned in Gerar." Gûr (to sojourn, dwell as a resident alien) — the same verb that defines Abraham's entire existence in Canaan (17:8; 21:23; 26:3; 28:4). Gerar is a Philistine city in the Negev region (between Kadesh and Shur) — the territory of a settled people with their own political structure. The narrative repeats the wife-sister motif of ch. 12 in a new setting; this is the second of the three iterations (12:10-20; here; 26:6-11 with Isaac and Rebekah).
20.2: אֲחֹתִי הִוא (ʾăḥōtî hîʾ, she is my sister) — the same deception formula as 12:13, now enacted in Philistine territory. Aḇîmelek melek-gĕrār (Abimelech king of Gerar) — Aḇîmelek = "my father is king" (a throne name or hereditary title in the region; used for multiple Philistine rulers: cf. 26:1; Ps 34 superscript). וַיִּשְׁלַח אֲבִימֶלֶךְ — sent for Sarah: the king's acquisition of a woman for his household is a royal prerogative in the ANE; the danger to the covenant line is immediate and concrete.
20.3: וַיָּבֹא אֱלֹהִים אֶל-אֲבִימֶלֶךְ בַּחֲלוֹם הַלָּיְלָה — "God came to Abimelech in a dream of the night." The divine name throughout ch. 20 is ʾĔlōhîm (God), not YHWH — the generic Semitic divine name rather than the covenant name. This is consistent with the chapter's setting in a non-Israelite context: God is revealing himself to a Philistine king through the form of divine communication available to all peoples (dreams — cf. Gen 41; Dan 2). הִנְּךָ מֵת (hinnĕḵā mēt, you are as good as dead) — the participial construction (hinneḵā + participle) expresses an imminent or already-determined state: "behold you [are] dead." The death-sentence is delivered before any wrongdoing has occurred, emphasizing that God's intervention precedes Abimelech's potential sin.
20.4: הֲגוֹי גַּם-צַדִּיק תַּהֲרֹג (hăgôy gam-ṣaddîq taharōḡ) — "will you destroy even a righteous nation?" Abimelech uses ʾAḏōnāy (Lord/Sovereign) — the Hebrew honorific used by Israelites before YHWH, suggesting that the narrator considers Abimelech's recognition of God as genuine. Ṣaddîq (righteous, in the right) — Abimelech claims moral innocence, using the covenant-justice vocabulary (ṣĕḏāqāh field of meaning). The question echoes Abraham's own challenge to divine justice in 18:25 ("will the judge of all the earth not do justice?") — a pagan king makes the same moral argument as the covenant patriarch.
20.5: בְּתָם-לְבָבִי וּבְנִקְיֹן כַּפַּי (bĕtom-lĕbābî ûbĕniqyōn kappāy) — "with the integrity of my heart and the cleanness of my hands." Tōm (wholeness, integrity) of the heart and niqyōn (cleanness, innocence) of the hands — the combination appears in Ps 24:4 (the worshipper who may ascend YHWH's mountain) and Ps 26:6; the moral idiom of clean hands/pure heart is a covenant-piety formula applied here by a non-Israelite king. Abimelech's self-defense is complete and legitimate — he acted on the information given.
20.6: גַּם אָנֹכִי יָדַעְתִּי כִּי בְתָם-לְבָבְךָ עָשִׂיתָ זֹּאת — "Yes, I myself also knew that you did this with the integrity of your heart." God confirms Abimelech's innocence — divine omniscience vindicates the claim. וָאֶחְשֹׂךְ גַּם-אָנֹכִי אוֹתְךָ מֵחֲטוֹ-לִי — "and I myself also withheld you from sinning against me." Ḥāśaḵ (to withhold, restrain, hold back) — God actively prevented Abimelech from sinning by means of the physical situation (not letting him touch Sarah). The divine protection operates at the providential level: God manages the circumstances to prevent the sin, then warns the king to reverse the situation.
20.7: כִּי-נָבִיא הוּא (kî-nāḇîʾ hûʾ, for he is a prophet) — the first and only time Abraham is explicitly called a nāḇîʾ (prophet) in Genesis. Nāḇîʾ — the Hebrew term for prophet (from nābaʾ, to speak as a mouthpiece; or possibly related to Akkadian nabû, to call/name). The prophetic office is here defined functionally: the prophet intercedes (יִתְפַּלֵּל, Hithpael of pālal, to pray/intercede) on behalf of others. Abraham's prophetic role is intercessory — he will pray for Abimelech's household (vv. 17-18). This is the OT's first connection between prophecy and intercession.
20.8: וַיַּשְׁכֵּם אֲבִימֶלֶךְ בַּבֹּקֶר — "Abimelech rose early in the morning." Hiškîm (Hiphil, to rise early) — the same word used for Abraham's early rising in 22:3 (for the Akedah) and 21:14 (to send Hagar away). The urgency of rising early marks responsiveness to divine word. Wayyiqrāʾ lĕḵol-ʿăḇāḏāyw (he summoned all his servants) — the king immediately marshals his household officials to deal with the divine threat, indicating he takes the dream-warning with complete seriousness.
20.9: מַה-עָשִׂיתָ לָּנוּ (mah-ʿāśîtā lānû) — "what have you done to us?" Abimelech's reproach mirrors exactly the formula of divine judicial inquiry (3:13; 4:10; 12:18) — and Pharaoh's rebuke in 12:18 used the same words. מַעֲשִׂים אֲשֶׁר לֹא-יֵעָשׂוּ (maʿăśîm ʾăšer lōʾ-yēʿāśû) — "deeds that should not be done." The Niphal passive construction with lōʾ creates a categorical prohibition: these are actions that lie outside the bounds of acceptable conduct. The pagan king invokes a moral absolute — a recognition of universal ethical norms independent of covenant. The reproach is the same as in 12:18-19: the moral credibility of the covenant family is implicated.
20.10: מָה רָאִיתָ כִּי עָשִׂיתָ אֶת-הַדָּבָר הַזֶּה — "what did you see, that you did this thing?" Rāʾāh (to see, perceive) in the sense of: what assessment, what observation of the situation led you to this action? The question asks for the underlying reasoning — not just what happened but why. Abraham's answer in vv. 11-13 is the longest self-defense in the patriarchal narratives, revealing his theological reasoning and domestic arrangement.
20.11: אַךְ אֵין-יִרְאַת אֱלֹהִים בַּמָּקוֹם הַזֶּה — "surely there is no fear of God in this place." Yirʾat ʾĔlōhîm (fear of God) — the phrase that in Proverbs 1:7 is "the beginning of wisdom" and that defines the morally ordered community; Abraham assumed that without YHWH-worship there would be no moral restraint. The narrative immediately subverts his assumption: Abimelech shows more moral sensitivity than Abraham. The patriarchal character is presented without sanitizing — Abraham's fear-based reasoning led to a deceptive action that placed his wife in danger.
20.12: וְגַם-אָמְנָה אֲחֹתִי בַת-אָבִי הִוא אַךְ לֹא בַת-אִמִּי — "and indeed she is truly my sister, daughter of my father, though not daughter of my mother." The half-sibling relationship between Abraham and Sarah (same father, different mothers) is revealed here for the first time. This makes the deception technically a half-truth — she is his half-sister — while functionally deceptive since she is also his wife. The law of Lev 18:9; 20:17 would prohibit this marriage, but the patriarchal narrative precedes the Sinai legislation and presents the situation without anachronistic condemnation.
20.13: כַּאֲשֶׁר הִתְעוּ אֹתִי אֱלֹהִים מִבֵּית אָבִי — "when God caused me to wander from my father's household." Tāʿāh (Hiphil: to cause to wander, lead astray) — with God as subject, this is not a negative but a description of the nomadic calling: God led Abraham out in a wandering way. The same verb in Ps 107:4 ("they wandered in the desert wasteland") describes Israel's wilderness sojourn as divine leading-through-wandering. Abraham's self-account frames the journey as divine leading; the wife-sister agreement was a precaution for that wandering. עַשְׂי חַסְדֵּךְ (ʿaśî ḥasdêḵ, do your covenant-loyalty/kindness to me) — ḥeseḏ (covenant loyalty, steadfast love) as the basis of the domestic agreement.
20.14: וַיִּקַּח אֲבִימֶלֶךְ צֹאן וּבָקָר וַעֲבָדִים וּשְׁפָחֹת — Abimelech's restitution mirrors Pharaoh's gifts in 12:16 — the same categories (sheep and cattle, male and female slaves). The parallel is deliberate: both pagan rulers respond to the divine threat by enriching Abraham; the material wealth of the covenant family grows through these encounters. The return of Sarah is the primary act: the goods are accompaniment to restoration of the wife.
20.15: הִנֵּה אַרְצִי לְפָנֶיךָ (hinnēh ʾarṣî lĕpānêḵā, behold my land is before you) — Abimelech offers Abraham open access to his territory; the formula "the land is before you" echoes Abraham's offer to Lot in 13:9 and the covenant land-promise (cf. Deut 1:8; 4:1). Bĕṭôḇ bĕʿênêḵā šēḇ (wherever is good in your eyes, dwell) — the idiom of personal choice and sovereign hospitality; Abimelech grants Abraham the right to settle anywhere in Gerar's territory.
20.16: הִנֵּה הוּא-לְךָ כְּסוּת עֵינַיִם (hinnēh hûʾ-lĕḵā kĕsût ʿênāyim, behold it is for you a covering of eyes) — the thousand shekels of silver is described as a "covering of eyes." The phrase kĕsût ʿênāyim is rare and its precise meaning is debated: (1) something that averts others' eyes from the offense — a payment to make people overlook what happened; (2) a face-veil or covering for Sarah's honor — restoring her dignity in the eyes of the community; (3) compensation for the "unveiled" or "uncovered" offense. The LXX reads "this shall be for you for the honor of your face" (τοῦτό σοι εἰς τιμήν). Whatever the precise idiom, the silver is restorative: וְנֹכַחַת לְכֹל — "you are fully vindicated/set right before all."
20.17: וַיִּתְפַּלֵּל אַבְרָהָם אֶל-הָאֱלֹהִים — "Abraham prayed to God." Hitpallēl (Hithpael of pālal, to intercede, pray) — the prophetic intercessory role announced in v. 7 is now fulfilled. Abraham prays for the pagan king's household — one of the clearest instances of the OT's intercessory prayer on behalf of non-Israelites. וַיִּרְפָּא אֱלֹהִים אֶת-אֲבִימֶלֶךְ — "and God healed Abimelech." Rāpāʾ (to heal) — healing as the restoration of divine blessing; the wombs closed by God's judgment (v. 18) are reopened by God's grace through prophetic intercession.
20.18: כִּי-עָצֹר עָצַר יְהוָה בְּעַד כָּל-רֶחֶם (kî-ʿāṣōr ʿāṣar YHWH bĕʿaḏ kol-reḥem) — "for the LORD had tightly closed (infinitive absolute + verb) every womb." The emphatic infinitive absolute ʿāṣōr ʿāṣar (surely shut up, firmly closed) uses the same verb as 16:2 where Sarai says "the LORD has shut me up from bearing." The closing of the Abimelech household's wombs is YHWH's name for the first time in the chapter — the narrator reveals that the divine action protecting Sarah was specifically YHWH even in this Elohim-narrative. The irony: the patriarch whose wife is barren temporarily makes others barren by his deception.

21.1: וַיהוָה פָּקַד אֶת-שָׂרָה (waYHWH pāqaḏ ʾet-śārāh) — "the LORD visited Sarah." Pāqaḏ (to visit, attend to, appoint, muster) — a verb with two contrasting senses that must be determined from context: divine visitation in blessing (Ruth 1:6; 1 Sam 2:21; Luke 1:68: "he has visited/redeemed his people") and divine visitation in judgment (Exod 32:34; Amos 3:2). Here unambiguously blessing: God fulfills the promise of 17:16-21 and 18:10. The covenantal attention expressed by pāqaḏ is intimate and purposeful — God did not merely allow nature to take its course; he attended to Sarah specifically.
21.2: לַמּוֹעֵד אֲשֶׁר-דִּבֶּר אֹתוֹ אֱלֹהִים (lammôʿēḏ ʾăšer-dibbēr ʾōtô ʾĔlōhîm) — "at the appointed time that God had spoken." Môʿēḏ (appointed time, fixed season, set meeting) — the same word used for the sacred assembly times of Israel's festival calendar (Lev 23; Num 28-29). Isaac's birth is at a divinely appointed môʿēḏ, not a random biological event — the covenant heir arrives at God's chosen moment, on God's schedule (announced in 18:10: "at the appointed time I will return to you").
21.3: וַיִּקְרָא אַבְרָהָם אֶת-שֶׁם-בְּנוֹ הַנּוֹלַד-לוֹ אֲשֶׁר-יָלְדָה-לּוֹ שָׂרָה יִצְחָק — "Abraham called the name of his son who was born to him, whom Sarah bore him, Isaac." Yiṣḥāq (Isaac) = "he laughs / he will laugh" — from ṣāḥaq (to laugh); God specified this name in 17:19 ("you will call his name Isaac"). The name encodes Sarah's laughter of disbelief (18:12-13) and Abraham's laughter of joyful astonishment (17:17) — both are transformed into the child's identity. The name is also the first Hebrew term in the Bible derived from the word for joy/laughter (ṣĕḥōq), suggesting the covenant heir's arrival is inherently a cause for rejoicing.
21.4: וַיָּמָל אַבְרָהָם אֶת-יִצְחָק בְּנוֹ בֶּן-שְׁמֹנַת יָמִים — "Abraham circumcised his son Isaac when he was eight days old." The circumcision is performed exactly as commanded in 17:12 ("every male among you eight days old shall be circumcised") — the narrative carefully records that the covenant sign was applied to the covenant heir on the prescribed day. כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה אֹתוֹ אֱלֹהִים (as God had commanded him) — the obedience formula; the narrator marks the fulfilled command as the structural point of the verse.
21.5: וְאַבְרָהָם בֶּן-מְאַת שָׁנָה — "and Abraham was a hundred years old." The chronological note emphasizes the impossibility: 100 years old (cf. 17:17; Rom 4:19: "his own body was as good as dead, being about a hundred years old"). The birth of Isaac is presented as a biological impossibility overcome by divine promise — the covenant heir's origin is not from natural human capacity but from divine faithfulness to the spoken word.
21.6: צְחֹק עָשָׂה-לִּי אֱלֹהִים (ṣĕḥōq ʿāśāh-lî ʾĔlōhîm) — "God has made laughter for me." Ṣĕḥōq (laughter, mirth) — the same root as Isaac's name, now completed: the name was given in anticipation, and the laughter it names has arrived. כָּל-הַשֹּׁמֵעַ יִצְחַק-לִי (all who hear will laugh with me/at me) — the verb yiṣḥaq-lî is the jussive form of Isaac's name: "everyone who hears will [yiṣḥaq = Isaac]" — the impossible birth makes the name a living event, a standing proclamation of divine power. The ambiguity of "laugh with me" (joy) or "laugh at me" (astonishment) is deliberate in the Hebrew.
21.7: מִי מִלֵּל לְאַבְרָהָם הֵינִיקָה בָנִים שָׂרָה — "Who would have said to Abraham that Sarah would nurse children?" Millēl (Pilpel of mālal, to say, speak — an Aramaicizing form used in poetic or archaic contexts) — a rhetorical impossibility: no human being would have dared to predict it. The nursing detail (hênîqāh bānîm, nurse sons) is specific — Sarah suckles the child, proving she has milk, proving the physiological restoration of a post-menopausal woman. כִּי-יָלַדְתִּי בֵן לִזְקֻנָיו (for I have borne a son in his old age) — the son born to an old man; the miracle belongs to both parents.
21.8: וַיִּגְדַּל הַיֶּלֶד וַיִּגָּמַל (wayyiḡdal hayyeleḏ wayyiggāmal) — "the child grew and was weaned." Gāmal (to wean, accomplish, repay, ripen) — the weaning feast marks a significant developmental milestone; ANE practice typically involved nursing for 2-3 years. מִשְׁתֶּה גָדוֹל (great feast) — the weaning celebration is a public, community event; Isaac's growth from birth to weaning is publicly acknowledged. The feast setting introduces the conflict of vv. 9-10: what Ishmael does, he does during this celebration of Isaac.
21.9: וַתֵּרֶא שָׂרָה אֶת-בֶּן-הָגָר הַמִּצְרִית אֲשֶׁר-יָלְדָה לְאַבְרָהָם מְצַחֵק — "Sarah saw the son of Hagar the Egyptian, whom she had borne to Abraham, mocking/laughing." Mĕṣaḥēq (Piel participle of ṣāḥaq) — the same root as Isaac's name (Yiṣḥāq). The Piel of ṣāḥaq has a stronger force than the Qal: the basic meaning is "playing, sporting, laughing," but the Piel elsewhere means "mocking" (39:14, 17 where Potiphar's wife accuses Joseph of the same verb), "making sport" with potential sexual or aggressive overtones, or engaging in idol-worship (32:6; Exod 32:6). The LXX simply reads "playing" (paizonta). Paul in Gal 4:29 uses the language of "persecution." The Hebrew text leaves the precise nature deliberately open; the narrative function is clear: Sarah perceives it as a threat to Isaac's inheritance.
21.10: גָּרֵשׁ הָאָמָה הַזֹּאת וְאֶת-בְּנָהּ (gārēš hāʾāmāh hazzōʾt wĕʾet-bĕnāh) — "drive out this slave woman and her son." Gāraš (to drive out, expel) — a strong term (used for the expulsion from Eden, 3:24; the driving out of nations, Exod 34:11). Sarah's demand is harsh: ʾāmāh (slave woman, maidservant) — she does not call Hagar by name or Ishmael by name; they are reduced to "this slave woman and her son." לֹא יִירַשׁ בֶּן-הָאָמָה הַזֹּאת עִם-בְּנִי עִם-יִצְחָק — "the son of this slave woman will not inherit with my son, with Isaac." The legal concern is inheritance: Ishmael, as Abraham's firstborn, has a claim; Sarah insists on Isaac's exclusive inheritance. The double "with my son, with Isaac" is emphatic contrast.
21.11: וַיֵּרַע הַדָּבָר מְאֹד בְּעֵינֵי אַבְרָהָם — "the matter was very displeasing to Abraham." Rāʿaʿ (to be evil, bad, displeasing) — the idiom rāʿaʿ bĕʿênê (evil/bad in the eyes of) is the standard formula for moral/emotional displeasure. עַל אוֹדֹת בְּנוֹ (on account of his son) — it is Ishmael Abraham grieves for: his firstborn, 13 years old at Isaac's birth (17:25), now being expelled. The narrative presents Abraham's grief as genuine parental love for Ishmael — the covenant narrowing is painful, not indifferent.
21.12: כִּי בְיִצְחָק יִקָּרֵא לְךָ זָרַע (kî ḇĕyiṣḥāq yiqqārēʾ lĕḵā zeraʿ) — "for through Isaac your offspring will be named/reckoned." The Niphal yiqqārēʾ (will be called, reckoned) — the covenant line passes through Isaac alone; Ishmael's exclusion is not rejection of Ishmael as a person but narrowing of the covenant inheritance. Paul cites this verse in Rom 9:7 and Heb 11:18 to make the theological point that physical descent alone does not define covenant membership — "not all who are descended from Israel are Israel" (Rom 9:6). The preposition bĕ (in/through Isaac) is partitive: only through this line.
21.13: וְגַם אֶת-בֶּן-הָאָמָה לְגוֹי אֲשִׂימֶנּוּ — "but I will also make the son of the slave woman into a nation." God's promise to Ishmael is restated: lĕgôy (into a nation) — a significant word: the same promise given to Abraham (12:2: "I will make you a great nation") is given in a modified form to Ishmael. כִּי זַרְעֲךָ הוּא (for he is your offspring) — God's care for Ishmael is rooted in Ishmael's relationship to Abraham, not to Sarah or to the covenant per se. The principle: God's covenant does not eliminate his care for those outside the primary covenant line.
21.14: וַיַּשְׁכֵּם אַבְרָהָם בַּבֹּקֶר — "Abraham rose early in the morning." The same word as 22:3 (the Akedah) and 20:8 (Abimelech's early response to God's warning) — the early rising marks urgency in response to divine direction. וַיִּתֵּן אֶל-הָגָר שָׂם עַל-שִׁכְמָהּ וְאֶת-הַיֶּלֶד — "he gave to Hagar, putting [them] on her shoulder, and the child" — the Hebrew syntax is difficult: food and water are given, placed on Hagar's shoulder, along with the child (how exactly the child was carried with these provisions is unclear). Wayyĕšallĕḥehā (he sent her away) — the same verb used for Adam's expulsion from Eden (3:23; שָׁלַח). Hagar departs for the "wilderness of Beersheba" — the dry steppe south of the settled zone.
21.15: וַיִּכְלוּ הַמַּיִם מִן-הַחֵמֶת — "the water in the skin was gone." Ḥēmet (skin bottle, water-skin) — water is life in the desert; the narrative precisely marks the crisis point: without water, the child will die. וַתַּשְׁלֵךְ אֶת-הַיֶּלֶד תַּחַת אַחַד הַשִּׂיחִם — "she cast the child under one of the bushes." Šālaḵ (to throw, cast) — the same verb used for casting the Egyptian children into the Nile (Exod 1:22) — Hagar is forced to do to her son what Pharaoh would later do to Hebrew children. The irony that the Egyptian woman abandons her child in the wilderness prefigures the Egyptian king abandoning Hebrew children in the river.
21.16: לֹא אֶרְאֶה בְּמוֹת הַיָּלֶד — "I cannot watch the boy die." Hagar moves away "a bowshot away" — the Hebrew bow-distance (kĕmĕṭaḥăwê qešet) is a precise practical measure: far enough not to see but close enough to hear. Wattiśśāʾ ʾet-qōlāh watēḇk (she lifted up her voice and wept) — the mourning formula; Hagar's weeping mirrors her weeping in ch. 16. The mother who received the divine promise of Ishmael's greatness (16:10-12) is now at the point of watching him die — the gap between promise and visible reality is absolute.
21.17: וַיִּשְׁמַע אֱלֹהִים אֶת-קוֹל הַנַּעַר (wayyišmaʿ ʾĔlōhîm ʾet-qôl hannāʿar) — "God heard the voice of the boy." The boy's name, Ishmael ("God hears"), is enacted: God hears (šāmaʿ) the one whose name means "God hears." The divine hearing is not Hagar's weeping but the boy's crying — the covenant of the name (16:11: "for the LORD has heard your affliction") is fulfilled in its next generation. מַלְאַךְ אֱלֹהִים (angel of God) — the second appearance of the divine messenger to Hagar (cf. 16:7); qārāʾ from heaven (called from heaven) — the heavenly voice rather than the visible manifestation of 16:7.
21.18: קוּמִי שְׂאִי אֶת-הַנַּעַר (qûmî śĕʾî ʾet-hannāʿar) — "rise, lift up the boy." The double imperative (qûmî, rise; śĕʾî, lift up) is urgent. כִּי-לְגוֹי גָּדוֹל אֲשִׂימֶנּוּ — "for I will make him into a great nation." The promise is the same as 17:20 (God's covenant with Ishmael: "I will make him a great nation") and echoes the Abrahamic promise of 12:2. The angel's words repeat the content of the name: hear it again, Hagar — what God said, God will do.
21.19: וַיִּפְקַח אֱלֹהִים אֶת-עֵינֶיהָ וַתֵּרֶא בְּאֵר מָיִם — "God opened her eyes and she saw a well of water." Pāqaḥ (to open, uncover — eyes) — the same verb as in 3:5, 7 where the serpent promises "your eyes will be opened" after eating the forbidden fruit, and in 3:7 where "the eyes of both of them were opened." The ironic reversal is clear: in Eden, eyes opened to shame and knowledge of evil; here, eyes are opened to water and life. The divine eye-opening is mercy, not judgment. The well was presumably there all along — Hagar could not see it until God opened her eyes.
21.20: וַיְהִי אֱלֹהִים אֶת-הַנַּעַר (wayhî ʾĔlōhîm ʾet-hannāʿar) — "God was with the boy." The summary formula YHWH/ʾĔlōhîm was with X is the highest divine-favor statement in the OT narrative (with Joseph, 39:2, 21, 23; with David, 1 Sam 16:18; 18:12, 14; with Jesus, Acts 10:38). Ishmael grows up with the divine presence — he is not abandoned. וַיְהִי רֹבֶה קַשָּׁת (he became an archer) — rōbeh qaššāt (bow-shooter, archer) — fulfilling the wild-donkey-man oracle of 16:12: independent, free, skilled in desert survival.
21.21: וַתִּקַּח-לוֹ אִמּוֹ אִשָּׁה מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם — "his mother took a wife for him from the land of Egypt." Hagar the Egyptian secures an Egyptian wife for Ishmael — he becomes the ancestor of peoples linked to Egypt, consistent with the Arabian/Ishmaelite peoples of the OT's geographic horizon. The mother arranges the marriage (the father's role is normally fulfilled here by the mother because Abraham has expelled them) — Hagar exercises the paternal prerogative of marriage arrangement for her son.
21.22: אֱלֹהִים עִמְּךָ בְּכֹל אֲשֶׁר-אַתָּה עֹשֶׂה — "God is with you in all that you do." Abimelech and Phicol recognize the divine presence with Abraham — the same recognition formula that Laban will use (31:5: "God of your father is with you") and that will be repeated for Isaac (26:28) and Joseph (39:2-3). Pîḵōl (Phicol) — the commander of Abimelech's forces; his inclusion in the treaty-request elevates it to a state treaty, not merely a personal agreement.
21.23: אִם-תִּשְׁקֹר לִי וּלְנִינִי וּלְנֶכְדִּי — "that you will not deal falsely with me, or with my son's son, or with my grandson." Šāqar (to deal falsely, betray — specifically covenantal betrayal, false-dealing within a sworn relationship; cf. Ps 44:17; 89:33). Nîn wāneḵeḏ (offspring and descendant) — a rare pair of terms for extended lineage, appearing also in Job 18:19 and Isa 14:22. Abimelech is asking for a multi-generational treaty — the covenant of kindness he showed Abraham should bind their descendants. כַּחֶסֶד אֲשֶׁר-עָשִׂיתִי עִמְּךָ (kaḥeseḏ ʾăšer-ʿāśîtî ʿimmĕḵā) — "as the kindness I have done with you" — ḥeseḏ (covenant-loyalty, steadfast love) is the word for Abimelech's conduct: he treated Abraham with ḥeseḏ even after being deceived.
21.24: אָנֹכִי אִשָּׁבֵעַ (ʾānōḵî ʾiššāḇēaʿ, I will swear) — Abraham accepts the treaty request with the emphatic first-person pronoun ʾānōḵî (I, myself) + the Niphal of šāḇaʿ (to swear, bind oneself by oath). The swearing establishes a formal covenant relationship between Abraham and the Philistine king — a non-Israelite political treaty rooted in recognized divine presence.
21.25: וְהוֹכִחַ אַבְרָהָם אֶת-אֲבִימֶלֶךְ (wĕhôkîaḥ ʾaḇrāhām ʾet-ʾăḇîmeleḵ) — "Abraham complained/argued with Abimelech." Yākaḥ (Hiphil, to reprove, argue, bring a legal complaint against) — a formal disputational term; Abraham raises a legal grievance. עַל-אֹדוֹת בְּאֵר הַמַּיִם (about a well of water) — water rights in the Negev were a matter of survival; disputes over wells are a recurring motif in the patriarchal narratives (26:15-22; Exod 2:17). Abraham complains that Abimelech's servants seized the well he dug.
21.26: Abimelech's denial: "I do not know who did this thing / you did not tell me / I heard about it only today" — three successive disclaimers creating a formal forensic defense. The three-part denial is a rhetorical unit that completely absolves Abimelech (by his own account) of responsibility for the well-seizure. The narrative does not adjudicate between the claims; it proceeds to the treaty that resolves the dispute.
21.27: וַיִּכְרְתוּ שְׁנֵיהֶם בְּרִית (wayyiḵrĕtû šĕnêhem bĕrît) — "the two of them cut a covenant." Kārat bĕrît (to cut a covenant) — the standard treaty formula (cf. 15:18 for the Abrahamic covenant, 31:44 for Jacob and Laban). The sheep and cattle Abraham gave (v. 27) are both the gift of friendship and possibly the sacrificial animals for the covenant ceremony (parties to a covenant sometimes offered sacrifices at the ratification).
21.28: וַיַּצֵּב אַבְרָהָם אֶת-שֶׁבַע כִּבְשֹׂת הַצֹּאן לְבַדְּהֶן — "Abraham set apart seven ewe lambs by themselves." Šeḇaʿ kĕḇāśôt (seven ewe lambs) — the word šeḇaʿ (seven) is the root of the Beersheba name: bĕʾēr šeḇaʿ = "well of seven" or "well of the oath" (šĕḇuʿāh = oath, from the same root šāḇaʿ). The seven lambs function as an oath-token — a material confirmation of the sworn covenant.
21.29: Abimelech's question about the seven lambs creates the legal dialogue through which Abraham explains the symbolic significance — a teaching moment embedded in the covenant ceremony. The question-and-answer pattern (Abimelech asks → Abraham explains) is the standard didactic structure for covenant ceremonies and legal transactions in the OT, where formal questioning ensures that all parties understand the legal significance of the symbolic act.
21.30: כִּי אֶת-שֶׁבַע כְּבָשֹׂת תִּקַּח מִיָּדִי בַּעֲבוּר תִּהְיֶה-לִּי לְעֵדָה אֲשֶׁר חָפַרְתִּי אֶת-הַבְּאֵר הַזֹּאת — "the seven lambs you shall accept from my hand so that it may be a witness for me that I dug this well." ʿEḏāh (witness, testimony) — the seven lambs serve as a legal witness object: the acceptance of the lambs by Abimelech constitutes acknowledgment that Abraham dug the well and owns the water rights. Legal documentation in the ANE used symbolic objects as substitute for written contracts.
21.31: עַל-כֵּן קָרָא לַמָּקוֹם הַהוּא בְּאֵר שָׁבַע (ʿal-kēn qārāʾ lammāqôm hahûʾ bĕʾēr šāḇaʿ) — "therefore that place was called Beersheba." The etymology is double: bĕʾēr šāḇaʿ = (1) Well of Seven (šeḇaʿ = seven — the seven lambs); or (2) Well of the Oath (šĕḇuʿāh = oath, from šāḇaʿ = to swear). Both meanings are activated simultaneously — the place name encodes the dual significance of the number seven and the covenant oath. Beersheba will become one of the key patriarchal sites (26:23-25, 33; 28:10; 46:1) and eventually the southern boundary marker of Israel ("from Dan to Beersheba").
21.32: אֶרֶץ פְּלִשְׁתִּים (ʾereṣ pĕlištîm, land of the Philistines) — the anachronistic reference to "the land of the Philistines" is noted by scholars: the Philistines as a settled people in Canaan's coastal plain are historically attested from the 12th century BCE (Sea Peoples), later than the patriarchal period. This may reflect: (1) an updating gloss by a later editor; (2) the use of an ancient ethnic name for peoples of that region before the canonical Philistine settlement; (3) a literary identification with the later Philistine territory.
21.33: וַיִּטַּע אֶשֶׁל בִּבְאֵר שָׁבַע וַיִּקְרָא-שָׁם בְּשֵׁם יְהוָה אֵל עוֹלָם — "Abraham planted a tamarisk tree at Beersheba and called there on the name of the LORD, the Eternal God." ʾĒšel (tamarisk) — a tree that provides shade in the harsh Negev; associated with sanctuary or memorial plantings (Saul also plants one in 1 Sam 31:13). ʾĒl ʿôlām (the Eternal God/God of eternity) — Abraham's divine name at Beersheba; ʿôlām is here used as a divine epithet ("the Eternal One") rather than modifying the covenant (cf. 17:7, 13, 19 where ʿôlām modifies the covenant itself). The attribute of God's eternal nature is new in the Genesis divine-name vocabulary.
21.34: וַיָּגָר אַבְרָהָם בְּאֶרֶץ פְּלִשְׁתִּים יָמִים רַבִּים — "Abraham sojourned in the land of the Philistines many days." Yāmîm rabbîm (many days) — an indefinite long period; the same phrase used for the sojourning of Jacob (37:34) and others. The extended Beersheba period forms the interval before the Akedah of ch. 22 — the narrative pause before the climactic test. Abraham the sojourner continues his pattern: possessing nothing permanently in the promised land, living as a guest among its inhabitants.

23.1: וַיִּהְיוּ חַיֵּי שָׂרָה מֵאָה שָׁנָה וְעֶשְׂרִים שָׁנָה וְשֶׁבַע שָׁנִים — "The years of Sarah's life were one hundred years and twenty years and seven years." The unusual enumeration (one hundred + twenty + seven, rather than the standard "127 years") may emphasize each unit separately or reflect an archaic form of numerical notation. Sarah is the only woman in the OT whose age at death is explicitly recorded — the covenant wife's lifespan is narratively as significant as the patriarch's. The phrase ḥayyê śārāh (the life/years of Sarah) gives the chapter its traditional Jewish title (Parashat Ḥayyê Śārāh).
23.2: וַתָּמָת שָׂרָה בְּקִרְיַת אַרְבַּע הִוא חֶבְרוֹן — "Sarah died in Kiriath-arba, that is Hebron." Qiryat ʾarbaʿ (city of four / city of Arba) — the ancient name for Hebron; Josh 14:15 identifies Arba as "the greatest man among the Anakim" (a giant). The parenthetical "(that is, Hebron)" uses the later, better-known name for the reader. Hebron is where Abraham had his altar (13:18) and his Amorite allies (14:13, 24). לִסְפֹּד לְשָׂרָה וְלִבְכֹּתָהּ (to mourn and to weep for Sarah) — two verbs: sāpad (to strike the breast, wail formally in lamentation — a ritualized mourning gesture) and bākāh (to weep — the emotional tears). Both forms of mourning are given to Sarah.
23.3: מֵעַל פְּנֵי מֵתוֹ (mēʿal pĕnê mētô, from before/beside his dead) — "from beside his dead wife." The Hebrew literally is "from the face of his dead one" — pāneh (face) in proximity language; Abraham had been sitting with the body, then rises to conduct the business of burial. The posture of rising from beside the dead body marks the transition from grief to practical action. וַיְדַבֵּר אֶל-בְּנֵי-חֵת (he spoke to the sons of Heth) — the Hittites (ḥittîm) are the resident population of Hebron; Abraham must negotiate with them for burial rights.
23.4: גֵּר-וְתוֹשָׁב אָנֹכִי עִמָּכֶם (gēr-wĕtôšāḇ ʾānōḵî ʿimmāḵem) — "I am a sojourner and resident-alien among you." The double status: gēr (sojourner, resident alien without citizen rights, legal protection through hospitality conventions) + tôšāḇ (temporary settler, one who dwells temporarily in a place that belongs to others). The combined phrase appears in Lev 25:23 where God tells Israel that "the land is mine; for you are strangers and sojourners (gērîm wĕtôšāḇîm) with me." The self-description is Abraham's own — he is the covenant heir of the land and yet has no legal ownership of any of it; he must buy burial space from its current occupants. 1 Pet 2:11 echoes this self-identification for Christians in the world.
23.5: The Hittites' response (vv. 5-6) follows a formal protocol: they address Abraham with honorific recognition, concede his request in principle, and offer the most generous possible terms — all family tombs are open to him. The negotiation unfolds in the formal pattern of ANE public transaction: open offer → counter-request (Abraham specifies what he actually wants) → second negotiation round (vv. 7-16). The repetition of bowing (wayyišaḥû, v. 7) and formal address marks each stage of the legal protocol.
23.6: נְשִׂיא אֱלֹהִים אַתָּה בְּתוֹכֵנוּ (nĕśîʾ ʾĔlōhîm ʾattāh bĕtôḵēnû) — "you are a prince of God among us." Nāśîʾ (chieftain, prince, elevated one) — the highest social title the Hittites can give; ʾĔlōhîm as a superlative intensifier (a "divine/god-like prince") or literally "God's prince" — acknowledging that divine blessing rests on Abraham. The same recognition occurs with Abimelech (21:22: "God is with you in all you do"). The pagan recognition of God's blessing on Abraham is a recurring narrative pattern. בְּמִבְחַר קְבָרֵינוּ קְבֹר אֶת-מֵתֶךָ — "bury your dead in the choicest of our tombs" — the offer is maximally generous; Abraham will nevertheless insist on buying a specific site.
23.7: וַיָּקָם אַבְרָהָם וַיִּשְׁתַּחוּ לְעַם-הָאָרֶץ — "Abraham rose and bowed down before the people of the land." Wayyiqqom wayyištaḥû — rising and bowing is the formal greeting gesture in ANE diplomatic and legal contexts; the bowing here acknowledges the Hittites' authority over their land and their willingness to negotiate. The politeness is part of the legal protocol — Abraham must be deferential to secure the sale on terms he desires.
23.8: אִם-יֶשׁ אֶת-נַפְשְׁכֶם לִקְבֹּר אֶת-מֵתִי מִלְּפָנַי — "if you are willing to bury my dead from before me." The Hebrew idiom im-yēš ʾet-napšĕḵem (if it is with your soul = if it is your will) treats the soul/will as the seat of desire and intention. Abraham frames his request as conditional on their genuine willingness — he is not demanding but soliciting consent. שִׁמְעוּנִי וּפִגְעוּ-לִי בְּעֶפְרוֹן — "hear me and intercede for me with Ephron" — pāgaʿ (to meet, encounter, fall upon; in the Hiphil: to cause to meet, to intercede, to approach on someone's behalf) — Abraham asks the assembly to approach Ephron as his intermediaries.
23.9: מְעָרַת הַמַּכְפֵּלָה אֲשֶׁר-לוֹ אֲשֶׁר בִּקְצֵה שָׂדֵהוּ — "the cave of Machpelah which belongs to him, which is at the end of his field." Maḵpēlāh — from kāpal (to double, fold over); possibly "the double cave" — a cave with two chambers. בְּכֶסֶף מָלֵא (bĕḵeseḵ mālēʾ, for the full price) — Abraham insists on paying the full market value; he will not receive the burial ground as a gift (which would leave a claim relationship on the property) or at a reduced price. Full payment = full legal title without ongoing obligation. The cave at Machpelah becomes the family tomb: Sarah (23:19), Abraham (25:9), Isaac and Rebekah, and Jacob and Leah (49:29-32; 50:13) will all be buried there.
23.10: וְעֶפְרוֹן יֹשֵׁב בְּתוֹךְ בְּנֵי-חֵת — "Ephron was sitting among the sons of Heth." Ephron was present in the assembly from the beginning — the narrative had been describing the community's corporate response; now the specific property owner is identified. The gate (šaʿar) is the commercial and judicial center of the ancient city: contracts, legal cases, marriages, and business were conducted "at the gate" (Ruth 4:1-12; Deut 21:19; Prov 31:23; Amos 5:15). Ephron responds publicly, in the hearing of all — the transaction is legally witnessed from the start.
23.11: לֹא-אֲדֹנִי שְׁמָעֵנִי הַשָּׂדֶה נָתַתִּי לָךְ — "No, my lord, hear me: I give you the field." Ephron's initial offer is the field plus the cave as a gift — three times he uses nātatî lĕḵā (I give to you): "I give you the field," "I give you the cave," "I give it to you in the presence of my people." The triple offer of a free gift is a formal ANE negotiating posture — generosity is offered publicly; the expected response is that the buyer will insist on paying. A gift-acceptance would create dependency and ongoing obligation; a purchase creates clean ownership.
23.12: וַיִּשְׁתַּחוּ אַבְרָהָם לִפְנֵי עַם הָאָרֶץ — "Abraham bowed before the people of the land." The second bow acknowledges Ephron's offer and the community's witnessing role. The repetition of bowing (vv. 7 and 12) frames Abraham's second speech; each bow marks a stage of negotiation. The public assembly functions as the legal court witnessing every step of the transaction.
23.13: אַךְ אִם-אַתָּה לוּ שְׁמָעֵנִי נָתַתִּי כֶּסֶף הַשָּׂדֶה קַח מִמֶּנִּי — "but please, if you will hear me — I will give the price of the field; accept it from me." Abraham insists on paying: keseḵ hassāḏeh (the silver/price of the field) — he will pay the commercial value. Qah mimmennî (accept from me) — the polite imperative requesting that Ephron receive payment. By insisting on payment, Abraham converts the gift-offer into a purchase, securing clean legal title without ongoing obligation to the Hittite community.
23.14: The transition verse — Ephron's reply names the price in v. 15. The formal structure of the negotiation (offer → counter → price-naming → payment) follows attested ANE patterns for real-estate transactions. The speech formula wayyaʿan ʾeprôn ʾet-ʾaḇrāhām (Ephron answered Abraham) marks the decisive turn in the negotiation: the price will now be stated publicly, before witnesses.
23.15: אַרְבַּע מֵאוֹת שֶׁקֶל-כֶּסֶף בֵּינִי וּבֵינְךָ מַה-הִוא — "four hundred shekels of silver — what is that between you and me?" The price named publicly is 400 shekels of silver — a high price for a field and cave. Comparable ANE property prices suggest 400 shekels is a premium price (Jeremiah purchases a field for 17 shekels, Jer 32:9; David purchases the threshing floor for 50 shekels, 2 Sam 24:24). Ephron's "what is that between you and me?" dismisses the price as negligible — a social fiction that simultaneously names the price and signals willingness to close the transaction.
23.16: וַיִּשְׁקֹל אַבְרָהָם לְעֶפְרֹן אֶת-הַכֶּסֶף (wayyišqōl ʾaḇrāhām lĕʿeprōn ʾet-hakkeseḵ) — "Abraham weighed out to Ephron the silver." Šāqal (to weigh) — the verb from which šeqel (shekel) is derived; the shekel was originally a weight standard, not a minted coin; silver was weighed on a balance scale in transactions. עֹבֵר לַסֹּחֵר (ʿōḇēr lassōḥēr, current among merchants) — silver at the weight-standard that passed/was accepted in commercial transactions; the commercial quality and purity specification ensures that both parties received equivalent value by recognized standards.
23.17: The deed-of-sale is recorded with legal precision. הַשָּׂדֶה וְהַמְּעָרָה אֲשֶׁר-בּוֹ וְכָל-הָעֵץ אֲשֶׁר בַּשָּׂדֶה — "the field and the cave in it and all the trees within the field" — three components: the field (land), the cave (specific structure), all trees within the borders (everything on the land). ANE property deeds commonly listed all components of the property, including trees, because trees represented independent value (for fruit, timber, shade). The precision mirrors actual ancient property deeds found at Nuzi and Ugarit.
23.18: לְאַבְרָהָם לְמִקְנָה לְעֵינֵי בְנֵי-חֵת — "to Abraham as a possession, in the eyes of the sons of Heth." Miqnāh (purchase, acquisition, something bought) — the key legal word: Abraham now owns the Machpelah by purchase, not by gift or divine covenant alone. בְּכֹל בָּאֵי שַׁעַר-עִירוֹ (before all who came in at the gate of his city) — the witnesses are "all who came in at the gate" — the entire civic assembly that regularly passes through the city gate is witness. Public transaction at the gate is the ANE equivalent of registered legal deed: the community is the witness.
23.19: וְאַחֲרֵי-כֵן קָבַר אַבְרָהָם אֶת-שָׂרָה אִשְׁתּוֹ — "afterward Abraham buried Sarah his wife." The legal transaction (vv. 3-18) precedes the burial — proper legal title secured before burial takes place. The burial location: bĕmĕʿārat śĕḏeh hamaḵpēlāh ʿal-pĕnê mamrēʾ hîʾ ḥeḇrôn bĕʾereṣ kĕnaʿan (in the cave of the field of Machpelah, before Mamre, which is Hebron, in the land of Canaan) — the full legal description of the burial site, exactly as in the deed (vv. 17-18). The repetition of the full description confirms that the burial fulfilled the terms of the purchase.
23.20: וַיָּקָם הַשָּׂדֶה וְהַמְּעָרָה אֲשֶׁר-בּוֹ לְאַבְרָהָם לַאֲחֻזַּת-קָבֶר מֵאֵת בְּנֵי-חֵת — "so the field and the cave in it were confirmed/transferred to Abraham as a burial possession from the sons of Heth." Wayyāqom (and it was confirmed/established, rose — Qal imperfect of qûm, to rise, stand, be confirmed; in legal contexts: to be established, confirmed as valid) — the deed is legally confirmed and standing. ʾAḥuzzat-qāḇer (burial possession/burial holding) — ʾăḥuzzāh (possession, holding, land title) is the legal term for territorial ownership used in the land-grant contexts (cf. Lev 25:10-13 for family land returns; Num 27:4; Josh 21:12). Abraham's one piece of legally owned land in all of Canaan is a burial cave — the covenant heir who was promised the whole land owns only a tomb.

24.1: וְאַבְרָהָם זָקֵן בָּא בַּיָּמִים (wĕʾaḇrāhām zāqēn bāʾ bayyāmîm) — "Abraham was old, coming in days." The formula zāqēn bāʾ bayyāmîm (old, entering in days) is the standard OT idiom for extreme old age (cf. 18:11 where the same phrase precedes the impossible-birth announcement for Sarah; Josh 13:1; 23:1 for Joshua; 1 Kgs 1:1 for David). The urgency it creates is narrative: Abraham must secure the covenant heir's marriage before he dies. וַיהוָה בֵּרַךְ אֶת-אַבְרָהָם בַּכֹּל (the LORD had blessed Abraham in everything) — bakkōl (in everything, in all things) — comprehensive blessing without qualification; the single word encapsulates the fulfillment of the twelve-chapter promise arc from 12:2-3.
24.2: שִׂים-נָא יָדְךָ תַּחַת יְרֵכִי (śîm-nāʾ yāḏĕḵā taḥat yĕrēḵî) — "place your hand under my thigh." The oath gesture of placing the hand under the thigh (yāreḵ) appears in the OT only here and 47:29 (Jacob's deathbed oath to Joseph). Yāreḵ (thigh, loins) is the seat of generative power and offspring (the "loins" from which children come: cf. 46:26; Exod 1:5). Swearing by the thigh of the patriarch is an oath sworn by the covenant seed — the very offspring at stake in the promise. The servant is being bound by the most solemn form of covenant oath available in the patriarchal household: one that invokes the covenant line itself as guarantor.
24.3: הִשְׁבַּעְתִּיךָ בַּיהוָה אֱלֹהֵי הַשָּׁמַיִם וֵאלֹהֵי הָאָרֶץ — "I make you swear by the LORD, God of heaven and God of earth." The double divine title — God of heaven and God of earth — emphasizes YHWH's universal sovereignty beyond the local land claim; it is an expansion of the usual "God of heaven" (17:3; Ezra 1:2). אֲשֶׁר לֹא-תִקַּח אִשָּׁה לִבְנִי מִבְּנוֹת הַכְּנַעֲנִי — the prohibition is absolute: lōʾ tiqqaḥ (you will not take) in the Qal imperfect with lōʾ = categorical prohibition. The Canaanite women are excluded categorically, not selectively — the covenant family must remain distinct from the people of the land.
24.4: כִּי אֶל-אַרְצִי וְאֶל-מוֹלַדְתִּי תֵּלֵךְ — "but to my land and to my birthplace you will go." ʾArṣî (my land) and môlaḏtî (my birthplace, kindred, origin) — exactly the terms Abraham left behind in 12:1 (ʾarṣĕḵā ûmimmôlaḏtĕḵā, your land and your birthplace), now reversed: the servant must go back to the origin-place while Abraham and Isaac remain in Canaan. The covenant heir cannot return to Mesopotamia (v. 6-8 make this explicit); only the proxy travels there. The covenant family remains in the promised land even while the covenant line is extended through a wife from the family of origin.
24.5: The servant's hypothetical question — "what if the woman refuses to come?" — is the practical obstacle to the mission and frames the conditional clauses that follow. ʾûlay (perhaps, what if) — the uncertainty introduced shows the servant's responsible planning; he is not asking for permission to fail but requesting contingency instructions. The question also sets up Abraham's response in vv. 6-8, which is not a practical workaround but a theological declaration: the mission's success depends on YHWH's angelic provision, not on the servant's human contingencies.
24.6: הִשָּׁמֶר לְךָ פֶּן-תָּשִׁיב אֶת-בְּנִי שָׁמָּה — "take care for yourself, lest you take my son back there." Hišāmer lĕḵā (take care for yourself, guard yourself) — the Niphal imperative of šāmar is a strong warning; the reflexive form intensifies personal responsibility. The prohibition against returning Isaac to Mesopotamia is absolute — Abraham foresees the danger: once in the home country, the pull of family and familiarity might overwhelm the covenant calling to remain in Canaan. Isaac staying in the promised land is not negotiable; the woman coming to Isaac is the only acceptable scenario.
24.7: יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵי הַשָּׁמַיִם אֲשֶׁר לְקָחַנִי מִבֵּית אָבִי — "the LORD, God of heaven, who took me from my father's house." Abraham's retrospective summary: YHWH is the one who lāqaḥ (took, fetched — the same verb as "take a wife" used throughout this chapter) Abraham from his origin-place. The verb connects: the God who took Abraham from Mesopotamia will take a wife from Mesopotamia for Isaac. הוּא יִשְׁלַח מַלְאָכוֹ לְפָנֶיךָ (he will send his angel before you) — the divine malʾāḵ going before the servant anticipates Exod 23:20 ("behold, I am sending an angel before you to guard you on the way"); the Exodus motif of divine angelic escort is here in its patriarchal precursor form. The promise is unconditional: YHWH will act.
24.8: וְנִקִּיתָ מִשְּׁבֻעָתִי (wĕniqqîtā miššĕḇûʿātî) — "you will be released/clean from my oath." Niqqa (Niphal of nāqāh, to be clean, free, acquitted) — the legal vocabulary of oath-release: if the woman refuses, the servant is legally acquitted from the sworn obligation; the oath's conditions were contingent on the woman's consent. רַק אֶת-בְּנִי לֹא-תָשֵׁב שָׁמָּה — only the returning of Isaac is non-negotiable even within this release clause. The oath's flexibility on the woman is matched by its absolute inflexibility on Isaac remaining in Canaan.
24.9: וַיָּשֶׂם הָעֶבֶד אֶת-יָדוֹ תַּחַת יֶרֶךְ אַבְרָהָם — the servant performs the oath gesture described in v. 2. The repetition is the legal confirmation: what was commanded (v. 2) is now executed (v. 9). The narrative structure of command → execution without intervening deliberation marks the servant's total obedience — he asked one practical question and received his answer; he then performs the oath without further objection. The oath (wayyiššāḇaʿ lô, he swore to him) is legally complete.
24.10: עֲשָׂרָה גְמַלִּים (ʿăśārāh ḡĕmallîm, ten camels) — ten camels loaded with gifts is an enormous display of wealth; camels were among the most valuable pack animals of the ANE. Wayyāqom wayyēleḵ ʾel-ʾărammnaharāyim — ʾĂram naharāyim (Aram of the two rivers) = the region of northwest Mesopotamia around the upper Euphrates and Habur rivers, modern southeastern Turkey and northern Syria; this is the Paddan-Aram of the later Jacob narratives (28:2). The servant travels from Canaan to this region — a journey of weeks by camel caravan. ʾEl-ʿîr nāḥôr (to the city of Nahor) — the city named after Abraham's brother (22:20-24), not necessarily named Nahor but associated with his household.
24.11: לְעֵת עֶרֶב לְעֵת צֵאת הַשֹּׁאֲבֹת — "at the time of evening, the time when women come out to draw water." The social realism of the well scene: women draw water in the evening when the heat has subsided. The servant positions himself at the well precisely at the time when young women from the town will appear — maximum opportunity for the sign-prayer to be answered. Wayyaḇrēk hagemalîm (he made the camels kneel) — bāraḵ (Hiphil: to cause to kneel; the same root as bĕrāḵāh, blessing — a homonym often noted in rabbinic commentary; the camels kneel as if in blessing-posture before the answer arrives).
24.12: וַיֹּאמַר יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵי אֲדֹנִי אַבְרָהָם הַקְרֵה-נָא לְפָנַי הַיּוֹם — the servant's prayer begins with the divine address "LORD, God of my master Abraham" — he prays in the name of his master's God, not his own (the narrative does not tell us the servant's personal faith status). Haqreh-nāʾ lĕpānay hayyôm — haqreh is the Hiphil imperative of qārāh (to happen, meet, encounter by chance); in the Hiphil: to cause to meet, to arrange a providential encounter. The prayer asks for a divinely managed "chance" meeting — the theology of providence embedded in the verb: what appears random is divinely arranged. וַעֲשֵׂה-חֶסֶד עִם אֲדֹנִי אַבְרָהָם (and show ḥeseḏ with my master Abraham) — ḥeseḏ is prayed for Abraham's sake; the servant subordinates his own need to his master's covenant.
24.13: הִנֵּה אָנֹכִי נִצָּב עַל-עֵין הַמָּיִם — "behold, I am standing by the spring of water." Niṣṣāḇ (Niphal participle of nāṣaḇ, stationed, positioned, standing firmly) — not merely standing but positioned, planted; the servant has placed himself deliberately at the well. The sign-prayer of vv. 13-14 is one of the most specific petitionary signs in the OT: the servant proposes an exact verbal exchange and a specific action (watering camels) as the divine sign, leaving no room for ambiguity. The specificity is part of the prayer's genre: it asks God to confirm a particular woman through a particular observable action.
24.14: הוֹכַחְתָּ לְעַבְדְּךָ לְיִצְחָק (hôḵaḥtā lĕʿaḇdĕḵā lĕyiṣḥāq) — "you have designated/decided upon [her] for your servant Isaac." Hôḵaḥtā is the Hiphil perfect of yāḵaḥ (to argue, prove, adjudicate, decide). In the Hiphil the verb can carry the legal force of "to reach a verdict, to determine definitively." The MKT translates "chosen," but the Hiphil nuance goes beyond preference to formal designation: the servant is asking God to reveal his verdict through this observable sign. The prayer essentially invites God to act as a divine arbiter of the marriage choice. וּבָהּ אֵדַע כִּי-עָשִׂיתָ חֶסֶד עִם-אֲדֹנִי (by her I will know that you have done ḥeseḏ with my master) — the sign is the proof of divine action, not a test of the woman.
24.15: וַיְהִי-הוּא טֶרֶם כִּלָּה לְדַבֵּר (wayhî-hûʾ ṭerem killāh lĕḏabbēr) — "before he had finished speaking." Ṭerem (before, not yet) + Piel of kālāh (to finish, complete) = the divine response comes before the prayer is completed. The immediacy is striking: the narrative does not allow even a pause between the petition and the answer; Rebekah's appearance interrupts the prayer mid-utterance. This is the most vivid "before you call, I will answer" moment in the patriarchal narratives (cf. Isa 65:24). בַּת-בְּתוּאֵל בֶּן-מִלְכָּה אֵשֶׁת נָחוֹר אֲחִי אַבְרָהָם — the full genealogical identification is inserted by the narrator before Rebekah speaks or the servant knows who she is: the reader sees the answer before the servant does.
24.16: וְהַנַּעֲרָ טֹבַת מַרְאֶה מְאֹד בְּתוּלָה וְאִישׁ לֹא יְדָעָהּ — "the young woman was very beautiful in appearance, a virgin, and no man had known her." The double specification of virginal status: (1) bĕtûlāh (virgin — the technical term for an unmarried woman who has not had sexual relations; cf. Deut 22:13-21; Lev 21:3; Joel 1:8); and (2) ʾîš lōʾ yĕḏāʿāh (no man had known her — the Qal of yāḏaʿ as sexual knowledge, Gen 4:1; 19:8). The redundancy is not careless: it creates legal certainty of her status for the marriage negotiation to follow. Wayyēreḏ hāʿayĕnāh (she went down to the spring) — Rebekah actively goes down to the spring and draws water; her initiative and industriousness appear before any words are spoken.
24.17: וַיָּרָץ הָעֶבֶד לִקְרָאתָהּ (wayyārāṣ hāʿeḇeḏ liqrāʾtāh) — "the servant ran to meet her." Rûṣ (to run) — the servant's urgency is expressed through running; in the Bible, running typically expresses excitement, hospitality, or urgent response (cf. Abraham's running to greet the divine visitors in 18:2; Laban's running in v. 29). הַגְמִיאִינִי נָא (haḡmîʾînî nāʾ) — "please let me sip/drink." Gāmaʾ (to swallow, gulp, sip) — an unusual verb for drinking (more common is šātāh); the Hiphil imperative haḡmîʾînî means "cause me to sip." The specific word choice may emphasize the modesty of the request — just a sip, not a full draw — making Rebekah's generous response (full drink + all the camels) all the more striking.
24.18: שְׁתֵה אֲדֹנִי (šĕtēh ʾaḏōnî, drink, my lord) — Rebekah's immediate response uses the respectful address ʾaḏōnî (my lord) — appropriate courteous address to a stranger of evident status. וַתְּמַהֵר (wattĕmahēr) — "she hurried." Māhar (to hurry, hasten) — this word will appear multiple times in the chapter (vv. 18, 20, 28, 46) as a verbal motif of Rebekah's energetic character: she is consistently the one who hastens. The hurrying marks the answer to the sign-prayer as immediate and enthusiastic, not merely compliant.
24.19: גַּם לִגְמַלֶּיךָ אֶשְׁאָב עַד אִם-כִּלּוּ לִשְׁתֹּת — "I will also draw for your camels until they have finished drinking." Ad ʾim-killû lištōt (until they have finished drinking) — the scope of the offer is enormous: ten camels can drink 25+ gallons each after a long journey; Rebekah is committing to draw hundreds of gallons of water from a spring. The sign-prayer asked that the woman say exactly these words (v. 14) — the match is precise. What the servant asked as a hypothetical test is now happening in reality; the prayer is being answered word by word.
24.20: וַתְּמַהֵר וַתְּעַר כַּדָּהּ אֶל-הַשֹּׁקֶת — "she quickly emptied her jar into the trough." Wattĕmahēr again (v. 20) — the speed motif continues; she empties, runs (wattārāṣ), draws (wattišʾaḇ) for all the camels. Three rapid verbs in sequence — the narrative's pacing mirrors Rebekah's own energy. Wayyišʾaḇ lĕḵol-gĕmallāyw (she drew enough for all his camels) — the enormity of the work is underscored: not just one camel, not a token draw, but enough for all ten camels to drink their fill after a desert crossing.
24.21: וְהָאִישׁ מִשְׁתָּאֵה לָהּ מַחֲרִישׁ (wĕhāʾîš mištāʾēh lāh maḥărîš) — "the man gazed at her, keeping silent." Mištāʾēh — the Hithpael participle of a verb usually read as "to gaze intently, to be dumbfounded" (from the same root as šāʾāh, to look; or possibly related to Aramaic šĕtā, to look); the Hithpael reflexive implies sustained or absorbed gazing. Maḥărîš (keeping silent, holding back speech) — the servant does not speak, does not react, does not interrupt; he watches in disciplined silence. לָדַעַת הַהִצְלִיחַ יְהוָה דַּרְכּוֹ (to know whether YHWH had prospered his way/journey) — derekh (road, way, journey) — the theological key word of the chapter; the servant's external journey is simultaneously an inner inquiry: has YHWH made this way succeed?
24.22: נֶזֶם זָהָב בֶּקַע מִשְׁקָלוֹ (nezem zāhāḇ beqaʿ mišqālô) — "a gold nose ring, a beka in weight." Beqaʿ (half-shekel weight) — confirmed in Exod 38:26 as the weight of the census ransom-half-shekel; its appearance here as a known commercial unit confirms the precision of the transaction. The two bracelets weigh ten shekels of gold — specific weights that match Ugaritic and Nuzi jewelry records for betrothal gifts. The giving of jewelry before the woman's family connection is known (the servant does not yet know who she is, v. 23) has troubled commentators; some argue the narrator is telescoping; others that the servant acts in confident faith before receiving the verbal confirmation.
24.23: בַּת-מִי אַתְּ הַגִּידִי נָא לִי (bat-mî ʾatt haggîḏî nāʾ lî) — "whose daughter are you? Please tell me." The servant's first words to Rebekah — after giving the jewelry — are a genealogical inquiry. The social protocol of establishing family connection before negotiating hospitality or marriage is standard ANE practice. הֲיֵשׁ בֵּית-אָבִיךְ מָקוֹם לָנוּ לָלִין (is there room in your father's house for us to spend the night?) — the hospitality inquiry is inseparable from the marriage inquiry; lodging is the physical expression of family openness to the stranger.
24.24: בַּת-בְּתוּאֵל אָנֹכִי בֶּן-מִלְכָּה אֲשֶׁר יָלְדָה לְנָחוֹר — Rebekah's self-identification: daughter of Bethuel, son of Milcah, who bore him to Nahor. The genealogical chain is complete: she is Abraham's grand-niece through Nahor (Abraham's brother) and Milcah (Nahor's wife). The servant's mission is accomplished in this one sentence — the woman at the well is of the right family. The genealogy of 22:20-24 introduced Rebekah precisely for this moment of recognition.
24.25: גַּם-תֶּבֶן גַּם-מִסְפּוֹא רַב עִמָּנוּ גַּם-מָקוֹם לָלוּן — "we have plenty of straw and fodder, and also room to spend the night." The triple gam (also, moreover) is emphatic accumulation: straw, fodder, lodging — all needs covered without hesitation. Rebekah answers the hospitality inquiry with abundance, not minimal sufficiency. The characterization of Rebekah as generous and quick-to-give is consistent throughout her first appearance: she offers water (v. 18-20), she answers immediately (v. 24-25), she will decide immediately on the marriage proposal (v. 58).
24.26: וַיִּקֹּד הָאִישׁ וַיִּשְׁתַּחוּ לַיהוָה — "the man bowed down and prostrated himself before the LORD." The servant's response to Rebekah's genealogical identification is immediate worship — he bows to YHWH before he speaks to Rebekah's household, before he eats, before he negotiates. The reflex of worship is the chapter's theological center: the mission's success is attributed to YHWH, not to the servant's cleverness or Rebekah's beauty. The prostration (wayyišaḥû, Hithpael of šāḥāh) is the full body-to-ground bow of covenant worship.
24.27: בָּרוּךְ יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵי אֲדֹנִי אַבְרָהָם — "Blessed be the LORD, God of my master Abraham." The blessing formula (bārûḵ YHWH) is a formal doxology: it does not confer blessing on God but acknowledges and acclaims the blessing that belongs to him. אֲשֶׁר לֹא-עָזַב חַסְדּוֹ וַאֲמִתּוֹ (who has not abandoned his ḥeseḏ and ʾemet) — the divine pair ḥeseḏ wĕʾemet (covenant-loyalty and faithfulness) appears here and in v. 49 as the theological summary of what YHWH has demonstrated through the providential encounter. This pair will become one of the central divine-character descriptions in the OT (Exod 34:6; Pss 25:10; 61:7; 89:14; Mic 7:20; and in the NT: John 1:14 "full of grace and truth"). נָחַנִי יְהוָה בְּדֶרֶךְ — "YHWH has led me in the way/road." Nāḥāh (to guide, lead — specifically divine guidance on a path; cf. Ps 23:3 "he leads me in paths of righteousness"; Ps 139:24; Isa 48:17). The word derekh (way, road) resonates with its literal meaning (the journey) and theological meaning (YHWH's ordered path of providence).
24.28: וַתָּרָץ הַנַּעֲרָ (wattārāṣ hannāʿărāh) — "the young woman ran." Running again (rûṣ) — Rebekah's fourth expression of energy and initiative; she runs home to tell her mother's household. Bêt ʾimmāh (her mother's household) — the mother's household rather than the father's is the domestic center where such news is first brought; this reflects the household structure of ANE women's social world (cf. Ruth 1:8 where Naomi sends her daughters-in-law back to their mother's house). Bethuel is barely mentioned in the narrative (v. 50) — Laban and the mother's household are the effective agents of the family response.
24.29: וּלְרִבְקָה אָח וּשְׁמוֹ לָבָן (ûlĕriḇqāh ʾāḥ ûšĕmô lāḇān) — "Rebekah had a brother and his name was Laban." Lāḇān (white) — the first appearance of a character who will become the dominant figure in the Jacob narrative (chs. 29-31). His motivation in v. 30 is immediately revealed: כִּרְאֹתוֹ אֶת-הַנֶּזֶם (when he saw the nose ring) — Laban's action is triggered by seeing the jewelry. The narrative is economical but pointed: he runs out because he saw the jewelry and heard Rebekah's account. The characterization of Laban as materially motivated is established on his first appearance, consistent with his later deceptions with Jacob over wages and inheritance (29:25; 31:7).
24.30: כִּרְאֹתוֹ אֶת-הַנֶּזֶם וְאֶת-הַצְּמִדִים עַל-יְדֵי אֲחֹתוֹ — the causal clause confirms: Laban's run to the man at the well is motivated by the sight of the nose ring and the bracelets on Rebekah's arms. The narrator does not editorialize further — the observation alone is sufficient characterization. Wayyiqqaḥ ʾet-hāʾîš (he took the man) — the hospitality formula of "taking" the guest home; Laban extends hospitality but the motivation is already disclosed.
24.31: בֹּא בְּרוּךְ יְהוָה (bōʾ bĕrûḵ YHWH) — "come in, you who are blessed by YHWH." Laban's greeting acknowledges the servant's blessing by YHWH — either a genuine religious acknowledgment or a social formula appropriate to someone whose master is known to be YHWH-blessed. The irony: Laban acknowledges YHWH's blessing on Abraham's servant while his own conduct in the next verse (v. 30: attracted by the jewelry) reveals a different orientation. Lammāh taʿămod baḥûṣ (why do you stand outside?) — the hospitable urgency of bringing the guest inside; the house and camel space are already prepared.
24.32: The hospitality hospitality protocol: camels unloaded, straw and fodder provided for the camels, water for the men's foot-washing. Wayyitten mayyim lirhōṣ ragĕlāyw (water was given to wash his feet) — foot-washing on arrival is a standard hospitality marker throughout the patriarchal narratives (18:4; 19:2); it signals the transition from journey to rest. The logical order (camels first, then men) is noted by rabbinic tradition as evidence of the servant's character: he attends to his master's animals before himself.
24.33: לֹא אֹכַל עַד אִם-דִּבַּרְתִּי דְּבָרָי — "I will not eat until I have spoken my words." The servant refuses food before completing his mission report. The refusal of hospitality before business is transacted is the reverse of typical ANE protocol (eat first, negotiate after) — it signals the urgency of the servant's commission. ʿAdattî (my words, my business, what I have to say) — dāḇār (word, thing, matter) has both meanings; here "my words" = "my business/purpose." The servant's single-mindedness is a character feature: he ran to her (v. 17), worshipped before eating (v. 26), and now refuses food before reporting.
24.34: עֶבֶד אַבְרָהָם אָנֹכִי (ʿeḇeḏ ʾaḇrāhām ʾānōḵî) — "I am a servant of Abraham." The servant introduces himself not by his own name but by his master's name — the only self-identification in the chapter. He has no independent identity in this narrative; his purpose is entirely defined by Abraham's commission. The emphatic ʾānōḵî (I, myself) with the predicate "servant of Abraham" is the complete self-presentation: this is who I am, this is what defines me. The anonymity of the servant throughout ch. 24 is a literary choice: he is the instrument of providence, not its subject.
24.35: וַיהוָה בֵּרַךְ אֶת-אֲדֹנִי מְאֹד — the servant's report to Laban and the household (vv. 34-49) recapitulates vv. 2-27 in virtually exact reported speech. This extended repetition is the chapter's most distinctive literary feature — in most Hebrew narrative, events are summarized when reported; here the servant repeats the conversation nearly verbatim. The effect is theological: the servant's account is not a mere social report but a second witness to divine providence. The exact repetition proves the story's truthfulness: two tellings, one story, one God behind it. The enumeration of Abraham's wealth (sheep, cattle, silver, gold, male and female servants, camels, donkeys) is ANE social positioning: the family will know that the marriage negotiation has real substance.
24.36: וַתֵּלֶד שָׂרָה אֵשֶׁת אֲדֹנִי בֵן לַאדֹנִי אַחֲרֵי זִקְנָתָהּ — "Sarah, my master's wife, bore a son to my master in her old age." The servant's account of Isaac's birth emphasizes the miraculous element: ʾaḥărê ziqnātāh (after her old age/after she had grown old). The word ziqnāh (old age) is feminine of zāqēn (v. 1) — the same root. Sarah's giving birth after this stage is presented as the ground for the covenant claims being made: the son of an impossible birth has an inheritance that must be protected by the right marriage. וַיִּתֶּן-לוֹ אֶת-כָּל-אֲשֶׁר-לוֹ — "he has given him everything he owns" — Isaac is sole heir; the inheritance at stake for the wife-choice is everything Abraham possesses.
24.37: The servant's restatement of Abraham's prohibition (vv. 37-38 = vv. 3-4) and the conditional oath-release (v. 41 = v. 8) in reported speech demonstrates the precision of the commission: he repeats it accurately, including the conditional clauses. The exact verbal repetition in the servant's account is a mark of responsible agency — he is not paraphrasing or adapting his master's instructions but faithfully reporting them. The reliability of the servant as a messenger is part of the narrative's character-building.
24.38: אֶל-בֵּית אָבִי תֵּלֵךְ (to my father's house you will go) — the servant quotes Abraham's direction using the second person imperative in reported speech, a feature of direct quotation embedded in narrative. The precision of the quotation continues: wĕʾel-mišpaḥtî (and to my clan/family) — mišpāḥāh (clan, extended family unit, kinship group) — the wider circle beyond the immediate household; the woman must come from both the paternal house and the clan/tribe of Abraham's origin.
24.39: The servant's account of his own hypothetical question (v. 39 = v. 5) and Abraham's answer (vv. 40-41 = vv. 7-8) continues the close repetition. The narrative device of repeating the servant's question functions rhetorically in the retelling: it establishes that the servant asked the right practical question, received a theological answer, and then saw the theological answer materialize. The retelling is an argument for divine providence built from the servant's own witness testimony.
24.40: יְהוָה אֲשֶׁר-הִתְהַלַּכְתִּי לְפָנָיו (YHWH ʾăšer-hithallaḵtî lĕpānāyw) — "the LORD before whom I have walked." Hithallaḵ (Hithpael of hālaḵ, to walk) in the reciprocal-reflexive: "to walk about before/with" — the pattern of Enoch ("Enoch walked with God," 5:22, 24) and Noah (6:9) and the command to Abraham himself (17:1: "walk before me and be blameless"). In the servant's retelling, Abraham describes his own covenant walk as the basis for confidence in divine angelic guidance: the God before whom Abraham has walked will guide the servant's steps.
24.41: תִּנָּקֶה מֵאָלָתִי (tinnāqeh mēʾālātî) — "you will be free/clean from my curse/oath." ʾĀlāh (oath-curse, binding sworn commitment) — the same oath that was šĕḇûʿāh (sworn commitment) in v. 8 is here called ʾālāh (oath with a self-curse element): oath-oaths in the ANE often involved an imprecation — "may X happen to me if I fail." The release formula tinnāqeh (Niphal: you will be acquitted, made clean) uses the legal vocabulary of innocence.
24.42: The servant's account of his arrival at the spring and his prayer (vv. 42-44 = vv. 12-14) continues the close repetition. One word difference is notable: in the original prayer (v. 12) the servant asks haqreh-nāʾ lĕpānay (cause to meet before me); in the retelling (v. 42) he says ʾim-yešĕḵā-nāʾ maṣlîaḥ darkî (if you are now making my way succeed). The retelling reformulates the prayer in retrospective terms — he is now reporting to the family what he prayed, not repeating the prayer itself; the slight variation is natural in oral retelling and not a textual inconsistency.
24.43: הִנֵּה אָנֹכִי נִצָּב עַל-עֵין הַמָּיִם — the servant quotes his own sign-prayer to the household (v. 43 = v. 13). The repetition of the prayer's exact terms in the retelling accomplishes a rhetorical goal: when the servant then describes Rebekah's appearance and actions (vv. 45-47), the match between what he prayed for and what happened is visible and precise to the household. They are being invited to draw the same conclusion he drew — this is from the LORD (v. 50).
24.44: The sign's verbal specification (v. 44 = v. 14): the woman must offer to draw water for the camels. The household, hearing both the prayer and its fulfillment, is presented with an irrefutable argument: the exact words, the exact action, the exact family lineage — "this is from the LORD" (v. 50) is the only conclusion the evidence supports. The servant's retelling is a piece of theological argument dressed in narrative form.
24.45: אֲנִי טֶרֶם אֲכַלֶּה לְדַבֵּר אֶל-לִבִּי (ʾănî ṭerem ʾăkalleh lĕḏabbēr ʾel-libbî) — "before I finished speaking to my heart." The phrase lĕḏabbēr ʾel-libbî (speaking to my heart) — the servant clarifies that the prayer of vv. 12-14 was an interior prayer, not voiced aloud to Rebekah or anyone else. The prayer was between the servant and YHWH alone; Rebekah's response was unprompted by any human request she could have heard. This detail confirms the sign's divine character: Rebekah cannot have overheard the prayer she was answering.
24.46: The servant's account of Rebekah's actions (v. 46 = vv. 18-20): she lowered her jar, gave him a drink, watered the camels. The match is exact. The narrative economy of the retelling shows what the servant noticed and retained: the specific verbal exchange, the specific actions. He has been watching closely (v. 21: he watched in silence to know if YHWH had prospered his way) and can now report precisely. The family receives a testimony that is both exact and first-hand.
24.47: וָאֶשְׁאַל אֹתָהּ וָאֹמַר בַּת-מִי אַתְּ — "I asked her, 'Whose daughter are you?'" The servant's account of the genealogical inquiry (v. 47 = v. 23). One detail differs from the earlier narrative: in vv. 22-23, the jewelry is given before the genealogical question; in v. 47, the servant says he asked the genealogical question first and then gave the jewelry after she answered. This apparent discrepancy is either: (1) a narrative telescoping where the order in the first telling is approximate; (2) a deliberate variation showing that the servant tidied the sequence in his retelling for social reasons (to avoid admitting he gave jewelry to an unknown woman); (3) a natural feature of oral narration where slight reorderings occur in retelling. The discrepancy is noted in both Jewish and Christian commentary; it does not affect the theological argument.
24.48: וָאֶקֹּד וָאֶשְׁתַּחֲוֶה לַיהוָה (I bowed and prostrated myself before YHWH) — the worship moment (v. 26-27) is reported in the retelling: the servant's immediate response to the genealogical confirmation was worship. ʾĂšer hiḏrîḵanî bĕdereḵ ʾemet (who led me in the way of truth) — bĕdereḵ ʾemet (in the way of truth/faithfulness) — ʾemet (truth, faithfulness, reliability) modifying the journey; the same word that appears in the divine-character pair ḥeseḏ wĕʾemet. The servant has walked in the reliable way that YHWH prepared for him — the word ʾemet connects the servant's journey to the divine character it manifested.
24.49: וְעַתָּה אִם-יֶשְׁכֶם עֹשִׂים חֶסֶד וֶאֱמֶת (wĕʿattāh ʾim-yeškem ʿōśîm ḥeseḏ wĕʾemet) — "and now, if you are doing ḥeseḏ and ʾemet." The servant now asks the family to do for Abraham what YHWH has already demonstrated: ḥeseḏ wĕʾemet (covenant-loyalty and faithfulness). The theological pair is applied first to God (v. 27: YHWH has not abandoned his ḥeseḏ wĕʾemet) and now to the human family (v. 49: will you do ḥeseḏ wĕʾemet?). The family is invited to imitate the divine character in their response. Waʾim-lāʾ haggîḏû lî wĕʾepĕneh ʿal-yāmîn ô ʿal-śĕmōʾl (if not, tell me so I may turn to the right or the left) — the servant requests a clear answer; ambiguity is not acceptable.
24.50: מֵיְהוָה יָצָא הַדָּבָר (mêYHWH yāṣāʾ haddāḇār) — "from YHWH this thing has gone forth." Laban and Bethuel's response is the theological climax of the servant's account: they recognize the divine hand in the sequence of events and cannot oppose it. Lōʾ nûḵal dabbēr ʾēleḵā rāʿ ô-ṭôḇ (we cannot say to you bad or good) — the idiom of "bad or good" (rāʿ ô-ṭôḇ) means something like "anything at all, either way" — they have no counterargument. The confession is remarkable: pagan Arameans from Nahor's family acknowledge YHWH's action and submit to it. The narrative presents them as recognizing divine providence when they see it.
24.51: הִנֵּה-רִבְקָה לְפָנֶיךָ קַח וָלֵךְ (hinnēh-riḇqāh lĕpānêḵā qaḥ wālēḵ) — "behold, Rebekah is before you, take her and go." Three imperatives in rapid sequence — qaḥ (take), wālēḵ (go), and implicitly hinnēh (behold, here she is). The family's consent is given immediately and completely, without conditions or further negotiation. Wattĕhî ʾiššāh lĕḇen-ʾăḏōnêḵā kaʾăšer dibber YHWH (let her become the wife of your master's son, as YHWH has spoken) — the final clause anchors the family's decision in divine speech: they are not giving Rebekah but fulfilling YHWH's word.
24.52: וַיִּשְׁתַּחוּ לָאָרֶץ לַיהוָה — "he prostrated himself to the ground before YHWH." The servant's worship response on hearing their consent mirrors his worship response on hearing Rebekah's genealogy (v. 26). The pattern is consistent throughout the chapter: news of divine action → immediate worship. The servant's theology is straightforward and practical: when YHWH acts, you bow.
24.53: כְּלֵי-כֶסֶף וּכְלֵי זָהָב וּבְגָדִים (silver articles and gold articles and garments) — the betrothal gifts flow from the servant's treasury to Rebekah, her brother, and her mother. The distribution pattern is socially precise: the bride receives jewelry; the brother and mother receive gifts as the household gatekeepers who consented to the marriage. ûlĕʾāḥîhā ûlĕʾimmāh (to her brother and to her mother) — notably not to Bethuel the father; the mother and Laban are the effective household agents throughout this chapter. The gift-giving formalizes the betrothal.
24.54: וַיֹּאמֶר שַׁלְּחֻנִי לַאדֹנִי (wayyōʾmer šallĕḥûnî laʾḏōnî) — "send me on my way to my master." Šāllaḥ (to send, release, dismiss) — the same verb repeatedly used for the sending of the servant on his mission. Now the servant asks to be released back to his master — mission complete, account given, gifts distributed, consent secured. The next morning arrives and his first words are return-focused. The servant's entire being is oriented toward Abraham and the commission; even after the celebration meal he thinks only of returning.
24.55: יֵשֵׁב הַנַּעֲרָ אִתָּנוּ יָמִים אוֹ עָשׂוֹר (yēšēḇ hannāʿărāh ʾittānû yāmîm ô ʿāśôr) — "let the young woman remain with us ten days or so." Yāmîm ô ʿāśôr (days or ten) — the idiom probably means "about ten days" or "a period of days or approximately ten." The family asks for a customary transition period before releasing Rebekah into marriage and departure — the request is understandable and socially appropriate. The servant's refusal in v. 56 is not social rudeness but commission-urgency: YHWH has granted success; delay introduces risk. The underlying theology: once God has acted, human delay is a form of hesitancy toward the divine will.
24.56: אַל-תְּאַחֲרוּ אֹתִי (ʾal-tĕʾahărû ʾōtî) — "do not delay me." ʾAḥar (Piel: to delay, hold back, cause to tarry) — the servant's refusal is stated as a negative command: stop delaying me. וַיהוָה הִצְלִיחַ דַּרְכִּי (and YHWH has granted success to my way/journey) — the servant's entire theological framework is explicit: YHWH has acted, the mission is complete, lingering serves no purpose. The word derekh appears again — the servant's journey-as-theological-event is concluded. The urgency is not impatience but faith: the God who has acted should not be kept waiting by human social convention.
