24.57: נִקְרָא לַנַּעֲרָ וְנִשְׁאֲלָה אֶת-פִּיהָ (niqrāʾ lannāʿărāh wĕnišʾălāh ʾet-pîhā) — "let us call the young woman and ask her mouth/ask her directly." ʾEt-pîhā (her mouth, literally) — the idiom "ask her mouth" = consult her directly, ask her in person. The family's suggestion to ask Rebekah directly is notable: the woman's consent is sought. In ANE marriage practice, the woman's family arranged marriages; direct consultation of the bride was not always required. Here the family recognizes that Rebekah herself must decide — an unusual degree of female agency in the narrative, but consistent with her active, decisive characterization throughout the chapter.
24.58: הֲתֵלְכִי עִם-הָאִישׁ הַזֶּה (hătēlĕḵî ʿim-hāʾîš hazzeh) — "will you go with this man?" The question is direct: a yes/no choice put to Rebekah. וַתֹּאמֶר אֵלֵךְ (wattōʾmer ʾēlēḵ) — "she said: I will go." ʾĒlēḵ — the Qal imperfect first-person singular: "I will go, I myself will go." One word — decisive, immediate, final. The character of Rebekah crystallized in a single verb: the woman who runs, who waters the camels, who answers immediately, now says "I will go" to the most consequential question of her life. The narrative does not describe hesitation, consultation, or qualification — just ʾēlēḵ.
24.59: וַיְשַׁלְּחוּ אֶת-רִבְקָה אֲחֹתָם — "they sent away Rebekah their sister." Šāllaḥ again — the verb of sending that has moved through the whole chapter: Abraham sent (šāllaḥ) the servant (v. 7); the servant asked to be sent back (v. 54, 56); now the family sends (wayyĕšallĕḥû) Rebekah. The sending vocabulary threads the narrative together: a commission is given, a mission is executed, a woman is sent — all with the same verb. אֶת-מֵינֶקֶת (her nurse) — Rebekah travels with her nurse; in 35:8 this nurse is named Deborah, who dies at Beth-el and is mourned under the "oak of weeping." The nurse's inclusion in the household is a mark of status.
24.60: אֲחֹתֵנוּ אַתְּ הֲיִי לְאַלְפֵי רְבָבָה (ʾăḥōtēnû ʾatt hăyî lĕʾalpê rĕḇāḇāh) — "our sister, may you become thousands of ten-thousands." The blessing formula: ʾalpê rĕḇāḇāh (thousands of ten-thousands, literally) — innumerability expressed by combining two large-number words. This echoes and extends the Abrahamic promise vocabulary: "I will make your offspring as numerous as the stars" (15:5; 22:17 — "stars of heaven and sand on the seashore"). The family blessing on Rebekah uses the same register as the divine promise to Abraham. וְיִרַשׁ זַרְעֵךְ אֵת שַׁעַר שֹׂנְאָיו (may your offspring possess the gate of their enemies) — identical language to the post-Akedah covenant oath in 22:17: "your offspring will possess the gate of his enemies." The family pronounces a blessing that repeats YHWH's own sworn promise — whether they know this or not, the narrator does.
24.61: וַתָּקָם רִבְקָה וְנַעֲרֹתֶיהָ (wattāqom riḇqāh wĕnaʿărōtehā) — "Rebekah and her young women rose up." The departure scene is economical: they rise, mount the camels, go with the man. Wayyiqqaḥ hāʿeḇeḏ ʾet-riḇqāh wayyēleḵ (the servant took Rebekah and left) — the mission concludes with the same verb (lāqaḥ, to take) used for Abraham's instruction to "take a wife" for Isaac (vv. 3-4). The commission is fulfilled: the servant took (from Mesopotamia) what Abraham commanded him to take (a wife for Isaac).
24.62: וְיִצְחָק בָּא מִבּוֹא בְּאֵר לַחַי רֹאִי (wĕyiṣḥāq bāʾ mibôʾ bĕʾēr laḥay rōʾî) — "and Isaac came from going to Beer-lahai-roi." Bĕʾēr laḥay rōʾî (the well of the Living One who sees me) — the well named by Hagar in 16:14 after the divine encounter that promised Ishmael's greatness. Isaac's presence at Hagar's well is an ironic narrative detail: the covenant heir comes from the place associated with the expelled half-brother. The final section of the chapter (vv. 62-67) shifts to Isaac's perspective — he has been absent from the narrative since the Akedah (ch. 22); now he reappears for his marriage. wĕhûʾ yôšēḇ bĕʾereṣ hannèḡeḇ (and he was living in the Negev) — the southern desert region where Beersheba is located.
24.63: וַיֵּצֵא יִצְחָק לָשׂוּחַ בַּשָּׂדֶה לִפְנוֹת עָרֶב — "Isaac went out to lāśûaḥ in the field in the evening." Lāśûaḥ — the key hapax of the verse. The verb śûaḥ (or śîaḥ) appears in Job 7:11; Pss 77:4 (to muse/complain) and Ps 105:2 (to meditate); the root śîaḥ as a noun means "meditation, reflection, complaint, speech." The Vulgate translates meditari (to meditate); the LXX ἀδολεσχῆσαι (to meditate, muse). The precise meaning of the verb in this context is deliberately ambiguous — the reader cannot be certain whether Isaac is meditating, praying, walking in reflection, or simply strolling. The word creates a moment of interior space before the meeting: Isaac is in a contemplative state when Rebekah arrives.
24.64: וַתִּשָּׂא רִבְקָה אֶת-עֵינֶיהָ וַתֵּרֶא אֶת-יִצְחָק — "Rebekah lifted up her eyes and saw Isaac." The mutual seeing: Isaac first appears in Rebekah's view (she sees him); then Rebekah appears to Isaac (he sees her, v. 63: "as he looked up, he saw camels approaching"). The narrative orders the seeing to give Rebekah the first active role: she sees the man she is about to marry. Wattippol mēʿal haggāmāl (she fell/dismounted from the camel) — the verb nāpal (to fall) is used idiomatically for dismounting; the action is deferential, appropriate when approaching a figure of evident significance.
24.65: מִי-הָאִישׁ הַלָּזֶה הַהֹלֵךְ בַּשָּׂדֶה לִקְרָאתֵנוּ — "who is that man walking in the field to meet us?" Rebekah asks the servant. Hāʾîš hazzeh (this man) — her question uses lāzeh, an unusual demonstrative form (found only in later texts: Gen 37:19; Judg 18:4; 1 Sam 21:16 — possibly archaic or dialectal). הוּא אֲדֹנִי (hûʾ ʾăḏōnî, he is my master) — the servant's answer gives Isaac his title before his name; in calling Isaac ʾăḏōnî (my master), the servant signals the transfer of authority: the servant who has called Abraham ʾăḏōnî (my master) throughout now applies the same term to Isaac — the covenant has passed to the next generation. וַתִּקַּח הַצָּעִיף וַתִּתְכָּס (she took the veil and covered herself) — the veil is the modesty/bridal gesture appropriate to the first meeting of husband and wife; it signals Rebekah's recognition of the situation and her acceptance of her new status.
24.66: וַיְסַפֵּר הָעֶבֶד לְיִצְחָק אֵת כָּל-הַדְּבָרִים אֲשֶׁר עָשָׂה — "the servant told Isaac all the things he had done." The servant's account to Isaac is the third telling of the chapter's events: first the events themselves (vv. 10-27), then the servant's report to Laban's household (vv. 34-49), now a brief mention of the report to Isaac. The third telling is not narrated in detail — the narrator summarizes it in one verse. The narrative economy at the end contrasts with the extended repetition in the middle: what needed proving to Laban's household is simply confirmed to Isaac.
24.67: וַיְבִאֶהָ יִצְחָק הָאֹהֱלָה שָׂרָה אִמּוֹ (wayyĕḇîʾehā yiṣḥāq hāʾōhĕlāh śārāh ʾimmô) — "Isaac brought her into the tent of Sarah his mother." The tent of Sarah (ʾōhel śārāh) — the definite article makes it specific: this is the tent, the known household of the covenant matriarch who died in ch. 23. The marriage is situated within Sarah's tent, connecting Rebekah directly to Sarah's household role. וַיֶּאֱהָבֶהָ (wayyeʾĕhāḇehā) — "and he loved her." The second use of the verb ʾāhab (to love) in Genesis — the first was 22:2, where God said "your son, your only son, whom you love" (using ʾāhab for Abraham's love for Isaac). Now ʾāhab is used for Isaac's love for Rebekah: the covenant line is preserved not merely through legal arrangement but through genuine love. וַיִּנָּחֵם יִצְחָק אַחֲרֵי אִמּוֹ (wayyinnāḥem yiṣḥāq ʾaḥărê ʾimmô) — "and Isaac was comforted after his mother." Nāḥam (Niphal: to be comforted, to receive comfort — the same root as Noah's name, 5:29: "this one will comfort us"; and the name Nahum). Isaac's grief over Sarah's death (ch. 23) is resolved by Rebekah: marriage and love are the form comfort takes in the covenant narrative.

25.1: Qĕṭûrāh — "Keturah," Abraham's second wife taken after Sarah's death. The name likely derives from a root related to incense or smoke (qāṭar, to burn incense). The narrative introduces a tension about her status: here she is called ʾiššāh (wife), but v6 and 1 Chr 1:32 call her a pîlegeš (concubine). The distinction matters legally: a concubine's sons receive gifts (mattānōt, v6) rather than full inheritance. The narrator may preserve two traditions or signal that Keturah held an intermediate status — a secondary wife rather than a slave-concubine like Hagar.
25.2: The six sons of Keturah — Zimran, Jokshan, Medan, Midyān (Midian), Ishbak, Shuah — are the genealogical founders of Arabian tribal peoples. Midian becomes particularly significant: the Midianites are enemies of Israel (Num 31; Judg 6-8) but also the family of Moses's father-in-law Jethro (Exod 2:15-22; 3:1). The genealogy places these peoples within the Abrahamic family while explaining why they stand outside the covenant line. The list is formulaic — paratactic names linked by waw — characteristic of genealogical registers throughout Genesis.
25.3: Yoqšān wālad ʾet-Šeḇāʾ wĕʾet-Dĕḏān — "Jokshan fathered Sheba and Dedan." Sheba (H7614, from šāḇaʿ, seven/oath) is the source of the Sabaean people of Arabia, famous for the Queen of Sheba episode (1 Kgs 10:1-13). Dedan appears in Isaiah (21:13; Isa 60:6) and Ezekiel (27:20; 38:13) as a trading people. The second-generation names Ashurites, Letushites, and Leummites are otherwise unattested — likely small Arabian tribal groups. The genealogy shows that Abraham's blessing extended beyond the covenant line to produce nations throughout the ancient world.
25.4: Bĕnê Miḏyān — "sons of Midian." The five Midianite sub-groups listed here (Ephah, Epher, Hanoch, Abida, Eldaah) are names that appear in later commercial and political contexts. Ephah is named in Isa 60:6 among the nations bringing gifts of gold and frankincense to Zion. The genealogical formula kol-ʾēlleh bĕnê qĕṭûrāh ("all these were sons of Keturah") closes the section — the same closing formula used for Ishmael's sons in v16 (kol-ʾēlleh bĕnê yišmāʿēl). Both sections frame the non-covenant lines that nonetheless descend from Abraham.
25.5: wayyittēn ʾaḇrāhām ʾet-kol-ʾăšer-lô lĕyiṣḥāq — "Abraham gave everything he had to Isaac." The verb nātan (gave) combined with kol-ʾăšer-lô (all that he had) is the formulaic language of inheritance transfer (cf. 24:36). This legal act is completed while Abraham is still alive — a form of inter vivos transfer rather than testamentary bequest. The exclusivity of Isaac's inheritance (ʾet-kol, everything) establishes the unambiguous covenant line before Abraham dies, eliminating any dispute after his death about which son is the primary heir.
25.6: mattānōt — "gifts." The noun mattānāh (H4976) = a gift, something presented rather than owed as a legal right, distinguishing the concubines' sons' portion from Isaac's yeruššāh (inheritance). Abraham sends them qēḏmāh (eastward) — the direction of non-covenant peoples in Genesis: Cain goes east (4:16), the tower builders are in the east (11:2), Lot chooses the east (13:11), and the sons of Keturah settle east. The east is the direction away from the covenant land. Bĕʿôḏennû ḥāy — "while he was still living" — the disposition during lifetime contrasts with inheritance received at death; Abraham settles matters before he dies.
25.7: wĕʾēlleh yĕmê šnê-ḥayyê ʾaḇrāhām — "These are the days of the years of Abraham's life." The life-summary formula for Abraham (175 years) parallels the genealogical summaries of the pre-flood patriarchs (5:3-32) and Ishmael's summary (v17). The explicit counting of years connects Abraham to the covenant-history framework of Genesis time. 175 = 5 × 5 × 7, which later Jewish tradition noted as a "perfect" number, though the text does not make this observation — the narrator simply records the completeness of the life without embellishment.
25.8: wayyiggwaʿ wayyāmat ʾaḇrāhām bĕśêḇāh ṭôḇāh zāqēn wĕśāḇēʿa wayyēʾāsep ʾel-ʿammāyw — "Abraham breathed his last and died in good old age, an old man and full of years, and was gathered to his people." The verb gāwaʿ (H1478, to expire, breathe one's last) — a rare verb used predominantly for death in Genesis (25:8,17; 35:29; 49:33) — differs from the common mût (to die); it emphasizes the physical cessation of breath. Śāḇēʿa (satisfied, full) — from śāḇaʿ, to be satisfied/sated — describes a life brought to its natural completion without deficit. Wayyēʾāsep ʾel-ʿammāyw — "gathered to his people" — a distinctive formula for honorable death within the covenant community (also used for Ishmael, 25:17; Isaac, 35:29; Jacob, 49:33; Aaron, Num 20:24; Moses, Num 27:13). The idiom implies existence beyond death — one is gathered to those who preceded him — though the text does not elaborate the mode of that existence.
25.9: wayyiqbĕrû ʾōtô yiṣḥāq wĕyišmāʿēl bānāyw — "Isaac and Ishmael his sons buried him." The two sons who have been separated since Ishmael's expulsion (ch 21) come together for their father's burial — a reunion the text records without editorial comment. The cave of Machpelah (mĕʿārat hammakpēlāh), purchased by Abraham in Gen 23 from Ephron the Hittite, now fulfills its purpose as the patriarchal burial place. The land purchase was specifically for this — ownership of a burial plot was the tangible beginning of the covenant inheritance in Canaan. Jacob and Leah will also be buried here (49:29-32; 50:13).
25.10: šĕḏēh qānāh ʾaḇrāhām mēʾēt bĕnê-ḥēt šāmmāh qubbar ʾaḇrāhām wĕśārāh ʾišĕtô — "the field Abraham had purchased from the Hittites — there Abraham was buried, and Sarah his wife." The burial of Abraham with Sarah is the completion of the Machpelah transaction (ch 23). The field (śāḏeh) is specifically the field; the narrator uses the legal term qānāh (purchased, acquired) that was used in the formal transaction of 23:16-18. Abraham's burial "with Sarah his wife" ties the covenant pair together in death as in life — the couple who received the promise together rest together in the covenant land.
25.11: wayĕḇāreḵ ʾĕlōhîm ʾet-yiṣḥāq bĕnô wayyēšeḇ yiṣḥāq ʿim-bĕʾēr laḥay rōʾî — "God blessed Isaac his son, and Isaac settled near Beer-lahai-roi." The first act after Abraham's death is God's blessing of Isaac — the covenant transfers immediately without interruption. Bĕʾēr laḥay rōʾî ("well of the Living One who sees me") is the well named by Hagar in 16:14 (ʾattāh ʾēl rōʾî, "you are the God who sees"). Isaac settles precisely where Hagar encountered God — the geography is theologically charged: the covenant heir dwells at the place named for the God who sees and provides.
25.12: wĕʾēlleh tôlĕḏōt yišmāʿēl ben-ʾaḇrāhām — "These are the generations of Ishmael son of Abraham." The tôlĕḏōt formula (H8435, generations/account) marks a major structural division in Genesis — the tenth and final use of the formula before the Jacob cycle begins. The Ishmael genealogy is placed here, between Abraham's death and the Isaac-Jacob narrative, to close out the non-covenant line completely before the covenant story resumes. The specification "son of Abraham, whom Hagar the Egyptian, Sarah's slave, bore to Abraham" rehearses the full genealogical and social context — emphasizing both the Abrahamic connection and the slave-mother status that excluded him from the primary inheritance.
25.13: šĕmôt bĕnê yišmāʿēl bišmōtām lĕtôlĕḏōtām — "the names of the sons of Ishmael by their names, by their generations." The twelve sons of Ishmael listed in vv13-15 fulfill the promise of 17:20 ("twelve princes shall he father"). The firstborn Nĕḇāyôt (Nebaioth) is linked with Kedar in Isa 60:7 as bringing offerings to YHWH in the eschatological gathering of nations. The number twelve is structurally significant: Ishmael's twelve princes parallel the twelve tribes of Israel (35:22-26) — God's promise to Ishmael is given the same numerical form as his promise to Jacob.
25.14: The names Mishma, Dumah, Massa appear in the list with no commentary in the text. Dumah (H1746, silence) becomes the name of an Edomite region and appears in Isa 21:11 as a place of prophetic oracle. Massa appears in Prov 30:1 and 31:1 as the oracle-source of Agur and Lemuel — if these are Ishmaelite tribal names, two of Proverbs' wisdom contributors may be descendants of Ishmael, broadening the wisdom tradition beyond Israel. The genealogical names are terse but their connections to later OT material give them weight beyond the list itself.
25.15: The final four sons of Ishmael — Hadad, Tema, Jetur, Naphish, Kedemah — represent Arabian tribes documented in Assyrian inscriptions. Tema appears in Job 6:19 and Isa 21:14 as an oasis city and caravan stop. Jetur and Naphish reappear in 1 Chr 5:19-20 as peoples against whom the Transjordanian tribes warred. The specificity of the genealogical names connects the biblical text to the identifiable political geography of the ancient Near East — these are not literary inventions but remembered peoples.
25.16: šĕnêm ʿāśār nĕśîʾim lĕʾummōtām — "twelve princes according to their clans." Nāśîʾ (H5387, prince, chief, one who lifts/carries) — from the root nāśāʾ (to lift up), signifying a leader who carries responsibility for the group. The word is used for tribal leaders throughout the Pentateuch (Num 1:16; 7:2,10; 34:18). Lĕʾummōtām (according to their clans/peoples) — leʾōm (H3816, a people, clan) appears in the oracle to Rebekah in v23 (ûšĕnê leʾummîm, "two peoples"); the same word is used for Ishmael's descendants and for Jacob/Esau in the womb — suggesting the structural parallelism between the twelve Ishmaelite princes and the twelve Israelite tribes.
25.17: wayyiggwaʿ wayyāmat wayyēʾāsep ʾel-ʿammāyw — "he breathed his last and died and was gathered to his people." Ishmael's death formula repeats Abraham's almost verbatim (v8): gāwaʿ (expire), mût (die), neeaph ʾel-ʿammāyw (gathered to his people). The identical formula for father and son suggests that Ishmael's end, though outside the covenant line, is an honorable death — the same language of completion is applied. Ishmael's 137 years (vs. Abraham's 175) marks him as fully flourishing without the covenant's full weight. The gathering-to-his-people language implies the same post-mortem continuity as Abraham's death.
25.18: wayyiškon mēḥăwîlāh ʿad-šûr ʾăšer ʿal-pĕnê miṣrayim bōʾăḵāh ʾaššûrāh — "He settled from Havilah to Shur, which is opposite Egypt, going toward Assyria." The territory described (from Havilah to Shur) matches the wilderness east of Egypt — cf. 1 Sam 15:7 for the same description of Saul's campaign against the Amalekites. ʿAl-pĕnê kol-ʾeḥāyw nāpāl — "He settled in hostility to all his brothers" — the verb nāpal (to fall, settle, live) in the phrase ʿal-pĕnê (over against, in the face of) echoes the earlier oracle about Ishmael: yādô bakkōl wĕyad kōl bô ("his hand against everyone, everyone's hand against him," 16:12). The fulfillment of the wilderness-dweller oracle closes the Ishmael genealogy.
25.19: wĕʾēlleh tôlĕḏōt yiṣḥāq ben-ʾaḇrāhām ʾaḇrāhām hôlîḏ ʾet-yiṣḥāq — "These are the generations of Isaac son of Abraham — Abraham fathered Isaac." The tôlĕḏōt formula marks the beginning of the Isaac-Jacob narrative as the dominant structural unit. The doubling "Isaac son of Abraham — Abraham fathered Isaac" is often noted as tautological, but it is a deliberate rhetorical emphasis: the covenant line runs specifically through the father-son chain of Abraham → Isaac, not through the Keturah or Hagar lines. The repetition drives home the genealogical and covenantal specificity.
25.20: wĕyiṣḥāq ben-ʾarbāʿîm šānāh bĕqaḥtô ʾet-riqāh bat-bĕtûʾēl hāʾărammî mippaddān ʾărām ʾăḥôt lāḇān hāʾărammî lô lĕʾiššāh — "Isaac was forty years old when he took Rebekah daughter of Bethuel the Aramean from Paddan Aram, sister of Laban the Aramean, as his wife." The age-marker (40 years) is the narrator's anchor for chronological reckoning. The triple repetition of "Aramean" (ʾărammî) in one verse identifies Rebekah's ethnic origin emphatically — she is from outside Canaan, from the ancestral homeland of the patriarchs in Aram-Naharaim. The specification "sister of Laban the Aramean" introduces Laban, who will be Jacob's antagonist in the Jacob cycle (chs 29-31). Laban's identity is established before Jacob is born.
25.21: wayyeʿtar yiṣḥāq laYHWH lĕnokaḥ ʾišĕtô kî ʿăqārāh hîʾ — "Isaac prayed to YHWH on behalf of his wife, because she was barren." The verb ʿātar (H6279, to pray, entreat, intercede) is not the common prayer verb pālal or the root of tĕpillāh; ʿātar has a specifically intercessory, entreating nuance — it appears for Moses's plague intercession (Exod 8:8-13,25-30; 9:28-33; 10:17-18) and prophetic intercession (1 Sam 8:6; 12:19). The verb places Isaac in the role of intercessor for his barren wife, as Abraham was for Abimelech's barren household (20:17). The barrenness motif (ʿăqārāh, H6135, barren, one who is rootless/childless) connects Sarah, Rebekah, Rachel, Hannah, Elizabeth — the line of covenant continuity runs through women whose barrenness is overcome by divine intervention, not natural fertility.
25.22: wayyitroṣṣĕṣû habbānîm bĕqirbāh — "The children struggled within her." The Hithpolel of rāṣaṣ (H7533, to crush, bruise, oppress) — an unusual verbal form suggesting violent struggle, not mere movement; the Hithpolel adds reciprocity: they were crushing each other. The word is used in Judg 9:53 for crushing a skull and in Eccl 12:6 for a wheel broken. Rebekah's distress is linguistically grounded: this is not normal fetal movement. Wattēleḵ liḏrōš ʾet-YHWH — "and she went to inquire of YHWH." The verb dāraš (H1875, to seek, inquire, consult) is the standard term for consulting a divine oracle or prophet (1 Sam 9:9; 1 Kgs 22:8; 2 Kgs 8:8; Isa 55:6). Rebekah's inquiry is a prophetic consultation; the oracle she receives (v23) is the interpretive key for the entire Jacob-Esau narrative.
25.23: šĕnê gôyim bĕḇiṭnēḵ ûšĕnê leʾummîm mimmēʿayiḵ yippārēḏû ûleʾōm miḵleʾōm yeʾĕmāṣ wĕraḇ yaʿăḇōḏ ṣāʿîr — "Two nations are in your womb, two peoples shall be separated from within you; one people shall be stronger than the other, and the older shall serve the younger." The oracle uses both gôyim (nations, H1471 — foreign peoples, Gentiles) and leʾummîm (peoples, H3816 — a rarer, more poetic term); the pair creates a formal parallelism. Yippārēḏû (Niphal imperfect of pāraḏ, to be separated) — from within the womb, separation is already decreed. The climactic line wĕraḇ yaʿăḇōḏ ṣāʿîr — "the elder/great shall serve the younger/small" — is the oracle's punch: raḇ (great, elder, older) will serve ṣāʿîr (small, younger, lesser). Paul cites this oracle in Rom 9:10-12 as the clearest OT statement of unconditional election: the oracle is given before the twins are born, "not because of works but because of him who calls."
25.24: wĕhinnēh tôʾmîm bĕḇiṭnāh — "And behold, there were twins in her womb." The exclamatory hinnēh ("behold!") marks this as the fulfillment-surprise: Rebekah received a word about two nations, and now the physical reality is revealed — twins. The word tĕʾômîm (H8380, twins) — from the root tāʾam (to be double, to be paired) — is used here and in 38:27 (Tamar's twins Perez and Zerah). The twin births in Genesis (Jacob/Esau, Perez/Zerah) both involve reversals of the firstborn's rights — Esau's birthright passes to Jacob, and Perez pushes past Zerah in the birth canal. The pattern of twin-births-with-reversals is structurally significant in the covenant genealogy.
25.25: admōnî kullô kĕʾadderet śēʿār wayyiqrĕʾû šĕmô ʿēśāw — "The first came out red/ruddy, all of him like a hairy mantle, and they called his name Esau." Two wordplays operate simultaneously: (1) admōnî (ruddy, reddish, H132) plays on ʾĕḏôm (Edom, H123), the name of Esau's descendants and the land they inhabit; (2) śēʿār (hairy, H8181) plays on Śēʿîr (Seir, H8165), the mountainous region that becomes Edom's homeland. Both wordplays are made explicit in 25:30 (Edom = red) and 32:3/36:8 (Seir = Esau's territory). The name ʿēśāw itself is of uncertain etymology — "made/done" from ʿāśāh (to make/do) has been proposed, but this is not certain.
25.26: wĕyādô ʾōḥezet bĕʿăqēḇ ʿēśāw wayyiqrāʾ šĕmô yaʿăqōḇ — "and his hand had grasped Esau's heel, and his name was called Jacob." ʾĒqeḇ (H6119, heel) — the name Jacob (yaʿăqōḇ) is a paronomasia (wordplay) on this word: yaʿăqōḇ = "one who seizes the heel" or, from the related verb ʿāqaḇ (H6117), "one who supplants/trips up/circumvents by the heel." The two semantic valences (heel-holder vs. supplanter) are both operative: the physical act of holding the heel in the womb becomes the characteristic description of Jacob's method of operating — grasping what belongs to another by cunning rather than by right. Esau makes this explicit in 27:36: wayyaʿqĕḇēnî zeh paʿămayim — "has he not supplanted me these two times?" — using the verb ʿāqaḇ. Hosea 12:3 picks up the wordplay: "In the womb he took his brother by the heel, and in his manhood he strove with God."
25.27: wayyiggĕḏal hanaʿarîm wayhî ʿēśāw ʾîš yōḏēaʿ ṣayid ʾîš śāḏeh wĕyaʿăqōḇ ʾîš tām yōšēḇ ʾohālîm — "the boys grew up and Esau became a skillful hunter, a man of the field, while Jacob was a quiet man, dwelling in tents." The characterizations are carefully crafted: yōḏēaʿ ṣayid ("knower of hunt") uses yāḏaʿ (H3045, to know) in a skilled-mastery sense. Esau is man of the open country (śāḏeh, field/open land) — the same word for the uncultivated land contrasted with settled cultivation. Jacob is tām (H8535, complete, blameless, whole) — the same word for Noah (6:9) and Abraham (17:1: tāmîm); not moral perfection but integrity of character. Yōšēḇ ʾohālîm ("tent-dweller") marks Jacob as the pastoral, settled type, contrasting with Esau's roving hunter character.
25.28: wayyeʾĕhab yiṣḥāq ʾet-ʿēśāw kî-ṣayid bĕpîw wĕriqāh ʾōheḇet ʾet-yaʿăqōḇ — "Isaac loved Esau, for game was in his mouth, but Rebekah loved Jacob." The verb ʾāhaḇ (H157, to love) is used for the parental love that generates the family conflict of the next chapters. The motivation for Isaac's love is startling: kî-ṣayid bĕpîw — "because game was in his mouth" — the love is grounded in sensory pleasure (wild game). This parallels the materiality of the birthright scene in vv29-34 where Esau's physical hunger drives a covenant decision. Rebekah's love for Jacob lacks this kind of appetitive explanation — the narrator leaves her motive unspecified, which may suggest it was grounded in the oracle she received (v23).
25.29: wayyāzeḏ yaʿăqōḇ nāzîḏ wayyāḇōʾ ʿēśāw min-haśśāḏeh wĕhûʾ ʿāyēp — "Jacob was cooking stew when Esau came in from the field, and he was exhausted." Nāzîḏ (H5138, stew/broth/pottage) — from the root zîḏ (H2102, to boil, to be presumptuous/arrogant); the same root is used in Exod 18:11 and Neh 9:10,16 for "presumptuously" acting. The double meaning (cooked presumptuous broth) may be a deliberate wordplay — Jacob's cooking is itself an act of presumptuous calculation. ʿĀyēp (H5889, exhausted, faint, weary) — physically drained from the hunt; Esau's physical state will govern his decision. The contrast between Jacob's indoor cooking and Esau's outdoor hunting sets up the exchange.
25.30: haʾăṭîṭēnî nāʾ min-hāʾāḏōm hāʾāḏōm hazzeh kî ʿāyēp ʾānōḵî — "Let me eat some of that red stuff — that red stuff — for I am exhausted!" The demand is grammatically crude: haʾăṭîṭēnî = "let me swallow" (H3938, a rare verb for gulping/swallowing quickly) — not "give me" but "pour it into me." The reduplication hāʾāḏōm hāʾāḏōm (the red, the red) shows impatience and tunnel-vision; Esau does not even name what he wants — just "that red stuff." ʿal-kēn qārāʾ-šĕmô ʾĕḏôm — "therefore his name was called Edom" — the etiological note makes the link explicit: Esau = Edom; the red demand = the red people. The wordplay is phonological: ʾāḏōm (red) → ʾĕḏôm (Edom).
25.31: miḵrāh kāyôm ʾet-bĕḵōrātĕḵā lî — "Sell me today your birthright." Bĕḵōrāh (H1062, birthright, primogeniture right) — the legal bundle of rights belonging to the firstborn, including double inheritance (Deut 21:17 specifies the double portion) and family leadership authority. The imperative miḵrāh (sell!) is from māḵar (H4376, to sell, transfer ownership by transaction). Jacob frames the exchange as a commercial transaction. The word kāyôm ("today") specifies immediacy — the sale is to be consummated now, not deferred. Jacob's timing is calculated: Esau is vulnerable in his exhaustion and hunger.
25.32: hinnēh ʾānōḵî hôlēḵ lāmût wĕlāmmāh-zeh lî bĕḵōrāh — "Behold, I am about to die! What good is the birthright to me?" Esau's response elevates hunger to mortal threat — hôlēḵ lāmût ("going to die") is hyperbole, as the narrator will clarify in v34 by noting that Esau ate, drank, rose, and went his way. The rhetorical question wĕlāmmāh-zeh lî bĕḵōrāh expresses the logic of immediate gratification over long-term covenant inheritance. Esau is reasoning correctly within his own value framework: if you die, birthright has no value. The flaw is the premise — he is not dying.
25.33: hiššāḇĕʿāh lî kāyôm wayyiššāḇaʿ lô wayyimkōr ʾet-bĕḵōrātô lĕyaʿăqōḇ — "Swear to me today. And he swore to him, and sold his birthright to Jacob." The verb šāḇaʿ (H7650, to swear, to take an oath) derives from the root šeḇa (seven) — to swear was literally "to seven oneself" or to repeat a solemn formula seven times. An oath (šĕḇûʿāh) made the transaction legally binding and irrevocable under covenant norms — this is why Esau's subsequent attempts to recover the birthright are futile (27:36). Wayyimkōr (and he sold) — the completed sale; the birthright has legally transferred. The oath transforms what might have been a casual exchange into a permanent legal act.
25.34: wayyiḇez ʿēśāw ʾet-habbĕḵōrāh — "And Esau despised the birthright." The verb bāzāh (H959, to despise, disesteem, treat as worthless) — the narrator's verdict on Esau's action. The word is used for showing contempt for the covenant (Num 15:31, "despised the word of YHWH"), for despising a parent (Prov 15:20), and for despising YHWH himself (1 Sam 2:30; 2 Sam 12:10). The narrator does not say Jacob sinned by purchasing; the ethical verdict is entirely on Esau for his contempt of the covenantal birthright. This is the evaluation the NT picks up in Heb 12:16: Esau is called bébēlos (profane, common, secular) — one who treats the sacred as ordinary.

26.1: wayhî rāʿāḇ bāʾāreṣ miḷḷĕḇad hārāʿāḇ hārīʾšôn ʾăšer hāyāh bîmê ʾaḇrāhām — "Now there was a famine in the land, besides the earlier famine that was in the days of Abraham." The narrator's parenthetical comparison sets up the structural parallel: Abraham went to Egypt in a famine (12:10); Isaac is told not to go (v2). The phrase miḷḷĕḇad (besides, apart from) explicitly distinguishes this famine from Abraham's, preventing the reader from conflating the episodes. The parallel invites comparison: Isaac's response to the same crisis will differ from his father's, and the differences are theologically instructive.
26.2: wayyērāʾ ʾēlāyw YHWH wayyōʾmer ʾal-tēreḏ miṣrāymāh — "YHWH appeared to him and said, 'Do not go down to Egypt.'" The theophany formula wayyērāʾ ʾēlāyw YHWH (YHWH appeared to him) — Niphal of rāʾāh — is the same form used for theophanies to Abraham (12:7; 17:1; 18:1). Isaac's first recorded direct encounter with YHWH begins with the prohibition against the Egypt option. The command ʾal-tēreḏ miṣrāymāh (do not go down to Egypt) reverses the Abraham pattern deliberately — the covenant land is where Isaac is to remain, even in famine. The same directive will be given to Jacob with a different result (46:3-4): Jacob is told to go down; Isaac is told to stay. The covenant's geographical particularity is being worked out differently in each generation.
26.3: gûr bāʾāreṣ hazzōʾt wĕʾehyeh ʿimmĕḵā ûʾăḇārĕḵekā — "Sojourn in this land, and I will be with you and will bless you." The verb gûr (H1481, to sojourn, live as a resident alien) — Abraham used the same term for his own status in Canaan (23:4, gēr). Isaac is told to sojourn, not settle permanently — he remains a temporary resident in the land of promise. The divine-presence formula ʾehyeh ʿimmĕḵā ("I will be with you") is the covenant presence formula that recurs throughout the patriarchal narratives (28:15; 31:3; 48:21) and becomes foundational for the Exodus (ʾehyeh ʾăšer ʾehyeh, "I am who I am/I will be what I will be," Exod 3:14).
26.4: wĕhirbêtî ʾet-zarʿăḵā kĕkoḵĕḇê haššāmayim wĕnātattî lĕzarʿăḵā ʾēt kol-hāʾărāṣōt hāʾēl wĕhitbārăḵû bĕzarʿăḵā kol gôyê hāʾāreṣ — "I will multiply your seed as the stars of the sky, and I will give to your seed all these lands, and through your seed all the nations of the earth shall bless themselves." The three-element promise to Abraham (12:2-3; 15:5; 17:4-8) is now confirmed to Isaac: multiplication of seed (stars), land, and blessing to all nations. Zarʿăḵā (your seed) is the covenant-continuity term throughout Genesis — the singular collective noun that will carry the Abrahamic promise forward. The blessing formula wĕhitbārăḵû bĕzarʿăḵā uses the Hithpael of bāraḵ — reflexive: "shall bless themselves by your seed."
26.5: ʿēqeḇ ʾăšer-šāmaʿ ʾaḇrāhām bĕqōlî wayyišmōr mišmartî miṣwōtay ḥuqqôtay wĕtôrōtāy — "because Abraham obeyed my voice and kept my charge, my commandments, my statutes, and my laws." Four terms accumulate for Abraham's obedience: mišmeret (charge, watch-keeping duty), miṣwāh (commandment, explicit directive), ḥuqqāh (statute, engraved ordinance), and tôrāh (instruction, law). The same tetrad appears in Lev 18:30; 22:9 for Israel's covenant obligations. This is remarkable: Abraham is credited with keeping the Mosaic-sounding categories of covenant law before Sinai — not because the Mosaic law existed yet, but because the covenant relationship with YHWH has always involved obedient hearing (šāmaʿ bĕqôlî, to hear/obey my voice). The grounding of Isaac's blessing in Abraham's past obedience shows that covenant blessing is cumulative and transmittable.
26.6: wayyēšeḇ yiṣḥāq bigrār — "So Isaac settled in Gerar." The bare compliance statement after the theophany and extensive covenant promise is a narrative device signaling immediate, simple obedience. Isaac stays where he is told to stay; his obedience is uncontested at this stage of the narrative. Gerar is the Philistine territory where Abraham also sojourned with Abimelech (20:1-18) — the geographic repetition reinforces the structural parallel between the Abraham and Isaac episodes in this chapter. The same place, the same king (Abimelech — likely a dynastic title rather than the same individual), the same deception, and the same resolution.
26.7: wayyišʾălû ʾanĕšê hammāqôm lĕʾišĕtô wayyōʾmer ʾăḥōtî hîʾ kî yārēʾ lēʾmōr ʾišĕtî — "The men of the place asked him about his wife. He said, 'She is my sister,' for he feared to say, 'She is my wife.'" The wife-sister deception occurs three times in Genesis (12:10-20 for Abraham in Egypt; 20:1-18 for Abraham with Abimelech; here for Isaac with the same Abimelech). The repetition is structurally deliberate: each patriarch faces the same temptation (protect yourself by endangering your wife) and each chooses the same deception. Yārēʾ (feared) — Isaac's motivation is stated explicitly: fear. The fear is legitimate (beautiful wives were dangerous assets), but the solution — deception — compromises the covenant wife's safety for the husband's benefit.
26.8: wayyaṣṣēp ʾăḇîmeleḵ melek pĕlištîm bĕʿaḏ haḥallôn wayyarʾ wĕhinnēh yiṣḥāq mĕṣaḥēq ʾēt riqāh ʾišĕtô — "Abimelech king of the Philistines looked out a window and saw — and behold — Isaac laughing/sporting with Rebekah his wife." The key word is mĕṣaḥēq — Piel participle of ṣāḥaq (to laugh, play, sport, caress) — the same root as Isaac's own name (yiṣḥāq = "he laughs"). The text makes the wordplay unavoidable: yiṣḥāq was seen mĕṣaḥēq — "Isaac was Isaacing." The Piel of ṣāḥaq has a range from laughing to playing to sexual intimacy (cf. 39:14,17 where Potiphar's wife uses it to accuse Joseph). The context of a husband and wife makes the sexual nuance primary — Abimelech concludes they are married from this intimate behavior, not from overhearing a conversation.
26.9: ʾāḵēn hinnēh ʾišĕtĕḵā hîʾ wĕʾêk ʾāmartā ʾăḥōtî hîʾ — "She is clearly your wife! How did you say, 'She is my sister'?" The adverb ʾāḵēn (surely, indeed, clearly) marks Abimelech's conclusion as certain — what he saw was unambiguous evidence of marriage. The king's challenge is direct and legitimate: Isaac's deception endangered the king's household by the potential of one of his men taking Isaac's wife — which would constitute adultery (the same concern Abimelech expressed to Abraham in 20:3-6). Isaac's response, "I thought I might die on account of her" (pen-ʾāmût ʿālehā), acknowledges the fear motivation without defending the deception.
26.10: mah-zōʾt ʿāśîtā llānû kĕmĕʿaṭ šāḵaḇ ʾaḥaḏ hāʿām ʾet-ʾišĕtĕḵā wĕhēḇêʾtā ʿālênû ʾāšām — "What is this you have done to us? One of the people might easily have lain with your wife, and you would have brought guilt upon us." ʾĀšām (H817, guilt, guilt-offering, offense requiring restitution) — in Levitical law the ʾāšām is the guilt-offering required when one has wronged another party and must make restitution (Lev 5:14-6:7). Abimelech's use of the term shows his awareness that lying with another man's wife is a legally accountable wrong. The king of the Philistines is applying the principle that YHWH applied to him in 20:6 — the same covenantal moral framework governs pagan as well as covenant interactions.
26.11: wayyĕṣaw ʾăḇîmeleḵ ʾet-kol-hāʿām lēʾmōr hannōgēaʿ bāʾîš hazzeh ûḇĕʾišĕtô môt yûmāt — "Abimelech commanded all the people: 'Whoever touches this man or his wife shall certainly be put to death.'" The death formula môt yûmāt (infinitive absolute + imperfect = "dying he shall die," emphatic death penalty) — the same formula used in the capital case laws of the Torah (Exod 21:12-17; Lev 20:2,9-16). Abimelech's protection order over Isaac and Rebekah is more emphatic than the ordinary protection a guest might expect. It reflects both his personal moral concern (expressed in v10) and the practical recognition that YHWH is with Isaac (which he will state explicitly in v28).
26.12: wayyizraʿ yiṣḥāq bāʾāreṣ hahîʾ wayyimṣāʾ baššānāh hahîʾ mēʾāh šĕʿārîm wayyĕḇāreḵēhû YHWH — "Isaac sowed in that land and reaped in that year a hundredfold, and YHWH blessed him." Mēʾāh šĕʿārîm (a hundred gates/measures) — the šaʿar here is a grain measure, not a city gate: "a hundredfold." In the ancient world a 10:1 return on seed was considered outstanding; 100:1 is miraculous. The causal wayyĕḇāreḵēhû YHWH (YHWH blessed him) — the narrator attributes the abundance directly to covenant blessing, not to Isaac's agricultural skill. The abundance in the famine year is a sign of YHWH's presence with the covenant heir in the land he commanded him to stay in.
26.13: wayyigdal hāʾîš wayyēlek hālôḵ wĕgāḏēl ʿaḏ kî-gāḏal mĕʾōḏ — "The man became great, and kept growing greater until he became very great." The Qal perfect gāḏal + the idiom hālôḵ wĕgāḏēl (going and growing) — the infinitive absolute of hālaḵ combined with the Qal participle of gāḏal creates the picture of continuous, ongoing growth. The same construction appears in 2 Sam 5:10 for David: "David grew and grew" (hālôḵ wĕgāḏôl). The threefold use of gāḏal in this verse (became great / growing greater / very great) is emphatic accumulation — the narrator insists on the magnitude of Isaac's prosperity to set up the Philistine envy in v14.
26.14: wayhî-lô miqnēh-ṣōʾn ûmiqnēh-ḇāqār waʿăḇuddāh rabbāh wayyĕqannĕʾû ʾōtô pĕlištîm — "He had flocks and herds and a great household, and the Philistines envied him." Qānnāʾ (Piel of qānāʾ, H7065, to be jealous/envious) — a strong word; the same root describes God's "jealousy" (ʾēl qannāʾ, Exod 20:5; 34:14) and the jealousy that drove Joseph's brothers to sell him (Acts 7:9). The Philistines' envy is the socio-political pressure that generates the expulsion of v16 and the well-disputes of vv19-21. Covenant blessing in the land of the foreigner creates tension — a pattern that recurs from Joseph in Egypt to Israel in Egypt to Daniel in Babylon.
26.15: wĕkol-habbĕʾērōt ʾăšer ḥāpĕrû ʿaḇdê ʾāḇîw bîmê ʾaḇrāhām ʾāḇîw sittĕmûm pĕlištîm wayyĕmalĕʾûm ʿāpār — "Now all the wells that his father's servants had dug in the days of his father Abraham, the Philistines stopped them up and filled them with dirt." The destruction of wells in the ancient Near East was an act of war and a deliberate denial of future habitation rights — wells represented permanent water-claim in a semi-arid landscape. The Philistines' actions against Abraham's wells are an erasure of the patriarchal covenant presence in the land. Isaac's response (v18) — reopening the same wells and giving them the same names — is an act of covenant reaffirmation: reclaiming what was his father's in the covenant territory.
26.16: ʿāṣamt mimmennû mĕʾōḏ — "you have become too powerful for us." The verb ʿāṣam (H6105, to be numerous, strong, mighty) — the same verb used for Israel's multiplication in Egypt (Exod 1:7,20) and for YHWH's mighty arm (Deut 9:29; 26:8). The Philistines' fear of Isaac's growing power parallels Pharaoh's fear of Israel's growth (Exod 1:9-10: "the people of Israel are more numerous and stronger than we"). Both situations involve the covenant people's prosperity creating host-nation anxiety that leads to expulsion or oppression. The pattern of blessing → growth → host-fear → expulsion is a recurring structure in the covenant narrative.
26.17: wayyēlek miššām yiṣḥāq wayyaḥan bĕnaḥal gĕrār wayyēšeḇ šām — "So Isaac moved away from there and camped in the Valley of Gerar and settled there." The verbs sequence — moved (hālaḵ), camped (ḥānāh), settled (yāšaḇ) — follow the patriarchal movement pattern of Abraham (12:9; 13:18; 21:34). Isaac's movement into the valley is a compromise: he leaves the city but stays in the region, pressing the question of how far he must go before he finds undisputed space. The Valley of Gerar (naḥal gĕrār) is a wadi/watercourse — the kind of terrain where digging wells would be productive, setting up the disputes of vv19-21.
26.18: wayyāšāb yiṣḥāq wayyaḥpōr ʾet-bĕʾĕrōt hammayim ʾăšer ḥāpĕrû bîmê ʾaḇrāhām ʾāḇîw — "Isaac dug again the wells of water that had been dug in the days of his father Abraham." Ḥāpar (H2658, to dig, bore into) — the verb used for digging wells throughout this chapter (vv18-22,25,32). Isaac's reversion to Abraham's wells is a legal and covenantal claim: these are not new wells but the restoration of Abraham's property rights. Wayyiqrāʾ lāhen šēmôt kĕšēmōt ʾăšer-qārāʾ lāhen ʾāḇîw — "he gave them the same names that his father had given them" — the renaming is continuity of ownership claim. Abraham's names mark the history; Isaac's repetition of those names asserts the covenant inheritance is unbroken even through the Philistine erasure.
26.19: wayyeḥpĕrû ʿaḇdê-yiṣḥāq bannāḥal wayyimṣĕʾû šām bĕʾēr mayim ḥayyîm — "Isaac's servants dug in the valley and found there a well of living/fresh water." Mayim ḥayyîm (living water, flowing spring water) — ḥāy (H2416, alive, living) — the phrase distinguishes spring-fed, flowing water from cistern water (stored rainwater). Living water was the highest quality — pure, fresh, reliable — and appears throughout OT legislation (Lev 14:5,50; 15:13; Num 19:17) and wisdom/prophetic literature (Jer 2:13; 17:13; Zech 14:8). Jesus's use of "living water" in John 4:10-14 and 7:38 is charged with this OT background — the new covenant blessing is described in the language of the freshest, purest, most life-sustaining water.
26.20: wayyĕrîḇû rōʿê gĕrār ʿim-rōʿê yiṣḥāq lēʾmōr lānû hāmāyim wayyiqrāʾ šēm-habbĕʾēr ʿēśeq kî hitʿassĕqû ʿimmô — "The herdsmen of Gerar quarreled with Isaac's herdsmen, saying, 'The water is ours!' So he named the well Esek, because they disputed with him." ʿĒśeq (H6219, contention, dispute) — from the root ʿāsaq (to dispute, press a claim). The naming convention in vv20-22 is an etiology: each well's name records what happened there. Isaac's response to the dispute is to move on (wayyelek miššām, v21) — he does not fight for the well. This patience under repeated opposition is Isaac's characteristic response throughout the chapter, culminating in the Rehoboth statement of v22.
26.21: wayyaḥpĕrû bĕʾēr ʾaḥeret wayyĕrîḇû gam-ʿāleyhā wayyiqrāʾ šĕmāh śiṭnāh — "They dug another well, and they quarreled over that one also, and he named it Sitnah." Śiṭnāh (H7855, opposition, hostility, accusation) — from the root śāṭan (H7853, to oppose, to be an adversary), the root that gives the title "Satan" (the Adversary). The naming of a well "Adversary/Accusation" reflects the experience of enmity that was purely hostile and unjust — not a legitimate competing claim but pure opposition. The progression Esek (contention) → Sitnah (adversarial hostility) shows escalation. Isaac again moves without fighting back.
26.22: wayyaʿtēq miššām wayyaḥpōr bĕʾēr ʾaḥeret wĕlōʾ rāḇû ʿāleyhā wayyiqrāʾ šĕmāh rĕḥōḇôt wayyōʾmer kî-ʿattāh hirḥîḇ YHWH lānû ûpārînû bāʾāreṣ — "He moved from there and dug another well, and they did not quarrel over it. He named it Rehoboth, saying, 'For now YHWH has made room for us, and we will be fruitful in the land.'" Rĕḥōḇôt (H7344, wide places, spacious areas) — from rāḥaḇ (H7337, to be wide, to make room). Isaac interprets the cessation of conflict as divine provision of space — hirḥîḇ YHWH lānû ("YHWH has made room for us"). The movement from Esek to Sitnah to Rehoboth is a theological narrative of patient endurance yielding to divine provision: God does not give the space at first but creates it through the progression of trials. ûpārînû bāʾāreṣ — "we will be fruitful in the land" — pārāh (H6509, to be fruitful) echoes the creation blessing (1:28) and the Abrahamic promise (17:6) — covenant fruitfulness finally has room to express itself.
26.23: wayyaʿal miššām bĕʾēr šāḇaʿ — "From there he went up to Beersheba." The movement wayyaʿal (went up) — the directional opposite of yāraḏ (to go down) — moves from the lowland Valley of Gerar back toward the highland of Beersheba, the location of the patriarchal covenant with Abimelech (21:22-34) and the altar of Abraham (21:33). The journey to Beersheba is a movement toward the sacred site of covenant history, and the theophany of v24 confirms this — YHWH appears at the place of covenant establishment.
26.24: ʾānōḵî ʾĕlōhê ʾaḇrāhām ʾāḇîḵā ʾal-tîrāʾ kî-ʾittĕḵā ʾānōḵî ûḇēraḵtîḵā wĕhirḇêtî ʾet-zarʿăḵā baʿăḇûr ʾaḇrāhām ʿaḇdî — "I am the God of your father Abraham. Do not be afraid, for I am with you; I will bless you and multiply your seed, for the sake of Abraham my servant." The self-identification formula ʾānōḵî ʾĕlōhê ʾāḇîḵā — "I am the God of your father" — is the covenantal self-introduction (cf. 28:13; 31:42; 32:9; Exod 3:6). The fear formula ʾal-tîrāʾ (do not be afraid) accompanies every major patriarchal covenant encounter (15:1; 21:17; 26:24; 28:13; 46:3). Baʿăḇûr ʾaḇrāhām ʿaḇdî — "for the sake of Abraham my servant" — the covenant benefits to Isaac are explicitly grounded in Abraham's prior covenant status. The title ʿeḇeḏ (servant) for Abraham is the highest honorific in the OT covenant vocabulary (also used for Moses, Joshua, David).
26.25: wayyiḇen šām mizḇēaḥ wayyiqrāʾ bĕšēm YHWH wayyeṭ-šām ʾohŏlô wayyikrû šām ʿaḇdê-yiṣḥāq bĕʾēr — "He built an altar there and called on the name of YHWH, and he pitched his tent there, and Isaac's servants dug a well there." The three-fold covenant response — altar, calling on YHWH's name, tent — mirrors the Abraham pattern exactly: Abraham built altars at Shechem (12:7), Bethel (12:8 — also "called on the name"), Mamre (13:18), and Beersheba (21:33). Isaac's replication of these covenant markers at Beersheba is a conscious continuation of the patriarchal covenant practice. The altar (mizḇēaḥ) is the formal site of encounter; calling on the name (qāraʾ bĕšēm YHWH) is the covenant worship formula; the tent marks covenant residence; the well marks covenant provision.
26.26: waʾăḇîmeleḵ hālaḵ ʾēlāyw migĕrār waʾăḥuzzat mērēʿēhû ûpîḵōl śar-ṣĕḇāʾô — "Abimelech came to him from Gerar with Ahuzzath his adviser and Phicol the commander of his forces." The same Abimelech-Phicol pair appeared in the Abraham covenant (21:22,32). If these are the same individuals, 80+ years have passed — more likely "Abimelech" is a Philistine dynastic title (like "Pharaoh" for Egypt) and "Phicol" similarly. Mēraʿēhû — "his friend/companion" (from rēaʿ, H7453, friend, associate, companion) — Ahuzzath is Abimelech's personal advisor, the king's inner council.
26.27: lāmmāh bāʾtem ʾēlay wĕʾattem śĕnēʾtem ʾōtî wattĕšalĕḥûnî mēʾittĕkem — "Why have you come to me, since you hated me and sent me away from you?" Isaac's challenge uses śānēʾ (H8130, to hate, to be hostile toward) — a strong term for covenant enmity. Isaac's memory of the Philistines' hostility (v15 — stopping his father's wells; v16 — expelling him) grounds his suspicion. The question is not rude but honest — Isaac names the prior hostility before engaging with the covenant request. This directness is not typical of ANE diplomatic discourse, which would normally open with politeness; Isaac's frankness shows both his awareness of the history and his willingness to negotiate from a position of truth.
26.28: rāʾôh rāʾînû kî-hāyāh YHWH ʿimmāḵ — "We have plainly seen that YHWH was with you." The emphatic construction rāʾôh rāʾînû (Qal infinitive absolute + perfect = "seeing we have seen") — the doubled form intensifies: their perception is certain, observed, undeniable. The basis for Abimelech's treaty request is the observation of divine favor: YHWH's presence with Isaac is so evident that even the Philistine king can name it. This pattern — covenant people's prosperity making YHWH's presence observable to Gentile rulers — recurs in Joseph (39:3), Moses (Exod 18:9-11), and the missional theology of Isa 2:2-4 and 60:1-3. The Gentile's recognition of YHWH's covenantal presence is the basis for covenant diplomacy.
26.29: ʾim-taʿăśeh ʿimmānû rāʿāh kaʾăšer lōʾ nĕgaʿnûḵā wĕkaʾăšer ʿāśînû ʿimmĕḵā raq-ṭôḇ wattĕšallĕḥăḵā bĕšālôm wĕʾattāh ʿattāh bĕrûḵ YHWH — "that you will not do us harm, just as we have not touched you and have done to you nothing but good and sent you away in peace. You are now the blessed of the LORD." The Philistines' self-description of their conduct is selective (Isaac experienced their stopping his wells and expelling him), but the diplomatic formula focuses on the present — they are appealing for treaty on the basis of not having physically harmed Isaac. ʿAttāh bĕrûḵ YHWH — "you are blessed of YHWH" — the Philistine king applies the covenant title to Isaac; the blessing Isaac carries is so visible that his adversary names it. The phrase will return in the Boaz-Ruth narrative (Ruth 3:10) and the Psalms (Ps 115:15; 128:4).
26.30: wayyaʿaś lāhem mišteh wayyōʾkĕlû wayyišĕtû — "He made them a feast and they ate and drank." The covenant meal (mišteh, H4960, feast/drinking occasion) seals the treaty: in ANE covenant practice, eating together was the ratification of an agreement — the shared table bound the parties morally and socially. Compare Abraham's covenant meal with Abimelech (21:27-31) and Jacob's covenant meal with Laban (31:54). The pattern of covenant ratification through shared eating connects this episode to the broader covenant tradition and anticipates the covenant-meal theology of Exod 24:11 and the Lord's Supper.
26.31: wayyaškimû babbōqer wayyiššāḇĕʿû ʾîš lĕʾāḥîw wayyĕšallĕḥēm yiṣḥāq wayyēlĕḵû mēʾittô bĕšālôm — "Early in the morning they swore an oath to one another. Then Isaac sent them off, and they went away from him in peace." Wayyaškimû (they rose early) — early rising for the covenant oath ratification parallels Abraham's early rising for covenant acts (22:3: the Moriah journey; 21:14: sending away Hagar). The oath (šĕḇûʿāh) formally sealed the covenant begun by the meal of v30. Bĕšālôm (in peace, in wholeness) — the word šālôm (H7965) here means covenant-peace, the wholeness of relations between two parties who have formalized mutual non-aggression. The Philistines leave in šālôm — the same word Isaac's servants will use for Beersheba (Beer-sheba = seven/oath).
26.32: wayyōʾmĕrû lô māṣāʾnû māyim — "They said to him, 'We have found water!'" The discovery of the new well on the same day as the covenant with Abimelech is a narrative juxtaposition — covenant with man and covenant provision from God are simultaneous. The servants' report uses māṣāʾ (H4672, to find, to come upon) — the discovery of water is framed as finding something given rather than digging something out; the providence is credited to discovery, not to labor. The timing (same day as the treaty) invites the reader to see YHWH's provision as confirming the covenant settlement.
26.33: wayyiqrāʾ ʾōtāh šiḇʿāh ʿal-kēn šēm-hāʿîr bĕʾēr šāḇaʿ ʿaḏ hayyôm hazzeh — "He called it Shibah; therefore the name of the city has been Beersheba to this day." The place name bĕʾēr šāḇaʿ carries a double etymology: (1) from šeḇa (H7651, seven) — Abraham's covenant with Abimelech involved seven lambs as a witness (21:28-31); (2) from šāḇaʿ (H7650, to swear) — both the Abraham covenant (21:31) and the Isaac covenant (v31 here) involve oaths. The name encodes both "well of seven" and "well of the oath" — both etymologies are simultaneously valid, reflecting the double oath (Abraham's and Isaac's) that attach to the location. ʿAḏ hayyôm hazzeh ("to this day") is the narrator's confirmation that the name has persisted to his own time.
26.34: wayhî ʿēśāw ben-ʾarbāʿîm šānāh wayyiqqaḥ ʾiššāh ʾet-yĕhûḏît bat-bĕʾērî haḥittî wĕʾet-bāśmat bat-ʾêlôn haḥittî — "When Esau was forty years old, he took as wife Judith daughter of Beeri the Hittite, and Basemath daughter of Elon the Hittite." Esau's marriages at age 40 mirror Isaac's marriage age (v20). But the contrast is sharp: Isaac married within the Abrahamic family (Rebekah from Paddan Aram); Esau marries Hittite women — Canaanites, outside the covenant family. The specific identification "Hittites" (ḥittî) echoes the Ephron-Hittite transaction of ch 23 — the non-covenant people from whom Abraham bought the burial cave. Esau's Hittite wives represent the same pattern as the birthright sale: trading the covenant for immediate local advantage.
26.35: wattihyênāh môrat rûaḥ lĕyiṣḥāq ûlĕriqāh — "and they were a source of bitterness of spirit to Isaac and Rebekah." The idiom môrat rûaḥ (bitterness of spirit) — mārar (H4843, to be bitter) + rûaḥ (H7307, spirit/breath) — a grief that sits in the breath, in the inner life. The same idiom appears in 1 Sam 1:10 for Hannah's anguish. The narrative coda to ch 26 connects directly to the motivation for Rebekah's schemes in ch 27: if Esau's Hittite wives already cause Isaac and Rebekah such distress, Rebekah's actions to prevent the covenant blessing from going to Esau become more comprehensible — she fears Jacob will marry Hittite women too (27:46; 28:1-2), and the covenant line will be completely absorbed into the Canaanite population.

27.1: וַיְהִי כִּי-זָקֵן יִצְחָק וַתִּכְהֶיןָ עֵינָיו מֵרְאֹת (wayhî kî-zāqēn yiṣḥāq wattikheynā ʿênāyw mērĕʾōt) — "when Isaac was old and his eyes grew dim from seeing." Kāhāh (Qal: to be dim, faint, darkened) — the same verb of eye-dimming used for Eli in 1 Sam 3:2 and the Servant's description of not quenching a smoldering wick in Isa 42:4. Isaac's blindness is the narrative pivot of the entire chapter; it creates the conditions for the deception. The stark contrast with Deut 34:7 — Moses at 120 whose eyes had not dimmed (lōʾ-kāhătāh ʿênô) — is implicit: Moses' eyes held; Isaac's do not. The dimming is a narrative device, not a theological judgment.
27.2: לֹא יָדַעְתִּי יוֹם מוֹתִי (lōʾ yāḏaʿtî yôm môtî) — "I do not know the day of my death." The declaration of existential ignorance creates urgency — Isaac believes he is near death; the narrative's irony is that Isaac will live many more years and witness Jacob's departure and Esau's family formation (26:34-35 already described Esau's marriages; 28:6-9 happens before Isaac dies, and 35:27-29 records his death at 180). Isaac's sense of imminent death is premature, which makes the urgency of the blessing scene both real and ironic.
27.3: כְּלֶיךָ תֶּלְיְךָ וְקַשְׁתֶּךָ (kĕlêḵā tĕlyĕḵā wĕqaštĕḵā) — "your weapons, your quiver and your bow." Tĕlî (quiver, the case for arrows — from תָּלָה, to hang; that which hangs from the shoulder) — the hunting equipment identifies Esau's domain; he is the outdoor hunter, the man of the field (25:27). Isaac's commission to Esau is built on their established relational pattern: hunt game, cook, bring to me — this has presumably happened before; now it is to precede the deathbed blessing.
27.4: וְעַשֵׂה-לִי מַטְעַמִּים כַּאֲשֶׁר אָהַבְתִּי (wĕʿăśēh-lî maṭʿammîm kaʾăšer ʾāhaḇtî) — "prepare for me delicacies as I love them." Maṭʿam (delicacy, tasty food, savory dish) from ṭāʿam (taste); the word appears first in 25:28 where Isaac loved Esau "because game was in his mouth" (kî-ṣayiḏ bĕpîw). Isaac's love for Esau is anchored in sensory pleasure — taste. בַּעֲבוּר תְּבָרֶכְךָ נַפְשִׁי (so that my soul may bless you) — napšî (my soul) — Isaac speaks of his nepeš blessing Esau, not merely his mouth or his authority; the whole self, the life-breath, the volitional center, confers the blessing. The nepeš-blessing is the most solemn form of personal benediction.
27.5: וְרִבְקָה שֹׁמַעַת (wĕriḇqāh šōmāʿat) — "and Rebekah was listening." The Qal participle šōmāʿat (was listening/hearing) — the continuous aspect of the participle implies sustained, deliberate attention: Rebekah was positioned to hear and did hear the entire exchange. The narrative does not editorialize whether she was eavesdropping or happened to overhear — the Hebrew simply states the fact. The oracle she received in 25:23 ("the elder will serve the younger") gives her interpretive context for what she now hears; her action in vv. 6-17 is driven by that prior divine word.
27.6: Rebekah immediately summons Jacob and transmits what she heard — exact reported speech of Isaac's commission to Esau. The precision of her quotation is notable: she repeats Isaac's words faithfully (with one addition in v. 7 — "in the presence of YHWH," which may reflect her own theological framing of the deathbed blessing). The mother's intervention is grounded in the oracle of 25:23, though she does not cite it explicitly; her action presupposes the divine word as her authorization.
27.7: לִפְנֵי יְהוָה לִפְנֵי מוֹתִי — "in the presence of YHWH before my death." Rebekah adds "before YHWH" to her quotation of Isaac's words — Isaac's original commission (v. 4) does not include this phrase. Whether Rebekah inserts it for rhetorical impact (to motivate Jacob) or whether she genuinely heard it in a longer exchange the narrator did not record is indeterminate. The addition elevates the blessing from a family ceremony to a covenantal religious act performed before the divine witness.
27.8: שְׁמַע בְּקֹלִי (šĕmaʿ bĕqōlî) — "listen to/obey my voice." The imperative šĕmaʿ + preposition bĕ (to hear in accordance with) = to obey, follow the instructions of. Rebekah assumes command authority over Jacob; the language is the same as a divine command (cf. Exod 19:5: "if you hear/obey my voice"). The irony: Rebekah commands Jacob to obey her (šĕmaʿ bĕqōlî), and Jacob's obedience to his mother will lead to a blessing he sought by disobedience to his father's plan.
27.9: שְׁנֵי גְּדָיֵי עִזִּים טֹבִים (šĕnê gĕḏāyê ʿizzîm ṭōḇîm) — "two choice young goats." Gĕḏî ʿizzîm (kid of the goats) — young male goat; the specification of two goats (plural) is practical: sufficient meat to prepare a meal that will pass for fresh-hunted game. The goat is domesticated livestock (from the flock), not game (from the field) — the substitution of domestic animal for wild game is the core deception in terms of the food itself. Isaac presumably recognizes the difference in flavor, but his sense of smell (v. 27, the clothes) and touch (vv. 22-23, the goatskins) are manipulated to override any taste-suspicion.
27.10: The plan's goal: וְאָכַל בַּעֲבוּר אֲשֶׁר יְבָרֶכְךָ לִפְנֵי מוֹתוֹ — "so that he may eat and bless you before his death." The chain: Jacob brings the food → Isaac eats → Isaac blesses. The blessing is contingent on the food; the food is the medium of the blessing encounter. The deception therefore operates through the food: replace the food (game with goat), replace the recipient's identity, receive the blessing intended for another.
27.11: הֵן עֵשָׂו אָחִי אִישׁ שָׂעִר וְאָנֹכִי אִישׁ חָלָק (hēn ʿēśāw ʾāḥî ʾîš śāʿir wĕʾānōḵî ʾîš ḥālāq) — "behold, my brother Esau is a hairy man and I am a smooth man." Śāʿir (hairy — from śēʿār, hair; also related to śāʿîr, goat/satyr) — the physical distinction between the twins was already stated at birth (25:25: Esau came out "red and hairy all over"). Ḥālāq (smooth, slippery — from the same root as ḥēleq, portion/flattery; the smoothness is Jacob's literal skin quality). Jacob's objection is not moral ("I shouldn't deceive my father") but practical ("I won't fool him because our skin feels different") — the text records this without moral commentary.
27.12: וְהָבֵאתִי עָלַי קְלָלָה וְלֹא בְרָכָה (wĕhāḇēʾtî ʿālay qĕlālāh wĕlōʾ ḇĕrāḵāh) — "I would bring curse upon myself and not blessing." Jacob uses the pair qĕlālāh (curse) and bĕrāḵāh (blessing) — the antonyms that dominate the chapter. His fear is of the curse-mechanism of false swearing: invoking blessing through deception inverts the blessing into curse. The theological realism is precise: a blessing received through deception risks becoming a curse. Rebekah's response in v. 13 assumes the curse-risk and accepts it personally.
27.13: עָלַי קִלְלָתְךָ בְּנִי (ʿālay qillāṭĕḵā ḇĕnî) — "upon me be your curse, my son." Rebekah accepts the curse-risk in a formula that deliberately echoes covenant curse-assumption language. The tragic irony: Rebekah's curse-acceptance is partially fulfilled — she will never see Jacob again (v. 45 suggests she expects a "few days"; the few days become years; 27:46-28:5 sends Jacob to Paddan-Aram, from which he returns only in ch. 31, after Rebekah has died — she is not mentioned as alive at his return). The "curse" she accepts is separation from her beloved son for the rest of her life.
27.14: Jacob obeys: he goes, gets the goats, brings them to Rebekah. The series of waw-consecutive imperfects is fast: wayyēleḵ wayyiqqaḥ wayyāḇēʾ (he went, he took, he brought). No hesitation, no further moral deliberation. Jacob moves from objection to obedience without transition — once Rebekah accepts the curse, he acts. Wattaʿaś ʾimmô maṭʿammîm kaʾăšer ʾāhab ʾāḇîw (his mother prepared delicacies as his father loved them) — the same phrase from v. 4 (what Isaac loves) is fulfilled by Rebekah's cooking, not Esau's hunting.
27.15: בִּגְדֵי עֵשָׂו בְּנָהּ הַגָּדֹל הַחֲמֻדֹת (biḡḏê ʿēśāw bĕnāh haggāḏōl haḥămuḏōt) — "the finest clothes of Esau her older son." Ḥămuḏōt (choice, desirable, precious — plural of excellence from ḥāmaḏ, to desire, covet — the same root as the tenth commandment's prohibition on coveting, Exod 20:17). Esau's "desirable garments" carry the smell of the field (v. 27), which is what triggers Isaac's blessing. The clothes are kept in Rebekah's custody (ʾittāh babbāyit, with her in the house) — either because Esau stored good clothes at home or because Rebekah had possession of them.
27.16: וְאֵת עֹרֹת גְּדָיֵי הָעִזִּים הִלְבִּישָׁה עַל-יָדָיו — "she covered his hands and smooth neck with goatskins." The tactile deception: the goatskins (ʿōrōt gĕḏāyê hāʿizzîm) replicate Esau's hairiness on Jacob's smooth hands and neck. The skin substitution is direct: Jacob, who does not have Esau's śēʿār (hair), is given goat-ʿôr (skin) to simulate it. The Hebrew wordplay between śēʿîr (Seir, the land of Edom/Esau) and śāʿir (hairy) is not explicit here but underlies the whole narrative — Esau's identity is bound up in his hairiness and his land.
27.17: Rebekah hands Jacob the prepared food and the bread. The provision is complete: the disguise (clothes + goatskin), the food (delicacies + bread). Jacob goes to his father with everything needed to complete the deception. The narrative is spare and sequential — no interior monologue, no hesitation from Jacob at the threshold. The story is driven by events and dialogue, not by psychological exposition.
27.18: וַיָּבֹא אֶל-אָבִיו וַיֹּאמֶר אָבִי — "he came to his father and said 'my father.'" וַיֹּאמֶר הִנֶּנִּי מִי אַתָּה בְּנִי — "he answered 'here I am — who are you, my son?'" Isaac's immediate question mî ʾattāh bĕnî (who are you, my son?) is the question the entire chapter hinges on. The question will be asked again in v. 24 (hăʾattāh zeh bĕnî ʿēśāw). Isaac cannot see and must rely on other senses — which have been systematically manipulated. The hinnēnî (here I am) of Isaac's response echoes the readiness-formula that marks covenant encounters (ch. 22); here it is Isaac who is present and available, but to be deceived.
27.19: אָנֹכִי עֵשָׂו בְּכֹרֶךָ (ʾānōḵî ʿēśāw bĕḵōreḵā) — "I am Esau your firstborn." The lie is direct, emphatic (ʾānōḵî, the strong first-person pronoun), and complete: identity + birth-order. Jacob claims the two things he most lacks: Esau's name and Esau's firstborn status. עָשִׂיתִי כַּאֲשֶׁר דִּבַּרְתָּ אֵלָי (I have done as you said to me) — the second lie: Isaac told Esau to hunt game; Jacob was sent by Rebekah to get goats. He claims his mother's commission as his father's. קוּם-נָא שְׁבָה וְאָכְלָה מִצֵּידִי (rise now, sit, and eat of my game) — calling domestic goat "my game" (ṣêḏî, from ṣûḏ, to hunt) is the third lie. Three deceptions in one verse.
27.20: מַה-זֶּה מִהַרְתָּ לִמְצֹא בְּנִי — "how did you find it so quickly, my son?" Isaac's practical suspicion: Esau's hunting would take hours; Jacob has returned too fast for game-hunting. כִּי הִקְרָה יְהוָה אֱלֹהֶיךָ לְפָנַי (kî hiqrāh YHWH ʾĕlōhêḵā lĕpānay) — "because the LORD your God caused it to happen before me." Hiqrāh is the Hiphil of qārāh (to meet, happen, encounter) — the identical verb the servant used in 24:12 when he prayed for genuine divine providential arrangement. Jacob attributes to YHWH a false providential success: the very vocabulary of honest prayer in ch. 24 becomes the vocabulary of lying in ch. 27. Jacob invokes YHWH's name in his deception — the theological weight is precise and disturbing.
27.21: גְּשָׁה-נָּא וַאֲמֻשְׁךָ בְּנִי (gĕšāh-nnāʾ waʾămušĕḵā bĕnî) — "come near so I may feel/touch you, my son." Māšaš (to feel, grope, touch for verification) — the diagnostic touch; Isaac substitutes his remaining functional senses (touch, smell, v. 27; hearing, v. 22) for his lost sight. הַאַתָּה זֶה בְּנִי עֵשָׂו אִם-לֹא (whether you really are my son Esau or not) — Isaac's direct skepticism is expressed; the syntax is a bipartite question with ʾim-lōʾ (or not): he is not fully persuaded and needs sensory confirmation. The touch-test is about to fail him.
27.22: הַקֹּל קוֹל יַעֲקֹב וְהַיָּדַיִם יְדֵי עֵשָׂו (haqqōl qôl yaʿăqōḇ wĕhayyāḏayim yĕḏê ʿēśāw) — "the voice is the voice of Jacob, but the hands are the hands of Esau." This is one of the most structurally elegant lines in Genesis — a perfect chiasm built on the perceptual conflict: voice (hearing) identifies Jacob; hands (touch) falsely identify Esau. The chiastic structure (voice-Jacob // hands-Esau) mirrors the conflict between two senses giving contradictory evidence. Rabbinical tradition reads this line as the paradigmatic statement of Jewish history: Jacob's voice (Torah) may dominate, but when Jacob's hands become like Esau's (violence), Esau prevails.
27.23: וְלֹא הִכִּירוֹ כִּי-הָיוּ יָדָיו כִּידֵי עֵשָׂו אָחִיו שְׂעִרֹת — "he did not recognize him because his hands were hairy like the hands of his brother Esau." Hikkîr (Hiphil of nākar, to recognize, identify, distinguish) — the failure of recognition is stated flatly. Isaac cannot nākar (recognize) his own son. The irony: the root nākar will be used when Joseph's brothers fail to recognize him in Egypt (42:8: "Joseph recognized his brothers, but they did not recognize him"). Failure to recognize by sight, by touch — recognition is the chapter's negative subplot. Wayyĕḇārăḵēhû (and he blessed him) — the blessing is given in genuine but deceived intention.
27.24: הַאַתָּה זֶה בְּנִי עֵשָׂו (haʾattāh zeh bĕnî ʿēśāw) — "are you really my son Esau?" A second direct question — Isaac's doubt persists even after touching; he must ask again. ʾĂnî (I am) — Jacob's response is the shortest possible affirmation; one word, unembellished, without the rhetorical elaboration of v. 19. The simple "I am" (ʾănî) in answer to "are you Esau?" is technically true only if we read it as "I am [here, present, the one speaking to you]" — but the question admits only one reading in context. Jacob answers the question he was not asked.
27.25: Isaac eats and drinks — the ritual meal that forms the context for the covenantal blessing. Wayyāšet lô yayin wayyēšt (he brought him wine and he drank) — wine as part of the solemn meal; the blessing that follows is given in an elevated, formal state. The eating and drinking before blessing mirrors the covenant ratification meals of the OT (Exod 24:11: the elders "ate and drank" before YHWH at Sinai; the Last Supper covenant meal context).
27.26: גְּשָׁה-נָּא וּשְׁקָה-לִּי בְּנִי (gĕšāh-nnāʾ ûšĕqāh-llî bĕnî) — "come near and kiss me, my son." The kiss (nāšaq) is both an act of filial affection and the physical proximity that allows Isaac to smell the clothing — the final sense-check before the blessing. The sensory progression: touch (v. 21-23), hearing (v. 22), taste (v. 25), now smell (v. 27). Every sense is engaged in the blessing encounter; the deception has been engineered to deceive all four.
27.27: וַיָּרַח אֶת-רֵיחַ בְּגָדָיו (wayyārāḥ ʾet-rêaḥ bĕḡāḏāyw) — "he smelled the smell of his garments." Rûaḥ / rêaḥ (the smell/scent; from the same root as rûaḥ, spirit/breath — both are about essence perceived through the breath). Isaac's blessing begins with a sensory declaration: רֵאֵה רֵיחַ בְּנִי כְּרֵיחַ שָׂדֶה אֲשֶׁר בֵּרֲכוֹ יְהוָה (the smell of my son is like the smell of a field that YHWH has blessed) — the field (śādeh) is Esau's domain (25:27: "Esau was a man of the field"); Isaac associates the outdoor smell with Esau's identity and with YHWH's blessing on the created order. The blessing opens with a creation metaphor: the fertile, YHWH-blessed field.
27.28: וְיִתֶּן-לְךָ הָאֱלֹהִים מִטַּל הַשָּׁמַיִם וּמִשְׁמַנֵּי הָאָרֶץ — "may God give you the dew of heaven and the fatness of the earth." The divine name shifts: the blessing uses hāʾĔlōhîm (the God) rather than YHWH — the more universal divine title, appropriate for a general agricultural blessing. Ṭal (dew) is the life-giving moisture of Canaan's dry summer months; mišmannê (fatness/richness — Hiphil noun from šāman, to be fat, prosperous) = the productive richness of fertile soil. Wĕrōḇ dāḡān wĕtîrōš (abundance of grain and new wine) — the two agricultural staples of the settled land; this blessing is the blessing of possession and agricultural productivity.
27.29: יַעַבְדוּךָ עַמִּים וְיִשְׁתַּחֲווּ לְךָ לְאֻמִּים — "may peoples serve you and nations bow down to you." The blessing transitions from agricultural to political: from produce to dominion. הֱוֵה גְבִיר לְאַחֶיךָ (be lord/master over your brothers) — geḇîr (master, lord — male form of gĕḇirāh, queen-mother; the masculine form is rare and formal, used only here and in v. 37 and in Isa 26:13); the lordship of Jacob over Esau is the fulfillment of 25:23 ("the elder shall serve the younger"). אֹרְרֶיךָ אָרוּר וּמְבָרְכֶיךָ בָּרוּךְ — "cursed are those who curse you and blessed are those who bless you." The covenant formula of 12:3 (Abraham's call: "those who curse you I will curse, those who bless you I will bless") is now transmitted through Isaac to Jacob — the covenant blessing-and-curse mechanism is formally transferred to the next generation.
27.30: אַךְ יָצֹא יָצָא יַעֲקֹב (ʾaḵ yāṣōʾ yāṣāʾ yaʿăqōḇ) — "as soon as Jacob had gone out." The emphatic infinitive absolute yāṣōʾ yāṣāʾ (had just barely gone out, had scarcely departed) — the timing is dramatically precise: Jacob exits and Esau immediately enters; the near-miss is as close as possible. wĕʾēśāw ʾāḥîw bāʾ miṣṣêḏô (his brother Esau came in from his hunting) — the word ṣêḏ (hunting, game) — Esau brings genuine game from genuine hunting; the contrast with Jacob's domestic-goat substitute is implicit but present.
27.31: יָקֻם אָבִי וְיֹאכַל מִצֵּיד בְּנוֹ — "let my father rise and eat of his son's game." Esau uses the same invitation formula as Jacob in v. 19 — "rise and eat" (qûm ʾĕḵol) — and the same word ṣêḏ (game) — but now it is genuine: genuine son, genuine game. The parallelism between Esau's speech and Jacob's earlier impersonation is a narrative device: when the real version appears, the deception that preceded it becomes more visible by comparison.
27.32: מִי-אַתָּה (mî-ʾattāh) — "who are you?" Isaac asks Esau the same question he asked Jacob in v. 18 (mî ʾattāh bĕnî). Esau answers truthfully: "I am your son, your firstborn, Esau." The word bĕḵōrĕḵā ʿēśāw (your firstborn Esau) repeats exactly the false claim Jacob made in v. 19 — heard now after the deception is complete, it registers as the truth that was displaced. Isaac must confront: the person who said these words before was lying; the person saying them now is telling the truth, and the blessing is gone.
27.33: וַיֶּחֱרַד יִצְחָק חֲרָדָה גְּדֹלָה עַד-מְאֹד (wayyeḥĕraḏ yiṣḥāq ḥărāḏāh gĕḏōlāh ʿaḏ-mĕʾōḏ) — "and Isaac trembled a very great trembling." The emphatic infinitive absolute construction (ḥăraḏ ḥărāḏāh gĕḏōlāh ʿaḏ-mĕʾōḏ) — the strongest Hebrew grammar for intensity: he trembled a trembling great exceedingly. Ḥāraḏ (to tremble, shake — typically in the context of divine encounter, fear of judgment, or extreme alarm; cf. Exod 19:18 when Sinai trembled at YHWH's presence; 1 Sam 4:13 when the city trembled at the battle news). וְגַם-בָּרוּךְ יִהְיֶה (and moreover he shall be blessed) — Isaac's trembling is not only shock but theological recognition: the blessing given under deception is irrevocable; the word has gone out and cannot be recalled. The blessing has its own ontological weight — it is not merely Isaac's wish but a covenantal pronouncement that stands.
27.34: צְעָקָה גְּדֹלָה וּמָרָה עַד-מְאֹד (ṣĕʿāqāh gĕḏōlāh ûmārāh ʿaḏ-mĕʾōḏ) — "a great and very bitter cry." Ṣāʿaq (to cry out — often in contexts of distress seeking divine hearing: Exod 2:23; 14:10; Gen 18:20-21 for Sodom's cry) — Esau's cry is at the same decibel level as a community crying to God for relief. Mārāh (bitter) — the bitterness of the cry evokes Mārāh, the bitter water of Exod 15:23; the bitter wailing of Rachel for her children (Jer 31:15); bitterness as the taste of loss and injustice. Hebrews 12:17 will cite this moment: "he found no place of repentance, though he sought it with tears."
27.35: בָּא אָחִיךָ בְּמִרְמָה וַיִּקַּח בִּרְכָתֶךָ (bāʾ ʾāḥîḵā bĕmirmāh wayyiqqaḥ birḵāteḵā) — "your brother came with deceit and took your blessing." Mirmāh (deceit, treachery — from rāmāh, to deceive, beguile; used also for Jacob's deception in Hos 12:3 and the broader judgment oracle). Isaac names the act without ambiguity: mirmāh is moral language — deceit as a deliberate act of betrayal. Yet Isaac does not rescind the blessing; the word stands. The declarative sentence has a resigned quality: this is what happened, and it cannot be undone.
27.36: הֲכִי קָרָא שְׁמוֹ יַעֲקֹב וַיַּעְקְבֵנִי זֶה פַּעֲמַיִם (hăḵî qārāʾ šĕmô yaʿăqōḇ wayyaʿqĕḇēnî zeh paʿămāyim) — "is it not rightly that his name is Jacob? For he has supplanted me (yaʿqĕḇēnî) these two times." Esau's wordplay: yaʿqōḇ (Jacob, from ʿāqēḇ, heel — the grasping-heel motif of 25:26 when Jacob grabbed Esau's heel at birth) → yaʿqĕḇēnî (he has supplanted/deceived me — the denominative verb from the name, meaning "he has Jacob-ed me"). The accusation encodes the name's dual meaning: Jacob = heel-grabber = one who supplants by deceptive maneuvering. Hos 12:3 will use the same wordplay: "in the womb he grasped the heel (ʿāqaḇ) of his brother." ʾEt-bĕḵōrātî lāqaḥ (he took my birthright) — the birthright was sold (25:33) but Esau's "he took" rewrites the transaction as theft; selective memory is part of the brother's cry.
27.37: הֵן גְּבִיר שַׂמְתִּיו לָךְ (hēn gĕḇîr śamtîw lāḵ) — "behold, I have made him lord/master over you." Isaac states the blessing's content in summary: lordship (geḇîr), possession of all relatives, grain and wine (dāḡān wĕtîrōš) — everything of substance has been given to Jacob. וּלְךָ אֵפוֹ מָה אֶעֱשֶׂה בְּנִי (so what can I do for you, my son?) — ʾēpôʾ (then, so, what now) — the particle of resigned inquiry; Isaac acknowledges the emptiness of his position. There is no equivalent blessing remaining; the entire content of the deathbed blessing has been given away under deception.
27.38: הֲבְרָכָה אַחַת הִיא-לְךָ אָבִי (hăḇĕrāḵāh ʾaḥat hîʾ-lĕḵā ʾāḇî) — "have you only one blessing, my father?" Esau's question presses the theological premise: is the deathbed blessing a finite resource? The answer is yes in this case — the main blessing has been given. Esau's cry (wayiśśāʾ ʿēśāw qōlô wayyēḇk, he lifted up his voice and wept) uses the mourning formula; the weeping is the same expression as Hagar's and Ishmael's at the expulsion (21:16) and Joseph's when he reveals himself (45:2). The tears of loss in the patriarchal narratives are the narrative's emotional register for irrevocable events.
27.39: הִנֵּה מִשְׁמַנֵּי הָאָרֶץ יִהְיֶה מוֹשָׁבֶךָ — "behold, from the fatness of the earth will be your dwelling." The Esau oracle (vv. 39-40) is syntactically ambiguous: the preposition min before mišmannê (fatness) can mean "of" (sharing in the fatness) or "away from" (separated from the fatness). The LXX and most modern translations prefer "away from" — the dwelling is away from productive farmland, in the rocky terrain of Edom/Seir. Ûmiṭṭal haššāmayim mēʿāl (and away from the dew of heaven above) — the negation of Jacob's blessing: where Jacob receives the agricultural bounty, Esau receives its absence. The oracle is not a curse but a realistic geographic forecast for Edomite settlement territory.
27.40: וְעַל-חַרְבְּךָ תִחְיֶה (wĕʿal-ḥarbĕḵā tiḥyeh) — "by your sword you will live." Esau's oracle shifts from agriculture (absent) to warfare (present): the sword (ḥereḇ) is Esau's means of subsistence where Jacob has grain and wine. וְהָיָה כַּאֲשֶׁר תָּרִיד (wĕhāyāh kaʾăšer tārîḏ) — "but when you grow restless" — tārîḏ (Hiphil of rûḏ, to rove, wander, have unrestricted movement; in the Hiphil: to achieve dominance, break free) — when Edom becomes strong enough, the yoke will be thrown off. The oracle is both realistic (Edom will be subdued by David, 2 Sam 8:14) and conditional-reversible (Edomite revolts in 2 Kgs 8:20-22 partially fulfill the "throw off the yoke" clause).
27.41: וַיִּשְׂטֹם עֵשָׂו אֶת-יַעֲקֹב (wayyiśṭōm ʿēśāw ʾet-yaʿăqōḇ) — "and Esau bore a grudge against Jacob." Śāṭam (to hate with hostility, bear a grudge, persecute — a stronger word than ordinary śānēʾ, hate; it implies sustained, active, hostile resentment; cf. 49:23 and 50:15 where the same root describes the hostility Joseph's brothers feared). יִמְרְאוּ יְמֵי אֵבֶל אָבִי (the days of mourning for my father are near) — Esau's plan is timed: after Isaac's death (the mourning days), he will kill Jacob.
