The plan shows both family loyalty (waiting for the mourning period) and murderous intent — the combination recalls Cain (4:5-8) in its internal logic.
27.42: וַיֻּגַּד לְרִבְקָה אֶת-דִּבְרֵי עֵשָׂו בְּנָהּ הַגָּדֹל — "Rebekah was told the words of her older son Esau." The passive wayyuggad (it was reported/told) — someone informed Rebekah; the narrative does not say who; the information network of the household is assumed. תְּנַחֵם לְךָ לְהָרְגֶךָ (tĕnaḥem lĕḵā lĕhorgĕḵā) — "he is consoling himself by planning to kill you." Nāḥam (to comfort, console, regret) in the reflexive: Esau is comforting himself (lĕḵā, reflexive dative) by planning revenge. The irony: nāḥam (comfort) and murder planning are combined — Esau's grief-processing is murderous. The same verb will be used positively when Isaac is comforted by Rebekah in 24:67.
27.43: בְּרַח-לְךָ אֶל-לָבָן אָחִי חָרָנָה (bĕraḥ-lĕḵā ʾel-lāḇān ʾāḥî ḥārānāh) — "flee to Laban my brother in Haran." Bāraḥ (to flee, run away) — the imperative of urgent escape; the same verb used for the flight of Moses from Egypt (Exod 2:15) and the flight of David from Absalom. Jacob must flee because Esau is planning murder. Rebekah directs Jacob to her own brother Laban in Haran — the same place from which she came (24:10); Jacob's flight will put him in the household of his mother's family, fulfilling the narrative thread begun with the servant's mission in ch. 24.
27.44: יָמִים אֲחָדִים (yāmîm ʾăḥāḏîm, a few days) — Rebekah anticipates that the separation will be brief; the same phrase is used in 29:20 where Jacob's seven years of labor for Rachel "seemed like only a few days" because of his love. The irony: Rebekah's "few days" of Jacob's absence becomes approximately twenty years. She never sees him again after his departure. The words "few days" are the narrative's saddest understatement in the patriarchal section.
27.45: לָמָה אֶשְׁכַּל גַּם-שְׁנֵיכֶם יוֹם אֶחָד (lāmmāh ʾeškkal gam-šĕnêḵem yôm ʾeḥāḏ) — "why should I be bereaved of both of you in one day?" Šāḵal (to be bereaved of children — the word for child-loss, used for Rachel weeping for her children in Jer 31:15; for the bereaving of Egypt in Exod 12:29; for the Servant bearing the grief of the bereaved in Isa 53:10). Rebekah fears a double bereavement: if Esau kills Jacob, the blood-guilt means Esau faces execution ("blood for blood") — she would lose both sons in one day. Wayyĕšallĕḥēhû — the send-verb that runs through the patriarchal narratives: Rebekah now sends Jacob just as Abraham's servant was sent (24:7).
27.46: קַצְתִּי בְחַיַּי מִפְּנֵי בְּנוֹת חֵת (qaṣtî ḇĕḥayyay mipĕnê ḇĕnôt ḥēt) — "I am disgusted with my life because of the Hittite women." Qûṣ (to be disgusted, loathe — used for Israel's disgust with manna in Num 21:5; for Rebekah's own disgust with Esau's marriages). Rebekah's complaint to Isaac about the Hittite wives (cf. 26:34-35: Esau's marriages to Judith and Basemath, Hittite women, which "made life bitter for Isaac and Rebekah") is the pretext she uses to send Jacob away without revealing the real reason (Esau's murder threat). The ruse works: Isaac is persuaded to formally send Jacob to Paddan-Aram for a wife, which gives Jacob's flight the appearance of a legitimate mission rather than an escape.

28.1: וַיִּקְרָא יִצְחָק אֶל-יַעֲקֹב וַיְבָרֶךְ אֹתוֹ — "Isaac called Jacob and blessed him." Despite the deception of ch. 27, Isaac now willingly and knowingly blesses Jacob — the second deliberate blessing from Isaac to Jacob, contrasting with the first (given unwittingly under deception). The explicit endorse of Jacob's departure to Paddan-Aram and the command not to marry a Canaanite woman formally authorize the mission. Isaac's cooperation with God's plan (25:23: the elder will serve the younger) is now conscious, even if it was unconscious in ch. 27. לֹא-תִקַּח אִשָּׁה מִבְּנוֹת כְּנָעַן (do not take a wife from the daughters of Canaan) — the prohibition echoes Abraham's command to his servant in 24:3; the covenant endogamy principle is transmitted from Abraham to Isaac to Jacob as explicit instruction.
28.2: פַּדֶּנָה אֲרָם (paddennāh ʾărām, Paddan-Aram) — "Paddan-Aram" (= the plain of Aram or the field/road of Aram — paddan possibly from Aramaic paddānāʾ, field, or Hebrew pāḏāh-related; it is the region around Haran in northwestern Mesopotamia, synonymous with ʾĂram naharāyim of ch. 24). Jacob is being sent to the same region the servant traveled in ch. 24 — the Nahorite homeland where Rebekah came from. מִבְּנוֹת לָבָן אֲחִי אִמֶּךָ (from the daughters of Laban, your mother's brother) — the kinship connection is specific: Laban's daughters, not merely any Aramean woman. The narrative is already preparing for Rachel and Leah (ch. 29).
28.3: וְאֵל שַׁדַּי יְבָרֵךְ אֹתְךָ (wĕʾēl šadday yĕḇārēḵ ʾōtĕḵā) — "May El Shaddai bless you." ʾĒl Šadday (El Shaddai) — the divine name used by God in his initial covenant with Abraham (17:1: "I am El Shaddai; walk before me and be blameless"). The name appears here for the second time in Genesis (cf. 17:1; repeated at 35:11; 43:14; 48:3; and throughout Job). Its precise meaning is debated: (1) "God Almighty" (traditional rendering; LXX: παντοκράτωρ); (2) "God of the mountain/highland" (from šad, mountain — cognate to Akkadian šadû); (3) "God of the field/wilderness" (from šāḏeh). The connection to the covenant-making context (17:1) suggests a formal, solemn divine name for the multiplication-and-possession promise. Isaac invokes this name specifically for the multiplication blessing that follows.
28.4: וְיִתֶּן-לְךָ אֶת-בִּרְכַּת אַבְרָהָם (wĕyittĕn-lĕḵā ʾet-birkat ʾaḇrāhām) — "may he give you the blessing of Abraham." The explicit transmission: this is the blessing of Abraham — the covenant blessing of 12:2-3; 17:1-8; 22:17-18 — now formally declared as Jacob's inheritance. לְרִשְׁתְּךָ אֶת-אֶרֶץ מְגֻרֶיךָ (that you may take possession of the land of your sojournings) — gûr (to sojourn, dwell as a resident alien) — the land where the patriarchs have lived as sojourners (mĕgûrîm) will become the land of possession (môrāšāh). The sojourner's land becomes inheritance — the transformation that the covenant promises.
28.5: וַיִּשְׁלַח יִצְחָק אֶת-יַעֲקֹב (wayyišlaḥ yiṣḥāq ʾet-yaʿăqōḇ) — "Isaac sent Jacob." The send-verb (šālaḥ) again — now it is Isaac who formally sends Jacob, transforming the flight from Esau into an authorized patriarchal mission. Lāḵet paddennāh ʾărāmāh (to go to Paddan-Aram) — the destination is established with legal formality. The narrative adds the full genealogical identification of Laban: Bethuel's son, Rebekah's brother, Jacob and Esau's mother's brother — layering in all the family connections that make the destination the right one.
28.6: Esau observes Isaac's blessing of Jacob and the explicit prohibition against Canaanite wives. The verb wayyarʾ ʿēśāw (Esau saw) — perception leading to action, as in 3:6 (Eve saw the tree) and 6:2 (the sons of God saw the daughters of men). What Esau sees prompts a correction in his marriage strategy (vv. 8-9). The narrative gives Esau credit for trying to please his father: he takes note of what displeases Isaac and attempts to rectify it — though the rectification (marrying Ishmael's daughter) is too late and beside the point of the birthright/blessing crisis.
28.7: שָׁמַע יַעֲקֹב אֶל-אָבִיו וְאֶל-אִמּוֹ (šāmaʿ yaʿăqōḇ ʾel-ʾāḇîw wĕʾel-ʾimmô) — "Jacob obeyed his father and his mother." Šāmaʿ ʾel (to hear/obey toward) — the syntax of obedience; Jacob is observed by Esau as obedient to both parents. The irony is multilayered: Jacob obeyed Rebekah's plan in ch. 27 (which deceived Isaac) and is now obeying Isaac's command (which Rebekah arranged); both acts of obedience are described with the same verb. Jacob's relationship with authority is complex: he obeys but within a web of family deception.
28.8: כִּי רָעוֹת בְּנוֹת כְּנַעַן בְּעֵינֵי יִצְחָק אָבִיו (kî rāʿôt bĕnôt kĕnaʿan bĕʿênê yiṣḥāq ʾāḇîw) — "the daughters of Canaan were displeasing in the eyes of his father Isaac." Rāʿôt (evil, bad, displeasing — plural) + the idiom bĕʿênê (in the eyes of) = the standard formula of moral evaluation. Esau now understands what he apparently did not grasp when he took his Hittite wives (26:34-35): his father Isaac is opposed to Canaanite marriages on principle. The realization comes too late to undo his prior marriages; he can only add to them by marrying within a group Isaac might approve.
28.9: וַיֵּלֶךְ עֵשָׂו אֶל-יִשְׁמָעֵאל — "Esau went to Ishmael." The marriage to Mahalath, Ishmael's daughter, is Esau's attempt to correct his marriage record by adding a wife from Abraham's family line (though through the non-covenant branch). Maḥălat bat-yišmāʿēʾl ben-ʾaḇrāhām — the full identification establishes the Abrahamic connection Esau is leveraging. ʿAl-nāšāyw (in addition to his wives) — the addition of Mahalath does not remove the displeasing Hittite wives; Esau's correction is additive, not remedial. The gesture reveals Esau's sincere desire to please his father, even if inadequately executed.
28.10: וַיֵּצֵא יַעֲקֹב מִבְּאֵר שָׁבַע וַיֵּלֶךְ חָרָנָה (wayyēṣēʾ yaʿăqōḇ mibbĕʾēr šāḇaʿ wayyēleḵ ḥārānāh) — "Jacob went out from Beersheba and walked toward Haran." The departure formula: he goes out (yāṣāʾ) from the covenant settlement and walks (hālaḵ) toward the ancestral homeland. Bĕʾēr šāḇaʿ (Beersheba) — the site of Abraham's covenant with Abimelech (21:31) and the place where YHWH appeared to Isaac (26:24-25); the departure from Beersheba is departure from the established covenant geography. Jacob takes nothing with him — no caravan, no camels, no servants, no dowry gifts — in striking contrast with the servant's mission in ch. 24 (ten camels loaded with gifts).
28.11: וַיִּפְגַּע בַּמָּקוֹם וַיָּלֶן שָׁם (wayyipgaʿ bammāqôm wayyālen šām) — "he encountered the place and spent the night there." Pāgaʿ (to meet, encounter, fall upon — often of encounters with persons: meeting an enemy, or of intercession, encountering God; cf. Isa 53:12 where the Servant pāgaʿ — intercedes for transgressors). The verb suggests an unplanned encounter: Jacob stumbles upon "the place" (hāmāqôm, the definite article implies either a known sacred site or the narrative's emphasis on its significance). The stone under his head is simply a headrest for the night; the holiness of the location is unknown to him until the dream. Kî-bāʾ haššemeš (because the sun had set) — the nightfall is the occasion for the dream; darkness is the setting for divine revelation.
28.12: וַיַּחֲלֹם וְהִנֵּה סֻלָּם מֻצָּב אַרְצָה וְרֹאשׁוֹ מַגִּיעַ הַשָּׁמָיְמָה — "he dreamed, and behold, a stairway set upon the earth with its top reaching to the heavens." Sullām — a hapax legomenon (appears only here in the OT); related to the root sāḵal (to raise up a road/ramp) or possibly Akkadian simmiltu (staircase, ramp — the type of processional ramp built into Mesopotamian ziggurats for cultic processions). The "ladder" translation (KJV, following LXX κλίμαξ) implies a climbing device; the Hebrew likely indicates a broad ramp or stairway suitable for processional traffic. וּמַלְאֲכֵי אֱלֹהִים עֹלִים וְיֹרְדִים בּוֹ (and angels of God ascending and descending on it) — the direction order is significant: angels ascend first, then descend — they are already present on earth at the start of the vision, going up to report and coming down commissioned. John 1:51 cites this vision in the mouth of Jesus: "you will see heaven opened and the angels of God ascending and descending on the Son of Man."
28.13: אֲנִי יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵי אַבְרָהָם אָבִיךָ וֵאלֹהֵי יִצְחָק — "I am the LORD, the God of your father Abraham and the God of Isaac." The divine self-identification formula: YHWH names himself by covenant relationship — not "God of heaven and earth" (the universal address of 24:3, 7) but "God of Abraham and Isaac." This is Jacob's first direct encounter with YHWH; the God who spoke to Abraham at Haran (12:1), at Bethel (12:7-8), and elsewhere now speaks to Jacob at this same Bethel location. הָאָרֶץ אֲשֶׁר אַתָּה שֹׁכֵב עָלֶיהָ (the land on which you are lying) — the covenant promise is given in the most immediate terms: this very piece of ground under Jacob's sleeping body is part of the promised land.
28.14: וּפָרַצְתָּ יָמָּה וָקֵדְמָה וְצָפֹנָה וָנֶגְבָּה — "you will spread out to the west and east, north and south." Pāraṣ (to break out, burst through, spread rapidly — the word for the violent breaking down of a barrier; cf. Exod 19:22: "lest the LORD break out against them"; 2 Sam 5:20: David "broke out against his enemies like a bursting flood"). The four directions of spreading imply the promise is for total territorial dominion — the offspring will break through every boundary. וְנִבְרְכוּ בְךָ כָּל-מִשְׁפְּחֹת הָאֲדָמָה (and all the families/clans of the earth will be blessed through you) — the universal blessing formula of 12:3 (Niphal passive: they will be blessed through you) is here given to Jacob, completing the transmission: Abraham → Isaac → Jacob receives the universal blessing-promise.
28.15: וְהִנֵּה אָנֹכִי עִמָּךְ (wĕhinnēh ʾānōḵî ʿimmāḵ) — "and behold, I am with you." The divine promise of presence — ʿimmĕḵā (with you) — the core assurance of the covenant. This formula will become the structural backbone of covenant faithfulness throughout the OT: YHWH with Moses (Exod 3:12), YHWH with Joshua (Josh 1:5, 9), YHWH with Gideon (Judg 6:12-16), YHWH with Jeremiah (Jer 1:8, 19). וּשְׁמַרְתִּיךָ בְּכֹל אֲשֶׁר-תֵּלֵךְ (and I will guard/keep you in all that you go) — šāmar (to keep, guard, preserve) — the same word used in the Aaronic blessing (Num 6:24: "may YHWH bless you and keep you"). לֹא אֶעֱזָבְךָ (I will not abandon you) — ʿāzaḇ (to abandon, forsake) — the negative formulation of covenant fidelity: YHWH will not do what Esau fears and what Jacob's own experience of being driven from home might suggest. Cited in Heb 13:5.
28.16: אָכֵן יֵשׁ יְהוָה בַּמָּקוֹם הַזֶּה וְאָנֹכִי לֹא יָדָעְתִּי (ʾāḵēn yēš YHWH bammāqôm hazzeh wĕʾānōḵî lōʾ yāḏāʿtî) — "surely YHWH is in this place, and I did not know it." ʾĀḵēn (surely, truly — an affirmative asseveration; cf. Job 1:10: "has not YHWH put a hedge around him?"; the word implies: despite contrary appearances, here is the truth). Jacob's theological discovery: the ordinary stopping place, chosen because the sun set and he was tired, is a place of divine presence. The OT theology of divine omnipresence emerges from these chance encounters: YHWH is not confined to the altar at Beersheba or the covenant location at Hebron — he meets Jacob in a field, at night, with a stone for a pillow.
28.17: מַה-נֹּורָא הַמָּקוֹם הַזֶּה (mah-nôrāʾ hāmāqôm hazzeh) — "how awesome/fear-inducing is this place!" Nôrāʾ — Niphal participle of yārēʾ (to fear/be afraid): "that which causes fear, that which is awe-inspiring." The same word is used for YHWH's deeds at the exodus (Exod 34:10) and in the worship vocabulary of the Psalms (Pss 65:5; 66:3, 5; 68:35; 96:4). Jacob's fear is not terror but the recognition of holiness — the appropriate response to unexpected divine encounter. בֵּית אֱלֹהִים (bêt ʾĔlōhîm, house of God) — the definition of what this place is: a bêt ʾĔlōhîm, anticipating the name Bethel. וְזֶה שַׁעַר הַשָּׁמָיִם (and this is the gate of heaven) — šaʿar (gate) — the civic/legal significance of a gate transferred to cosmic geography: where the sullām touches earth, the gate of heaven stands. This is the point of access between the human and the divine; the vertical axis of the universe passes here.
28.18: וַיַּשְׁכֵּם יַעֲקֹב בַּבֹּקֶר (wayyaškem yaʿăqōḇ babbōqer) — "Jacob rose early in the morning." Hiškîm (to rise early, the dawn-rising) — the same word as Abraham's early rising for the Akedah (22:3) and the servant's early rising in Gerar (20:8). Early rising marks urgency and responsiveness to the previous night's divine encounter. וַיַּצֵּב אֹתָהּ מַצֵּבָה (wayyaṣṣēḇ ʾōtāh maṣṣēḇāh) — "he set it up as a standing stone/pillar." Maṣṣēḇāh (standing stone, commemorative pillar — from nāṣaḇ, to stand firm; cf. 31:13, 45, 51-52 where Jacob and Laban also erect pillars) — a cultic memorial object marking a theophanic encounter. The later law (Deut 16:22: "you shall not erect a maṣṣēḇāh, for the LORD your God hates it") reflects a later prohibition against such standing stones when they had become associated with Canaanite fertility cult; here the practice is presented without condemnation as an appropriate memorial to theophany. Wayyiṣṣōq šemen ʿal-rōšāh (he poured oil on its top) — the anointing of the stone consecrates it as a sacred object and marker.
28.19: וַיִּקְרָא אֶת-שֵׁם-הַמָּקוֹם הַהוּא בֵּית-אֵל — "he called the name of that place Beth-El." Bêt-ʾēl = "house of God" (bêt = house + ʾēl = God) — the name directly expresses Jacob's theological experience: this is where he encountered God's house (v. 17). Wĕʾûlām lûz šem-hāʿîr lāriʾšōnāh (but the former name of the city was Luz) — the editorial note marks the transition: an ancient Canaanite city named Luz (possibly "almond tree" or related to Ugaritic lz, "to turn aside") receives a new Hebrew name encoding Israel's theological encounter. Bethel will become one of the most important sacred sites in Israel's history (cf. 35:6-15; Josh 8:9; Judg 20:18, 26; 1 Sam 7:16; 1 Kgs 12:28-29 where Jeroboam establishes the golden calves there — the site's significance is its downfall).
28.20: וַיִּדַּר יַעֲקֹב נֶדֶר לֵאמֹר (wayyiddār yaʿăqōḇ neḏer lēʾmōr) — "Jacob made a vow, saying." Nāḏar (to vow, make a conditional pledge) — the standard OT vow formula; neḏer (vow) is the noun. The OT vow is a conditional promise to God: "if you do X, I will do Y." This structure is the standard form of the pilgrimage vow (cf. Num 30; Deut 23:21-23; Judg 11:30-31 — Jephthah's tragic vow; 1 Sam 1:11 — Hannah's vow; Ps 22:25 — fulfilling vows in the assembly). The conditionality of Jacob's vow ("if God will be with me... then YHWH will be my God") should be read as a standard vow form, not as Jacob setting terms for his relationship with God. He is framing a pledge in the standard conditional idiom of vow-making.
28.21: וְשַׁבְתִּי בְשָׁלוֹם אֶל-בֵּית אָבִי (wĕšaḇtî ḇĕšālôm ʾel-bêt ʾāḇî) — "and I return in peace to my father's house." Šālôm (peace, wholeness, safety) — the condition of the vow: safe return. וְהָיָה יְהוָה לִי לֵאלֹהִים (then YHWH will be my God) — the vow's pledge: covenantal loyalty to YHWH in return for divine protection and provision. The relationship Jacob is pledging is not merely acknowledgment of YHWH's existence but a covenantal claim: "YHWH will be lî (to me, for me) as God." The lî (for me, mine) carries the covenantal intimacy of "my God" (Exod 15:2: "this is my God and I will praise him").
28.22: וְהָאֶבֶן הַזֹּאת אֲשֶׁר-שַׂמְתִּי מַצֵּבָה יִהְיֶה בֵּית אֱלֹהִים — "and this stone that I have set up as a pillar will be God's house." The pillar-stone becomes the material anchor of Bethel's sacred geography: what was just a random stone becomes the marker of "God's house." The vow transforms a provisional camping stone into a permanent theological monument. וְכֹל אֲשֶׁר תִּתֶּן-לִי עַשֵּׂר אֲעַשְּׂרֶנּוּ לָךְ (wĕḵōl ʾăšer tittĕn-lî ʿaśśēr ʾăʿaśśĕrennû lāḵ) — "and of all you give me I will surely tithe a tenth to you." The emphatic Piel infinitive absolute ʿaśśēr ʾăʿaśśĕrennû (I will surely tithe a tithe) — the pattern of absolute dedication: 10% of all divine provision returns to God. This is the first explicit tithe vow in the OT (Abraham's tithe in 14:20 was a voluntary post-victory gift; this is a forward-looking covenant pledge). The tithe at Bethel establishes the pattern that will be codified in Lev 27:30-33; Num 18:21-32; Deut 14:22-29; Mal 3:10.

29.1: wayyiśśāʾ yaʿăqōḇ raglāyw — "Jacob lifted his feet." The MKT renders this "continued on his journey," but the literal is more vivid: nāśāʾ raglāyw (to lift one's feet) is an idiom for setting out with energy and purpose — the feet are lifted rather than dragged. The phrase captures Jacob's renewed momentum after the Bethel theophany (28:10-22); he departs with the covenant promise fresh in his ears. The expression appears only here and in Ezek 1:7 (for the living creatures' straight legs) — it is unusual enough to be marked as a deliberate stylistic choice by the narrator.
29.2: wayyarʾ wĕhinnēh bĕʾēr baśśāḏeh — "And he saw — and behold — a well in the field." The narrative well-meeting is a recognized type-scene in biblical literature: a young man travels to a foreign land, meets a young woman at a well, a betrothal follows (cf. Abraham's servant and Rebekah, 24:11-27; Moses and Zipporah, Exod 2:15-21). The exclamatory hinnēh (behold!) marks the moment of recognition — the well is the narrative signal that a betrothal-meeting is about to occur. The large stone (ʾeḇen gĕḏōlāh) over the well's mouth is the obstacle that will display Jacob's strength and eagerness in v10.
29.3: wĕnĕʾespû-šāmmāh kol-hāʿĕḏārîm wĕgālălû ʾet-hāʾeḇen — "When all the flocks were gathered, they would roll the stone." The communal requirement — all flocks assembled before the stone is moved — establishes a social norm that Jacob will violate in v10 (rolling the stone alone, before all flocks arrive). The Qal perfect verbs here describe habitual action (wĕnĕʾespû... wĕgālălû): what was customarily done. The stone's communal guarding of the water reflects a water-rights culture in which shared wells had strict rules of access — one group could not monopolize the water at the expense of others.
29.4: ʾaḥay mēʾayin ʾattem — "My brothers, where are you from?" Jacob's address ʾaḥay (my brothers) is the conventional greeting to strangers of one's approximate social standing in the biblical world — not claiming literal kinship but establishing social solidarity. The inquiry about origin (mēʾayin ʾattem) is the standard opening of a self-identification dialogue — the same pattern in Ruth 2:5 (Boaz's inquiry about Ruth) and 1 Sam 30:13 (David's inquiry of the Egyptian servant). The dialogue format (question / answer / question / answer) follows the convention of biblical encounter narratives.
29.5: hăyĕḏaʿtem ʾet-lāḇān ben-nāḥôr — "Do you know Laban, son of Nahor?" Jacob identifies Laban by grandfather rather than by father (Bethuel) — possibly because Nahor was more well-known as the founder of the clan, or because Bethuel's status in the narrative was reduced (he appears only briefly in 24:50). The verb yāḏaʿ (to know) in this inquiry is social knowledge — do you recognize/know who he is? The affirmative answer (yāḏāʿnû, "we know him") uses the same verb, confirming that they have direct acquaintance with him.
29.6: hăšālôm lô — "Is he well?" The idiom šālôm lô (is it peace/wholeness to him?) is the standard Hebrew greeting inquiry — not a casual "how is he" but a covenantal inquiry about the total wellbeing of a person. Šālôm (H7965) encompasses physical health, social harmony, relational wholeness. The shepherds' answer (šālôm — "peace/well") completes the formulaic exchange. The additional information — "here comes his daughter Rachel with the sheep" — arrives as confirmation while Jacob is still processing the positive answer; the narrative timing creates suspense.
29.7: lōʾ-ʿēt hēʾāsep hammiqneh — "It is not time for the livestock to be gathered." Jacob's instruction to the shepherds (water the sheep and take them back to pasture) is either a desire for privacy with Rachel, or a reflection of his knowledge of pastoral customs that the shepherds are flouting. The observation about the sun still being high (gāḏôl hayyôm, the day is great/big) uses the same adjective (gāḏôl) as the greatness of the stone in vv2-3 — the great stone, the great day — both obstacles to the encounter Jacob wants.
29.8: lōʾ nûḵal ʿaḏ ʾăšer yēʾāsĕpû kol-hāʿĕḏārîm — "We cannot, until all the flocks are gathered." The shepherds' response appeals to the communal water-rights custom of v3 — the stone cannot be moved by less than the full assembly. The social constraint creates dramatic irony: Jacob is about to violate the very rule they cite by rolling the stone alone. Their "we cannot" (lōʾ nûḵal) is answered by Jacob's unilateral action in v10 — what the community could not do alone, Jacob does for love.
29.9: wĕrāḥēl bāʾāh ʿim-haṣṣōʾn ʾăšer lĕʾāḇîhā kî rōʿāh hîʾ — "Rachel came with her father's flock, for she was their shepherdess." The word rōʿāh (shepherdess, Qal feminine participle of rāʿāh) is unusual — women shepherds appear in biblical narrative at significant moments (Moses's future wife Zipporah is a shepherdess in Exod 2:16-17; the Song of Solomon's beloved is associated with pastoral imagery). The feminine participle here marks Rachel's role as active keeper of the flock — she is tending, not merely accompanying. The temporal phrase "while he was still talking with them" compresses the timing so that Rachel's arrival is simultaneous with the failed instruction to the shepherds.
29.10: wayyāgar yaʿăqōḇ wayyāgel ʾet-hāʾeḇen — "Jacob went over and rolled the stone." The verse breaks the communal rule established in vv3 and 8: Jacob rolls the stone alone, immediately, without waiting for all flocks to gather. The verb gālal (H1556, to roll, to roll away) will recur at the naming of Galeed (31:47-48) and Galilee's association with rolling/revealing. The physical act of single-handedly moving what normally required the assembled community is a display of extraordinary strength driven by emotional urgency. Notably, Jacob does this for his uncle's sheep, not his own — the act is an unrequested gift of labor, foreshadowing the fourteen years of service he will give to Laban.
29.11: wayyiššaq yaʿăqōḇ lĕrāḥēl wayyiśśāʾ ʾet-qōlô wayyēḇk — "Jacob kissed Rachel and raised his voice and wept." The kiss (nāšaq, H5401, to kiss; also used for battle-joining and equipment-touching — a contact verb) and the weeping are simultaneous. Wayyiśśāʾ ʾet-qōlô — "he raised his voice" — is the standard idiom for crying aloud (cf. 21:16 for Hagar's weeping; 27:38 for Esau's cry). Jacob's tears are explained in v12 — it is the recognition of finding his family after the flight from home. The emotional rawness of the moment is preserved without editorializing — the narrator does not explain the tears; the reader fills in the isolation, fear, and relief that generated them.
29.12: wayyaggēḏ lĕrāḥēl kî ʾăḥî ʾāḇîhā hûʾ wĕḵî ben-riqāh hûʾ — "He told Rachel that he was a kinsman of her father and a son of Rebekah." The word ʾāḥ (brother/kinsman, H251) is used broadly in Hebrew for both siblings and extended kin — Jacob is actually a nephew of Laban, not a brother. The term ʾăḥî ʾāḇîhā ("kinsman of her father") is the narrator's gloss of what Jacob said; the fuller kinship identifier "son of Rebekah" (Laban's sister) establishes the genealogical connection that will ground the subsequent labor-covenant.
29.13: wayyārāṣ liqrāʾtô wayyĕḥabbēq-lô wayyĕnaššeq-lô — "He ran to meet him, and embraced him, and kissed him." Laban's greeting uses the same three verbs — run (rûṣ), embrace (ḥābaq), kiss (nāšaq) — that will appear in the father's reception of the prodigal son in Luke 15:20 (and in Abraham's servant's reception in 24:29-31, another Laban episode). The warm reception may reflect genuine hospitality or, as the narrative will reveal, Laban's recognition of a useful kinsman. Wayyĕsappēr lĕlāḇān ʾēt kol-haddĕḇārîm hāʾēlleh — "Jacob told him all these things" — the contents of the telling are not specified; the reader is left to imagine the account of the deception, flight, and Bethel theophany that constitute Jacob's recent history.
29.14: ʾaḵ ʿaṣmî ûḇĕśārî ʾattāh — "Surely you are my bone and my flesh." Laban's kinship declaration uses the same formula Adam used for Eve in 2:23 (ʿeṣem mēʿăṣāmay ûḇāśār mibĕśārî, "bone of my bones and flesh of my flesh") — the formula of belonging-by-substance. In 2:23 the formula establishes marriage; here it establishes covenant kinship. The words will acquire ironic weight in the subsequent narrative: the one who claims Jacob as bone and flesh will exploit him for twenty years. Ḥōḏeš yāmîm — "a month of days" — the Hebrew idiom for a full month; the time-reckoning before the labor-covenant is proposed.
29.15: kî-ʾāḥî ʾattāh waʿăḇaḏtanî ḥinnām haggîḏāh-lî mah-maškoroteyḵā — "Just because you are my kinsman, should you work for me for nothing? Tell me what your wages should be." Laban's framing is ostensibly generous: kinship should not mean free labor. But the question shifts the relationship from household-kinship (where Jacob might legitimately expect hospitality) to labor-contract. The word māśkōret (H4909, wages, remuneration from śāḵar = to hire) introduces the commercial register that will dominate the Jacob-Laban relationship. Laban's opening move to set wages — when Jacob has not asked for pay — reveals his calculative nature: he wants to fix the terms before Jacob's expectations grow.
29.16: ûlĕlāḇān šĕtê ḇānôt šēm hāgĕḏōlāh lēʾāh wĕšēm haqqĕṭannāh rāḥēl — "Laban had two daughters; the name of the elder was Leah, and the name of the younger was Rachel." The narrator introduces both daughters at this point to set up the deception of v23. The age-ordering — elder (gĕḏōlāh) Leah, younger (qĕṭannāh) Rachel — echoes the elder-younger tension that runs throughout Genesis: Cain/Abel, Ishmael/Isaac, Esau/Jacob. The names: Lēʾāh may derive from an Akkadian cognate for "wild cow" or a Hebrew word for "weary"; Rāḥēl = "ewe" (the young female sheep) — both names are pastoral, fitting the shepherd family.
29.17: wĕʿênê lēʾāh rakkôt wĕrāḥēl hāyĕtāh yĕpat-tōʾar wîpat marʾeh — "Leah's eyes were soft/weak, but Rachel was beautiful in form and beautiful in appearance." The description of Leah's eyes is the most-debated phrase in this chapter: rakkôt (H7390, tender, soft, weak) applied to eyes can mean: (a) tender/lovely — eyes with a gentle, pleasing quality (a compliment in some ANE traditions); (b) weak/dull — eyes lacking the luster that ancient beauty standards prized. Most modern translators choose the negative reading because it contrasts with Rachel's beauty. The double beauty formula for Rachel — yĕpat-tōʾar (beautiful of form/shape) and yĕpat marʾeh (beautiful of appearance/face) — is the superlative double used elsewhere only for Joseph (39:6, yĕpeh-tōʾar wîpeh marʾeh) and Esther (2:7). Rachel and Joseph share the same beauty formula, another connection between mother and son.
29.18: wayyeʾĕhaḇ yaʿăqōḇ ʾet-rāḥēl wayyōʾmer ʾeʿĕḇāḏĕḵā šeḇaʿ šānîm bĕrāḥēl bittĕḵā haqqĕṭannāh — "Jacob loved Rachel and said, 'I will serve you seven years for Rachel your younger daughter.'" The verb ʾāhaḇ (to love) is stated before any marriage discussion — love precedes and grounds the labor-contract. Jacob's offer is extraordinarily high: the standard bride-price (môhar) was thirty shekels of silver (Deut 22:29); seven years of labor at a typical daily wage was worth far more. The specificity "your younger daughter" (bittĕḵā haqqĕṭannāh) is Jacob's legally precise identification — naming Rachel by birth-order specifically so there is no ambiguity. Laban's subsequent deception will exploit exactly this contractual specificity.
29.19: ṭôḇ tittî ʾōtāh lĕḵā mimmittî ʾōtāh lĕʾîš ʾaḥēr šĕḇāh ʿimmādî — "It is better that I give her to you than to another man. Stay with me." Laban's acceptance is conditional only that Jacob stay — he does not explicitly promise to give Rachel, a legally significant evasion. He says tittî ʾōtāh lĕḵā (I will give her to you) — apparently confirming the covenant — but the lack of explicit naming of which daughter will later be exploited. The parallel between Laban's deception of Jacob and Jacob's deception of Isaac is structural: both involve a covered identity, a darkened or confused setting, and the wrong person receiving what was promised to another.
29.20: wayyihyû bĕʿênāyw kĕyāmîm ʾăḥāḏîm bĕʾahaḇātô ʾōtāh — "They were in his eyes like a few days, in his love for her." The phrase kĕyāmîm ʾăḥāḏîm (like a few days, like individual days) is psychologically acute: prolonged time subjectively compressed by intense love. The preposition bĕʾahaḇātô (in his loving of her) — the Qal infinitive construct — makes the love the instrument of the temporal distortion. Seven years of labor became experientially negligible because of love's transformation of experience. This is one of the most celebrated compressed love-statements in world literature — the understatement is the point.
29.21: hāḇāh ʾet-ʾišĕtî kî mālĕʾû yāmāy wĕʾāḇôʾāh ʾēleyhā — "Give me my wife, for my days are fulfilled, and I will go in to her." Jacob's demand uses the legal formula mālĕʾû yāmāy ("my days are full/completed") — the fulfillment of the contractual term. The verb mālaʾ (H4390, to fill, complete) signals legal completion. ʾĀḇôʾāh ʾēleyhā — "I will come to her" — the idiom for sexual consummation of marriage (cf. 6:4; 16:2; 38:8). Jacob's directness is not rude but legally assertive: the contract is complete; the transfer of the wife should now occur.
29.22: wayyeʾĕsōp lāḇān ʾet-kol-ʾanĕšê hammāqôm wayyaʿaś mišteh — "Laban gathered all the men of the place and made a feast." The wedding feast (mišteh, H4960, drinking feast, from šātāh, to drink) was the public covenant ratification of marriage in the ANE — analogous to the covenant meals of diplomatic agreements (26:30; 31:54). The communal gathering ("all the men of the place") establishes the public witness that makes the marriage legally valid. The feast is also the cover for Laban's deception: in the evening darkness, after the drinking, the wrong daughter is brought.
29.23: wayhî ḇāʿereḇ wayyiqqaḥ ʾet-lēʾāh bittô wayyāḇēʾ ʾōtāh ʾēlāyw — "In the evening he took his daughter Leah and brought her to him." The deception operates by darkness (ḇāʿereḇ, in the evening) — the same mechanism Jacob used against his blind father: concealed identity in a dark room. Jacob blinded his father with goat skins and a deceptive voice; Laban blinds Jacob with darkness and a veil. The verb wayyāḇēʾ (he brought) mirrors the language of bringing the bride (v23) but with the wrong subject — it is the father bringing, not the bride coming voluntarily. The irony is structural and explicit: the deceiver is deceived with the same method he used.
29.24: wayyittēn lāḇān lāh ʾet-zilpāh šipĕḥātô lĕlēʾāh bittô šipĕḥāh — "Laban gave his servant Zilpah to his daughter Leah as her servant." The parenthetical note introduces Zilpah, who will become the mother of Gad and Asher (30:9-13). The gift of a servant to a daughter upon marriage was standard ANE custom — the servant formed part of the bride's dowry. Bilhah will similarly be given to Rachel (v29). Both servants-turned-surrogate-mothers are introduced at their respective marriages; their role in the birth-narrative of ch 30 is thus legally prepared here.
29.25: wayhî ḇabbōqer wĕhinnēh-hîʾ lēʾāh wayyōʾmer ʾel-lāḇān mah-zōʾt ʿāśîtā llî — "In the morning — behold, it was Leah!" Jacob's discovery is marked by the same exclamatory hinnēh used at the well-meeting (v2) — but here the surprise is horror, not delight. Lāmmāh rimmîtānî — "why have you deceived me?" — the verb rāmāh (H7411, to throw/betray/deceive) is used here; in 27:35 Jacob is described using mirmāh (deceit, H4820, from the same root) to take Esau's blessing. The verbal link is the narrator's ironic commentary: Jacob, who obtained his father's blessing through deception (mirmāh), is now deceived by the same mechanism (rāmāh).
29.26: lōʾ-yēʿāśeh ḵēn bimmĕqômēnû lātet haṣṣĕʿîrāh lipnê habbĕḵîrāh — "It is not done this way in our place — to give the younger before the firstborn." Laban's appeal to custom (mišpāṭ implied in yēʿāśeh) as justification turns Jacob's own principle against him: Jacob displaced his older brother; now Laban invokes the elder-before-younger rule to justify the deception. The vocabulary of ṣĕʿîrāh (younger/lesser) and bĕḵîrāh (firstborn/elder) echoes the Esau-Jacob terminology throughout — bĕḵôr (H1060, firstborn) was what Jacob seized from Esau; now Laban uses the bĕḵôr principle to justify depriving Jacob of what he wanted.
29.27: mallēʾ šĕḇuaʿ zōʾt wĕnittĕnāh lĕḵā gam-ʾet-zōʾt — "Complete this week, and we will give you the other one also." Šāḇûaʿ (H7620, week, period of seven) — the bridal week was the seven-day celebration following a wedding (cf. Judg 14:12 — Samson's seven-day riddle period for the feast). Laban proposes to let Jacob complete the customary bridal week with Leah before receiving Rachel — requiring another seven years of labor in exchange. The double-seven structure (seven years + seven days bridal week + seven more years) saturates the Laban narrative with the "seven" of covenant and completeness, though Laban exploits these patterns for his own gain.
29.28: wayyaʿas yaʿăqōḇ kēn wayyĕmal šĕḇuaʿ zōʾt wayyittēn-lô ʾet-rāḥēl bittô lô lĕʾiššāh — "Jacob did so; he completed the week with her, and then Laban gave him Rachel his daughter as his wife." The narrative compression — the entire second seven-year period is covered in the first half of v30 — shows that the narrator's interest is in the structural outcome (Jacob's double marriage), not the laboring years. Jacob completes (millēʾ = fulfills, same root as v21's "my days are fulfilled") the bridal week — covenant fidelity even within the exploitative system. His compliance with Laban's terms under duress will generate the covenant grounds for his eventual departure in ch 31.
29.29: wayyittēn lāḇān lĕrāḥēl bittô ʾet-bilhāh šipĕḥātô lāh lĕšipĕḥāh — "Laban gave his servant Bilhah to Rachel his daughter as her servant." Bilhah's introduction parallels Zilpah's in v24 — both servants are gifts from Laban at the marriage of each daughter. The servant-gift is a dowry custom documented in ANE marriage contracts (the Nuzi tablets contain examples of fathers giving slave-girls to daughters upon marriage, with the stipulation that if the daughter is childless, the slave's children will count as the daughter's). This legal background explains Rachel's surrogate-motherhood proposal in 30:3: she is activating a legally recognized option, not improvising.
29.30: wayyeʾĕhaḇ gam-ʾet-rāḥēl mimmillēʾāh — "he loved Rachel more than Leah." The comparative construction gam-ʾet-rāḥēl mimmillēʾāh — literally "also Rachel more than Leah" — with min (from/more than) expressing comparison. The double love is stated (Jacob loved both), but the comparative love for Rachel is the narrative tension that drives ch 30. Wayyaʿăḇōḏ ʿimmô ʾôḏ šeḇaʿ-šānîm ʾăḥērôt — "he worked for him another seven years" — the second labor period is dispatched in a single clause. The fourteen-year total is implicit in the structure; Jacob's love is measured in labor.
29.31: wayyarʾ YHWH kî-śĕnûʾāh lēʾāh wayyipteaḥ ʾet-raḥmāh wĕrāḥēl ʿăqārāh — "When YHWH saw that Leah was hated/unloved, he opened her womb; but Rachel was barren." Śĕnûʾāh (Qal passive participle of śānēʾ, to hate) — the Hebrew term "hated" functions as a comparative idiom in biblical law and narrative: Deut 21:15-17 discusses the rights of the son of the "hated" wife (śĕnûʾāh), meaning the less-favored wife. The "hatred" is comparative, not absolute — Leah is loved less, not despised. Wayyipteaḥ ʾet-raḥmāh — "he opened her womb" — peṯaḥ (to open) applied to reḥem (H7358, womb). Notably, reḥem shares the root with raḥămîm (H7356, compassion, mercy) — God opens the womb in an act of compassionate attention to the unloved woman. YHWH acts where the husband does not love.
29.32: wayyiqrāʾ šĕmô rĕʾûḇen kî ʾāmĕrāh kî-rāʾāh YHWH bĕʿonyî kî ʿattāh yeʾĕhāḇanî ʾîšî — "She called his name Reuben, for she said, 'YHWH has seen my affliction; surely now my husband will love me.'" The name rĕʾûḇen encodes a wordplay: it sounds like rāʾāh-ben ("see, a son!") and also encodes rāʾāh bĕʿonyî ("YHWH has seen my affliction"). ʿOnî (H6040, affliction, humiliation, misery — from ʿānāh, to be bowed down/afflicted) is the word used for Israel's oppression in Egypt (Exod 3:7, 17: "I have seen the affliction of my people"). Leah's personal affliction anticipates the national affliction of her descendants. The hope "now my husband will love me" (yeʾĕhāḇanî ʾîšî) will be disappointed — the pattern of divine compassion compensating for human lovelessness continues through all four name-sayings in this chapter.
29.33: wayyiqrāʾ šĕmô šimʿôn kî-šāmaʿ YHWH kî-śĕnûʾāh ʾānōḵî wayyittēn-lî gam-ʾet-zeh — "She named him Simeon, for YHWH has heard that I am unloved, and he gave me this one also." Šimʿôn = "one who hears" — from šāmaʿ (H8085, to hear, to obey). The divine hearing is the covenant mechanism: YHWH hears the cry of the afflicted (Exod 3:7; Ps 22:24; 34:17). Leah's naming of her sons is a running theological commentary: YHWH sees (Reuben), YHWH hears (Simeon), YHWH will join (Levi), I will praise YHWH (Judah). The escalating intimacy of the names — from observed affliction to spontaneous praise — traces Leah's theological journey through the birth of four sons.
29.34: wayyiqrāʾ šĕmô lēwî kî ʾāmĕrāh ʿattāh yillāweh ʾîšî ʾēlay — "She named him Levi, for she said, 'Now my husband will be joined to me.'" Lēwî from lāwāh (H3867, to join, to accompany, to attach) — the name expresses Leah's hope for conjugal attachment. The Levites' name will carry this meaning forward: in Num 18:2-4, the Levites are "joined" (yillāwû) to Aaron in priestly service — the name of the tribe that becomes the priestly caste encodes the longing of the unbeloved mother to be joined to her husband. The irony deepens: Leah wanted to be joined to Jacob; her descendants will be joined to YHWH in the priesthood.
29.35: wayyiqrāʾ šĕmô yĕhûḏāh kî ʾāmĕrāh happaʿam ʾôḏeh ʾet-YHWH — "She named him Judah, for she said, 'This time I will praise YHWH.'" Yĕhûḏāh from yādāh (H3034, to throw/praise/give thanks, Hiphil: to praise) — the name means "praise/thanksgiving." In the arc of Leah's four birth-sayings, Judah's naming is the climax: she no longer asks for Jacob's love or God's intervention; she simply praises. The theological movement is from petition (see my affliction) through thanksgiving (God heard) through hope (will be joined) to pure doxology (I will praise). This shift — from petition to praise — is the pattern of the Psalms of lament and the shape of covenant prayer. Waṯṯaʿămōḏ milddet — "and she stopped bearing" — the narrative pause prepares for the crisis of ch 30.

30.1: hāḇāh-lî ḇānîm wĕʾim-ʾayin mētāh ʾānōḵî — "Give me children, or I will die!" Rachel's outcry frames barrenness as mortal threat. Mētāh ʾānōḵî ("I am dying/I will die") uses the Qal participle — ongoing state of dying rather than a sudden death threat. In the ANE, barrenness was social death: a woman without children had no standing in the household, no security in old age, no memorial after death. Rachel's "I will die" is existential, not merely dramatic. Qānnēʾ — "she was jealous/envied" (Piel of qānāʾ, H7065) — the same verb used for the Philistines' envy of Isaac (26:14) and for God's jealousy (ʾēl qannāʾ, Exod 20:5). The word is strong: active, consuming rivalry.
30.2: hătaḥat ʾĕlōhîm ʾānōḵî ʾăšer mānaʿ mimmēḵ pĕrî-ḇāṭen — "Am I in the place of God, who has withheld from you the fruit of the womb?" Jacob's rhetorical question is theologically precise: taḥat ʾĕlōhîm (in the place of God) — Jacob cannot grant what God alone controls. Mānaʿ (H4513, to withhold, restrain, deny) — the same verb as in 1 Sam 1:5-6 for Hannah's barrenness (YHWH had closed her womb). Pĕrî-ḇāṭen — "fruit of the womb" — pĕrî (H6529, fruit) applied to the womb uses agricultural language for human fertility (cf. Ps 127:3: "children are a heritage of YHWH, a fruit of the womb his reward"). Jacob's angry response is theologically correct but pastorally tone-deaf — he names the theological truth without the compassion the situation requires.
30.3: wĕtēled ʿal-birkkāy wĕʾibbāneh gam-ʾānōḵî mimmennāh — "and she will bear on my knees, and I too will be built up through her." The phrase yālaḏ ʿal-birkkāy ("bear on my knees") — a ritual of adoption in the ANE; the one on whose knees the child is placed legally acknowledges the child as their own (cf. 50:23 where Joseph's grandchildren are placed on his knees). Rachel's proposal activates the legal surrogate-mother practice documented in Nuzi tablets. ʾIbbāneh — "I will be built up/have a house" — the Niphal of bānāh (to build), the same root as bēn (son) — a wordplay: "I will be built/have sons" (bānāh/bēn). The social "building" of a household through children is the structural logic of Levirate marriage and surrogate motherhood in the Torah.
30.4: wayyāḇōʾ ʾēleyhā yaʿăqōḇ — "Jacob slept with her." The narrative compression is characteristic: the event that will generate Dan is stated in five Hebrew words. The narrator records the legal and genealogical outcomes without lingering on the personal dynamics. Bilhah's voicelessness throughout this chapter — she is given, slept with, conceives, bears — contrasts with the vocal theological commentary of Leah and Rachel in naming their children. The matriarchal naming-speeches dominate; the surrogate mothers are recorded by function, not by speech.
30.5: wattar ḇilhāh waṯṯēled lĕyaʿăqōḇ bēn — "Bilhah conceived and bore Jacob a son." The formulaic birth-record (hārāh... wayyēled) — "conceived and bore" — is the standard genealogical formula of Genesis. Each of the twelve sons follows a variation of this formula, linking the patriarchal family to the creation-genealogy pattern of chs 5 and 10. The use of Jacob's name (lĕyaʿăqōḇ) rather than "her husband" specifies the genealogical fact: the child belongs to Jacob, legally through the surrogate arrangement.
30.6: dānannî ʾĕlōhîm wĕgam šāmaʿ bĕqōlî wayyittēn-lî bēn ʿal-kēn qārĕʾāh šĕmô dān — "God has vindicated me — he has also heard my voice and given me a son. Therefore she named him Dan." Dānannî — "God has judged/vindicated me" — the Qal perfect of dîn (H1777, to judge, to argue a legal case, to vindicate). Dan's name encodes a legal verdict: God has ruled in Rachel's favor in the rivalry with Leah. The term dîn is used for judicial advocacy throughout the OT (Ps 9:4; 72:4; 82:3). Rachel frames her childlessness as a legal dispute and God's response as judicial vindication. The combination — God judged AND heard — links to the Simeon-naming pattern (YHWH heard Leah's affliction) and shows the two wives appealing to the same divine judicial-compassionate register.
30.7: wattar ʿôḏ waṯṯēled bilhāh šipĕḥat rāḥēl bēn šēnî lĕyaʿăqōḇ — "Rachel's servant Bilhah conceived again and bore Jacob a second son." The formulaic repetition — "again... bore a second son" — follows the same narrative economy as the Leah-birth sequence. Bilhah's two births are compressed; the theological significance is in Rachel's naming speech in v8, not in the births themselves. The count (šēnî, second) establishes the number of children the surrogate arrangement has produced before Rachel changes strategy.
30.8: naptûlê ʾĕlōhîm niptaltî ʿim-ʾăḥōtî gam-yāḵoltî wayyiqrāʾ šĕmô naptālî — "With God's wrestlings I have wrestled with my sister, and I have prevailed. So she named him Naphtali." The name naptālî from pātal (H6617, to twist, wrestle, be tortuous) — a rare verb suggesting both physical struggle and moral entanglement. The phrase naptûlê ʾĕlōhîm (wrestlings of God) uses the same root that will appear at Peniel (32:25,28 — though there the verb is ʾāḇaq, to wrestle). The "god-wrestlings" construction uses ʾĕlōhîm as an intensifier (a Hebraism for superlative degree: "tremendous/mighty wrestlings") — the same usage as "mountains of God" (Ps 36:7) for mighty mountains. Rachel's wrestling with her sister through proxy-births anticipates Jacob's wrestling with God — the same family pattern of striving expressed at the personal and cosmic levels.
30.9: waṯṯiqqaḥ ʾet-zilpāh šipĕḥātāh waṯṯittēn ʾōtāh lĕyaʿăqōḇ lĕʾiššāh — "Leah took her servant Zilpah and gave her to Jacob as a wife." Leah's response to her own cessation of bearing is to mirror Rachel's strategy — giving her servant Zilpah as a surrogate. The symmetry is structural: Rachel → Bilhah → Dan and Naphtali; Leah → Zilpah → Gad and Asher. Four sons are born to the two maidservants, paralleling the four sons already born to Leah directly. The twelve tribes emerge from this pattern of competition, surrogacy, and divine provision — a deeply human process that generates the covenant genealogy.
30.10: waṯṯēled zilpāh šipĕḥat lēʾāh lĕyaʿăqōḇ bēn — "Leah's servant Zilpah bore Jacob a son." The birth-formula is at its most compressed — no "conceived" (hārāh), just "bore." The formulaic economy accelerates as the birth-sequence multiplies. The twelve-tribe genealogy that will become Israel is generated in a narrative that is both economically told and humanly fraught — the tension between the personal pain of the mothers and the cosmic purpose of the genealogy is held together by the terse narrative style.
30.11: waṯṯōʾmer lēʾāh bāgāḏ wayyiqrāʾ ʾet-šĕmô gāḏ — "Leah said, 'Good fortune!' and she named him Gad." The exclamation bāgāḏ (or bā gāḏ — "fortune has come") uses gāḏ (H1409, fortune, luck). The Qere/Ketiv textual tradition has two readings: the written text has bāgāḏ (as a single word) while the marginal note reads bā gāḏ (fortune has come). The name Gad is associated with the Canaanite god of fortune in the ancient Near East — though Leah's statement does not invoke the deity but uses the common noun. Isa 65:11 condemns those who "set a table for Gad" — the deity of fortune — suggesting the Canaanite background is present in the name even when not invoked theologically.
30.12: waṯṯēled zilpāh šipĕḥat lēʾāh bēn šēnî lĕyaʿăqōḇ — "Leah's servant Zilpah bore Jacob a second son." The second birth through Zilpah is noted with the same compressed formula as the first. The narrative's brevity for the surrogate births contrasts with the extended naming speeches for Leah's direct births — the maidservants' children are genealogically registered but narratively backgrounded. The number "second" (šēnî) signals completeness of the Zilpah sequence: two through each maidservant is the pattern.
30.13: waṯṯōʾmer lēʾāh bĕʾošrî kî ʾišĕrûnî ḇānôt wayyiqrāʾ ʾet-šĕmô ʾāšēr — "Leah said, 'Happy am I! For women will call me happy.' So she named him Asher." ʾĀšēr from ʾeśer (H835, happiness, blessedness, the state of being fortunate) — used in the plural-of-excellence ʾašrê ("Blessed is the one who..."), the beatitude formula of the Psalms (Ps 1:1; 2:12; 32:1-2; etc.). The social dimension — "women will call me happy" (ʾišĕrûnî ḇānôt) — shows that Leah's sense of blessedness is validated by female community recognition; her social standing is being restored through sons. The Asher tribe will receive Moses's blessing: "Let Asher be blessed more than other sons... dip his foot in oil" (Deut 33:24).
30.14: wayyēlek rĕʾûḇēn bîmê qĕṣîr-ḥiṭṭîm wayyimṣāʾ dûḏāʾîm baśśāḏeh — "During the wheat harvest Reuben went and found mandrakes in the field." Dûḏāʾîm (H1736, mandrakes — Mandragora officinarum) — a plant with a forked root that resembles a human figure; the name may derive from dôḏ (H1730, beloved, uncle, love-token), connecting mandrakes to love and sexuality. Mandrakes were widely believed in the ANE and Mediterranean world to be aphrodisiacs and fertility stimulants — the Mishnah and Pliny both reference their use. The timing — wheat harvest — dates this to May/June. Reuben's discovery by his firstborn son is significant: the elder (who will lose his birthright) brings the fertility plant that will ironically not help Rachel (her children come from God, not mandrakes) but will help Leah conceive again.
30.15: halĕʾāṭ qāḥalt ʾet-ʾîšî wĕlāqaḥat gam ʾet-dûḏāʾê bĕnî — "Was it not enough that you took my husband? Will you also take my son's mandrakes?" Leah's accusation — "you took my husband" (qāḥalt ʾet-ʾîšî) — is the bitterest statement of the Rachel-Leah rivalry: Jacob's love for Rachel constitutes a form of husband-taking from Leah's perspective. The subsequent arrangement (Rachel trades Jacob's conjugal time for mandrakes) is darkly ironic: Rachel gets the reputed fertility plant but will only conceive through divine action (v22); Leah, who gives up the mandrakes, conceives immediately from the traded conjugal night (v17). The mandrakes are shown to be irrelevant — it is God who opens and closes wombs.
30.16: śāḵōr śĕḵartîḵā bĕdûḏāʾê bĕnî — "I have hired you with my son's mandrakes." The emphatic infinitive absolute śāḵōr śĕḵartîḵā (hiring I have hired you) — Leah addresses Jacob with the same assertiveness Rachel showed in v1 ("give me children"). The word śāḵōr/śāḵar (H7936/H7939, to hire, wages) is the commercial-contract term that dominates the Jacob-Laban narrative (29:15; 30:28,32-33; 31:7,41). Leah now applies it to her own marriage: she has "hired" her husband's conjugal attention with mandrakes. The irony of the commercial language applied to marriage is the narrator's sharp commentary on the state of the household.
30.17: wayyišmaʿ ʾĕlōhîm ʾel-lēʾāh waṯṯahar waṯṯēled lĕyaʿăqōḇ bēn ḥămîšî — "God heard Leah, and she conceived and bore Jacob a fifth son." The divine hearing (šāmaʿ ʾĕlōhîm) directly following the mandrake-for-conjugal-night exchange makes the theological point: God's hearing is what produces the conception, not the mandrakes Rachel has just obtained. The fifth son is the fifth direct birth Leah produces — she has now borne more children than any other woman in Jacob's household. God's consistent attention to the unloved wife is one of Genesis's sustained theological statements about divine preferential care for the vulnerable.
30.18: nātan ʾĕlōhîm śĕḵārî ʾăšer nātattî šipĕḥātî lĕʾîšî wayyiqrāʾ šĕmô yiśśāḵār — "God has given me my wages because I gave my servant to my husband. So she named him Issachar." Yiśśāḵār — the etymology here is folk: "there is reward/wages" (yēš śāḵār) or "man of wages" (ʾîš śāḵār). Leah interprets the birth as divine payment (śāḵar, H7939) for giving Zilpah as surrogate. The commercial vocabulary returns: the birth is śāḵār (wages), as if God has settled the labor account. The Issachar tribe receives a distinctive blessing in Gen 49:14-15 — a strong donkey crouching between two burdens, accepting conscript labor — which may echo the labor/wages etymology of the name.
30.19: waṯṯahar ʿôḏ lēʾāh waṯṯēled bēn-šiššî lĕyaʿăqōḇ — "Leah conceived again and bore Jacob a sixth son." The sixth son of Leah (including two through Zilpah) — the number "six" (šiššî) becomes part of Leah's naming speech in v20: "because I bore him six sons." The count matters: at six sons, Leah has generated more than half the tribal fathers of Israel through her own household (direct + surrogate). The sixth birth marks the completion of Leah's bearing sequence before the birth of Dinah (v21).
30.20: zĕḇālanî ʾĕlōhîm ʾōtî zeḇed ṭôḇ happaʿam yizĕḇĕlēnî ʾîšî kî-yālaḏtî lô šiššāh ḇānîm wayyiqrāʾ ʾet-šĕmô zĕḇûlûn — "God has endowed me with a precious endowment. This time my husband will honor/dwell with me." Zĕḇûlûn — from zāḇal (H2082, to dwell, to honor/exalt); the name plays on zeḇed (H2065, a gift/endowment) and zāḇal. Two meanings operate: (1) God has gifted me (zeḇed ṭôḇ, a good gift); (2) my husband will now honor/dwell with me (yizĕḇĕlēnî). Leah's hope for Jacob's honor/habitation — articulated at son #1 (Reuben: "now my husband will love me") and again at son #6 — shows that the desire for covenantal conjugal presence remains unfulfilled. The six sons have not secured what Leah ultimately wants.
30.21: wĕʾaḥar yālaḏāh bat waṯṯiqrāʾ ʾet-šĕmāh dînāh — "Afterwards she bore a daughter and named her Dinah." The one daughter named in the Jacob narrative — Dînāh, from dîn (to judge) — is introduced without birth-speech or etymology, in contrast to every son. The brevity of her introduction contrasts with the extended narrative she will receive in ch 34 (Dinah and Shechem). Her name's root (dîn, judgment) anticipates the violent "judgment" her brothers will enact on Shechem. The fact that she is named at all, while other daughters are not mentioned (31:41 says Jacob had daughters), marks her as significant for the subsequent narrative.
30.22: wayyizkor ʾĕlōhîm ʾet-rāḥēl wayyišmaʿ ʾēleyhā ʾĕlōhîm wayyipteaḥ ʾet-raḥmāh — "God remembered Rachel; God heard her and opened her womb." The formula wayyizkor ʾĕlōhîm (God remembered) — the zāḵar (H2142) covenant-memory verb — is used for God's response to covenant distress: 8:1 (God remembered Noah), 19:29 (God remembered Abraham for Lot), Exod 2:24 (God remembered the covenant with Abraham/Isaac/Jacob for enslaved Israel). Rachel's remembrance follows the same covenantal pattern: the memory is not mere recollection but active intervention. The triad — remembered / heard / opened — compresses three divine actions into one verse. The "opening" of the womb (peṯaḥ raḥmāh) uses the same verb as in 29:31 for Leah.
30.23: wathar waṯṯēled bēn waṯṯōʾmer ʾāsap ʾĕlōhîm ʾet-ḥerpātî — "She conceived and bore a son and said, 'God has taken away my reproach.'" ʾĀsap ʾĕlōhîm ʾet-ḥerpātî — "God has gathered/removed my reproach." ʾĀsap (H622, to gather, remove, take away) — one of the Joseph etymologies; the other is in v24 (yāsap = may he add). Ḥerpāh (H2781, reproach, disgrace) — in the ANE and OT, barrenness was experienced as social shame/reproach: the barren woman had failed her primary social duty. Hannah uses the same word (1 Sam 1:6-11); Elizabeth in Luke 1:25 ("he has taken away my reproach among men") uses the same theological language. The removal of reproach is divine vindication of the woman the social order had judged as deficient.
30.24: waṯṯiqrāʾ ʾet-šĕmô yôsēp lēʾmōr yōsēp YHWH lî bēn ʾaḥēr — "She named him Joseph, saying, 'May YHWH add to me another son.'" The name yôsēp encodes two etymologies simultaneously: (1) from ʾāsap (v23, "God has removed my reproach") — the ʾāsap root; (2) from yāsap (H3254, to add, to continue) — the prayer for a second son. The name is a bilingual pun: it looks backward (reproach removed) and forward (may God add more). The two-directional naming formula is unique in the Genesis birth-speeches. Joseph's name thus carries both gratitude and hope — which characterizes his entire narrative: what is lost is restored and multiplied. YHWH's name is invoked — the covenant name — marking the prayer as more than a fertility wish.
30.25: wayhî kaʾăšer yālaḏāh rāḥēl ʾet-yôsēp wayyōʾmer yaʿăqōḇ ʾel-lāḇān šallĕḥēnî wĕʾēlĕḵāh ʾel-mĕqômî ûlĕʾarṣî — "When Rachel had borne Joseph, Jacob said to Laban, 'Send me away that I may go to my place and to my land.'" The birth of Joseph is the trigger for Jacob's departure request — suggesting that in Jacob's mind, Joseph's birth completed a cycle. The phrase mĕqômî ûlĕʾarṣî (my place and my land) uses māqôm (H4725, place) — a term that acquired sacred resonance in Jacob's journey: the "place" where he slept at Bethel (28:11,16-17) and the "place" of Peniel (32:31). Jacob's homeland is both geographical and covenantally charged.
30.26: tĕnāh ʾet-nāšay wĕʾet-yĕlāḏay ʾăšer ʿāḇaḏtî ʾōtĕḵā bāhen wĕʾēlēḵāh — "Give me my wives and my children for whom I have served you, and I will go." The legal claim is precise: Jacob's wives and children are the result of his labor-contract (ʿāḇaḏtî ʾōtĕḵā bāhen, "I served you for them"). In the ANE labor-contract framework, a laborer who worked for a bride-price had rights to the bride and her children — they were legally his even if born in the employer's household. Jacob's claim is legally grounded: the fourteen years of labor have purchased his household, and he is entitled to take them.
30.27: niḥaštî wayyĕḇāreḵēnî YHWH biglālĕḵā — "I have divined that YHWH has blessed me because of you." Laban uses the verb nāḥaš (H5172, to practice divination, to observe signs, to divine) — a term for omen-reading and divination practices common in the ANE (Num 23:23; Deut 18:10 prohibit it for Israel). Laban is a diviner who has discerned through his oracular practices that Jacob's presence is the source of YHWH's blessing on his household. The irony: a Aramean diviner attributes his prosperity to the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. The theological truth he names correctly (YHWH has blessed me because of you) is the same truth Joseph's Egyptian masters will discern (39:3, 5).
30.28: nāqĕḇāh śĕḵārĕḵā ʿālay wĕʾettēnāh — "Name your wages and I will give it." Laban's invitation to Jacob to set his own wages is the same opening gambit as 29:15 — Laban invites Jacob to name terms in a negotiation Laban intends to control. The verb nāqaḇ (H5344, to pierce, to designate/specify) — "designate/specify your wages" — is a technical term for naming a price or figure explicitly. Laban will subsequently manipulate the terms (31:7: he changed Jacob's wages ten times), so the invitation to "name your wages" is less generous than it sounds.
30.29: ʾattāh yāḏaʿtā ʾēt ʾăšer ʿăḇaḏtîḵā wĕʾēt ʾăšer-hāyāh miqnĕḵā ʾittî — "You know how I have served you and how your livestock has fared with me." Jacob's appeal to Laban's own knowledge (ʾattāh yāḏaʿtā) is a rhetorical strategy — making Laban the acknowledging witness to Jacob's faithful labor. The verb yāḏaʿ (to know) here functions as evidence-appeal: you have seen, you know, the facts are as I state them. Jacob does not merely assert his good service; he calls Laban as his own witness. The livestock's prosperity "with me" (ʾittî) echoes the covenant formula "YHWH was with Jacob" — the implication is that YHWH's presence with Jacob produced the flourishing.
30.30: kî mĕʿaṭ ʾăšer-hāyāh lĕḵā lĕpānay wayyiprōṣ lārōḇ wayyĕḇāreḵ YHWH ʾōtĕḵā lĕraglî — "For the little you had before I came has grown greatly, and YHWH has blessed you wherever I walked." The phrase lĕraglî (literally "at my feet," meaning wherever I went/because of me) echoes the language of the covenant blessing through Abraham (12:3: "through you all nations will be blessed"). Jacob is the carrier of the Abrahamic blessing, and Laban has been the incidental beneficiary. Wayyiprōṣ lārōḇ — "he has burst forth greatly" — the verb pāraṣ (H6555, to break through, burst forth) used here will be applied to Jacob himself in 28:14 (your offspring will "burst forth" in all directions) and to Joseph's increase in Egypt.
30.31: lōʾ-tittēn-lî mĕʾûmāh ʾim-taʿăśeh-llî haddāḇār hazzeh — "You shall not give me anything; if you will do this one thing for me." Jacob's counter-proposal reverses Laban's offer: instead of a wage paid in existing livestock, Jacob will take only the future offspring of a specific category. The apparent generosity ("give me nothing") is the opening of a strategic arrangement. Mĕʾûmāh (H3972, anything, something) — the strongest negative: "not a single thing." Jacob's refusal of existing payment while claiming future increase reflects his confidence in divine provision (which will be confirmed in 31:10-13).
30.32: kol-śeh nāqōḏ wĕṭālûʾ wĕḵol-śeh-ḥûm baḵḵĕśāḇîm — "every speckled and spotted sheep and every dark lamb." The animal-classification system — speckled (nāqōḏ, H5348), spotted (ṭālûʾ, H2921), dark/black (ḥûm, H2345) — establishes the wage-category. The rarer colors (in a flock where solid-colored animals predominate) are Jacob's wages. Laban will remove all existing striped/spotted animals to his sons' care (v35), so Jacob begins with zero of the designated animals. His future increase will be entirely from the solid-colored breeding stock — which makes the subsequent proliferation of striped/spotted offspring miraculous.
30.33: wĕʿānĕtāh bî ṣiḏqātî bĕyôm māḥār — "and my honesty will answer for me in the future." Ṣiḏqāh (H6666, righteousness, justice, what is right) — Jacob appeals to his integrity (ṣĕḏāqāh) as the guarantor of the agreement. The phrase ʿānāh bî (answer for/testify against me) positions righteousness as a witness that will speak on Jacob's behalf — the same personification of righteousness as a covenant witness that appears in the Psalms and prophets (Ps 85:10-11: "righteousness and peace have kissed"). Jacob's appeal to his own righteousness before God is bold — it will be tested by the subsequent narrative.
30.34: hēn lû yĕhî kiḏĕḇāreḵā — "Good, let it be as you say." Laban's quick agreement — no negotiation, no counter-offer — reveals his confidence that the arrangement favors him. He has removed all existing striped animals to a three-day journey's distance (v35), ensuring Jacob starts with nothing. From Laban's perspective, the odds of sufficient striped offspring emerging from a solid-colored flock to constitute meaningful wages are low. Laban's confidence is entirely rational by ordinary agronomy; what he does not factor is divine intervention.
30.35: wayyāsar bayyôm hahûʾ ʾet-hattĕyāšîm hāʿăquddîm wĕhaṭṭĕlūʾîm — "That same day he removed all the male goats that were streaked or spotted." Laban's immediate action — removing all existing striped/spotted animals on the same day — closes every natural pathway to Jacob's wages. Hāʿăquddîm (striped/banded, H6124) and haṭṭĕlūʾîm (spotted, H2921) — the same categories Jacob proposed. Wayyittēn bĕyad-bānāyw — "he gave them into the hand of his sons" — Laban puts his own sons in charge of the separated flock, ensuring Jacob has no access. The three-day journey's distance (v36) creates a geographic buffer. Laban's manipulation is thorough; God's provision will be thorougher.
30.36: wayyāśem derek šĕlōšet yāmîm bênô ûḇên yaʿăqōḇ — "He put a three-day journey between himself and Jacob." Derek šĕlōšet yāmîm (three days' journey distance) is the maximum practical separation in pastoral travel — far enough that the flocks cannot intermingle or be secretly transferred. The same "three days' journey" will appear in the Exodus narrative (3:18; 5:3) for the distance into the wilderness Israel requests for worship — another connection between the Jacob-Laban narrative and the Exodus typology.
30.37: wayyiqqaḥ-lô yaʿăqōḇ maqqal liḇneh laḥ wĕlûz weʿarĕmôn wayyĕpaṣṣēl bāhen paṣṣĕlôt lĕḇānôt — "Jacob took fresh branches of poplar, almond, and plane trees and peeled white streaks on them." Jacob's branch-peeling technique — creating striped branches in the watering troughs — is folk-genetic practice based on the ancient belief that what a pregnant animal sees during conception affects the offspring's appearance. The three tree species (liḇneh = poplar/white wood; lûz = almond; ʿarĕmôn = plane/chestnut) produce white inner wood when stripped. Whether this technique could actually affect animal coloration is biologically questioned; the narrative's own answer comes in 31:10-12 where God reveals to Jacob in a dream that he (God) has been causing the striped offspring — the branches are not the mechanism.
30.38: wayyaṣṣēg ʾet-hammaqqĕlôt ʾăšer piṣṣēl bārehaṭîm bĕšiqĕtôt hammayim — "He placed the peeled branches in the troughs, in the watering channels, in front of the flocks." The watering troughs (rahaṭîm, H7298) are the site of Jacob's intervention. The connection to the well-meeting of ch 29 is deliberate: the same wells/troughs where Jacob met Rachel and rolled the stone are now the site of Jacob's prosperity scheme. The narrative geography is coherent. The timing — placing branches "when the flocks came to drink, since they mated when they came to drink" (baʿălōt haṣṣōʾn liḇôʾ lišĕtôt) — shows Jacob's understanding of pastoral seasonality.
30.39: wayyeḥĕmû haṣṣōʾn ʾel-hammaqqĕlôt wayyēlĕḏû haṣṣōʾn ʿăquddîm nĕquddîm ûṭĕluʾîm — "The flocks bred in front of the branches and bore streaked, speckled, and spotted young." The three result-categories match the three wage-categories Jacob proposed (v32): streaked (ʿăquddîm), speckled (nĕquddîm), spotted (ṭĕluʾîm). The supernatural proliferation of the minority-colored offspring defies ordinary agronomy — this is the point the narrative is building toward. Jacob's scheme (branches) appears to be the cause; God's dream-revelation (31:10-12) will clarify that the breeding was divinely orchestrated through Jacob's selective pairing, not through the branches' magical influence on conception.
30.40: wayyapred yaʿăqōḇ ʾet-haḵĕśāḇîm wayyittēn pĕnê haṣṣōʾn ʾel-ʿāqōḏ wĕḵol-ḥûm bĕṣōʾn lāḇān — "Jacob set apart the lambs and had the flocks face toward the streaked and dark animals of Laban." The selective pairing strategy — having the solid-colored females face striped/dark animals during mating — reflects Jacob's understanding of selective breeding, whatever folk-genetic theory underlies it. Wayĕhî-lô ʿăḏārîm lĕḇaddô — "he made separate flocks for himself and did not put them with Laban's" — Jacob maintains strict separation of his emerging flock from Laban's, preventing intermingling that would muddy the ownership claim.
30.41: wĕhāyāh bĕkol-yaḥēm haṣṣōʾn hamĕquššārôt wĕśām yaʿăqōḇ ʾet-hammaqqĕlôt lĕʿênê haṣṣōʾn bārehaṭîm lĕyaḥamnāh bammaqqĕlôt — "Whenever the stronger females were in heat, Jacob placed the branches in the troughs in front of the animals." The selective application — branches only for the "strong/vigorous" (meqûššārôt, H6989, knotted/vigorous) females — is Jacob's key economic insight: he reserves his breeding intervention for the productive stock. The Hebrew meqûššārôt is a hapax legomenon (appears only here) — possibly related to qāšar (to bind/be strong).
30.42: ûbĕhaʿăṭip haṣṣōʾn lōʾ yāśîm — "but for the weak animals he would not place them." The weak animals (haʿăṭupîm, H5848, the faint/feeble ones) produce Laban's share; the strong ones (hameqūšārôt) produce Jacob's. The result is that Jacob's flock is selectively composed of robust stock while Laban's declines in quality — an outcome that 31:9 attributes directly to God's action: "God has taken away your father's livestock and given them to me." The breeding scheme is presented as Jacob's stratagem, but the theological framing makes God the agent.
30.43: wayyiprōṣ hāʾîš mĕʾōḏ mĕʾōḏ wayhî-lô ṣōʾn rabbôt ûšĕpāḥôt waʿăḇāḏîm ûgĕmallîm waʿăḇāḏîm wăḥămōrîm — "So the man grew exceedingly great and came to have large flocks, and female and male servants, and camels and donkeys." The accumulation formula mĕʾōḏ mĕʾōḏ (very very much) — the same double superlative used for Abraham's covenant wealth (17:2) and Isaac's Philistine-envied prosperity (26:13-14). Jacob's prosperity follows the same covenant-blessing pattern. The four-category inventory (flocks, female servants, male servants, camels and donkeys) mirrors the wealth formulas used for Abraham (12:16; 24:35) — Jacob is being established as a third-generation carrier of the Abrahamic covenant prosperity. The chapter ends where it began, with the matriarchal rivalry — but now Jacob's prosperity is the narrative frame enclosing both the domestic strife and the divine provision.

31.1: לָקַח יַעֲקֹב אֵת כָּל-אֲשֶׁר לְאָבִינוּ — "Jacob has taken everything belonging to our father." The verb lāqaḥ (to take) — used here accusatorially; Laban's sons describe Jacob's legitimate wage-earnings as a taking/seizure. וּמֵאֲשֶׁר לְאָבִינוּ עָשָׂה אֵת כָּל-הַכָּבֹד הַזֶּה (and from what belonged to our father he has made all this wealth) — kāḇôḏ (wealth, honor, glory — the root meaning is "heaviness"; wealth is what weighs heavily; cf. 13:2 where Abram is described as "heavy/wealthy" in cattle, silver, and gold). The sons perceive Jacob's prosperity as their loss — a zero-sum view of wealth that is explicitly corrected in v. 9: God, not Jacob, transferred the livestock.
31.2: פְּנֵי לָבָן (pĕnê lāḇān, the face of Laban) — "Jacob saw that Laban's face was not with him as before." Pānîm (face) is the chapter's primary perceptual-relational indicator: one reads the face of another to determine their attitude, goodwill, or hostility. The "face" idiom runs through vv. 2 and 5: Jacob reads Laban's face and sees the changed relationship. The same face-vocabulary appeared in 32:20 and 33:10 (seeing Jacob's face is like seeing the face of God) and will appear at the Peniel encounter in 32:30. The OT uses "face" as a synecdoche for the entire relational stance of a person.
31.3: וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל-יַעֲקֹב שׁוּב אֶל-אֶרֶץ אֲבוֹתֶיךָ — "the LORD said to Jacob: return to the land of your fathers." Šûḇ (to return, turn back) — the verb of covenantal return; the same word used for moral repentance (Isa 55:7; Jer 3:12-14) carries a more literal sense here: physically return to the covenant land. Wĕʾel-môlaḏtĕḵā (and to your birthplace) — the same word as 12:1 where Abraham was told to leave his môlaḏāh; here Jacob is commanded to return to Abraham's môlaḏāh. וְאֶהְיֶה עִמָּךְ (and I will be with you) — the divine presence promise of 28:15 ("I am with you and will watch over you") is repeated as the warrant for the return; YHWH's presence is portable, not confined to Bethel or to Canaan.
31.4: Jacob summons Rachel and Leah to the field — away from Laban's household — to speak privately. The location (field, away from the house) is tactically chosen: Jacob knows the domestic space is surveilled by Laban's household; he chooses the open field where his flocks are, his domain, for the family council. The summoning of both wives together for a joint decision is notable — Jacob treats them as equal partners in the decision to flee, though the decision has already been divinely mandated (v. 3).
31.5: פְּנֵי אֲבִיכֶן אֵינֶנּוּ אֵלַי כִּתְמֹל שִׁלְשׁוֹם — "your father's face is not toward me as yesterday and the day before." The idiom kitmōl šilšôm (as yesterday and the day before) = formerly, previously; the standard Hebrew expression for a prior established state. Jacob reads Laban's face (v. 2) and now articulates it to Rachel and Leah: the relational warmth has ended. וֵאלֹהֵי אָבִי הָיָה עִמָּדִי (but the God of my father has been with me) — the contrast: Laban's face has changed, but God's faithfulness has not. The "God of my father" (ʾĔlōhê ʾāḇî) becomes Jacob's standard divine reference throughout this chapter (vv. 5, 29, 42, 53).
31.6: בְּכָל-כֹּחִי עָבַדְתִּי אֶת-אֲבִיכֶן (bĕḵol-kōḥî ʿāḇaḏtî ʾet-ʾăḇîḵen) — "with all my strength I have served your father." Kōaḥ (strength, power, capacity) — Jacob claims maximal effort: not partial, not adequate, but total commitment. The legal context of this claim will appear in vv. 38-42 where Jacob defends his service record in detail. The witness to Rachel and Leah is important: he is establishing the factual record of his labor before the family audience.
31.7: וַיַּחֲלֵף אֶת-מַשְׂכֻּרְתִּי עֲשֶׂרֶת מֹנִים (wayyaḥălēp ʾet-maśkurtî ʿăśeret mônîm) — "he changed my wages ten times." Māśkōret (wages, hire — from śāḵar, to hire; the payment due to a hired worker; cf. the teraphim-related hire in 30:18 where Leah "hired" Jacob for a night with the mandrakes). ʿĂśeret mônîm (ten times) — a round number expressing complete repetition of wrongdoing (cf. Num 14:22 where Israel tested YHWH "these ten times" — the idiom of maximal accumulative offense). וְלֹא-נְתָנוֹ אֱלֹהִים לְהָרַע עִמָּדִי (but God did not allow him to harm me) — the theological interpretation: Laban's serial wage-changes were overridden by divine protection. Jacob's account gives God the agency in his prosperity, not his own cleverness with the breeding rods (30:37-42).
31.8: Jacob explains the mechanism of God's overriding of Laban's wage manipulations: whenever Laban specified "speckled as your wages," all the animals bore speckled young; whenever "streaked," all bore streaked. The narrative here revises the ch. 30 account (where Jacob seemed to use selective breeding techniques with rods) by attributing the outcome to divine action. The dream-revelation in vv. 10-12 gives Jacob the divine perspective: God showed him the mechanism. The two accounts are complementary, not contradictory: Jacob used the rods; God overrode the natural odds to produce the contracted pattern.
31.9: וַיַּצֵּל אֱלֹהִים אֶת-מִקְנֵה אֲבִיכֶם וַיִּתֶּן-לִי — "God has taken away your father's livestock and given it to me." Hiṣṣîl (Hiphil of nāṣal, to deliver, rescue, strip away) — the same verb used for deliverance from slavery and from enemies (Exod 3:8; 18:9-10; Judg 6:9). God "strips away" Laban's livestock and transfers them to Jacob: the language of divine deliverance applied to an economic reallocation. The theological claim is significant: Jacob's wealth is not the result of his skill or Laban's generosity but divine redistribution.
31.10: בְּעֵת יַחֵם הַצֹּאן (bĕʿēt yaḥem haṣṣōʾn) — "in the breeding season of the flock." Yāḥam (to be in heat, to warm — the verb for sexual arousal/readiness in animals; used for the male goats coming to mate; cf. Jer 2:24 for the same verb). Jacob's dream occurs at the moment of maximum biological activity — the breeding season — which is precisely when the selective breeding of ch. 30 was employed. The dream at the breeding season contextualizes the revelation: God reveals his management of the very moment Jacob thought he was managing.
31.11: מַלְאַךְ הָאֱלֹהִים (malʾaḵ hāʾĔlōhîm, angel of God) — the divine messenger speaks to Jacob in the dream. The identification is the same as the messenger who appeared to Hagar (16:7; 21:17), who stopped Abraham at the Akedah (22:11-15), and who will accompany the Exodus (Exod 23:20). In the immediately following divine speech (v. 13), the messenger shifts from third-person divine speech to first-person self-identification ("I am the God of Bethel") — the angel and YHWH blur in the theophanic pattern of Genesis. Hinnēnî — Jacob's response (here I am, ready) — the covenant-readiness formula of Abraham (22:1, 7, 11) and Isaiah (6:8) is now Jacob's.
31.12: כִּי רָאִיתִי אֵת כָּל-אֲשֶׁר לָבָן עֹשֶׂה לָּךְ — "for I have seen all that Laban has been doing to you." Rāʾîtî (I have seen) — the divine seeing that precedes divine action (cf. 29:32 where YHWH "sees" Leah's affliction; 16:13 where Hagar names the God who sees; 31:42 where God has "seen" Jacob's hardship). The divine declaration of having seen is the warrant for the command to return: God has observed the injustice and is now acting to rectify it. The vision reveals the ram patterns as a divine sign, not merely a natural occurrence managed by Jacob's rods.
31.13: אָנֹכִי הָאֵל בֵּית-אֵל (ʾānōḵî hāʾēl bêt-ʾēl) — "I am the God of Bethel." An unusual divine self-identification: YHWH/the angel identifies himself not by covenant relationship ("God of Abraham, God of Isaac") but by a specific place and event — the Bethel theophany of 28:10-22. The identification links back to Jacob's dream at Bethel (the pillar, the oil, the vow) and confirms that the God who met Jacob at that night is the same God now commanding his return. ʾĂšer māšaḥtā šām maṣṣēḇāh (where you anointed the pillar) — the specific act (anointing the pillar with oil, 28:18) is cited as the identifying event. ʾĂšer nāḏartā lî šām neḏer (where you made me a vow there) — the Bethel vow (28:20-22) is now being called in: Jacob vowed; God kept his end (provision, protection, return); now the vow's condition is fulfilled and Jacob must return to the God who met him at Bethel.
31.14: הַעוֹד לָנוּ חֵלֶק וְנַחֲלָה בְּבֵית אָבִינוּ — "do we still have any share and inheritance in our father's house?" Ḥēleq (portion, share) and naḥălāh (inheritance, inherited possession) — the legal inheritance vocabulary of the OT. Leah and Rachel are raising the question of their own disinheritance: have they been treated as heirs by their father or as commodities? The question is rhetorical — they already know the answer (v. 15: he has treated them as foreigners).
31.15: הֲלוֹא נָכְרִיּוֹת נֶחְשַׁבְנוּ לוֹ (hălōʾ nāḵriyyôt neḥšaḇnû lô) — "have we not been regarded as foreigners by him?" Nāḵriyyāh (foreign woman, outsider — feminine of nāḵrî, foreign, alien, stranger; same root as hikkîr, to recognize — the outsider is the one not recognized).
