The daughters of Laban have been treated as non-family by their own father: kî-ḵāhāh môḵerānû wayyōḵal gam-ʾāḵôl ʾet-kasĕpēnû (he has sold us and even used up the bride-price paid for us) — the bride-price (môhar) should have gone to the bride's family and eventually to the bride herself; Laban consumed it, treating his daughters as sources of revenue rather than as family members with inheritance rights.
31.16: וְעַתָּה כֹּל אֲשֶׁר אָמַר אֱלֹהִים אֵלֶיךָ עֲשֵׂה — "now, whatever God has told you, do it." Rachel and Leah align themselves completely with Jacob's divine mandate. Their endorsement is the family consensus that Jacob needed: the departure is not just divinely commanded but family-sanctioned. The wealth Jacob is taking belongs, they argue, to them and their children anyway (v. 16: "surely all the wealth God took from our father belongs to us and our children") — so there is no theft in the departure. The daughters' theological alignment ("whatever God says") is their equivalent of the covenant-obedience formula.
31.17: Jacob acts: children and wives on camels, livestock ahead, goods packed. The departure sequence is rapid and comprehensive — the narrative lists the cargo categories to show that nothing is left behind. The movement is urgent: Rachel has already taken the teraphim (v. 19) and the departure must be completed before Laban returns from the sheep-shearing.
31.18: מִקְנֵהוּ אֲשֶׁר רָכַשׁ (miqnēhû ʾăšer rāḵaš) — "his livestock that he had acquired." Rāḵaš (to acquire, accumulate — used for the accumulated wealth of Abraham in 12:5 and Esau in 36:6; a word for legally owned acquired property, not inherited). Jacob's departure with his legally acquired livestock parallels Abraham's departure from Egypt with his acquired wealth (12:16; 13:2) — both patriarchs depart enriched by foreign sojourn. Paddān ʾărāmāh (Paddan-Aram) → ʾereṣ kĕnaʿan (land of Canaan): the geographical movement is explicitly stated.
31.19: וַתִּגְנֹב רָחֵל אֶת-הַתְּרָפִים אֲשֶׁר לְאָבִיהָ (wattiḡnōḇ rāḥel ʾet-hattĕrāpîm ʾăšer lĕʾāḇîhā) — "Rachel stole her father's household gods." Gānaḇ (to steal) — the same verb as Jacob "stealing" Laban's heart (v. 20) and Laban's accusation about the teraphim (v. 30). The verb creates a pattern of stealing: Rachel steals gods, Jacob steals the departure. Tĕrāpîm — household idols used in divination (1 Sam 15:23 links tĕrāpîm to divination; Ezek 21:21; Hos 3:4; Zech 10:2). Their function as potential inheritance-claim objects (Nuzi texts from the 15th-14th c. BCE indicate that possession of the paternal household gods could support an inheritance claim) may explain Rachel's motivation, though the text does not explicitly state it. The teraphim subplot creates narrative tension (Laban searches; Jacob unknowingly vows death on the thief, v. 32; Rachel hides them) that is resolved without resolution — the teraphim are never found or returned.
31.20: וַיִּגְנֹב יַעֲקֹב אֶת-לֵב לָבָן הָאֲרַמִּי (wayyiḡnōḇ yaʿăqōḇ ʾet-lēḇ lāḇān hāʾărammî) — "Jacob stole the heart of Laban the Aramean." The idiom gānaḇ lēḇ (steal the heart) = to act without someone's knowledge, to deceive someone's mind: "he stole Laban's heart" by not telling him he was leaving. The verbal echo of v. 19 (gānaḇ for both Rachel's physical theft and Jacob's deceptive departure) is deliberate: both theft-acts occur simultaneously; the family's departure is a complex of taking (lāqaḥ) and stealing (gānaḇ). ʿAl-bĕlî higgîḏ lô kî bōrēaḥ hûʾ (by not telling him that he was fleeing) — bāraḥ (to flee, run away) — Jacob's departure is a flight, not a dignified leave-taking.
31.21: וַיַּעֲבֹר אֶת-הַנָּהָר (wayyaʿăḇōr ʾet-hannāhār) — "he crossed the river." ʿĀḇar (to cross over, pass through) — the same verb for the crossing of the Euphrates that Abraham made in reverse at the beginning of the patriarchal narrative (the ʿîḇrî, "Hebrew," is "the one who crossed over"). Jacob crosses the Euphrates going the opposite direction from Abraham — he is returning from the origin-country to the covenant land. Wayyāśem ʾet-pānāyw har haggīlʿāḏ (he set his face toward the hill country of Gilead) — the idiom śûm pānîm (set the face) = to determine, resolve, fix one's direction; cf. Luke 9:51 where Jesus "set his face" toward Jerusalem.
31.22: בַּיּוֹם הַשְּׁלִישִׁי (bayyôm haššĕlîšî) — "on the third day." The three-day gap between Jacob's departure and Laban's notification repeats the three-day structure of significant intervals in Genesis (22:4 — Abraham reaches Moriah on the third day). Laban learns of the departure three days after the fact — Jacob has a significant head start. The third-day structure here may be purely narrative (a realistic travel interval) or may carry the weight of the pattern of decisive action following a period of delay.
31.23: וַיִּרְדֹּף אַחֲרָיו דֶּרֶךְ שִׁבְעַת יָמִים — "he pursued him for seven days." Laban pursues with his relatives for seven days — a full week of travel — to catch Jacob in the Gilead hills. The pursuit is overtly hostile: he takes his kinsmen, implying the threat of force. Wayyaḏḇēq ʾōtô bĕhar haggīlʿāḏ (he overtook him in the hill country of Gilead) — dāḇaq (to cling, stick, overtake — the same word for the covenant loyalty of Ruth 1:14 ["Ruth clung to her"]; here it describes hostile pursuit). Laban closes the gap; without the divine intervention of v. 24, the confrontation could be violent.
31.24: פֶּן-תְּדַבֵּר עִם-יַעֲקֹב מִטּוֹב עַד-רָע (pen-tĕdabbēr ʿim-yaʿăqōḇ miṭṭôḇ ʿaḏ-rāʿ) — "lest you speak to Jacob from good to bad." The polar merism miṭṭôḇ ʿaḏ-rāʿ (from good to bad) = anything at all; a merism that comprehends the entire spectrum between two extremes (cf. 24:50: Laban said he could say "nothing to you good or bad"). The identical prohibition was given (in inverse form) for the treaty in 24:50; here it is a divine restriction on Laban. The warning is absolute: God forbids Laban from using any speech — favorable or unfavorable — to alter Jacob's course. The divine protection of Jacob's return is verbal as well as physical.
31.25: Laban overtakes Jacob in the Gilead hills; both parties camp. The confrontation is deferred by the overnight camp — the actual confrontation begins in vv. 26-42. The tent-setting (ṭāpā ʾet-ʾōhŏlô) marks territorial establishment: Laban sets up camp in Jacob's territory (the hill country where Jacob has fled). The spatial dynamic: Laban has pursued Jacob into Jacob's chosen refuge; Jacob cannot flee further. The confrontation is forced.
31.26: מֶה עָשִׂיתָ וַתִּגְנֹב אֶת-לְבָבִי (meh ʿāśîtā wattignōḇ ʾet-lĕḇāḇî) — "what have you done? You have stolen my heart." Laban's accusation uses both the heart-stealing idiom (v. 20) and the captive-metaphor: kišĕḇûyôt ḥāreḇ (like captives taken by the sword). The sword-captive metaphor is hyperbolic — Jacob left peacefully with his family and livestock — but it expresses Laban's sense of violation: his daughters have been taken as if by force, without ceremony or farewell. Lāmāh naḵaḇtā lāḇōraḥ (why did you hide yourself in fleeing?) — naḵāh (to hide oneself, steal away) — Laban's complaint is about the secrecy, not about the departure itself.
31.27: לָמָּה גָנַבְתָּ אֹתִי — "why did you steal away from me?" Laban's complaint about the theft of departure contrasts with what he says he would have done: send Jacob off with joy, song, timbrels (tuppîm), and harps (kinnôr). The festive-farewell vocabulary describes the opposite of a fugitive's midnight flight. Whether Laban would actually have done this (or used the occasion to prevent Jacob's departure) is not resolvable from the text; Jacob himself answers in v. 31 that he was afraid Laban would have taken his daughters by force.
31.28: לֹא נְטַשְׁתַּנִי לְנַשֵּׁק לְבָנַי וְלִבְנֹתָי — "you did not allow me to kiss my grandchildren and my daughters." Laban's complaint shifts from legal accusation (theft) to relational grief: he was denied the farewell kiss. The kiss of blessing and farewell is a covenantal-relational gesture in the ANE (cf. 29:13 where Laban kissed Jacob at his arrival; 31:55 where he will kiss his grandchildren before departing). Nāšaq (to kiss) in the patriarchal narratives marks recognition, acceptance, and blessing-transfer. Laban claims relational injury as well as legal injury.
31.29: יֶשׁ-לְאֵל יָדִי לַעֲשׂוֹת עִמָּכֶם רָע (yĕš-lĕʾēl yāḏî laʿăśôt ʿimmāḵem rāʿ) — "it is in my power to do you harm." The phrase lĕʾēl yāḏî (to my hand is El/power) — ʾēl here means "power, ability" rather than the divine name (though the orthography is identical); the idiom yĕš lĕyāḏ = "it is within one's ability/reach"; cf. Deut 28:32; Prov 3:27: "when it is in your power to do good." Laban explicitly acknowledges his restraint is God-imposed: the divine dream-warning (v. 24) is his reason for not using this power. The admission is a back-handed confession: God has overridden Laban's hostile capacity.
31.30: לָמָּה גָנַבְתָּ אֶת-אֱלֹהָי (lāmmāh gānaḇtā ʾet-ʾĕlōhāy) — "why did you steal my gods?" Laban's final question in this speech is about the teraphim. The phrase ʾĕlōhāy (my gods) — Laban uses the possessive; these are his personal household deities, the objects of his religious practice. The irony that Jacob, the YHWH-worshipper, is accused of stealing Laban's gods (which he himself has called "my gods") is sharp: the narrative implicitly contrasts the portable, universal YHWH with Laban's localized household gods, which can be stolen and sat upon (v. 35).
31.31: כִּי יָרֵאתִי (kî yārēʾtî, because I was afraid) — Jacob's one-word explanation for the secret departure: fear. The admission is honest and unsentimental. Pen-tiḡzōl ʾet-bĕnōtêḵā mēʿimmāḏî (lest you take your daughters from me by force) — gāzal (to seize by force, tear away — a word for violent seizure: Lev 19:13; Mic 2:2; Mal 1:13). Jacob's fear was that Laban would use force to end the marriage; given Laban's track record of deception (the Leah/Rachel substitution, the ten wage-changes), the fear is reasonable. The flight was driven by self-protective fear, not hostility.
31.32: אֲשֶׁר תִּמְצָא אֶת-אֱלֹהֶיךָ לֹא יִחְיֶה (ʾăšer timṣāʾ ʾet-ʾĕlōhêḵā lōʾ yiḥyeh) — "whoever has your gods shall not live." Jacob's death-curse on the teraphim-thief is uttered without knowing Rachel is the thief. The dramatic irony is complete: the man who unknowingly pronounced a death-curse on his favorite wife will spend years grieving her death in childbirth (35:16-20). Whether the curse has any causal relationship to Rachel's death is theologically unresolved by the narrator; the connection is structural in the narrative. Wĕlōʾ-yāḏaʿ yaʿăqōḇ kî rāḥēl gĕnāḇātam (Jacob did not know that Rachel had stolen them) — the narrator's explicit note prevents the reader from blaming Jacob; he acts in good faith.
31.33: Laban's search: he goes through Jacob's tent, Leah's tent, and the tents of the two female servants (Bilhah and Zilpah) — a systematic, ordered search. The detail that Rachel's tent is searched last (after the others have been cleared) creates narrative suspense: will the teraphim be found? The order also reveals the household tent-arrangement: Jacob, Leah, servants, Rachel — the four household units.
31.34: וְרָחֵל לָקְחָה אֶת-הַתְּרָפִים — Rachel has placed the teraphim inside the camel-saddle (kār, camel cushion/saddle-bag) and is sitting on them. Wayyĕmaššēš lāḇān ʾet-kol-hāʾōhel wĕlōʾ māṣāʾ (Laban searched the whole tent and did not find them) — the thorough but unsuccessful search. The household gods, objects of Laban's religious practice, are hidden under Rachel in a posture that renders them ritually unclean (menstruation, v. 35); the gods that were Laban's strength are powerless to reveal their location.
31.35: כִּי-דֶרֶךְ נָשִׁים לִי (kî-ḏereḵ nāšîm lî) — "because the way of women is with me." The idiom ḏereḵ nāšîm (the way of women) is a euphemism for menstruation (cf. the only other OT occurrence in this idiom-sense). Rachel claims to be menstruating and therefore unable to rise — a socially and ritually compelling excuse in an ancient Near Eastern context where menstruation involved both social conventions of seclusion and ritual impurity concerns (Lev 15:19-30). The teraphim hidden under a menstruating woman are effectively hidden in a place no searching hand would go. Rachel's deception of her father mirrors Jacob's deception of his father in ch. 27 — the family talent for strategic misdirection is shared.
31.36: וַיִּחַר לְיַעֲקֹב וַיָּרֶב בְּלָבָן (wayyiḥar lĕyaʿăqōḇ wayyāreḇ bĕlāḇān) — "Jacob was angry and argued with Laban." Ḥārāh (to burn, be hot — the idiom of anger: ḥārāh ʾaph, the anger burned); the anger here burns in Jacob after the failed search proves he has nothing to hide. Rîḇ (to contend, argue — the word for legal dispute: the same as the covenant dispute in Mic 6:1-2 "YHWH has a dispute [rîb] with his people"). Jacob's defense is a legal speech (vv. 36-42) that would be at home in a legal covenant dispute context: "what is my crime? What have I wronged you with?"
31.37: שִׂים כֹּה נֶגֶד אַחַי וְאַחֶיךָ וְיוֹכִיחוּ בֵּין שְׁנֵינוּ — "put it here before my relatives and yours, and let them judge between the two of us." Jacob proposes public adjudication: lay out everything before the assembled relatives (Jacob's and Laban's) and let them serve as judges. The word yôḵîḥû (Hiphil of yāḵaḥ — to judge, adjudicate, decide) — the same root as the sign-prayer word in 24:14 (the servant asked God to hôḵîaḥ, designate, the right woman). Jacob's confident invitation to judgment expresses his consciousness of innocence on the theft charge.
31.38: עֶשְׂרִים שָׁנָה אָנֹכִי עִמָּךְ (ʿeśrîm šānāh ʾānōḵî ʿimmāḵ) — "twenty years I have been with you." Jacob's formal defense begins with the chronological frame: twenty years (14 for the two daughters, 6 for the livestock, v. 41). רְחֵלֶיךָ וְעִזֶּיךָ לֹא שִׁכֵּלוּ (your ewes and female goats have not miscarried) — šikkēl (Piel of šāḵal, to be bereaved of children; in animals, to miscarry — the same root as the child-bereavement of 27:45 and Isa 49:21). The claim is that under Jacob's shepherding, the flocks reproduced perfectly: no miscarriages, no excess predation.
31.39: טְרֵפָה לֹא-הֵבֵאתִי אֵלֶיךָ אָנֹכִי אֲחַטֶּנָּה — "I did not bring you what was torn by beasts; I bore the loss myself." The legal background: Exod 22:12-13 (later codified) specifies that if an animal in a shepherd's care is killed by a predator, the shepherd is released from liability if he shows the carcass; but if the animal is stolen, the shepherd must make restitution. Jacob claims to have gone beyond legal obligation: he covered even the losses from predation himself, absorbing costs that legally should have been Laban's. מִיָּדִי תְּבַקְשֶׁנָּה גְּנֻבְתִי יוֹם וּגְנֻבְתִי לָיְלָה (you held me responsible for whatever was stolen by day or night) — Jacob worked under a stricter standard than the law required.
31.40: הָיִיתִי בַיּוֹם אֲכָלַנִי חֹרֶב (hāyîtî ḇayyôm ʾăḵālanî ḥōreḇ) — "by day the heat devoured me." Ḥōreḇ (scorching heat, drought — the same word as the sacred mountain Horeb; the dual meaning of the dry wilderness and the heat of the day). Qeraḥ ballāylāh (cold at night) — the temperature extremes of pastoral life in the Syro-Palestinian uplands: intense daytime heat and cold nights. וַתִּדַּד שְׁנָתִי מֵעֵינָי (sleep fled from my eyes) — nāḏaḏ (to flee, wander away — used for birds fleeing a nest, Prov 27:8; for fugitives, Isa 10:31; 21:14); here sleep is personified as something that has fled. The image of sleepless, temperature-abused labor is vivid and serves Jacob's legal argument.
31.41: זֶה-לִּי עֶשְׂרִים שָׁנָה בְּבֵיתֶךָ — "these twenty years of mine in your house." The second statement of the twenty-year frame; Jacob now itemizes: fourteen years for the two daughters, six years for the flocks. וַתַּחֲלֵף אֶת-מַשְׂכֻּרְתִּי עֲשֶׂרֶת מֹנִים (and you changed my wages ten times) — the same phrase as v. 7; the repetition in the formal defense speech creates the rhetorical structure of a legal indictment: the wrong was repeated systematically, not once or accidentally. The twenty-year period and ten wage-changes form the factual core of Jacob's case against Laban.
31.42: לוּלֵי אֱלֹהֵי אָבִי אֱלֹהֵי אַבְרָהָם וּפַחַד יִצְחָק הָיָה לִי — "if the God of my father, the God of Abraham and the Paḥad of Isaac, had not been with me." Paḥad yiṣḥāq (the Fear/Dread of Isaac, or the God whom Isaac fears) — one of the most unusual divine epithets in the OT; it appears only here and in v. 53. Paḥad (fear, dread, terror — the awesome reverence before YHWH; cf. Job 4:14; Ps 119:120; Isa 2:10, 19, 21 where God's presence produces this paḥad). The epithet names God from Isaac's subjective experience of theophanic encounter: the God Isaac knows through fear/awe at the revelations of his life (26:24-25; the Akedah's aftermath). כִּי-רָאָה אֱלֹהִים אֶת-עָנְיִי (because God has seen my affliction) — ʿŏnî (affliction, poverty, hardship) — the word used for Israel's bondage in Egypt (Exod 3:7: "I have seen the affliction of my people"). Wayyôḵaḥ (and he rebuked) — God's rebuke of Laban through the dream-warning was the decisive intervention that neutralized Laban's power.
31.43: הַבָּנוֹת בְּנֹתַי וְהַבָּנִים בָּנַי — "the daughters are my daughters, the children are my children." Laban's response to Jacob's defense is not a legal counter-argument but a sweeping ownership claim: everything Jacob has is mine (kōl ʾăšer-ʾattāh rōʾeh lî-hûʾ, all that you see is mine). The claim is technically true from Laban's patriarchal perspective — daughters and their offspring belonged to the father's household. וְלִבְנֹתַי מָה-אֶעֱשֶׂה לָאֵלֶּה (what can I do today about my daughters?) — Laban shifts from possession-claim to helplessness; the rhetorical move acknowledges that the situation is irreversible. His verbal dominance (everything is mine) is immediately qualified by practical inability to change anything.
31.44: וְעַתָּה לְכָה נִכְרְתָה בְרִית אֲנִי וָאָתָּה — "now come, let us cut a covenant, you and I." Kārat bĕrît (to cut a covenant) — the standard covenant-making formula (cf. 15:18; 21:27, 32; 26:28). Having failed to recover the teraphim and having been confronted by Jacob's effective defense, Laban proposes the only remaining option: a covenant that establishes a formal, enforceable boundary. The covenant is Laban's face-saving move: he cannot take his daughters back by force (v. 29: God has forbidden it), he cannot prove the teraphim theft (v. 34-35), and Jacob's labor defense is airtight (vv. 38-42); the covenant allows Laban to exit the confrontation with a structure he can live with.
31.45: וַיִּקַּח יַעֲקֹב אֶבֶן וַיְרִימֶהָ מַצֵּבָה — "Jacob took a stone and set it up as a pillar." The standing stone (maṣṣēḇāh) — the same object Jacob erected at Bethel (28:18) for a different purpose: the Bethel pillar was a cultic memorial to theophany; this Galeed pillar is a legal boundary marker for a treaty. The stone serves as both witness and monument: gal (stone heap) + ʿēḏ (witness) = Galeed.
31.46: Jacob instructs his relatives to gather stones; they pile a heap. The heap is built by communal effort — different from the single standing stone Jacob set up (v. 45); the heap requires multiple people, making it a communal/collective monument. They eat beside the heap — the covenant meal that ratifies the treaty (cf. 26:30 where Isaac's covenant with Abimelech was ratified by eating and drinking; Exod 24:11 where the elders "ate and drank" before YHWH at Sinai).
31.47: יְגַר שָׂהֲדוּתָא (yĕḡar śāhăḏûtāʾ, Jegar Sahadutha) — Laban's name in Aramaic: yĕḡar = heap (Aramaic; cf. Hebrew gal) + śāhăḏûtāʾ = of witness (Aramaic; cognate to Hebrew ʿēḏût). גַּלְעֵד (galʿēḏ, Galeed) — Jacob's Hebrew name: gal (heap) + ʿēḏ (witness). This is the only bilingual place-name episode in the Torah — the same place receives both an Aramaic and a Hebrew name that mean the same thing. The bilingualism marks the treaty boundary not just geographically but linguistically: Laban speaks Aramaic, Jacob speaks Hebrew; the two languages are the two cultures whose boundary this heap marks.
31.48: הַגַּל הַזֶּה עֵד בֵּינִי וּבֵינְךָ הַיּוֹם — "this heap is a witness between me and you today." The heap is formally invested with legal witness-status — it will "see" future violations of the treaty. The use of a material object as a covenant witness is common in the OT (cf. Josh 24:27 where Joshua uses a stone as a witness; Isa 55:4 where David is named as a witness for the nations). ʿAl-kēn qārāʾ-šĕmô galʿēḏ — the Galeed name-explanation is repeated, anchoring it to the covenant-witness function.
31.49: הַמִּצְפָּה אֲשֶׁר אָמַר יִצֶף יְהוָה בֵּינִי וּבֵינֶךָ — "and Mizpah, because he said: may YHWH watch between me and you." Miṣpāh (watchtower, lookout — from ṣāpāh, to look out, watch; ṣōpeh = watchman, sentinel). The place is named from the function YHWH is asked to perform: to watch (yiṣep = Qal imperfect of ṣāpāh) between the two parties when they are absent from each other. The Mizpah formula ("may YHWH watch between us") has become a Christian farewell blessing; in its original context it is a covenant-enforcement mechanism: YHWH is the unseen witness who oversees the treaty when no human witness is present. The connotation is not merely friendly watching but surveillance of potential violations.
31.50: The covenant's specific terms: no mistreatment of Laban's daughters and no additional wives. אִם-תְּעַנֶּה אֶת-בְּנֹתַי (if you mistreat my daughters) — ʿānāh (to afflict, oppress, humble) — the covenant protects Leah and Rachel from physical or domestic abuse. אִם-תִּקַּח נָשִׁים עַל-בְּנֹתַי (if you take wives besides my daughters) — the monogamy/family-limitation clause; Jacob already has four women (Leah, Rachel, Bilhah, Zilpah), but Laban forbids any additions. ʾĒn ʾîš ʿimmānû rĕʾēh ʾĔlōhîm ʿēḏ bênî ûḇênêḵā (though no one is with us, remember God is witness between me and you) — the appeal to divine witness precisely when no human witness is present: the covenant is operative beyond the reach of human surveillance.
31.51: Laban's formal statement of the boundary markers: the heap and the pillar. The heap was built communally (v. 46); the pillar was set by Jacob (v. 45). Both serve as witnesses; both are named in the treaty declaration. The formal enumeration of the witness objects matches ANE treaty-witness conventions where all witness parties (gods, natural objects, mountains) are named.
31.52: עֵד הַגַּל הַזֶּה וְעֵדָה הַמַּצֵּבָה — "this heap is a witness and the pillar is a witness." The two-witness formula; in Israelite law two witnesses were required for legal validity (Deut 17:6; 19:15). The heap and the pillar together constitute the required pair of treaty witnesses. ʾAm-lōʾ-ʾeʿĕḇōr... lĕrāʿāh (I will not cross... to harm you) — the non-crossing-for-harm formula is mutual: both Laban and Jacob are bound not to cross the boundary with hostile intent. The treaty establishes a permanent territorial separation between the two family-nations.
31.53: אֱלֹהֵי אַבְרָהָם וֵאלֹהֵי נָחוֹר יִשְׁפְּטוּ בֵינֵינוּ — "may the God of Abraham and the God of Nahor judge between us." The invocation uses a plural verb (yišpĕṭû, let them judge) with two divine referents — the God of Abraham and the God of Nahor — apparently treating them as separate divine entities (or as a plural of majesty/emphasis). Jacob's response corrects this: וַיִּשָּׁבַע יַעֲקֹב בְּפַחַד אָבִיו יִצְחָק (Jacob swore by the Paḥad of his father Isaac) — Jacob does not swear by the syncretic pairing Laban invokes but specifically by the God of his own covenant line, the Paḥad (Fear) of Isaac. The distinction marks a theological boundary: Laban's God of Nahor is not identified with YHWH; Jacob's oath is to YHWH alone, identified through the Isaac-epithet.
31.54: וַיִּזְבַּח יַעֲקֹב זֶבַח בָּהָר (wayyizbaḥ yaʿăqōḇ zebaḥ bāhār) — "Jacob offered a sacrifice on the mountain." Zebaḥ (sacrificial meal-offering — a sacrifice of which the worshippers participate; the animal is slaughtered (zāḇaḥ) and then eaten communally; cf. the "peace offering" [šĕlāmîm] structure of Lev 3 where the meat is shared between altar, priests, and worshippers). The covenant is ratified through sacrifice and communal eating — the same pattern as 26:30 (Isaac-Abimelech covenant) and Exod 24:11 (Sinai covenant). They eat and spend the night.
31.55: וַיַּשְׁכֵּם לָבָן בַּבֹּקֶר וַיְנַשֵּׁק לְבָנָיו וְלִבְנֹתָיו — "Laban rose early in the morning and kissed his grandchildren and daughters." The early morning rising (hiškîm, to rise at dawn — as Abraham in 22:3; 20:8) is here Laban's, not Jacob's: he wakes before the departure to kiss and bless (wayyĕḇāreḵ) his family. The final gesture is relational, not legal — blessing his daughters and grandchildren. Wayyāšob limqōmô (he returned to his place) — the covenant executed, Laban returns; the separation is permanent. The narrator closes the Laban episode without sentimentality: he went home. The twenty-years of relationship end with a morning blessing and a boundary heap.

32.1: וְיַעֲקֹב הָלַךְ לְדַרְכּוֹ (wĕyaʿăqōḇ hālaḵ lĕḏarkô) — "and Jacob went on his way." The phrase lĕḏarkô (on his way/road) — derekh (road, journey, way); the covenant word of the patriarchal narratives: the servant's journey in ch. 24 was the derekh YHWH prospered; the derekh Jacob vowed to keep safe in 28:20; now his return derekh begins. וַיִּפְגְּעוּ-בוֹ מַלְאֲכֵי אֱלֹהִים (and the angels of God met him) — pāgaʿ (to encounter, meet, fall upon — the same verb Jacob's servant used to describe providential meeting in 24:14; and the wrestling encounter verb in 32:25). The angels who met Jacob going into exile (28:12 — angels on the stairway) now meet him returning from exile.
32.2: מַחֲנֵה אֱלֹהִים זֶה (maḥănēh ʾĔlōhîm zeh) — "this is the camp of God!" Maḥăneh (camp, encampment) — a military term for an armed force in camp; Jacob names the angelic assembly a divine military camp. וַיִּקְרָא שֵׁם-הַמָּקוֹם הַהוּא מַחֲנָיִם (he called that place Mahanaim) — Maḥănāyim = "two camps" (dual of maḥăneh). The dual name anticipates Jacob's own tactical division of his household into two camps (v. 7): the divine double-camp inspires the human double-camp strategy. Mahanaim will later become an important administrative center (2 Sam 2:8; 17:24; 1 Kgs 2:8).
32.3: וַיִּשְׁלַח יַעֲקֹב מַלְאָכִים לְפָנָיו (wayyišlaḥ yaʿăqōḇ malʾāḵîm lĕpānāyw) — "Jacob sent messengers before him." The word malʾāḵîm (messengers, the same word as "angels") — Jacob sends his own human messengers (malʾāḵîm) to Esau just as God sent divine malʾāḵîm to Jacob (v. 1). The verbal echo is deliberate: Jacob, who has just encountered divine messengers, now sends his own. ʾEl-ʾaḥîw ʿēśāw ʾarṣāh śēʿîr śĕḏeh ʾĔḏôm (to his brother Esau in the land of Seir, the field of Edom) — the full geographical identification: Esau is in his territory (Seir/Edom, the land whose name connects to his redness and to the hairy goat, 25:25).
32.4: כֹּה תֹאמְרוּן לַאדֹנִי לְעֵשָׂו (kōh tōʾmĕrûn laʾḏōnî lĕʿēśāw) — "thus you shall say to my lord Esau." Jacob addresses Esau as ʾăḏōnî (my lord) — the title of social superiority. This is the irony of the blessing: Jacob received the blessing that promised "be lord over your brothers" (27:29: geḇîr lĕʾaḥêḵā) and "may the sons of your mother bow down to you" — now Jacob himself uses the submission formula of ʾăḏōnî and calls himself "your servant Jacob" (ʿaḇdĕḵā yaʿăqōḇ). The covenant-heir approaches Esau in the language of a subordinate approaching a superior.
32.5: The message's content (v. 5): Jacob catalogues his wealth — cattle, donkeys, sheep, male and female servants — and says he is sending this message "that I may find favor in your eyes." Limṣōʾ-ḥēn bĕʿênêḵā (to find favor in your eyes) — the standard formula of petition to a superior (cf. Joseph finding favor, 39:4; Moses finding favor, Exod 33:12-13; Hannah, 1 Sam 1:18). Jacob is positioning himself as the subordinate seeking favor from Esau — the social reversal of the blessing scene is complete in Jacob's own self-presentation.
32.6: גַּם הֹלֵךְ לִקְרָאתְךָ וְאַרְבַּע-מֵאוֹת אִישׁ עִמּוֹ — "and also he is coming to meet you, and four hundred men are with him." Four hundred men — a significant military force; the same number appears in 1 Sam 22:2 (David's initial militia at the cave of Adullam); and in 1 Sam 25:13 (David's force against Nabal). The number signals a prepared, organized military contingent — Esau is coming with what appears to be a war party. Jacob's fear in v. 7 is the rational response to this intelligence.
32.7: וַיִּירָא יַעֲקֹב מְאֹד וַיֵּצֶר לוֹ (wayyîrāʾ yaʿăqōḇ mĕʾōḏ wayyēṣer lô) — "Jacob feared greatly and was distressed." The double emotional expression: yārēʾ (to fear — the existential recognition of threat) + yāṣar (to be narrow, distressed — literally "he was cramped/confined," the claustrophobic experience of being trapped with no way out). Both verbs together describe the complete paralysis of terror. וַיַּחַץ אֶת-הָעָם (wayyaḥaṣ ʾet-hāʿām) — "he divided the people." Ḥāṣāh (to divide, halve — cf. the root of Mahanaim [two camps]: Jacob implements the two-camp strategy suggested by the double-camp of the angels). Strategy emerges from fear: the division of the group into two halves increases the chance that at least one half survives an attack.
32.8: אִם-יָבוֹא עֵשָׂו אֶל-הַמַּחֲנֶה הָאַחַת וְהִכָּהוּ — "if Esau comes to one camp and attacks it." Jacob's military thinking: if one camp (maḥăneh) falls, the remaining camp (maḥăneh — same word as Mahanaim) escapes. The two-camp strategy is survival-oriented, not victory-oriented: Jacob is planning escape, not defense. The word maḥăneh (camp) appears four times in vv. 7-8, connecting the Mahanaim angel-encounter to Jacob's human response: the divine two-camp (v. 2) generates the human two-camp (vv. 7-8).
32.9: וַיֹּאמֶר יַעֲקֹב אֱלֹהֵי אָבִי אַבְרָהָם וֵאלֹהֵי אָבִי יִצְחָק — "Jacob prayed: O God of my father Abraham and God of my father Isaac." Jacob's prayer (vv. 9-12) is the most developed intercessory prayer in the patriarchal narratives and a model of OT petition: (1) invocation of the covenant God by covenant relationship; (2) appeal to YHWH's own prior promise (v. 9b: "the LORD who said to me 'return' "); (3) confession of unworthiness (v. 10); (4) rehearsal of the current situation (v. 10b: "I have become two camps"); (5) petition for deliverance (v. 11); (6) appeal to the covenant promise (v. 12). The structure anticipates the formal prayer patterns of the Psalms (complaint, confession, petition, trust).
32.10: קָטֹנְתִּי מִכֹּל הַחֲסָדִים וּמִכָּל-הָאֱמֶת (qāṭontî mikkōl haḥăsāḏîm ûmikkol-hāʾemet) — "I am unworthy of all the ḥeseḏ and all the faithfulness." Qāṭan (to be small, too little — in the Qal perfect: I have become small, I count myself small; cf. 1 Sam 15:17: "when you were small in your own eyes"). Jacob's confession of smallness before divine generosity is the opposite of his earlier self-assertion (ch. 27: he claimed Esau's identity; ch. 30: he negotiated shrewdly for the livestock; ch. 31: he argued his legal case confidently). Here, faced with his brother and his past, he is small. כִּי בְמַקְלִי עָבַרְתִּי (for with only my staff I crossed) — the staff (maqqēl) is the possession of the empty-handed traveler; Jacob departed from Canaan with only a walking stick; he returns with two camps.
32.11: הַצִּילֵנִי נָא מִיַּד אָחִי מִיַּד עֵשָׂו (haṣṣîlēnî nāʾ miyyaḏ ʾāḥî miyyaḏ ʿēśāw) — "deliver me, please, from the hand of my brother, from the hand of Esau." The doubled petition — "from the hand of my brother" then "from the hand of Esau" — names both the relational (ʾāḥ, brother) and the personal (Esau) identity of the threat. The doubling emphasizes the relational tragedy: it is his brother from whom Jacob needs deliverance. Kî yārēʾ ʾānōḵî ʾōtô (for I am afraid of him) — the same fear-verb as v. 7; Jacob brings his fear directly to God as the content of the prayer, not as an embarrassment to confess away.
32.12: וְאַתָּה אָמַרְתָּ הֵיטֵב אֵיטִיב עִמָּךְ (wĕʾattāh ʾāmartā hêṭēḇ ʾêṭîḇ ʿimmāḵ) — "and you said, I will surely do you good." The emphatic infinitive absolute hêṭēḇ ʾêṭîḇ (I will surely do good) — the unconditional divine promise cited as the basis of the petition: YHWH made a promise; Jacob appeals to it. Wĕśamtî ʾet-zarʿĕḵā kĕḥôl hayyām (and I will make your offspring like the sand of the sea) — the covenant promise of 22:17 and 28:14; Jacob quotes God's word back to God as the ground for his request. The prayer moves from confession of smallness to citation of divine promise: the two poles of OT petition.
32.13: Jacob spends the night after the prayer. The narrative pause between prayer (v. 9-12) and gift-selection (v. 13-21) is theologically significant: Jacob prays first, then acts. The order signals that the diplomatic strategy of vv. 13-21 is undertaken after and in the context of prayer — it is not a substitute for prayer but a complement to it. The gift-selection is Jacob's human response alongside YHWH's expected divine response.
32.14: The gift catalogue: 200 female goats, 20 male goats, 200 ewes, 20 rams. The ratios (10:1 female to male) reflect standard ANE flock proportions for productive breeding — the females are the productive units; the males are the minimal breeding complement. Jacob is giving Esau productive, breeding-capable livestock, not just culled animals. The total gift is substantial: 530 animals plus donkeys and camels (vv. 15), approaching the inventory of a wealthy man's flock.
32.15: The full gift: 30 nursing camels with foals, 40 cows, 10 bulls, 20 female donkeys, 10 male donkeys. The inclusion of nursing camels (gĕmallîm mêyniqôt, nursing camels with their young) shows the thoughtfulness of the gift: these are productive animals with their next generation already present. The total gift spans all the livestock categories Jacob catalogued to Esau's messengers (v. 5: cattle, donkeys, sheep, goats) — a comprehensive representation of his wealth.
32.16: עֵדֶר עֵדֶר לְבַדּוֹ (ʿēḏer ʿēḏer lĕḇaddô) — "each herd by itself." The gifts are not sent as one mass but in separate droves with space between them. The spatial arrangement is tactical: each drove arrives separately with its own messenger speaking the same prepared message (vv. 17-19). The effect is a series of encounters: Esau meets one drove, then another, then another — each time receiving the same submissive message ("they are a gift sent to my lord Esau"). The repeated encounters are designed to soften Esau's anger through progressive reconciliation before Jacob himself appears.
32.17: כִּי-יִפְגָּשְׁךָ עֵשָׂו אָחִי וּשְׁאֵלְךָ לֵאמֹר לְמִי-אַתָּה — "when Esau my brother meets you and asks 'to whom do you belong...?'" The question Esau will ask is the standard challenge-question for a traveling group (lĕmî-ʾattāh, whose are you/what group do you belong to). Jacob prepares his servants with exact scripted answers — the preparation of speech for a diplomatic encounter mirrors the preparation of speech in ch. 24 (the servant's prayer and sign-request). Both chapters involve carefully planned speech before a decisive meeting.
32.18: מִנְחָה הִיא שְׁלוּחָה לַאדֹנִי לְעֵשָׂו (minḥāh hîʾ šĕlûḥāh laʾḏōnî lĕʿēśāw) — "it is a gift sent to my lord Esau." Minḥāh (gift, tribute, offering — the same word used for the grain offering in the Levitical system; in diplomatic contexts it is tribute from an inferior to a superior). Jacob sends a minḥāh to Esau — the language of tribute from a subordinate to a lord, consistent with his use of ʾăḏōnî (my lord) throughout the message.
32.19: The three separate droves carry the same message, delivered by separate servants. The repetition is strategic: Jacob has scripted a consistent story and coached all his servants to deliver it identically. The scripted approach is the opposite of the Laban confrontation, where Jacob's speech was spontaneous legal defense. Here everything is planned, controlled, and aimed at one goal: preventing Esau's anger from becoming violence.
32.20: כִּי-אֲכַפְּרָה פָנָיו בַּמִּנְחָה הַהֹלֶכֶת לְפָנָי (kî-ʾăḵappĕrāh pānāyw bamminhāh hahōleket lĕpānay) — "I will pacify/cover his face with the gift going before me." Kipper (Piel of kāpar, to cover, atone, appease — the word used throughout Leviticus for the atonement of sin; yôm hakkippurîm, Day of Atonement). Jacob uses the atonement vocabulary: he will "kipper" Esau's face — appease, cover over, propitiate — with gifts. The secular use of kipper for human-to-human appeasement shows that the word's core meaning is to "cover/wipe away" hostility through compensatory action, whether between humans or between humans and God. ʾĂḵappĕrāh pānāyw = "cover his face" — the face (pānîm) that bears the expression of hostility will be covered (its hostility wiped away) by the gift.
32.21: וַתַּעֲבֹר הַמִּנְחָה עַל-פָּנָיו (wattaʿăḇōr hamminḥāh ʿal-pānāyw) — "the gift passed before him." The gifts go on ahead; Jacob remains in the camp. The word ʾal-pānāyw (before his face) — the gift is literally going "before Esau's face" to prepare the way; the gift-going-before is a form of advance diplomacy. Wĕhûʾ lān ballayĕlāh hahûʾ (he himself spent the night in the camp that night) — Jacob is alone with his plan and his fear before the morning encounter. The next verse will interrupt his night with the wrestling encounter.
32.22: וַיָּקָם בַּלַּיְלָה הוּא וַיִּקַּח אֶת-שְׁתֵּי נָשָׁיו — "he rose in the night and took his two wives." Jacob's nocturnal action: he rises during the night and sends his family across the Jabbok ford. The sequence is precise: wives → female servants → eleven children → all possessions. ʾēt maʿăḇar yaḇḇōq (the ford of the Jabbok) — Yabbōq is the modern Zarqa river, the northern tributary of the Jordan in Transjordan. The name yaḇḇōq begins with the same three consonants as yaʿăqōḇ (Jacob) — the place name and the person name echo each other in the consonantal text. The wrestling of Jacob (yaʿqōḇ) at the Jabbok (yaḇḇōq) using the verb "wrestle" (yēʾābēq) creates a triple wordplay: yod-ayin-qoph / yod-beth-qoph / yod-aleph-beth-qoph.
32.23: Jacob sends all his possessions across the ford — the total evacuation of his camp to the other side of the Jabbok. After this verse, Jacob is entirely alone (v. 24: "Jacob was left alone"). The deliberate stripping away of all family, servants, and goods is Jacob's self-exposure: he crosses over nothing, he has sent everything ahead. The physical aloneness precedes the spiritual encounter.
32.24: וַיִּוָּתֵר יַעֲקֹב לְבַדּוֹ (wayyiwwātēr yaʿăqōḇ lĕḇaddô) — "Jacob was left alone." Yāthar (Niphal: to be left over, to remain — what remains after everything else has gone). Jacob is the remainder: the last one left behind on his side of the ford. וַיֵּאָבֵק אִישׁ עִמּוֹ עַד עֲלוֹת הַשָּׁחַר (wayyēʾābēq ʾîš ʿimmô ʿaḏ ʿălôt haššāḥar) — "and a man wrestled with him until the breaking of the dawn." ʾĀḇaq (to wrestle/grapple — a hapax in this form; possibly from ʾāḇāq, dust, suggesting rolling in the dust of a wrestling match; or related to a root meaning to clasp, hold). The wrestling partner is identified as ʾîš (a man) — not "angel," not "God" — but the identity is deliberately ambiguous. The narrative withholds the identity; only Jacob's interpretation in v. 30 ("I have seen God face to face") and Hosea's retroactive identification (Hos 12:4: "he strove with an angel") name the wrestler after the fact.
32.25: כִּי לֹא יָכֹל לוֹ (kî lōʾ yāḵōl lô) — "when he saw that he could not prevail against him." The wrestler (ʾîš) is unable to overpower Jacob — the one who claims to be divine (or is) is stalemate against the human. וַיִּגַּע בְּכַף-יְרֵכוֹ (wayyiggaʿ bĕḵap-yĕrēḵô) — "he touched the socket of Jacob's hip." Nāgaʿ (to touch — often of divine touch with transformative effect: Isa 6:7 where the seraph touches Isaiah's lips; Jer 1:9 where YHWH touches Jeremiah's mouth; Dan 10:16, 18). The divine touch on the hip-socket (kap-yāreḵ, the hollow/socket of the thigh joint) dislocates it: watteqaʿ kap-yereḵ yaʿăqōḇ bĕhēʾāḇqô ʿimmô (the socket of Jacob's hip was wrenched as he wrestled). Yāreḵ is the same word as the oath-thigh of 24:2 and 47:29 — the seat of generative power and oath; here it is the point of divine touch and permanent alteration.
32.26: שַׁלְּחֵנִי כִּי עָלָה הַשָּׁחַר (šallĕḥēnî kî ʿālāh haššāḥar) — "let me go, for the dawn is breaking." Šālaḥ (to send away, release, let go) — the send-verb that runs through the patriarchal narratives; now Jacob is the one asked to release. The dawn-deadline (kî ʿālāh haššāḥar) is an urgency that has no plain explanation: why must the wrestling end at dawn? Various interpretations: (1) divine beings cannot be seen in daylight; (2) the dawn breaks the theophanic night; (3) the impending day of meeting Esau makes the night's encounter a preparation that must conclude. לֹא אֲשַׁלֵּחֲךָ כִּי אִם-בֵּרַכְתָּנִי (I will not let you go unless you bless me) — Jacob's reply is pure Jacob: he turns the wrestling into a blessing-extraction. From the moment he grabbed Esau's heel at birth to the stolen blessing of ch. 27, Jacob grasps; now he grasps the divine wrestler for blessing. The irony is that the blessing he extracted through deception from Isaac he now seeks legitimately through struggle with God.
32.27: מַה-שְּׁמֶךָ (mah-šĕmeḵā) — "what is your name?" The question reverses the pattern of ch. 27: Isaac asked "who are you, my son?" and Jacob answered falsely ("I am Esau"). Now the divine wrestler asks "what is your name?" and Jacob answers truly: yaʿăqōḇ (Jacob). The name-question in the OT often precedes a name-change (Abram → Abraham, 17:5). The answer "Jacob" is simultaneously a self-identification and a confession: the supplanter, the heel-grabber, the deceiver — this is who I am. Saying the name truthfully to God may be the first step of the transformation that follows.
32.28: לֹא יַעֲקֹב יֵאָמֵר שִׁמְךָ כִּי אִם-יִשְׂרָאֵל (lōʾ yaʿăqōḇ yēʾāmer šĕmeḵā kî ʾim-yiśrāʾēl) — "your name shall no longer be Jacob but Israel." The name-change as covenant moment — Abraham (17:5), Sarah (17:15), and now Jacob. Yiśrāʾēl — etymology in the text: kî-śārîtā ʿim-ʾĔlōhîm wĕʿim-ʾănāšîm wattûḵal (for you have striven with God and with humans and have prevailed). Śārîtā (you have striven/persisted — Qal of śārāh, to persist, exert oneself, strive; possibly related to śar, prince — giving the alternate reading "for you have been a prince with God"). The name Israel = yiśrā (from śārāh) + ʾēl (God) = "he who strives with God" or "God strives/prevails." The wrestling is not Jacob's defeat but his prevailing — wattûḵal (and you have prevailed): Jacob won the wrestling match in some sense, receiving the blessing he demanded.
32.29: הַגִּידָה-נָּא שְׁמֶךָ (haggîḏāh-nnāʾ šĕmeḵā) — "tell me your name, please." Jacob asks the wrestler's name — a request the wrestler refuses: lāmmāh zeh tišʾal lišmî (why do you ask my name?). The divine refusal to reveal a name is a consistent pattern: in Exod 3:14 YHWH gives "I AM WHO I AM" — not a proper name but a self-referential formula; in Judg 13:18 the angel tells Manoah "why do you ask my name? It is wonderful/beyond understanding" (wĕhûʾ-pelîʾî). The name of God is not a formula that can be possessed and used; it remains in divine sovereignty. Yet the one who refuses to give his name gives his blessing: wayyĕḇāreḵ ʾōtô šām (he blessed him there). The blessing without the name is itself the covenant: YHWH acts without being controlled by the one who knows his name.
32.30: וַיִּקְרָא יַעֲקֹב שֵׁם הַמָּקוֹם פְּנִיאֵל (wayyiqrāʾ yaʿăqōḇ šēm hammāqôm pĕnîʾēl) — "Jacob called the name of the place Peniel." Pĕnîʾēl = "face of God" (pāneh + ʾēl); the place is named from Jacob's experience: כִּי-רָאִיתִי אֱלֹהִים פָּנִים אֶל-פָּנִים (for I have seen God face to face). Pānîm ʾel-pānîm (face to face) — the same expression used for Moses' intimacy with YHWH (Exod 33:11: "YHWH spoke to Moses face to face as a man speaks to his friend"; Deut 34:10: "Moses whom YHWH knew face to face"). Jacob's face-to-face encounter with God is the patriarchal precedent for the intimacy that will be named most fully in Moses. וַתִּנָּצֵל נַפְשִׁי (yet my life has been delivered/spared) — the standard formula for surviving a divine encounter that was expected to be fatal (cf. Isa 6:5: "I am undone, for I have seen the King, YHWH of armies"; Judg 13:22: "we shall surely die, for we have seen God").
32.31: וַיִּזְרַח-לוֹ הַשֶּׁמֶשׁ כַּאֲשֶׁר עָבַר אֶת-פְּנוּאֵל (wayyizraḥ-lô haššemeš kaʾăšer ʿāḇar ʾet-pĕnûʾēl) — "the sun rose upon him as he passed Penuel." The sun-rising is both chronological (dawn, as anticipated in v. 26) and symbolic: Jacob wrestled in darkness and emerges into light. The play on the name Peniel/Penuel (variant spellings) — "face of God" — and the rising sun creates a visual of light where the face of God was. Wĕhûʾ ṣōlēaʿ ʿal-yĕrēḵô (he was limping because of his hip) — the limp is the permanent physical mark of the divine encounter. Jacob walks differently after Peniel: every step for the rest of his life is a reminder of the night he wrestled with God. The blessing and the wound come together from the same encounter.
32.32: עַל-כֵּן לֹא-יֹאכְלוּ בְנֵי-יִשְׂרָאֵל אֶת-גִּיד הַנָּשֶׁה (ʿal-kēn lōʾ-yōʾḵĕlû ḇĕnê-yiśrāʾēl ʾet-gîḏ hannāšeh) — "therefore the children of Israel do not eat the tendon of the hip socket." The etiological formula ʿal-kēn (therefore, on account of this) closes the Peniel episode with an explanation for a continuing Israelite dietary practice: abstaining from the sciatic nerve (gîd hannāšeh, the sinew of the hollow — possibly the sciatic nerve or the adjacent connective tissue). Gîḏ hannāšeh (gîḏ = sinew, tendon; nāšeh = displaced, moved out of joint — the tendon that moves/is displaced at the joint) — the prohibition is not in the Mosaic law directly but is attested in the Talmud as a halakhic practice derived from this narrative. The closing etiology grounds the community's embodied practice in the patriarchal narrative: every observant Israelite's eating practice is a living memorial to Jacob's night at the Jabbok.

33.1: wayyiśśāʾ yaʿăqōḇ ʿênāyw wayyarʾ wĕhinnēh ʿēśāw bāʾ — "Jacob looked up and saw — behold, Esau coming." The wĕhinnēh (behold!) signals a dramatic reversal: Jacob has arranged his household by descending order of love (v2 — servants first, Rachel/Joseph last), placing those he loves most at the rear, the position of greatest protection. The division reflects Jacob's triage under fear: the loved ones are shielded by the expendable. The narrative does not editorialize; the arrangement speaks for itself.
33.2: wayyāśem ʾet-hašĕpāḥôt wĕʾet-yaldêhen bāriʾšōnāh wĕʾet-lēʾāh wĕyaldêhā ʾaḥărōnîm wĕʾet-rāḥēl wĕʾet-yôsēp ʾaḥărōnîm — "He put the servants and their children first, Leah and her children next, and Rachel and Joseph last." The ordering mirrors the inverse of Jacob's emotional priorities (29:30 — he loved Rachel more than Leah). To place someone last in a procession before an armed encounter is to place them furthest from danger. The twice-repeated ʾaḥărōnîm (last) for Rachel and Joseph emphasizes their protected position. This arrangement will recur in v6-7 as each group approaches and bows.
33.3: wĕhûʾ ʿāḇar lipnêhem wayyištaḥû ʾarṣāh šeḇaʿ pĕʿāmîm ʿaḏ-giššetô ʾel-ʾāḥîw — "He passed ahead of them, bowing to the ground seven times until he came near his brother." Wayyištaḥû ʾarṣāh (bowing to the ground) — šāḥāh (H7812, to bow down, prostrate oneself) is both the posture of worship before deity and of submission before royalty. Seven full prostrations (šeḇaʿ pĕʿāmîm) is the protocol of a vassal approaching a suzerain, confirmed by the Amarna diplomatic letters (14th c. BCE): "I have bowed seven times, on the stomach and back." In Gen 27:29 the blessing Jacob stole included "nations bow down to you"; now Jacob bows to the very brother he supplanted. The seven prostrations are a complete reversal of the stolen blessing.
33.4: wayyāraṣ ʿēśāw liqrāʾtô wayyĕḥabbĕqēhû wayyippōl ʿal-ṣawwārô wayyiššāqēhû wayyiḇkû — "Esau ran to meet him, and embraced him, and fell on his neck, and kissed him. And they wept." The four verbs — rûṣ (ran), ḥābaq (embraced), nāšaq (kissed), bāḵāh (wept) — form the only tetracolon of reunion in Genesis. The identical three-verb cluster (rûṣ/ḥābaq/nāšaq) appeared at Laban's greeting of Jacob in 29:13, but there the tears are absent; Esau's tears mark the emotion as genuine rather than political. Luke 15:20's description of the father who "ran and fell on his neck and kissed" his returning son uses the same gesture-vocabulary, suggesting the Esau-Jacob reunion is the narrative archetype Jesus draws on for the prodigal parable.
33.5: wayyiśśāʾ ʾet-ʿênāyw wayyarʾ ʾet-hannāšîm wĕʾet-hayyĕlāḏîm wayyōʾmer mî-ʾēlleh lāḵ wayyōʾmer hayyĕlāḏîm ʾăšer-ḥānān ʾĕlōhîm ʾet-ʿaḇdĕḵā — "Jacob said, 'The children God has graciously given your servant.'" The verb ḥānan (H2603, to be gracious, to show favor, to give as a gift of grace) — the root of the name chen (grace, favor) — is Jacob's theological attribution: these children are not an achievement of his own striving but a gift of divine grace. After twenty years in which Jacob's household was built through rivalry, deception, and labor, he presents his family to Esau with the vocabulary of undeserved gift. The address ʿaḇdĕḵā (your servant) continues the diplomatic humility of v3.
33.6: waṯṯiggašnāh hašĕpāḥôt hēnnāh wĕyaldêhen waṯṯišṯaḥăwênāh — "The female servants and their children came forward and bowed down." The approach sequence mirrors the procession order of v2 — servants first. The Hithpael of šāḥāh (waṯṯišṯaḥăwênāh, they prostrated themselves) is the same bow Jacob performed in v3. The entire household enacts the diplomatic submission Jacob modeled. The narrative records the bowing without dialogue; the servants have no named speech-act here.
33.7: waṯṯiggaš gam-lēʾāh wĕyaldêhā wayyišṯaḥăwû wĕʾaḥar niggaš yôsēp wĕrāḥēl wayyišṯaḥăwû — "Then Leah and her children came and bowed down. Last of all came Joseph and Rachel, and they bowed down." The sequential introduction — servants, then Leah's group, then Joseph and Rachel — is the reverse of social standing (Rachel is the beloved) and the reverse of birth order (Reuben was born first). The narrator preserves the emotional order of protection that Jacob established in vv1-2. Joseph, the last-born and most beloved (his name means "may God add"), is introduced last — a placement that will frame his role in the subsequent Joseph narrative.
33.8: maḥăneh hazzeh ʾăšer pāgāštî — "What is the meaning of all this camp I met?" The word maḥăneh (H4264, camp, company) — the same word Jacob used for his own camp in 32:2 (Mahanaim) and for Esau's approaching camp (32:8). Esau's inquiry prompts Jacob's language of gift in v8b: limṣōʾ ḥēn bĕʿênê ʾăḏōnî — "to find favor (ḥēn) in the eyes of my lord." The gift (minḥāh, H4503) is a cultic/tribute term — the same word for the cereal offering brought before deity in the Levitical system. Jacob has selected the tribute-vocabulary deliberately.
33.9: wayyōʾmer ʿēśāw yeš-lî rāḇ ʾāḥî yĕhî-lĕḵā ʾăšer-lāḵ — "Esau said, 'I have plenty, my brother. Keep what is yours.'" Yeš-lî rāḇ — "there is to me much/plenty." The word rāḇ (H7227, much, many, great) is Esau's self-assessment. Jacob will counter in v11 with yeš-lî ḵōl — "I have everything/all." The contrast is theologically significant: Esau has rāḇ (much) — abundance by human measure; Jacob has ḵōl (all) — a covenantal totality. The address ʾāḥî (my brother) — the first time Esau uses this word in the reconciliation — signals that he is ending the hostility of ch 27.
33.10: rāʾîtî pāneyḵā kirʾōt pĕnê ʾĕlōhîm waṯṯirṣēnî — "To see your face is like seeing the face of God, now that you have received me favorably." The comparison kirʾōt pĕnê ʾĕlōhîm (like seeing the face of God) is a direct echo of 32:30, where Jacob named the Peniel site penî-ʾēl because "I have seen God face to face and my life was preserved." To see a powerful figure whose acceptance you feared is existentially analogous to the theophanic encounter. The minḥāh Jacob presses on Esau functions as a tribute-offering brought before a potentially wrathful lord — the cultic register is intentional. Waṯṯirṣēnî (you have accepted me favorably, Qal imperfect of rāṣāh, H7521) — the same verb used for divine acceptance of offerings in Lev 1:4; 22:21.
33.11: qah-nāʾ ʾet-birḵātî ʾăšer hūḇāʾt lāḵ kî-ḥannannî ʾĕlōhîm wĕḵî yeš-lî-ḵōl — "Please accept my blessing (birḵātî) which is brought to you." Birḵāh (H1293, blessing) — the gift of animals is called Jacob's birḵāh. The word choice is striking: in 27:35 Isaac said to Esau "your brother came with deceit and took your blessing (birḵāteḵā)." Now Jacob names his tribute to Esau as birḵātî (my blessing). Whether Jacob is consciously returning what was stolen is not stated, but the narrative use of the same noun creates an interpretive frame. ḵî ḥannannî ʾĕlōhîm — "because God has been gracious to me" — the second use of ḥānan in this chapter (cf. v5), marking grace as the theological signature of Jacob's household narrative.
33.12: wayyōʾmer nisĕʿāh wĕnēlēḵāh wĕʾēlĕḵāh lĕnegdĕḵā — "Let us journey on, and I will go alongside you." Esau's proposal to accompany Jacob — lĕnegdĕḵā (before/alongside you) — is the gesture of a protective escort. Whether Esau's offer is magnanimous or carries a latent territorial dimension (escorting Jacob through his domain) is ambiguous. Jacob's response in vv13-14 declines politely; the subsequent action (v17) reveals that Jacob goes not toward Seir as promised, but to Succoth in the opposite direction.
33.13: hayyĕlāḏîm rakkîm wĕhаṣṣōʾn wĕhabbāqār ʿālôt ʿālāy — "The children are tender (rakkîm) and the nursing ewes and cows are a concern to me." Rakkîm (plural of rakh, H7390, tender, soft, delicate) — the same adjective applied to Leah's eyes in 29:17. Jacob uses the vulnerability of his children and animals as his pretext for travelling separately. The concern for the nursing animals (meyniqqot) reflects authentic pastoral knowledge — overdriving nursing stock for even one day risks their deaths.
33.14: yaʿăḇōr-nāʾ ʾăḏōnî lipnê ʿaḇdô wĕʾănî ʾetnahălāh lĕʾiṭṭî lĕregeḡ hammĕlāʾḵāh ʾăšer-lĕpānay — "Let my lord go ahead while I move slowly, at the pace of the work (lĕregeḡ hamĕlāʾḵāh) before me." Regeḡ (H7272, the foot/pace) — a late and rare term for "at the pace/step of"; the phrase lĕregeḡ hamĕlāʾḵāh means "at the step-rate of the livestock." The courtly address (ʾăḏōnî, my lord; ʿaḇdô, his servant) is sustained throughout — Jacob does not relax his deferential register even while arranging the separation. His stated destination in Seir is a narrative fiction; he goes to Succoth (v17).
33.15: wayyōʾmer ʿēśāw ʾaṣṣîḡāh-nāʾ ʿimmĕḵā minh-āʿām ʾăšer ʾittî wayyōʾmer lāmmāh zeh ʾemṣāʾ-ḥēn bĕʿênê ʾăḏōnî — "'Let me leave some of my men with you,' said Esau. 'Why should that be? Let me only find favor in my lord's eyes.'" Jacob's deflection — "why should that be?" — is gracious but firm. His use of māṣāʾ ḥēn bĕʿênê ʾăḏōnî (find favor in my lord's eyes) echoes the covenant-favor language of 6:8 (Noah found favor), 30:27 (Laban found favor through Jacob), and 32:5 (Jacob's own message to Esau). Jacob keeps his freedom of movement while closing the encounter with honor.
33.16: wayyāšāḇ bayyôm hahûʾ ʿēśāw lĕḏarkô śēʿîrāh — "So that day Esau returned on his way to Seir." The narrative's economy is stark: the anticipated armed encounter (32:7-8) resolves in a single chapter; Esau departs the narrative quietly. Jacob promised to come to Esau at Seir (v14); the narrative shows him going to Succoth instead (v17). Whether this is deliberate evasion or a planned delay that is never fulfilled, the narrator does not comment. The silence about Jacob's Seir-promise creates a moral ambiguity that the text neither resolves nor ignores.
33.17: wĕsukkōt ʿāśāh lô ḇāyit ûlĕmiqnēhû ʿāśāh sukkōt ʿal-kēn qārāʾ šēm-hammāqôm sukkôt — "He built a house for himself and made booths for his livestock; therefore the place is called Succoth." Sukkôt (H5523) — the toponym derived from sukkōt (H5521, booths/shelters, plural of sukkāh). The Feast of Booths (Ḥag hasukkôt) will commemorate Israel's wilderness wandering in booths (Lev 23:42-43); the settlement at Succoth in the Jordan valley (modern Tell Deir ʿAlla) is the first named settlement of Jacob's household in Canaan proper, and it is named for the agricultural/pastoral character of his construction rather than for a theophany — unlike virtually every other patriarchal place-name.
33.18: wayyāḇōʾ yaʿăqōḇ šālēm ʿîr šĕḵem ʾăšer bĕʾereṣ kĕnaʿan — "Jacob came safely (šālēm) to the city of Shechem." The word šālēm (H8003, complete, whole, unharmed) is a deliberate word-choice: it echoes šālôm (covenant peace/wholeness) and may also function as a proper noun — the ancient city name Shalem (cf. Ps 76:2, Salem = Jerusalem, city of Melchizedek). Jacob's arrival "in peace/wholeness" fulfills the vow condition of 28:21 ("if I return safely to my father's house"). The city of Shechem is the site of the first covenant meal with Canaanites (v20) — and the site of the violence that will follow in ch 34.
33.19: wayyiqen ʾet-ḥelqat haśśāḏeh ʾăšer nāṭāh-šām ʾohălô miyyaḏ bĕnê-ḥămôr ʾăḇî šĕḵem bĕmēʾāh qĕśîṭāh — "He bought the plot of ground where he pitched his tent from the sons of Hamor for a hundred qĕśîṭāh." Qĕśîṭāh (H7192) — a monetary unit of uncertain weight or composition, occurring only here, in Josh 24:32 (for the same Shechem plot, confirmed as Joseph's burial place), and in Job 42:11. The purchase legally grounds Jacob's household in Canaan — not merely as a sojourner camping with permission, but as a landowner with a purchased title. This plot will eventually contain Joseph's bones (Exod 13:19; Josh 24:32; Acts 7:16).
33.20: wayyaṣṣeḇ-šām mizḇēaḥ wayyiqrāʾ-lô ʾēl ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl — "He erected an altar there and called it El Elohe Israel — God, the God of Israel." The altar-name ʾēl ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl is a declaration: the God of the covenant is identified as the God of the man now named Israel. Jacob does not yet build the Bethel altar (that is commanded in 35:1); the Shechem altar is a spontaneous act of worship. The use of his new covenant name (Israel) in the altar's name is the first recorded instance of Jacob publicly using the Peniel identity. The naming pattern — "God is the God of Israel" — will become the covenant formula repeated throughout the OT (Exod 5:1; Josh 7:13; 2 Kgs 18:31).

34.1: waṯṯēṣēʾ dînāh bat-lēʾāh ʾăšer yālaḏāh lĕyaʿăqōḇ liroot bĕḇĕnôt hāʾāreṣ — "Dinah went out to see (lirʾōt) the daughters of the land." The verb yāṣāʾ (to go out) combined with rāʾāh (to see) describes normal social mobility for a young woman — visiting female peers was customary. The narrator's use of rāʾāh (to see) is ironic: the following verse opens with Shechem "seeing" (wayyarʾ) her. The two seeings — Dinah's social seeing and Shechem's predatory seeing — are placed in immediate narrative juxtaposition. Dinah is identified by her maternal lineage (daughter of Leah) rather than the expected paternal lineage, perhaps emphasizing her position within the household hierarchy.
34.2: wayyarʾ ʾōtāh šĕḵem ben-ḥămôr haḥiwwî nĕśîʾ hāʾāreṣ wayyiqqaḥ ʾōtāh wayyiškaḇ ʾōtāh wayĕʿannehā — "When Shechem saw her, he took her and lay with her and violated her." Four verbs — rāʾāh (saw), lāqaḥ (took), šāḵaḇ (lay with), ʿinnāh — form a rapid sequence of predatory action. The Piel of ʿānāh (H6031) — to humble, afflict, violate by force — is the technical term for sexual violence in the legal and narrative texts (2 Sam 13:12,14,22,32 for Amnon's rape of Tamar; Deut 22:24,29). The word is not "seduced" or "took by persuasion" — the narrative's moral vocabulary is explicit. Shechem's title nĕśîʾ hāʾāreṣ (prince/ruler of the land) is a narrative detail: the one who commits the act is the one with the most social power to do it with impunity.
34.3: waṯṯiḏbaq napšô bĕḏînāh bat-yaʿăqōḇ wayyeʾĕhaḇ ʾet-hannaʿărāh wayyĕḏabbēr ʿal-lēḇ hannaʿărāh — "His soul clung to Dinah and he loved the young woman and spoke tenderly to her." Three phrases of attachment follow the rape: dāḇaq (H1692, to cling, cleave — the marriage-bonding verb of 2:24: "a man shall cleave to his wife"), ʾāhaḇ (loved), and dāḇar ʿal-lēḇ (spoke to the heart — the idiom for persuasive, intimate speech in Judg 19:3 [Levite to his concubine]; Isa 40:2 [God comforting Jerusalem]). The irony is structural: the language of covenantal love and bonding in Gen 2:24 is applied here to a relationship initiated by violence. The post-hoc attachment does not undo the act; the narrative refuses to allow the subsequent "love" to reframe the rape as seduction.
34.4: wayyōʾmer šĕḵem ʾel-ḥămôr ʾāḇîw lēʾmōr qah-lî ʾet-hayyaldāh hazzōʾt lĕʾiššāh — "Shechem said to his father Hamor, 'Get me this girl as my wife.'" The demand form — qah-lî (take for me, imperative of lāqaḥ, the same verb used for his taking of Dinah in v2) — treats the woman as an object of acquisition. The word yaldāh (girl, young woman, H3207) rather than ʾiššāh (woman) in the description but ʾiššāh in the desired outcome — "as my wife" — shows Shechem casting the relationship in legitimate marital terms without acknowledging what he has done. The transaction is directed to the father (Hamor), following ANE betrothal custom where the father negotiates.
34.5: wĕyaʿăqōḇ šāmaʿ kî ṭimmēʾ ʾet-dînāh bittô ûḇānāyw hāyû ʾet-miqnēhû baśśāḏeh wĕheḥĕrîš yaʿăqōḇ ʿaḏ-bōʾām — "Jacob heard that his daughter Dinah had been defiled (ṭimmēʾ), but he kept silent (heḥĕrîš) until his sons came home." Ṭimmēʾ (Piel of ṭāmēʾ, H2930, to defile, make impure) encompasses both moral and ritual impurity — the same root used for the holiness code's pollution categories. Heḥĕrîš (Hiphil of ḥārash, H2790, to be silent, to make no sound) — Jacob's silence contrasts sharply with his sons' fury in v7. The brothers' anger will be described as ḥārāh (v7); Jacob's words in v30 reveal his silence was prudential rather than moral — he was calculating the risk to himself.
34.6: wayyēṣēʾ ḥămôr ʾăḇî-šĕḵem ʾel-yaʿăqōḇ lĕḏabbēr ʾittô — "Hamor went out to Jacob to speak with him." The verbs of movement (yāṣāʾ, went out) and speech (dāḇar, to speak) frame the formal diplomatic encounter. Hamor approaches Jacob directly, bypassing the convention of allowing the woman's family to initiate. This puts Jacob in a reactive rather than active position — he must listen to Hamor's proposal before the brothers arrive. The narrator's pace slows at this point, anticipating the brothers' arrival and their deceptive counter-proposal.
34.7: wĕhēmmāh ḥārû wayyihar ʾănaššîm mĕʾōḏ kî nĕḇālāh ʿāśāh bĕyiśrāʾēl liškōḇ ʾet-bat-yaʿăqōḇ wĕḵēn lōʾ yēʿāśeh — "They were furious, for Shechem had committed an outrage (nĕḇālāh) in Israel — a thing that ought not to be done." Nĕḇālāh (H5039, disgrace, shameful folly, outrage) is a technical legal term for the most serious violations of social and moral order — used for Achan's sin (Josh 7:15), Amnon's rape of Tamar (2 Sam 13:12: "don't do this nĕḇālāh"), and sexual disorder in Judges 19:23-24. The accompanying formula wĕḵēn lōʾ yēʿāśeh (and so it ought not to be done) is a legal prohibition-formula signaling that the act constitutes a category violation. The phrase "in Israel" (bĕyiśrāʾēl) anticipates the people of Israel before the nation exists — projecting the tribal identity backward onto Jacob's household.
34.8: wayyĕḏabbēr ḥămôr ʾittām lēʾmōr šĕḵem bĕnî ḥāšĕqāh napšô bĕḇitteḵem — "My son Shechem — his soul longs for your daughter." Ḥāšaq (H2836, to long for, to be strongly attached to) is a rare verb expressing intense desire or bonding — used for YHWH's attachment to Israel in Deut 7:7 ("YHWH set his love on you") and 10:15 ("YHWH delighted in your fathers"). The verb frames Shechem's desire in the language of covenant-election love, which makes Hamor's proposal sound more compelling while obscuring the assault that initiated it. The diplomatic presentation of Shechem's desire as ḥāšaq-love is a rhetorical strategy.
34.9: wĕhitḥattĕnû ʾōtānû bĕnōtêḵem tittĕnû-lānû wĕʾet-bĕnōtênû tiqqĕḥû lāḵem — "Intermarry with us — give us your daughters, take our daughters for yourselves." Hithḥatthēn (Hithpael of ḥātan, H2859, to become related by marriage) — the verb for entering the covenant of marital alliance. In Deut 7:3 this specific verb is prohibited for Israel with Canaanite peoples: lōʾ tithḥatthēn bām (you shall not intermarry with them). Hamor's proposal is precisely what the Deuteronomic law will forbid — the assimilation of the covenant people into Canaanite social structure through intermarriage. The brothers' response is a refusal to assimilate, though their means of refusal (deception and massacre) is not commended.
34.10: wĕʾittānû tēšēḇû wĕhāʾāreṣ tihyeh lipnêḵem šĕḇû wĕsĕḥārûhā wĕhēʾāḥăzû ḇāh — "Dwell with us; the land is before you — live in it, trade in it, acquire property in it." Three verbs — šāḇaṯ (dwell), sāḥar (H5503, to go about trading, to trade/traffic), ʾāḥaz (H270, to seize, hold, acquire) — describe the economic integration being offered. Sāḥar (the merchant-verb) is from the same root as Issachar and the commercial vocabulary of Genesis 37-45. Hamor's offer is genuine assimilation — not merely marital but economic and territorial. The phrase "the land is before you" (hāʾāreṣ tihyeh lipnêḵem) echoes the formulation YHWH will use for the promised land.
34.11: wayyōʾmer šĕḵem ʾel-ʾāḇîhā wĕʾel-ʾaḥêhā ʾemṣāʾ-ḥēn bĕʿênêḵem wĕʾăšer tōʾmĕrû ʾēlāy ʾettēn — "Let me find favor (ḥēn) in your eyes, and I will give whatever you say." Shechem appropriates the covenantal favor-language Jacob used before Esau (32:5; 33:8,10,15) and that Jacob had used before Laban (30:27). Māṣāʾ ḥēn bĕʿênê (to find grace/favor in the eyes of) is the formula of covenantal petition — seeking the acceptance of a greater party by a lesser. Shechem's adoption of this formula positions him as the suppliant; the irony is that he violated the very person in whose family he is now seeking inclusion.
34.12: harḇû ʿālay mĕʾōḏ môhar ûmattān wĕʾettĕnāh kaʾăšer tōʾmĕrû ʾēlāy ûtĕnû-lî ʾet-hannaʿărāh lĕʾiššāh — "Make the bride-price and gift very high — I will pay whatever you say." Môhar (H4119, bride-price) — the payment made by the groom's family to the bride's family, establishing the legal transfer of the woman into marriage. Mattān (H4976, gift) — an additional gift, perhaps to the bride herself. Shechem's willingness to pay any price reveals both his desire and his family's wealth; it also signals that the subsequent circumcision-demand is within reach for a wealthy household. The commercial vocabulary of marriage negotiation is explicit here — marriage as legal-economic transaction.
34.13: wayyaʿănû ḇĕnê-yaʿăqōḇ ʾet-šĕḵem wĕʾet-ḥămôr ʾāḇîw bĕmirmāh wayyĕḏabbĕrû ʾăšer ṭimmēʾ ʾēt dînāh ʾăḥōtām — "Jacob's sons answered Shechem and his father Hamor with deceit (bĕmirmāh)." Mirmāh (H4820, deceit, treachery) — the same word used for Jacob's own deceit of Isaac in 27:35: "your brother came bĕmirmāh and took your blessing." The narrator names the deception explicitly before describing the proposal, signaling that what follows is to be read as manipulation rather than covenant negotiation. The pattern of familial deception — Isaac deceived by Jacob, Jacob deceived by Laban, Shechem/Hamor deceived by Jacob's sons — runs through Genesis as a recurring moral motif without divine endorsement.
34.14: lōʾ nûḵal laʿăśōt haddāḇār hazzeh lātēt ʾet-ʾăḥōtênû lĕʾîš ʾăšer-lô ʿorlāh kî-ḥerpāh hîʾ lānû — "We cannot give our sister to a man who has a foreskin — that would be a disgrace (ḥerpāh) to us." Ḥerpāh (H2781, reproach, disgrace, public shame) — the same word Rachel used for barrenness (30:23: "God has taken away my reproach"). Circumcision as the boundary marker of covenant membership is deployed here as a deceptive demand — the brothers use the covenant sign as a weapon rather than as a genuine invitation to covenant inclusion. The irony is mordant: the sign of Abraham's covenant becomes the mechanism of its desecration.
34.15: bĕzōʾt niʾôt lāḵem ʾim tihyû ḵāmōnû lĕhimmōl lāḵem kol-zāḵār — "On this one condition we will agree with you — if you become like us by circumcising all your males." The condition is stated in terms of equivalence: lĕhimmōl lāḵem (to circumcise yourselves) and the goal "if you become like us" (ḵāmōnû). The brothers frame circumcision as the entry condition for covenant-community membership — which is its genuine theological meaning — but their purpose is not inclusion. The Niphal niʾôt (we will consent) from ʾāwāh (to desire, consent) marks their agreement as conditional and transactional.
34.16: wĕnāḇōʾ ʾittĕḵem wĕhāyînû lĕʿam ʾeḥāḏ — "We will come among you and become one people (lĕʿam ʾeḥāḏ)." The phrase ʿam ʾeḥāḏ (one people) — the goal of political assimilation. The covenant formula "one people" is the positive outcome of the brothers' deceptive negotiation. The narrator's reader, who has been told in v13 that this is mirmāh, knows this outcome will not be realized. The language of becoming "one people" will return in Ezra-Nehemiah's crisis of intermarriage and in the NT (Eph 2:14-15) as the actual theological reality in Christ.
34.17: wĕʾim-lōʾ tišmĕʿû ʾēlênû lāqaḥtî ʾet-bittênû wĕhālaḵnû — "If you do not listen to us, we will take our sister and go." The ultimatum employs lāqaḥ (take) — the same verb used for Shechem's taking of Dinah in v2. The brothers' threat mirrors the structure of Shechem's act: he took what was not given; they will take back what was taken. The rhetorical symmetry may be intentional — the narrative is structured around takings and counter-takings.
34.18: wayyîṭĕḇû diḇrêhem bĕʿênê ḥămôr ûḇĕʿênê šĕḵem ben-ḥămôr — "Their proposal seemed good in the eyes of Hamor and Shechem." The idiom yāṭaḇ bĕʿênê (to be good in the eyes of) is the standard formula for approval or assessment. Hamor and Shechem evaluate the proposal by the pleasure it brings Shechem and the commercial advantage it offers the city (v23: "will not their livestock become ours?"). The mutual evaluations — the brothers calculating deception (v13), the Shechemites calculating advantage (v23) — meet at the circumcision-covenant as the space where both groups believe they are getting what they want.
34.19: wĕlōʾ-ʾiḥar hannaʿar laʿăśôt haddāḇār kî ḥāpēṣ bĕḇat-yaʿăqōḇ wĕhûʾ niḵbāḏ mikkōl bêt ʾāḇîw — "The young man wasted no time doing it, for he delighted in Jacob's daughter; and he was the most honored (niḵbāḏ) in all his father's household." Niḵbāḏ (Niphal of kāḇaḏ, H3513, to be heavy/weighty/honored) — the most honored member of Hamor's household. The combination of social honor and emotional desire (ḥāpēṣ, H2654, delighted, took pleasure in) makes Shechem's compliance immediate. His haste and honor are both turned against him by the deception.
34.20: wayyāḇōʾ ḥămôr ûšĕḵem bĕnô ʾel-šaʿar ʿîrām wayyĕḏabbĕrû ʾel-ʾanĕšê ʿîrām lēʾmōr — "Hamor and his son Shechem came to the gate of their city and spoke to the men of their city." The city gate (šaʿar, H8179) was the civic assembly space and judicial forum in ANE cities — where elders sat, contracts were ratified, and legal cases adjudicated (cf. Ruth 4:1-12; Amos 5:12,15; Prov 31:23). Addressing the men at the gate is the formal civic mechanism for ratifying a community decision. Hamor and Shechem present the circumcision-covenant to the whole city.
34.21: hāʾănāšîm hāʾēlleh šĕlēmîm hēm ʾittānû wĕyēšĕḇû ḇāʾāreṣ wĕyisĕḥărû ḇāh wĕhāʾāreṣ hinněh raḥăḇat-yāḏayim lipnêhem — "These men are peaceable toward us. Let them live in the land and trade in it — the land is wide enough for them." Šĕlēmîm (peaceful, from šālôm) — Hamor describes Jacob's family as peaceable/covenant-friendly; the word ironically shares the root with the šālēm of 33:18 where Jacob arrived "safely." The commercial appeal (yisĕḥărû, to trade, same root as merchant) frames the proposal to the city in economic terms. The men of the city are convinced by the prospect of trade and land-sharing, not by love of Shechem.
34.22: ʾaḵ-bĕzōʾt yēʾōtû lānû hāʾănāšîm lāšeḇet ʾittānû lihyôt lĕʿam ʾeḥāḏ bĕhimmôl lānû kol-zāḵār — "Only on this condition will the men consent to live with us — to become one people, if every male among us is circumcised." The condition is presented as merely the procedural requirement for the commercial union. The city's acceptance of circumcision as a reasonable price for economic integration underscores that the sign of Abraham's covenant has been fully secularized in this negotiation — it is treated as a cultural adaptation for commercial advantage rather than as a covenant mark with theological content.
34.23: miqnēhem wĕqinyānām wĕkol-bĕhemtām halōʾ lānû hēm ʾaḵ nēʾôtāh lāhem wĕyēšĕḇû ʾittānû — "Won't their livestock, property, and all their animals become ours?" Hamor's commercial appeal to the city's men reveals the transaction's true logic: circumcision is the price for absorbing a wealthy pastoral clan. The rhetorical question (halōʾ) invites the men to calculate the economic benefit. This is the narrator's subtle indictment: both parties are transacting in bad faith — the brothers intending massacre, the Shechemites intending economic absorption.
34.24: wayyišmĕʿû ʾel-ḥămôr wĕʾel-šĕḵem bĕnô kol-yōṣĕʾê šaʿar ʿîrô wayyimmōlû kol-zāḵār — "All who went out of the city gate agreed with Hamor and Shechem, and every male was circumcised." The legal assembly (gate = civic forum) ratifies the decision unanimously. The mass circumcision of the city's males is presented as a collective civic act — one that will render every man in the city incapacitated on the third day (v25). The irony that the covenant sign of Israel becomes the mechanism of a city's destruction runs through the entire chapter.
34.25: wayhî bayyôm haššĕlîšî bihyôtām kōʾăḇîm wayyiqqĕḥû šĕnê ḇĕnê-yaʿăqōḇ šimʿôn wĕlēwî ʾăḥê dînāh ʾîš ḥarḇô wayyāḇōʾû ʿal-hāʿîr beṭaḥ — "On the third day, when they were in pain (kōʾăḇîm), two of Jacob's sons, Simeon and Levi, Dinah's brothers, took their swords and attacked the unsuspecting city." Bayyôm haššĕlîšî (on the third day) — the third day is a significant temporal marker throughout the OT: the day of accountability, of revelation, of reversal (cf. Gen 22:4 — Abraham's third-day arrival at Moriah; Hos 6:2 — "on the third day he will raise us up"). The pain (kōʾeḇ, H3510, anguish/pain) renders the entire male population defenseless. Beṭaḥ (H983, in security/unsuspecting) — the same word used for Israel's covenant-security promise when obedient (Lev 26:5; Deut 33:28); here it marks the city's vulnerability as the very security they were promised.
34.26: wĕʾet-ḥămôr wĕʾet-šĕḵem bĕnô hārĕgû lĕpî-ḥāreḇ wayyiqqĕḥû ʾet-dînāh mibbêt šĕḵem wayyēṣēʾû — "They killed Hamor and his son Shechem with the sword and took Dinah from Shechem's house and left." The inclusion of wayyiqqĕḥû ʾet-dînāh (they took Dinah) — using the same verb lāqaḥ as Shechem's taking in v2 — reframes the rescue as a counter-taking. The narrative throughout ch 34 is structured around lāqaḥ (take): Shechem took Dinah (v2), Jacob's sons took their swords (v25), they took Dinah back (v26), they took the city's livestock (v28). The tit-for-tat structure of taking frames the entire episode.
34.27: ḇĕnê yaʿăqōḇ bāʾû ʿal-haḥălālîm wayyāḇōzzû hāʿîr ʾăšer ṭimmĕʾû ʾăḥôtām — "The sons of Jacob came upon the dead and plundered the city that had defiled their sister." The narrator calls them ḇĕnê yaʿăqōḇ (sons of Jacob) rather than ḇĕnê yiśrāʾēl (sons of Israel) — the pre-covenant name, perhaps marking the action as falling short of covenant identity. ḇāzaz (H962, to plunder, spoil) — the verb of warfare plunder. The stated justification — "the city that defiled their sister" — applies the guilt of Shechem to the entire city, a collective attribution the text leaves morally unresolved.
34.28: ʾet-ṣōʾnām wĕʾet-bĕqārām wĕʾet-ḥămōrêhem wĕʾēt ʾăšer-bāʿîr wĕʾet-ʾăšer baśśāḏeh lāqāḥû — "Their flocks and herds and donkeys — whatever was in the city and in the field — they took." The comprehensive plunder — ṣōʾn (flocks), bāqār (cattle), ḥămōr (donkeys) — is the full pastoral inventory. The inventory formula (livestock + field) is standard for plunder descriptions in the OT warfare tradition (cf. Num 31:9; Josh 8:27). The magnitude of the plunder transforms a personal honor-crime into a war of conquest with its full economic consequences.
34.29: wĕʾet-kol-ḥêlām wĕʾet-kol-ṭappām wĕʾet-nĕšêhem šāḇû wayyāḇōzzû wĕʾēt kol-ʾăšer babbāyit — "All their wealth and all their children and their women they took captive, and they plundered everything in the houses." The taking of women and children as captives (šāḇā, H7617, to take captive) is a war practice the Deuteronomic law will regulate (Deut 20:14; 21:10-14). Jacob's explicit protest in v30 focuses on practical danger to his household, not on the moral dimensions of enslaving the survivors.
34.30: wayyōʾmer yaʿăqōḇ ʾel-šimʿôn wĕʾel-lēwî ʿăḵarttem ʾōtî lĕhabbîʾšenî bĕyōšēḇ hāʾāreṣ baKĕnaʿănî ûḇapperizzî — "Jacob said to Simeon and Levi, 'You have brought trouble on me (ʿăḵarttem) by making me odious (lehabbîšenî)…'" ʿāḵar (H5916, to trouble, to roil/disturb) — the same root as the name Achan (Josh 7:1) and the Valley of Achor; it means to trouble someone by stirring up difficulty that brings consequences. Bahʾ (Hiphil of bāʾaš, H887, to stink/make odious) — Jacob's concern is his reputation and the safety of his household among the Canaanites, not the justice or injustice of the act. The contrast between Jacob's self-focused prudential concern (vv30) and the brothers' honor-defense (v31) is the moral fulcrum of the chapter.
34.31: wayyōʾmĕrû hăḵizonāh yaʿăśeh ʾet-ʾăḥōtênû — "Shall he treat our sister like a prostitute (zônāh)?" Zônāh (H2181, harlot, prostitute — Qal participle of zānāh, to commit sexual immorality) — the brothers' final word reframes Dinah's defilement through the honor-shame category of sexual availability to multiple men without covenant. In their logic, Shechem's act without marriage-covenant treated Dinah as a zônāh — a woman available outside covenant protection. The question-form of their closing statement is rhetorically unanswerable; Jacob's objection is not replied to. The narrative ends without resolving the competing claims — Jacob's safety, the brothers' honor, Dinah's silence.

35.1: wayyōʾmer ʾĕlōhîm ʾel-yaʿăqōḇ qûm ʿălēh ḇêt-ʾēl wĕšeḇ-šām wĕʿăśēh-šām mizbēaḥ lāʾēl hannirʾeh ʾêleḵā bĕḇorḥăḵā mippĕnê ʿēśāw ʾāḥîḵā — "Go up to Bethel and settle there, and build an altar to God who appeared to you when you fled from your brother Esau." ʿĂlēh (Qal imperative of ʿālāh, to go up) — ascending to Bethel is an ʿălîyāh (ascent); Bethel is hill country above the Jordan valley. The description of God as hannirʾeh ʾêleḵā bĕḇorḥăḵā (who appeared to you when you were fleeing) recapitulates the Bethel theophany of ch 28 as the constitutive moment of Jacob's covenant relationship. The command comes after the violence of ch 34; the return to the altar is a covenant-renewal following moral contamination.
35.2: wayyōʾmer yaʿăqōḇ ʾel-bêtô wĕʾel kol-ʾăšer ʿimmô hāsirû ʾet-ʾĕlōhê hannēḵār ʾăšer bĕtōḵĕḵem wĕhiṭṭahărû wĕhahălîpû śimlōtêḵem — "Put away the foreign gods among you, purify yourselves, and change your garments." The triad — remove foreign gods (ʾĕlōhê hannēḵār, H5236, strange/foreign gods), purify (ṭāhēr, ritual purification), change garments (ḥālaph śimlāh) — is the preparation formula for meeting a holy God. The identical preparation is commanded before the Sinai theophany in Exod 19:10,14 (wash garments, consecrate).
