Judah's movement "up" (wayyaʿal) to Timnah while Tamar acts to intercept him is the narrative setup.
38.13: wayyuggad lĕtāmār lēʾmōr hinnēh ḥāmîḵ ʿōleh timnatāh liggōz ṣōʾnô — "Tamar was told, 'Your father-in-law is on his way up to Timnah to shear his sheep.'" Tamar's being informed by an unnamed messenger (wayyuggad — Hophal: it was told) — the passive form suggests a community network of information. Tamar has been waiting in her father's house without Shelah being given to her; the sheep-shearing occasion presents the opportunity for action. The narrator presents Tamar as a strategic agent responding to information: she has been denied her legal right, and she will act to claim it through the only avenue available.
38.14: waṯṯāsar bigḏê ʾalmĕnûtāh mēʿāleyhā waṯṯĕḵas baṣṣāʿîp waṯṯitʿallāp waṯṯēšeḇ bĕpetaḥ ʿênayim ʾăšer ʿal-dereḵ timnatāh — "She removed her widow's garments and covered herself with a veil to disguise herself." Bigḏê ʾalmĕnûtāh (widow's garments, from ʾalmānāh) — the clothing of social status; removing them is an act of identity-disguise. Ṣāʿîp (H6809, veil/wrapper) — a large covering garment that obscures identity. Waṯṯitʿallāp (wrapped herself, Hithpael of ʿālap, H5968, to cover/veil oneself) — she actively performs the disguise. Petaḥ ʿênayim (opening of the eyes/Enaim) — possibly a place name meaning "two springs" or "crossroads"; the narrator's note that it was "on the road to Timnah" makes Judah's encounter unavoidable. Her motive is explicitly stated: Shelah had grown up and she had not been given to him.
38.15: wayyirʾehā yĕhûḏāh wayyaḥšĕḇehā lĕzônāh kî kissĕtāh pānêhā — "Judah saw her and took her for a prostitute (zônāh), for she had covered her face." Zônāh (H2181, harlot/prostitute — Qal participle of zānāh, to commit sexual immorality) — Judah's assumption based on the covered face and the roadside position. Notably, Judah will use a different word in v21 when he instructs Hirah to find her: qĕḏēšāh (H6948, a consecrated/dedicated woman — a cultic term for a woman devoted to a deity, often associated with temple prostitution in ANE practice). Hirah uses the more socially respectable term; the men of the place say there was no qĕḏēšāh there. The distinction between zônāh (ordinary prostitute) and qĕḏēšāh (sacred devotee) reflects two different social-religious categories, and Judah/Hirah's word choice tracks this distinction.
38.16: wayyēṭ ʾēleyhā ʾel-haddereḵ wayyōʾmer hābāh-nāʾ ʾāḇôʾāh ʾēlayin kî lōʾ yāḏaʿ kî kallātô hîʾ — "He turned aside to her on the road and said, 'Come now, let me come to you' — for he did not know she was his daughter-in-law." The narrator's moral-ironic note (kî lōʾ yāḏaʿ kî kallātô hîʾ — he did not know she was his daughter-in-law) is repeated at v26 for structural emphasis. The unknowing frames Judah's act as unconscious but not innocent — he is propositioning a woman at a roadside, which is itself the moral problem. The subsequent recognition in v26 will require him to acknowledge the double-irony: the woman he violated is more righteous than he is.
38.17: wayyōʾmer ʾānōḵî ʾăšallaḥ gĕḏî-ʿizzîm min-haṣṣōʾn waṯṯōʾmer ʾim-tittēn ʿērāḇôn ʿaḏ šolḥeḵā — "'I will send a young goat from the flock,' he said. 'Will you give a pledge until you send it?'" Gĕḏî-ʿizzîm (kid/young goat from the goat-flock) — the same animal used for Jacob's deception of Isaac (27:9: goat-kids for the savory dish; 27:16: goat-skins for the arm). ʿĒrāḇôn (H6162, pledge/security deposit) — a commercial-legal term used only here in the OT (vv17,18,20); cognate with the Akkadian erābū and Greek arrabon (Eph 1:14: "the Spirit as the pledge/deposit of our inheritance"). The pledge transforms a transactional encounter into a legally binding arrangement — Judah must reclaim his identifying items or remain legally accountable.
38.18: wayyōʾmer mah-hāʿērāḇôn ʾăšer ʾettēn-lāḵ waṯṯōʾmer ḥôtāmĕḵā ûpĕtîleḵā ûmaṭṭĕḵā ʾăšer bĕyāḏeḵā — "She answered, 'Your seal, your cord, and your staff in your hand.'" Ḥôtām (H2368, signet seal — a cylinder seal or stamp seal worn on a cord used to authenticate documents and ownership) + pĕtîl (H6616, cord/thread/bracelet — the cord on which the seal hangs) + maṭṭeh (H4294, staff/rod — also the word for a tribal staff/scepter). These three items are Judah's most personally identifying possessions — the equivalent of modern ID documents. Tamar's shrewd choice of pledge items ensures that when the truth is revealed, the evidence is irrefutable. The maṭṭeh (staff) in particular carries the weight of tribal authority: the same word is used for Jacob's tribal staffs (ch 30) and for Moses's staff before Pharaoh.
38.19: waṯṯāqom waṯṯēleḵ waṯṯāsar ṣāʿîpāh mēʿāleyhā waṯṯilḇaš bigḏê ʾalmĕnûtāh — "She rose and went, removing her veil and putting on her widow's garments again." The reversal is complete: the widow's garments come off for the disguise; they go back on afterward. Tamar's act is not permanent abandonment of her status but a targeted intervention. The garment-change as identity-transformation is a recurring Genesis motif (Joseph's coat of many colors: status; Rebekah's disguising Jacob; Jacob changing clothes for Bethel in 35:2); here it is Tamar who controls the garment-sign.
38.20: wayyišlaḥ yĕhûḏāh ʾet-gĕḏî hāʿizzîm bĕyaḏ rēʿēhû hāʿăḏullāmî lāqaḥat hāʿērāḇôn miyyaḏ hāʾiššāh wĕlōʾ mĕṣāʾāh — "Judah sent the young goat by his friend the Adullamite, to recover the pledge from the woman, but he did not find her." The recovery attempt uses the intermediary Hirah, possibly because Judah is embarrassed to return in person. The failure to find her sets up the inquiry of v21 — Hirah asks for a qĕḏēšāh (shrine devotee) rather than a zônāh (prostitute), using the more dignified term even in this awkward errand.
38.21: wayyišʾal ʾet-ʾanĕšê mĕqōmāh lēʾmōr ʾayyēh haqqĕḏēšāh hîʾ ʿal-hāʿênayim ʿal-haddāreḵ wayyōʾmĕrû lōʾ-hāyĕtāh ḇāzeh qĕḏēšāh — "He asked the men of the place, 'Where is the shrine devotee (qĕḏēšāh) who was beside the road at Enaim?' 'There has been no shrine devotee here,' they said." Qĕḏēšāh (H6948, feminine of qāḏôš = holy/set-apart — but in this cultic context designating a woman dedicated to a deity, often associated with fertility cult in Canaanite religion) — Hirah's use of this term rather than zônāh (common prostitute) reflects either social courtesy or his belief that the veiled woman at the roadside was a cultic figure. The men's answer — there has been no qĕḏēšāh here — closes the inquiry. The evidence of the pledge items remains with Tamar.
38.22: wayyāšāḇ ʾel-yĕhûḏāh wayyōʾmer lōʾ mĕṣāʾtîhā wĕgam ʾanĕšê hammāqôm ʾāmĕrû lōʾ-hāyĕtāh ḇāzeh qĕḏēšāh — "He returned to Judah and reported, 'I didn't find her; and the men of the place said there has been no shrine devotee here.'" Hirah's report closes the pledge-recovery attempt. The word qĕḏēšāh appears twice in vv21-22 — both times by Hirah. The narrator has Judah use zônāh in v15 (his private assumption) and v23 (his frustrated resignation); Hirah consistently uses the more honorable term. The distinction traces the social distance between Judah's private act and its public presentation.
38.23: wayyōʾmer yĕhûḏāh tiqqaḥ-lāh pen niḇûz hinnēh šālaḥtî haggĕḏî hazzeh wĕʾattāh lōʾ mĕṣāʾtāh — "Judah said, 'Let her keep the pledge, or we will become a laughingstock (lĕḇûz). At least I sent the young goat, but you couldn't find her.'" Lĕḇûz (contempt/laughingstock, H937, from bāzāh, to despise) — Judah's concern is reputation: being known as the man who could not recover his pledge from a prostitute is humiliating. The shame-concern leads him to abandon the search. His words "at least I sent the goat" (šālaḥtî haggĕḏî) acknowledge his contractual obligation without resolving the unredeemed pledge — which remains in Tamar's possession as the evidence she needs.
38.24: wayhî kĕmiššĕlōšāh ḥŏḏāšîm wayyuggad lĕyĕhûḏāh lēʾmōr zāntāh tāmār kallāteḵā wĕgam hinnēh hārāh liznûnîm wayyōʾmer yĕhûḏāh hôṣîʾûhā wĕtissārēp — "About three months later Judah was told, 'Your daughter-in-law Tamar has played the harlot, and indeed she is pregnant from harlotry.' Judah said, 'Bring her out and burn her!'" The three-month interval allows the pregnancy to be visible and unmistakable. Judah's pronouncement of capital punishment — wĕtissārēp (let her be burned, Niphal of śārap, H8313, to burn) — reflects the penalty for a betrothed woman's sexual infidelity (Lev 21:9 prescribes burning specifically for the daughter of a priest; the broader principle of Deut 22:23-24 prescribes stoning). The irony is that Judah is pronouncing sentence on the very act he initiated — and the sentence reveals his moral double standard: he propositioned a roadside woman but expects his daughter-in-law to maintain sexual fidelity to a levirate promise he has not honored.
38.25: hîʾ mûṣĕʾāh wĕhîʾ šālĕḥāh ʾel-ḥāmîhā lēʾmōr lĕʾîš ʾăšer-ʾēlleh lô ʾānōḵî hārāh waṯṯōʾmer hakkēr-nāʾ lĕmî haḥōtāmet wĕhapĕtîlîm wĕhammaṭṭeh hāʾēlleh — "As she was brought out, she sent word to her father-in-law: 'I am pregnant by the man whose these are.' And she said, 'Recognize, please, whose seal, cord, and staff these are.'" Hakkēr-nāʾ (recognize/identify, please — Hiphil imperative of nāḵar, H5234, with the particle nāʾ) — this is the precise verbal formula used in 37:32 when Joseph's brothers brought his blood-stained coat to Jacob: hakkēr-nāʾ hakkĕtōnet bĭnĕḵā hîʾ (recognize please, is this your son's coat?). The same words by which Jacob was deceived about Joseph are now spoken to Judah about Tamar — a narrative wordplay linking the two deceptions. Tamar's method of disclosure is indirect — she sends the evidence rather than accusing directly, allowing Judah to make the identification himself.
38.26: wayyakkēr yĕhûḏāh wayyōʾmer ṣāḏĕqāh mimmennî kî-ʿal-kēn lōʾ-nĕtattîhā lĕšēlāh bĕnî wĕlōʾ-yāsap ʿôḏ lĕḏaʿtāh — "Judah recognized them and said, 'She is more righteous than I (ṣāḏĕqāh mimmennî), for I did not give her to my son Shelah.'" Ṣāḏĕqāh mimmennî — the Qal perfect of ṣāḏaq (H6663, to be righteous, to be in the right) with the comparative preposition min: "she is more in the right than I." This is one of the most direct moral self-indictments in Genesis — a patriarchal figure explicitly acknowledging a woman's superior righteousness. Judah names his own sin of omission: lōʾ nĕtattîhā lĕšēlāh (I did not give her to Shelah). Tamar acted under the pressure of a denied legal right; Judah acted out of self-interest. Wĕlōʾ-yāsap ʿôḏ lĕḏaʿtāh — "he knew her no more" — closing the sexual relationship permanently.
38.27: wayhî bĕʿēt liḏtāh wĕhinnēh tĕʾômîm bĕḇiṭnāh — "When it was time for her to give birth, behold, twins were in her womb." The exclamatory wĕhinnēh (and behold!) marks the surprise: a twin birth. Twin births in Genesis are theologically significant: Esau and Jacob (25:24) were twins whose birth order was reversed; now Perez and Zerah's reversed emergence will replay the motif. The twin birth signals a covenant-structural event rather than a routine genealogy.
38.28: wayhî bĕliḏtāh wayyittēn-yāḏ waṯṯiqqaḥ hamĕyalleḏet waṯṯiqšōr ʿal-yāḏô šānî lēʾmōr zeh yāṣāʾ riʾšōnāh — "As she gave birth, one put out his hand; the midwife tied a scarlet thread (šānî) on his wrist and said, 'This one came out first.'" Šānî (H8144, scarlet/crimson) — the scarlet thread designating the firstborn echoes the scarlet thread that Rahab will hang from her window to mark her household for protection (Josh 2:18-21). The scarlet thread as a marking-device that saves and identifies recurs in both texts, though the specific connections are typological rather than direct allusion.
38.29: wayhî kĕmēšîḇ yāḏô wĕhinnēh yāṣāʾ ʾāḥîw waṯṯōʾmer mah-pāraṣtā ʿāleḵā pāreṣ wayyiqrāʾ šĕmô pāreṣ — "But as he drew back his hand, his brother came out! She said, 'What a breaking-out you have broken! (mah-pāraṣtā ʿāleḵā pāreṣ).' So he was named Perez." Pāreṣ (H6557) from pāraṣ (H6555, to break through, burst out, make a breach) — the birth-reversal naming-speech: the one who seemed to come second burst through first. Pāraṣ is the verb of covenant-overflow: Gen 28:14 (your offspring shall pāraṣ in all directions), 30:30 and 43 (Jacob's flocks pāraṣ). Perez is the direct ancestor of David (Ruth 4:18-22: "Perez begot Hezron... Obed begot Jesse... Jesse begot David") and of the Messiah (Matt 1:3). The birth-reversal of the Tamar narrative — the unexpected supplanter rather than the designated heir — joins the chain of covenant-reversals in Genesis: Abel/Cain, Isaac/Ishmael, Jacob/Esau, Joseph/Reuben, Perez/Zerah.
38.30: wĕʾaḥar yāṣāʾ ʾāḥîw ʾăšer ʿal-yāḏô hašŝānî wayyiqrāʾ šĕmô zāraḥ — "Then his brother who had the scarlet thread on his wrist came out, and he was named Zerah." Zāraḥ (H2226) — possibly related to zāraḥ (H2224, to rise, shine — used for the sun's rising) or zārak (to sprinkle/scatter). The "shining one" who was marked first but born second — his name may encode the light of the scarlet thread that promised him precedence. Zerah appears in the genealogies (1 Chron 2:4,6) but it is Perez who carries the covenant line to David. The narrative principle is established: the thread of human expectation is not the thread of divine election.

39.1: wĕyôsēp hûraḏ miṣrāymāh wayyiqnēhû potîpar sĕrîs parʿōh sar haṭṭabbāḥîm ʾîš miṣrî miyyaḏ hayišmĕʿēlîm ʾăšer hôrĕḏuhû šāmmāh — "Joseph had been brought down to Egypt. Potiphar, a court official of Pharaoh, captain of the guard (sar haṭṭabbāḥîm), an Egyptian, bought him from the Ishmaelites." Hûraḏ (Hophal of yāraḏ) — "was brought down" — the passive descent; Joseph is not acting but being acted upon. Sar haṭṭabbāḥîm (captain of the slaughterers/bodyguard, H2876 ṭabbāḥ) — ṭabbāḥ literally means slaughterer/butcher; the title refers to the royal executioner-bodyguard, the officer responsible for the king's personal security and capital punishment. The same title appears in 2 Kgs 25:8 for Nebuchadnezzar's commander who destroys Jerusalem. Joseph has been bought by the man who controls life and death in Pharaoh's household — a narratively significant placement.
39.2: wayhî YHWH ʾet-yôsēp wayhî ʾîš maṣlîaḥ wayhî bĕḇêt ʾăḏōnāyw hammiṣrî — "YHWH was with Joseph, so he prospered (maṣlîaḥ); and he lived in the house of his Egyptian master." The formula YHWH ʾet-yôsēp (YHWH was with Joseph) — the covenant-presence formula of the patriarchal tradition: "I will be with you" (26:3 to Isaac; 28:15 to Jacob; 31:3 to Jacob). This is the first time in the Joseph narrative that YHWH is named as an active agent; the name had been absent since Jacob's last theophany (35:9-15). Maṣlîaḥ (prospering, Hiphil participle of ṣālaḥ, H6743, to succeed, advance, thrive) — the prosperity is explicitly attributed to divine presence, not to Joseph's native ability. The pattern will repeat at v21 and 23, forming the structural skeleton of the chapter.
39.3: wayyarʾ ʾăḏōnāyw kî YHWH ʾittô wĕḵōl ʾăšer-hûʾ ʿōśeh YHWH maṣlîaḥ bĕyāḏô — "His master saw that YHWH was with him and that YHWH gave him success in everything he did." The remarkable statement that an Egyptian recognized YHWH's presence in Joseph — a pagan slave-master discerning the divine hand behind his slave's work. This recognition pattern — non-Israelites seeing YHWH's blessing on the covenant people — recurs throughout the Joseph narrative (39:5; 40:15 implies Potiphar's household; 41:38-39 Pharaoh) and foreshadows the Exodus account where Pharaoh and Egypt will be forced to recognize YHWH (Exod 9:16; 14:18). Maṣlîaḥ bĕyāḏô (succeeded in his hand) — "in his hand" frames success as something YHWH places in Joseph's hands.
39.4: wayyimṣāʾ yôsēp ḥēn bĕʿênāyw wayyĕšāret ʾōtô wayyapqidēhû ʿal-bêtô wĕḵol-yeš-lô nātan bĕyāḏô — "Joseph found favor (ḥēn) in his eyes and became his attendant. He put him in charge of his household and entrusted to his care all that he owned." Māṣāʾ ḥēn (found grace/favor) — the covenant-favor formula (6:8 for Noah; 30:27 for Laban; 32:5 for Jacob before Esau). Mĕšārēt (personal attendant/minister, Piel participle of šārat, H8334, to serve, minister) — the term used for Joshua as Moses's attendant (Exod 24:13; Josh 1:1), for temple service, and for angelic service (Ps 103:21). Joseph's role in Potiphar's household mirrors the role of a Joseph-type in every subsequent context: elevated to administrator, entrusted with total care.
39.5: wayhî mēʾāz hipqîḏ ʾōtô bĕḇêtô wĕʿal kol-ʾăšer yĕš-lô wayyĕḇāreḵ YHWH ʾet-bêt hammiṣrî biglal yôsēp — "From the time he put him in charge of his household and all he owned, YHWH blessed the Egyptian's household for Joseph's sake." The blessing-formula wayyĕḇāreḵ YHWH ʾet-bêt hammiṣrî biglal yôsēp is the Abrahamic covenant promise extending outward through Joseph: "I will bless those who bless you" (12:3). The Egyptian household is blessed not because of anything Egyptian, but through the covenant-bearer in their midst. The same pattern will appear in 39:23 (prison), 41:37-45 (all of Egypt), and later in Daniel, Esther, and Ezra — the covenant people in exile blessing the surrounding nations.
39.6: wĕyôsēp hāyāh yĕpeh-tōʾar wîpeh marʾeh — "Now Joseph was well-built and handsome (yĕpeh-tōʾar wîpeh marʾeh)." The double beauty formula — yĕpeh-tōʾar (beautiful of form/shape) + yĕpeh marʾeh (beautiful of appearance/face) — is identical to the description of his mother Rachel in 29:17 (yĕpat-tōʾar wîpat marʾeh). Joseph inherits Rachel's beauty formula — a narrative signal connecting mother and son that the reader of the prior chapters will recognize. The same superlative double appears for Esther (Est 2:7) and David is described with a similar formula (1 Sam 16:12). The beauty serves the narrative: Potiphar's wife will see and desire (v7), echoing the pattern of the predatory gaze (Shechem/Dinah in 34:2).
39.7: wayhî ʾaḥar haddĕḇārîm hāʾēlleh waṯṯiśśāʾ ʾēšet-ʾăḏōnāyw ʾet-ʿênêhā ʾel-yôsēp waṯṯōʾmer šiḵḇāh ʿimmî — "After these things his master's wife cast her eyes on Joseph and said, 'Lie with me!'" Waṯṯiśśāʾ... ʾet-ʿênêhā (she lifted her eyes) — the predatory gaze construction used for Shechem (34:2: "he saw her"), for Lot's wife (19:26), and for Laban (29:1: Jacob lifted his feet after looking). Here it is the woman who is the desiring agent. The invitation šiḵḇāh ʿimmî (lie with me) is bare, direct, and without the rhetorical setup Potiphar's wife will provide in v17 when she accuses Joseph — there the same words become a false accusation.
39.8: wayĕmāʾēn wayyōʾmer ʾel-ʾēšet ʾăḏōnāyw hēn ʾăḏōnî lōʾ-yāḏaʿ ʾittî mah-babbāyit wĕḵōl ʾăšer-yeš-lô nātan bĕyāḏî — "But he refused. He said to his master's wife, 'My master does not concern himself with anything in the house; everything he owns he has put in my care.'" Joseph's refusal is grounded in the covenant-faithfulness to Potiphar: the trust placed in him. Wayĕmāʾēn (he refused, Piel of māʾen, H3985) — a strong refusal-verb; used for Moses's refusal to be called Pharaoh's son (Heb 11:24), for Israel's refusal to be comforted (Gen 37:35), and for Pharaoh's refusal to let Israel go (Exod 7:14). Joseph's refusal of Potiphar's wife anticipates these later refusals-in-faith.
39.9: ʾêḵāh ʾeʿĕśeh hārāʿāh hag-gĕḏōlāh hazzōʾt wĕḥāṭāʾtî lēʾlōhîm — "How could I do this great evil and sin against God?" Wĕḥāṭāʾtî lēʾlōhîm (and sin against God, H430) — Joseph grounds the refusal not only in loyalty to Potiphar (rāʿāh lēʾḏōnî, evil against my master, v8) but in theological ethics: sin against God. The word ḥāṭāʾ (H2398, to sin, to miss the mark, to commit a moral offense) — the standard OT term for moral and religious transgression. Joseph's theological framing of sexual ethics is remarkable in the narrative context: he identifies the sin as directed against God, not merely as a breach of social loyalty. This anticipates the covenant ethic of the Decalogue (Exod 20:14) and psalmic wisdom (Ps 51:4: "against you, you only, have I sinned").
39.10: wayhî kĕḏabbĕrāh ʾel-yôsēp yôm yôm wĕlōʾ-šāmaʿ ʾēleyhā liškōḇ ʾeṣlāh lihyôt ʿimmāh — "She spoke to Joseph day after day; he refused to lie beside her or be with her." The phrase yôm yôm (day after day, day by day) — the persistence of the temptation. The narrative's insistence on Joseph's sustained refusal over repeated solicitation is theologically significant: his virtue is not a single act of will but a sustained pattern. Wĕlōʾ-šāmaʿ ʾēleyhā (he did not listen to her) — using the hearing-verb šāmaʿ for moral compliance: to "hear" someone was to obey; his refusal to "hear" her is his moral consistency.
39.11: wayhî kĕhayyôm hazzeh wayyāḇōʾ habbāytāh laʿăśôt mĕlaʾḵtô wĕʾên ʾîš mēʾanĕšê habbayit šām babbāyit — "One day he went into the house to attend to his duties, and none of the household servants was inside." The narrative detail — no household servants present — is the circumstantial condition that makes Potiphar's wife's entrapment possible. Laʿăśôt mĕlaʾḵtô (to do his work/task) — Joseph is present for professional duty, not for personal desire. The absence of witnesses is the condition for both the assault and the false accusation that follows; there is no corroborating testimony for either party.
39.12: waṯṯiṯpĕśēhû bĕḇiḡḏô lēʾmōr šiḵḇāh ʿimmî wayyaʿăzōḇ biḡḏô bĕyāḏāh wayyānos wayyēṣēʾ haḥûṣāh — "She grabbed him by his garment (ḇegeḏ) saying 'Lie with me!' But he left his garment in her hand and fled outside." Begeḏ (H899, garment/cloak) — garments in the Joseph narrative are consistently the material sign of identity, status, and evidence: the coat of many colors (37:3,23,31-32: torn and blood-stained as evidence of death), this garment (39:12-16: as evidence of assault), the royal robes of ch 41 (as evidence of elevation), the five changes of clothes for Benjamin (45:22). Joseph leaves his begeḏ as involuntary evidence — the same mechanism by which his coat was used against him in ch 37. Wayyānos (he fled, from nûs, H5127) — the same verb used in Exod 14:25 for the Egyptians fleeing from Israel and in Joseph's blessing (49:21).
39.13: wayhî kirʾōtāh kî-ʿāzaḇ biḡḏô bĕyāḏāh wayyānos haḥûṣāh — "When she saw that he had left his garment in her hand and fled outside…" The begeḏ becomes the evidence-item for her false accusation. The same garment that Potiphar's wife grabbed as an instrument of seduction is now transformed into an instrument of accusation. The repeated mention of the garment (vv12,13,15,16,18) shows that the narrator wants the reader to track the garment's transformation from seduction-tool to false-evidence.
39.14: waṯṯiqrāʾ lĕʾanĕšê ḇêtāh waṯṯōʾmer lāhem lēʾmōr rĕʾû hēḇîʾ lānû ʾîš ʿiḇrî lĕṣaḥeq ḇānû — "She called to the men of the household and said, 'See, he brought us a Hebrew (ʿiḇrî) to mock us!'" ʿIḇrî (Hebrew, H5680, from ʿēḇer, H5677 — one from beyond/the other side; the ethnic designation for Israelites used by outsiders) — Potiphar's wife uses the ethnic label as a derogatory marker: "this Hebrew" (ʾîš ʿiḇrî) dehumanizes Joseph by his foreignness. In the Joseph narrative, "Hebrew" is always used by Egyptians about Joseph (39:14,17; 41:12) or by Joseph self-identifying to the cupbearer (40:15). The ethnic insult functions to deflect suspicion from herself by making Joseph's alleged aggression a racial as well as personal offense.
39.15: wayhî kĕhārîmî qôlî wāʾeqrāʾ wayyaʿăzōḇ beḡḏô ʾeṣlî wayyānos haḥûṣāh — "When I raised my voice and screamed, he left his garment beside me and ran outside." The false accusation inverts the narrative: her grabbing becomes his fleeing; his leaving the garment (which she grabbed) becomes evidence of his assault. The begeḏ left "beside me" (ʾeṣlî) is the linchpin of her case — physical evidence she controls and can present to Potiphar. The identical language between her accusation and the narrator's account (vv12-13) allows the reader to track what actually happened versus what she claims.
39.16: waṯṯannah beḡḏô ʾeṣlāh ʿaḏ-bôʾ ʾăḏōnāyw ʾel-bêtô — "She kept his garment beside her until his master came home." The preserved garment is deliberate: it functions as exhibit A. The time gap — keeping it until Potiphar returns — suggests she is preparing her narrative rather than acting in immediate outrage. The narrator notes this without comment; the detail itself indicts her.
39.17: waṯṯĕḏabbēr ʾēlāyw kaddĕḇārîm hāʾēlleh lēʾmōr bāʾ-ʾēlay hāʿeḇeḏ hāʿiḇrî ʾăšer-hēḇēʾtā lānû lĕṣaḥeq bî — "She told him this same story: 'The Hebrew slave (hāʿeḇeḏ hāʿiḇrî) you brought us came to me to mock me.'" Now the double label is applied: hāʿeḇeḏ hāʿiḇrî (the Hebrew slave) — both his slave-status and his ethnicity are deployed as the accusation's framing. Before the servants (v14) she said "a Hebrew man"; before Potiphar she says "the Hebrew slave" — the second formulation adds the social-status insult to the ethnic one. The phrase implicates Potiphar himself ("you brought us") — she holds him partially responsible for bringing this danger into their house.
39.18: wayhî kĕhārîmî qôlî wāʾeqrāʾ wayyaʿăzōḇ beḡḏô ʾeṣlî wayyānos haḥûṣāh — "When I raised my voice and screamed, he left his garment beside me and ran outside." The verbatim repetition of v15 is the narrator's device for emphasizing the lie's consistency — she tells the same story twice to make it stick. The repetition also underscores what the reader already knows to be false, creating the tension between testimony and truth that will only be resolved by God's providence (41:1).
39.19: wayhî kišmōaʿ ʾăḏōnāyw ʾet-diḇrê ʾištô ʾăšer dibbĕrāh ʾēlāyw lēʾmōr kaddĕḇārîm hāʾēlleh ʿāśāh lî ʿaḇdeḵā wayyiḥar ʾappô — "When his master heard his wife's story, his anger burned." Wayyiḥar ʾappô (his anger burned, H2734 + H639 — literally "his nose burned hot") — the standard idiom for intense anger; ʾap (nose) because the nostrils flare with rage. The narrator does not say Potiphar believed his wife; only that his anger burned. The subsequent punishment (imprisonment rather than execution — the expected penalty for rape of a master's wife) may suggest Potiphar's partial skepticism: he punishes but does not execute, possibly because he knows Joseph's character.
39.20: wayyiqqaḥ ʾăḏōnê yôsēp ʾōtô wayyittĕnēhû ʾel-bêt hassohar mĕqôm ʾăšer-ʾăsûrê hammelek ʾăsûrîm — "Joseph's master put him in the prison (bêt hassohar), the place where the king's prisoners were held." Bêt hassohar (house of the sohar, H5470) — sohar appears to be an Egyptian loanword or a Hebrew word for a circular/round place (sāḥar, H5437, to go around); possibly referring to an Egyptian-style round or pit-prison. The location — "where the king's prisoners were confined" — is a royal facility, higher-status than a common jail. This placement is significant: Joseph will meet Pharaoh's own officials (the cupbearer and baker in ch 40) precisely because they are imprisoned in the same royal facility. What appears to be a punishment is actually a providential placement.
39.21: wayhî YHWH ʾet-yôsēp wayyēṭ ʾēlāyw ḥāseḏ wayyittēn ḥinnô bĕʿênê śar bêt-hassohar — "But YHWH was with Joseph and showed him ḥeseḏ (covenant-loyalty/lovingkindness) and gave him favor (ḥēn) in the eyes of the prison warden." The renewal of the YHWH ʾet-yôsēp formula (from v2) at the transition to prison is the structural signal: the divine presence does not depend on circumstance. Ḥeseḏ (H2617, covenant-loyalty, steadfast love, lovingkindness) — the most theologically dense word in the OT's relational vocabulary; it carries the weight of covenant obligation, faithfulness to relationship, and active benevolence. YHWH's ḥeseḏ is the foundation of the psalmic covenant vocabulary (Ps 118:1; 136:1-26; Lam 3:22-23). Joseph in prison receives the same covenant-love that the patriarchs received in freedom.
39.22: wayyittēn śar bêt-hassohar bĕyaḏ-yôsēp ʾēt kol-hāʾăsûrîm ʾăšer bĕḇêt-hassohar wĕʾēt kol-ʾăšer ʿōśîm šām hûʾ hāyāh ʿōśeh — "The warden put Joseph in charge of all the prisoners and everything done there." The prison chapter exactly mirrors the Potiphar chapter: in vv2-6 Potiphar saw YHWH was with Joseph, entrusted everything to his care, and was unconcerned about anything; in vv21-23 the warden sees YHWH is with Joseph, entrusts everything to his care, and is unconcerned about anything. The mirror structure establishes a pattern: wherever Joseph is placed, YHWH turns it into a place of authority-delegation. The structural repetition is the theological argument.
39.23: ʾên śar bêt-hassohar rōʾeh ʾet-kol-mĕʾûmāh bĕyāḏô baʾăšer YHWH ʾittô waʾăšer-hûʾ ʿōśeh YHWH maṣlîaḥ — "The warden paid no attention to anything in Joseph's care, because YHWH was with him; and whatever he did YHWH made it prosper (maṣlîaḥ)." The closing formula mirrors the opening of the chapter (v2: "YHWH was with Joseph and he was a maṣlîaḥ man") — maṣlîaḥ (Hiphil of ṣālaḥ) bookends the chapter. The chapter structure is: YHWH is with Joseph (v2) → Potiphar entrusts everything (vv3-6) → temptation and accusation (vv7-19) → prison (v20) → YHWH is with Joseph (v21) → warden entrusts everything (vv22-23). The covenant presence is the constant; the circumstances are the variable.

40.1: wayhî ʾaḥar haddĕḇārîm hāʾēlleh ḥāṭĕʾû mašqēh melek-miṣrayim wĕhāʾōpeh laʾăḏōnêhem lĕmelek miṣrāyim — "Some time later, the cupbearer and the baker of the king of Egypt offended their master." Mašqeh (H4945, cupbearer/butler — one who causes to drink, from šāqāh, H8248, to give drink) — the royal cupbearer was a high-ranking official in the ancient Near East, responsible for tasting and serving the king's wine and often serving as personal confidant (Nehemiah was a cupbearer to the Persian king, Neh 1:11). ʾōpeh (H644, baker, Qal participle of ʾāpāh, to bake) — another high-ranking court official. Both men's offense (ḥāṭĕʾû, from ḥāṭāʾ, to sin/offend) is unspecified — the narrative interest is in their dreams, not their crimes.
40.2: wayyiqṣōp parʿōh ʿal šĕnê sārîsāyw ʿal śar hammašqîm wĕʿal śar hāʾōpîm — "Pharaoh was angry (wayyiqṣōp) with his two officials, the chief cupbearer and the chief baker." Qāṣap (H7107, to be furious, to be angry) — a strong anger-verb; the same word used for Moses's anger at the Israelites returning from battle (Num 31:14) and for God's anger in prophetic contexts (Isa 8:21; 47:6). The elevating of "chief" (śar) for both officials distinguishes them from lesser staff — these are senior court officers, which explains both their access to the royal prison facility and their dream's connection to Pharaoh's fate.
40.3: wayyittēn ʾōtām bĕmiš̀mar bêt śar haṭṭabbāḥîm ʾel-bêt hassohar mĕqôm ʾăšer yôsēp āsûr šām — "He put them in custody in the house of the captain of the guard, in the same prison where Joseph was confined." The geographical identification — the same prison where Joseph is (mĕqôm ʾăšer yôsēp āsûr šām) — is the providential coincidence of the narrative. The captain of the guard's facility is Potiphar's household facility: this is the same man who imprisoned Joseph (39:1). The narrative loop closes: the man who imprisoned Joseph now houses the two officials who will connect Joseph to Pharaoh.
40.4: wayyipqōḏ śar haṭṭabbāḥîm ʾet-yôsēp ʾittām wayyĕšāret ʾōtām wayyihyû yāmîm bĕmišmar — "The captain of the guard assigned them to Joseph, and he attended them (wayyĕšāret). They remained in custody for some time." Wayyĕšāret (Piel of šārat, H8334, to serve/minister) — the same term used for Joseph's service to Potiphar (39:4). The assignment is casual from the captain's perspective; from the narrative's perspective it is the mechanism by which Joseph encounters the officials. Yāmîm (days/a period of time) — the indefinite duration in custody sets up the night of the dreams.
40.5: wayyaḥălĕmû ḥălôm šĕnêhem ʾîš ḥălōmô bĕlaylāh ʾeḥāḏ ʾîš kĕpitrôn ḥălōmô hammašqeh wĕhāʾōpeh ʾăšer lĕmelek miṣrāyim ʾăšer ʾăsûrîm bĕḇêt hassohar — "Each of the two men — the cupbearer and the baker of the king of Egypt — had a dream the same night, each with its own interpretation (pitrôn)." Pitrôn (H6623, interpretation/explanation, from pātar, H6622, to interpret/solve) — the interpretation is described as inherent to the dream: each dream has its own pitrôn. The word pātar appears only in the Joseph narrative in the OT; it is possibly an Egyptian loanword for the professional dream-interpretation (ptr in Egyptian). Joseph will claim in v8 that pitrōnîm belong to God — using the very professional vocabulary to deny the professional claim.
40.6: wayyāḇōʾ ʾălêhem yôsēp babbōqer wayyarʾ ʾōtām wĕhinnām zōʿăpîm — "When Joseph came to them in the morning, he saw they were dejected (zōʿăpîm)." Zōʿăpîm (H2196, Qal participle plural of zāʿap, to be troubled/gloomy/sad) — this word for downcast appearance occurs only here in the Pentateuch; it describes a visible emotional state that Joseph reads on their faces. Joseph's attentiveness to the emotional state of those in his care — asking "why do you look so sad?" (v7) rather than proceeding to his duties — is a mark of the pastoral-administrator figure he has become.
40.7: wayyišʾal ʾet-sārîsê parʿōh ʾăšer ʾittô bĕmiš̀mar bêt ʾăḏōnāyw lēʾmōr maddûaʿ pĕnêḵem rāʿîm hayyôm — "He asked Pharaoh's officials who were in custody with him, 'Why are your faces downcast (rāʿîm) today?'" Pĕnêḵem rāʿîm (your faces are evil/downcast) — raʿ (H7451, bad/evil/sad) applied to facial appearance; the same word used for Er's wickedness (38:7) and later for the seven gaunt cows of Pharaoh's dream (41:3). Joseph's direct question rather than passive observation is a narrative signal: he is actively engaging with those in his care. This pastoral attentiveness is the condition for the dream-disclosure that will follow.
40.8: wayyōʾmĕrû ʾēlāyw ḥălôm ḥālamnu ûpōtēr ʾên ʾōtô wayyōʾmer ʾălêhem yôsēp hălôʾ lēʾlōhîm pitrōnîm sappĕrû-nāʾ lî — "They answered, 'We have had dreams and there is no one to interpret them.' Joseph said, 'Do not interpretations belong to God (hălôʾ lēʾlōhîm pitrōnîm)? Tell me your dreams.'" The theological claim is the interpretive key to the entire dream-sequence. Hălôʾ lēʾlōhîm pitrōnîm — the rhetorical question expects "yes" — interpretations belong to God, not to professional dream-interpreters. Joseph refuses the position of wise-man-for-hire (which Egyptian dream-interpretation was) and instead attributes interpretation to divine disclosure. This places him in the prophetic tradition rather than the mantic tradition: he does not decode symbols by professional expertise but receives revelation. The claim is borne out when God gives the interpretation (vv12-19 show Joseph receiving, not calculating, the meaning).
40.9: wayyĕsappēr śar-hammašqîm ʾet-ḥălōmô lĕyôsēp wayyōʾmer lô baḥălōmî wĕhinnēh-gefen lĕpānāy — "So the chief cupbearer told Joseph his dream: 'In my dream I saw a vine in front of me.'" The vine (gefen, H1612) — a central symbol of covenant blessing in the OT (Ps 128:3: "your wife like a fruitful vine"; Isa 5:1-7: YHWH's vineyard as Israel; Mic 4:4: "each man under his vine and fig tree"). In the cupbearer's dream, the vine is the source of Pharaoh's cup — the productive symbol that defines the cupbearer's entire vocational existence. The dream's simplicity — vine, branches, grapes, cup, Pharaoh's hand — maps directly onto the cupbearer's daily professional actions.
40.10: ûḇaggepen šĕlōšāh śārîgîm wĕhîʾ kĕporāḥat ʿālĕtāh niṣṭāh hēḇîʾû ʾašĕkĕlōtêyhā ʿănāḇîm — "On the vine were three branches. As it budded, it blossomed, and its clusters ripened into grapes." The three branches (šĕlōšāh śārîgîm) develop in accelerated sequence — bud, blossom, ripe grapes — suggesting a compressed time-vision rather than ordinary agricultural time. The acceleration from bud to harvest in the dream-vision is the narrative signal that an interpretation is required: ordinary vines do not compress agricultural seasons into a single moment. ʿĂnāḇîm (grapes, H6025) — the specific fruit that will be expressed into Pharaoh's cup.
40.11: wĕkôs parʿōh bĕyāḏî wāʾeqqaḥ ʾet-hāʿănāḇîm wāʾeqōṣ ʾōtām ʾel-kôs parʿōh wāʾettēn ʾet-hakôs ʿal-kap parʿōh — "Pharaoh's cup was in my hand, and I took the grapes, squeezed them into Pharaoh's cup and put the cup in Pharaoh's hand." The dream recreates the cupbearer's professional function: take grapes, squeeze into cup, deliver to Pharaoh. The cup (kôs, H3563) is the central object of the cupbearer's ministry — it appears three times in this single verse. The restoration-dream has the cupbearer doing exactly what he is supposed to do. Joseph's interpretation (v13) will confirm that doing one's proper function in Pharaoh's presence = restoration to honor.
40.12: wayyōʾmer lô yôsēp zeh pitrōnô šĕlōšet haśśārîgîm šĕlōšet yāmîm hēm — "Joseph said, 'This is its interpretation: the three branches are three days.'" Zeh pitrōnô (this is its interpretation) — the confident, direct formula for dream interpretation; no hedging, no conditional language. Šĕlōšet yāmîm hēm (they are three days) — the symbol-to-referent identification: branches = days. The number three recurs in both dreams (three branches, three baskets) and will be fulfilled on the third day (v20). The "third day" as the day of fulfillment is a temporal marker that carries covenantal weight throughout the OT (Gen 22:4; Exod 19:11,16; Hos 6:2).
40.13: bĕʿôḏ šĕlōšet yāmîm yiśśāʾ parʿōh ʾet-rōʾšĕḵā ûhăšîḇĕḵā ʿal-kannāteḵā wĕnātattā kôs-parʿōh bĕyāḏô — "Within three days Pharaoh will lift up your head (yiśśāʾ... ʾet-rōʾšĕḵā) and restore you to your position." Yiśśāʾ ʾet-rōʾšĕḵā (he will lift your head) — a Hebrew idiom for restoration to honor and position. The same idiom will be used in v19 for the baker's execution — "he will lift off your head" — but with a different verb and referent. The wordplay is dark: both men receive "head-lifting" from Pharaoh, but the type differs entirely. Joseph's positive interpretation comes first, establishing the idiom before inverting it.
40.14: kî ʾim-zĕḵartanî ʾittĕḵā kaʾăšer yîṭaḇ lāḵ wĕʿāśîtā-nāʾ ʿimmî ḥeseḏ wĕhizkarttanî ʾel-parʿōh wĕhôṣēʾtanî min-habbayit hazzeh — "But remember me when it goes well with you; show me ḥeseḏ and mention me to Pharaoh, and get me out of this house." Joseph's request uses the covenant-vocabulary: zakar (H2142, to remember — the covenant-memory verb; God "remembered" Noah, Rachel, Abraham for Lot) and ḥeseḏ (H2617, covenant-loyalty/lovingkindness). He asks the cupbearer to show the same covenantal faithfulness that YHWH shows to Joseph. Hazzeh (this house) — Joseph refers to the prison as a "house" (bêt hassohar, house of the prison, 39:20) — the same vocabulary as Potiphar's house where YHWH blessed everything. The irony: both houses have become sites of YHWH's presence with Joseph.
40.15: kî-gunnōḇ gunnabbatî mēʾereṣ hāʿiḇrîm wĕgam-pōh lōʾ-ʿāśîtî mĕʾûmāh kî-śāmû ʾōtî babbôr — "I was indeed stolen (gunnōḇ gunnabbatî) from the land of the Hebrews, and even here I have done nothing to deserve being put in a pit (babbôr)." Gunnōḇ gunnabbatî — the Qal infinitive absolute + perfect (emphatic form): "I was truly stolen/kidnapped." Gānab (H1589, to steal, kidnap) — the Eighth Commandment verb (Exod 20:15). Joseph's self-description as a stolen person positions his slavery as a crime against himself, not a legitimate status. ʾEreṣ hāʿiḇrîm (land of the Hebrews) — the first use of this phrase, identifying Canaan from the perspective of the Egyptian diaspora. Babbôr (the pit/cistern/dungeon, H953) — the same word used for the pit Joseph's brothers threw him into in 37:24 (habôr rēq, the empty pit); the Egyptian prison is linguistically identified with the original abandonment. Joseph's narrative of descent is a single story: pit → Potiphar's prison → pit again.
40.16: wayyarʾ śar-hāʾōpîm kî ṭôḇ pātar wayyōʾmer ʾel-yôsēp ʾap-ʾănî baḥălōmî wĕhinnēh šĕlōšāh sallê ḥōrî ʿal-rōʾšî — "When the chief baker saw that Joseph had given a favorable interpretation (pātar), he said: 'I also had a dream.'" The baker's disclosure is motivated by the cupbearer's positive outcome — he wants the same. Pātar (H6622, to interpret dreams) — the baker sees that Joseph's interpretation was "good" (ṭôḇ) and decides to share his own dream. The narrative irony is that the baker's eagerness leads him to hear a death sentence. The basket imagery — three baskets on his head — contrasts with the cupbearer's active hand-delivering image; the baker is passive, and the birds eat the food before it reaches Pharaoh.
40.17: ûḇassal hāʿelyôn mikkōl maʾăḵal parʿōh maʿăśēh ʾōpeh wĕhāʿôp ʾōḵēl ʾōtām min-hassal mēʿal rōʾšî — "In the top basket were all kinds of baked goods for Pharaoh, but the birds were eating them from the basket on my head." The birds eating the food before it reaches Pharaoh is the key detail: in the cupbearer's dream the cup reaches Pharaoh's hand; in the baker's dream the food never reaches Pharaoh at all — intercepted by birds. The baker cannot fulfill his professional function (providing food to Pharaoh) even in his own dream — the dream itself signals a disruption of service that the waking interpretation confirms.
40.18: wayyaʿan yôsēp wayyōʾmer zeh pitrōnô šĕlōšet hasisallîm šĕlōšet yāmîm hēm — "Joseph answered, 'This is its interpretation: the three baskets are three days.'" The identical interpretation formula — zeh pitrōnô... šĕlōšet yāmîm hēm — as in v12 signals that the pattern is the same. Both dreams involve three items = three days. The parallelism confirms the method of Joseph's interpretation and sets up the contrast: same formula, opposite outcome.
40.19: bĕʿôḏ šĕlōšet yāmîm yiśśāʾ parʿōh ʾet-rōʾšĕḵā mēʿāleḵā wĕtālāh ʾôtĕḵā ʿal-ʿēṣ wĕʾāḵal hāʿôp ʾet-bĕśārĕḵā mēʿāleḵā — "Within three days Pharaoh will lift off your head (yiśśāʾ... mēʿāleḵā) and impale you on a pole, and the birds will eat your flesh." The same idiom — yiśśāʾ ʾet-rōʾšĕḵā (lift your head) — is used for both interpretation (v13), but here with the preposition mēʿāleḵā (from off you/away from you) — lifting the head away from the body, i.e., decapitation. The birds that ate the food in the baker's dream (v17) will eat the baker's flesh. The wordplay between the two interpretations using identical vocabulary with opposite valence is the narrator's demonstration of Joseph's interpretive precision — the same words apply to both, but the prepositions and referents differ fatally.
40.20: wayhî bayyôm haššĕlîšî yôm hullelet ʾet-parʿōh wayyaʿaś mišṭeh lĕḵōl-ʿăḇāḏāyw wayyiśśāʾ ʾet-rōʾš śar hammašqîm wĕʾet rōʾš śar hāʾōpîm bĕtôḵ ʿăḇāḏāyw — "On the third day — Pharaoh's birthday (yôm hullelet) — he gave a feast for all his officials and lifted the heads of the chief cupbearer and the chief baker in the presence of his officials." Yôm hullelet ʾet-parʿōh (the day of Pharaoh's birth) — the birthday feast is historically attested for Egyptian practice; the LXX and Josephus confirm this reading. The third day is both the dream-fulfillment day (as Joseph predicted) and the king's birthday — a day of royal clemency and judgment. Both men's "heads are lifted" simultaneously — the idiom fulfilled in its double meaning within a single verse.
40.21: wayyāšeb ʾet-śar hammašqîm ʿal-mašqēhû wayyittēn hakōs ʿal-kap parʿōh — "He restored the chief cupbearer to his position, so that he put the cup in Pharaoh's hand." The restoration is described in the exact vocabulary of the cupbearer's own dream (v11: "I put the cup in Pharaoh's hand"). The dream-vision and the fulfillment share the identical final image — cup in Pharaoh's hand. This exact correspondence confirms Joseph's interpretive credibility, which will matter in ch 41 when Pharaoh dreams.
40.22: wĕʾēt śar hāʾōpîm tālāh kaʾăšer pātar lāhem yôsēp — "But he hanged the chief baker, just as Joseph had said." Tālāh (H8518, to hang/impale) — the execution verb. The closing confirmation — kaʾăšer pātar lāhem yôsēp (just as Joseph had interpreted for them) — is the narrator's validation of Joseph's divine-interpretive authority. The fulfillment of both interpretations, one positive and one fatal, proves that Joseph's claims to divine interpretation were true. This double-fulfillment verification sets up the credibility transfer to Pharaoh's own dreams in ch 41.
40.23: wĕlōʾ-zāḵar śar-hammašqîm ʾet-yôsēp wayyiškāḥēhû — "Yet the chief cupbearer did not remember Joseph; he forgot him." The closing double-verb of forgetting — lōʾ-zāḵar (did not remember) + wayyiškāḥēhû (he forgot him) — is the narrative's most poignant moment. Zāḵar (H2142, to remember) is the covenant-memory verb: God "remembered" Noah (8:1), God "remembered" Rachel (30:22), God "remembered" Abraham when Lot was in danger (19:29); Israel is commanded to "remember" (Deut 5:15; 8:2). Human forgetting and divine remembering are the two poles of the covenant memory tradition. The cupbearer forgets; the reader is left with the knowledge that YHWH will remember (41:1: "after two full years, Pharaoh dreamed"). The chapter ends on human failure; the next chapter will open with divine timing.

41.1: מִקֵּץ שְׁנָתַיִם יָמִים (miqqēṣ šĕnātayim yāmîm) — "at the end of two full years." Miqqēṣ (at the end of — from qēṣ, end, limit; cf. 8:6 where the same phrase marks the end of Noah's waiting period). Šĕnātayim (dual: two years) + yāmîm (days — a supplementary indicator of full years; "two years of days" = two complete years; the idiom emphasizes that the entire period elapsed). Two full years after the cupbearer's restoration and Joseph's forgotten petition — the divine timetable operates on its own schedule. עֹמֵד עַל-הַיְאֹר (standing by the Nile) — yĕʾôr (the Nile — an Egyptian loanword, from Egyptian j'rw, "river/canal"; the standard Hebrew word for the Nile throughout the Exodus narrative; its presence here is one of several authentically Egyptian elements in the Joseph narrative).
41.2: שֶׁבַע פָּרוֹת בְּרִיאוֹת וּטֹבוֹת מַרְאֶה (šeḇaʿ pārôt bĕrîʾôt ûṭōḇôt marʾeh) — "seven cows, fat and good of appearance." Pārāh (cow — female bovine specifically; appears 26× in this chapter, uniquely concentrated; in Egyptian religion, the cow was sacred to Hathor, goddess of fertility and beauty; the dream operates within the Egyptian religious register without acknowledging it). Bārîʾ (fat, healthy, well-fed — from a root meaning to be fat/strong; the same adjective used in Dan 1:15 for the healthier-looking Daniel and his companions). בָּאַחוּ (bāʾaḥû, among the reeds) — ʾāḥû (marsh grass, reeds — an Egyptian loanword from Egyptian ꜣḫw, "marsh plants"; a rare word in Hebrew appearing only here and in Gen 41:18 and Job 8:11; its Egyptian origin anchors the dream in the Nile-delta landscape).
41.3: רָעוֹת מַרְאֶה וְדַקּוֹת בָּשָׂר (rāʿôt marʾeh wĕdaqqôt bāśār) — "ugly of appearance and lean of flesh." Rāʿ marʾeh (ugly/bad of appearance) — the opposite of the good-appearance formula; daq bāśār (thin of flesh) — the emaciated cattle of famine; bāśār (flesh, body) used for the physical condition of the animal. The seven lean cows standing beside the seven fat ones on the riverbank create the dream's symbolic tableau: the abundance and the famine exist simultaneously in the vision before the historical sequence unfolds.
41.4: וַתֹּאכַלְנָה הַפָּרוֹת רָעוֹת הַמַּרְאֶה — "the ugly-looking cows ate the seven fat, good cows." The narrative reversal embedded in the dream: the weaker consume the stronger. Wayyîqaṣ parʿōh (Pharaoh woke up) — the first awakening between the two dreams; the structure of double-dream-with-awakening is a recognized dream-type in ANE literature; the doubling confirms and intensifies the message (cf. v. 32). Pharaoh's waking — seeing it was a dream — increases his disturbance (v. 8) rather than relieving it.
41.5: שֶׁבַע שִׁבֳּלִים עֹלוֹת בְּקָנֶה אֶחָד (šeḇaʿ šibbŏlîm ʿōlôt bĕqāneh ʾeḥāḏ) — "seven heads of grain growing on a single stalk." Šibbōlet (head of grain, ear of grain — the same word that becomes the famous pronunciation test in Judg 12:6 ["say šibbōlet"]; here its agricultural meaning governs). Bĕrîʾôt wĕṭōḇôt (healthy and good) — the same pair of adjectives as the fat cows (v. 2); the parallel vocabulary between the two dreams signals their identical meaning. A single stalk producing seven heads is itself a symbol of extraordinary abundance.
41.6: שֶׁבַע שִׁבֳּלִים דַּקּוֹת וּשְׁדוּפֹת קָדִים (šeḇaʿ šibbŏlîm daqqôt ûšĕḏûpōt qāḏîm) — "seven thin heads scorched by the east wind." Šĕḏûpāh (scorched, blasted — Qal passive participle of šāḏap, to scorch, blight; the word for crop-disease/blight appears in Deut 28:22 as a covenant curse; 1 Kgs 8:37; Amos 4:9; Hag 2:17 — grain-blight is always in the context of divine judgment). Qāḏîm (east wind — the hot, dry sirocco from the east/southeast; cf. Ezek 17:10; 19:12; Hos 13:15; Jon 4:8; the east wind withers, scatters, and destroys in OT poetry and prophecy).
41.7: וַתִּבְלַעְנָה הַשִּׁבֳּלִים הַדַּקּוֹת — "the thin heads swallowed the seven fat heads." The same swallowing-verb (bālaʿ) as the lean cows in v. 4 (tōʾkalnāh); the second dream uses a different verb but the same action-structure. Wayyîqaṣ parʿōh wĕhinnēh ḥălôm (Pharaoh woke up — and behold it was a dream) — the wĕhinnēh (and behold) of abrupt realization; the twice-awakened Pharaoh now knows he has had two vivid dreams; his morning disturbance (v. 8) shows they did not feel like ordinary dreams.
41.8: וַיְהִי בַבֹּקֶר וַתִּפָּעֶם רוּחוֹ (wayhî ḇabbōqer wattippāʿem rûḥô) — "in the morning his spirit was troubled." Pāʿam (Niphal: to be disturbed, agitated — used for the agitation of the spirit before divine revelation; cf. Dan 2:1, 3 where Nebuchadnezzar's spirit is nippāʿam after his dream; the Niphal expresses an involuntary disturbance of the interior). חַרְטֻמֵּי מִצְרָיִם (ḥarṭummê miṣrāyim, the magicians of Egypt) — ḥarṭummîm (an Egyptian loanword from Egyptian ḥr-tp, "chief lector priest" or "keeper of the sacred books"; the professional dream-interpreters and ritual specialists of the Egyptian court; the same word appears in Exod 7:11, 22; 8:3, 14-15; 9:11 where the same class fails against Moses' signs). Their failure here is theologically significant: the interpretive tradition of Egypt cannot read what God has revealed; וְחַכְמֵי מִצְרָיִם (the wise men of Egypt) — the ḥăḵāmîm (wisdom-professionals) whose failure underscores that ḥōkmāh (wisdom) in Egypt is insufficient to decode God's revelation.
41.9: אֶת-חֲטָאַי אֲנִי מַזְכִּיר הַיּוֹם (ʾet-ḥăṭāʾay ʾănî mazkîr hayyôm) — "my shortcomings I am making known today." The cupbearer uses ḥēṭ (sin, offense, shortcoming — the standard word for sin: moral wrong, offense against a superior, falling short of a standard); here likely referring to a failure of his official duty or his neglect of Joseph. Mazkîr (Hiphil participle of zākar, to remember; Hiphil = to cause to remember, to mention, to record) — "I am causing to be remembered" = I am now bringing to mind what I should have mentioned before; the verb of memory is used for both the cupbearer's self-disclosure and for Joseph's earlier "remembering" of the dreams (42:9).
41.10: The cupbearer recaps the events of ch. 40: Pharaoh's anger (qāṣap, to be angry — the same verb used for Pharaoh's anger in 40:2), the imprisonment in the captain of the guard's house (bêt śar haṭṭabbāḥîm — the same house where Joseph was confined). The recap serves to introduce Joseph to Pharaoh's attention as a reliable dream-interpreter with a proven track record.
41.11: וַנַּחַלְמָה חֲלוֹם בְּלַיְלָה אֶחָד אֲנִי וָהוּא — "we each dreamed a dream in one night, he and I." The single-night double-dream confirms the simultaneous divine communication to both prisoners. Kĕpitron ḥălōmô (according to the interpretation of his dream) — pitrôn (interpretation — an Egyptian loanword from Egyptian ptr, "to understand/interpret"; appears only in the Joseph narrative: 40:5, 8, 12, 16, 18; 41:11); the concentration of this loanword in the Joseph narrative is part of its Egyptian vocabulary cluster.
41.12: נַעַר עִבְרִי (naʿar ʿiḇrî, a young Hebrew man) — "a young Hebrew there with us." Naʿar (young man — the same word applied to Joseph in 37:2; the cupbearer remembers Joseph as a subordinate; the word carries the social implication of servant/junior). ʿIḇrî (Hebrew — the ethnic designation; from ʿēḇer, one who crosses over; the term is used to designate Israelites to non-Israelites throughout the Exodus narrative: 1:15, 16, 19; 2:7, 11, 13; 3:18 etc.; the cupbearer identifies Joseph by his ethnicity, not his name or status). ʿEḇeḏ śar haṭṭabbāḥîm (servant of the captain of the guard) — the social position the cupbearer remembers Joseph as holding; Joseph was Potiphar's servant, then the captain's prisoner — the cupbearer's memory focuses on the prison context.
41.13: כַּאֲשֶׁר פָּתַר-לָנוּ כֵּן הָיָה (kaʾăšer pātar-lānû kēn hāyāh) — "just as he interpreted to us, so it was." Pātar (to interpret a dream — a verb of uncertain etymology, possibly also Egyptian in background; used exclusively with dream-interpretation in Genesis: 40:8, 12, 16, 18, 22; 41:8, 12, 13, 15); the fulfilled interpretation is the certification of Joseph's interpretive gift: it was not guesswork but genuine revelation. Wayyitlēhû (and he was impaled) — tālāh (to hang, impale — the form of execution; whether this was hanging on a gallows or impalement on a post is debated; the verb covers both; the baker's fate is confirmed as the dream predicted).
41.14: וַיְרִיצֻהוּ מִן-הַבּוֹר (wayyĕrîṣûhû min-habbôr) — "they rushed him from the pit/dungeon." Rîṣ (Hiphil: to cause to run, to bring quickly — the urgency of the royal summons); bôr (pit, cistern, dungeon — the same word as the dry cistern of 37:24 into which the brothers threw Joseph; Joseph's prison in Egypt is also called a bôr; the word brackets Joseph's entire period of humiliation: from the pit in Canaan to the pit-dungeon in Egypt, and now the release from both). וַיְגַלַּח וַיְחַלֵּף שִׂמְלֹתָיו (he shaved and changed his clothes) — gāllaḥ (to shave — Egyptians, unlike Hebrews, shaved their entire bodies including facial hair; Herodotus 2.36 notes that Egyptian priests shave; Joseph's shaving is cultural adaptation for the royal court appearance; Pharaoh's court required a clean-shaven face).
41.15: Pharaoh's statement — "I have heard it said of you that when you hear a dream you can interpret it" — the reputation precedes Joseph; the cupbearer's report has established him as a dream-interpreter. The verb šāmaʿ (to hear, to hear about) — Pharaoh has "heard" about Joseph's gift; the same hearing-verb that drives the patriarchal narrative of providence.
41.16: בִּלְעָדָי אֱלֹהִים יַעֲנֶה אֶת-שְׁלוֹם פַּרְעֹה (bilʿāḏāy ʾĔlōhîm yaʿăneh ʾet-šĕlôm parʿōh) — "apart from me — God will answer Pharaoh's welfare." Bilʿāḏāy (apart from me, without me — the disclaimer formula; Joseph explicitly removes himself from the interpretive credit before he has even heard the dream). Yaʿăneh (Qal imperfect of ʿānāh, to answer, respond — future action) — the answer will come; its source is God, not Joseph. Šĕlôm parʿōh (Pharaoh's šālôm) — not a greeting but "what will bring Pharaoh to peace/resolution"; the šālôm-word applied to the resolution of Pharaoh's disturbed spirit (v. 8). Joseph's humility before Pharaoh echoes and inverts the brothers' self-humiliation before Joseph — one is genuine, the other forced.
41.17: Pharaoh retells his dreams in vv. 17-24 — a near-verbatim repetition of vv. 1-7, with minor variations that the narrator includes: Pharaoh says "I had never seen such ugly cows in all the land of Egypt" (v. 19 — an intensification not in the original account); "they looked just as ugly as before" (v. 21 — a detail added in the retelling). The variations in Pharaoh's retelling versus the narrator's original account is a common feature of biblical narrative (cf. Abraham's servant retelling the commission story in ch. 24 with variations); the repetition with minor variations is not an error but reflects the narrator's technique of characterization through speech.
41.18: The fat cows in Pharaoh's retelling: bĕrîʾôt ûṭōḇôt marʾeh (fat and good of appearance) — the same adjectives as v. 2 but in reverse order (v. 2: sleek and fat; v. 18: fat and sleek); the minor reordering is characteristic of retelling. The detail of the Nile-bank setting is preserved: the connection between the Nile, Egypt's fertility, and Pharaoh's dream is maintained throughout the retelling.
41.19: וְהִנֵּה שֶׁבַע-פָּרוֹת אֲחֵרוֹת עֹלוֹת אַחֲרֵיהֶן דַּלּוֹת וְרָעוֹת תֹּאַר מְאֹד — Pharaoh adds the intensifier mĕʾōḏ (very much) in his retelling: "very ugly" — the emotional impact of the disturbing cows is expressed more emphatically in his direct report. לֹא-רָאִיתִי כָהֵנָּה בְּכָל-אֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם לָרֹעַ (I had never seen such ugly cows in all the land of Egypt) — the superlative "never seen such" is Pharaoh's subjective memory-addition to the dream account; Egypt is famous for its productive cattle; cows this poor were an aberration from the Egyptian agricultural norm.
41.20: The eating of the fat cows by the lean: Pharaoh's retelling compresses the narrative slightly; the detail that "no one could tell they had eaten them" appears in v. 21 as an addition unique to Pharaoh's version. The famine that is coming will be so severe that the years of abundance will be completely forgotten — the retrospective look will show no evidence of prior prosperity.
41.21: וְלֹא נוֹדַע כִּי-בָאוּ אֶל-קִרְבֶּנָה (wĕlōʾ nôḏaʿ kî-ḇāʾû ʾel-qirḇennāh) — "no one could tell that they had entered their belly." Nôḏaʿ (Niphal of yāḏaʿ, to be known — passive: it was not known); the visible condition of the lean cows after consuming the fat ones was unchanged — the sign of an absolute famine that leaves no trace of former abundance. Qereb (inner part, belly — used for the interior of the body; the cows' physical appearance contradicted what they had consumed).
41.22: The grain dream retold by Pharaoh: the single-stalk-seven-heads image is repeated. The parallel structure of the two dream-pairs (cows/grain; fat-then-lean/full-then-withered) is noted by Pharaoh himself in his retelling — he connects both dreams into a single interpretive puzzle. The accuracy of the retelling confirms that the dream was vivid and memorable.
41.23: צְנֻמוֹת דַּקּוֹת שְׁדוּפוֹת קָדִים (ṣĕnumôt daqqôt šĕḏûpôt qāḏîm) — "withered, thin, scorched by the east wind." Pharaoh adds a third adjective in his retelling: ṣānûm (withered, dried up — from a root related to dryness; appearing only here) alongside daqqôt (thin) and šĕḏûpôt qāḏîm (east-wind scorched). The intensification of the description of the withered grain parallels the intensification of the ugly-cow description (v. 19).
41.24: וָאֹמַר אֶל-הַחַרְטֻמִּים וְאֵין מַגִּיד לִי (wāʾōmar ʾel-haḥarṭummîm wĕʾên maggîḏ lî) — "I told this to the magicians, but none could explain it to me." The ḥarṭummîm's failure is confirmed in Pharaoh's own words: professional Egyptian dream-interpretation could not decode what God revealed. Maggîḏ (Hiphil participle of nāḡaḏ, to tell, declare, make known — the same root used for Joseph's self-disclosure: wayyaggēḏ yôsēp when he reveals himself in 45:1); no one among Egypt's professionals could "make known" the interpretation.
41.25: חֲלוֹם פַּרְעֹה אֶחָד הוּא (ḥălôm parʿōh ʾeḥāḏ hûʾ) — "the dream of Pharaoh is one." Joseph's opening declaration: the two dreams have a single meaning. ʾEḥāḏ (one) — the unifying declaration; the God of Israel who is ʾeḥāḏ (one, Deut 6:4) speaks in one message even through two dreams. אֵת אֲשֶׁר הָאֱלֹהִים עֹשֶׂה הִגִּיד לְפַרְעֹה (what God is doing, he has declared to Pharaoh) — higgîḏ (Hiphil of nāgaḏ, to declare, to make known — the prophetic vocabulary of divine disclosure); the dreams are not random or self-generated but divine disclosure. Note the shift: the ḥarṭummîm could not "make known" the interpretation; God "makes known" what he is about to do.
41.26: שֶׁבַע פָּרֹת הַטֹּבֹת שֶׁבַע שָׁנִים הֵנָּה — "the seven good cows are seven years." Joseph gives the interpretation in the form of equation: dream-symbol = historical reality. The identification of symbolic elements with years is standard ANE dream-interpretation; Joseph's identification connects Egypt's agricultural patterns (Nile flooding → crop production) to a seven-year divine plan. Hûʾ ḥălôm ʾeḥāḏ hûʾ (it is one dream, it is one) — the emphatic repetition of the unity-declaration; the doubling of ʾeḥāḏ hûʾ in v. 26 reinforces what v. 25 established.
41.27: שֶׁבַע שְׁנֵי רָעָב הֵנָּה (šeḇaʿ šĕnê rāʿāḇ hēnnāh) — "these are seven years of famine." Rāʿāḇ (famine, hunger — the word used for the famines of Abraham's era [12:10], Isaac's era [26:1], and now the great famine of Joseph's time; it will recur in the era of Ruth and Naomi [Ruth 1:1] and the time of Elijah [1 Kgs 18:2]). The parallel identification: seven good cows = seven good years; seven lean cows = seven famine years; seven good heads = seven good years; seven withered heads = seven famine years. The double-dream means the meaning is confirmed twice: Joseph will explain the mechanism in v. 32.
41.28: The restatement of Joseph's summary: "it is just as I said to Pharaoh: God has shown Pharaoh what he is about to do." Hĕrʾāh (Hiphil of rāʾāh, to cause to see, to show — God caused Pharaoh to see/know his plan). The repetition (v. 25: "God has declared"; v. 28: "God has shown") uses two different disclosure verbs (nāḡaḏ and rāʾāh), both attributing the dreams' content to divine initiative.
41.29: שֶׁבַע שָׁנִים בָּאוֹת שָׂבָע גָּדוֹל (šeḇaʿ šānîm bāʾôt śāḇāʿ gāḏôl) — "seven years of great abundance are coming." Śāḇāʿ (abundance, satiety — from śāḇaʿ, to be satisfied, to have enough; used for the complete satisfaction of having eaten well; Prov 3:10; Joel 2:26; the word describes not just sufficiency but fullness beyond need). Bāʾôt (Qal active participle, plural: coming — the participle of approach; the abundance is already on its way, already in motion).
41.30: וְנִשְׁכַּח כָּל-הַשָּׂבָע (wĕniškaḥ kol-haśśāḇāʿ) — "all the abundance will be forgotten." Šāḵaḥ (to forget — the verb that the cupbearer used when he "forgot" Joseph [40:23]; now the entire period of Egyptian abundance will be forgotten by the famine that follows; the forgetting-verb is ironically rich in the Joseph narrative). Wĕḵillāh hārāʿāḇ ʾet-hāʾāreṣ (the famine will ravage/destroy the land) — kālāh (Piel: to finish off, consume, destroy — the intensive destruction; the Piel is stronger than the simple completion: it destroys thoroughly).
41.31: וְלֹא-יִוָּדַע הַשָּׂבָע בָּאָרֶץ (wĕlōʾ-yiwwāḏaʿ haśśāḇāʿ bāʾāreṣ) — "the abundance will not be known in the land." The future passive: the abundance will not be knowable; it will leave no evidence. This echoes the dream's detail (v. 21): the lean cows that consumed the fat ones "looked just as ugly as before" — the famine erases the abundance visually in the dream and historically in reality.
41.32: וְעַל הִשָּׁנוֹת הַחֲלוֹם אֶל-פַּרְעֹה פַּעֲמָיִם כִּי-נָכוֹן הַדָּבָר מֵעִם הָאֱלֹהִים — "the reason the dream was given twice is that the matter is established from with God." Nāḵôn (Niphal of kûn, to be established, confirmed, prepared — the certainty of fixity; a Niphal participle: "it is established"); mēʿim hāʾĔlōhîm (from with God — ʿim = with, beside; the matter issues from God's deliberated will). וּמְמַהֵר הָאֱלֹהִים לַעֲשֹׂתוֹ (and God is hastening to do it) — māhar (Piel: to hasten, act urgently — the same urgency as the Passover eating [Exod 12:11: "you shall eat it with urgency"]; the divine plan is not only certain but imminent). The two-dream confirmation (v. 32) is a theological principle: God confirms his word through repetition (cf. Deut 17:6; 19:15 — two witnesses; 2 Cor 13:1).
41.33: וְעַתָּה יֵרֶא פַרְעֹה אִישׁ נָבוֹן וְחָכָם (wĕʿattāh yēreh parʿōh ʾîš nāḇôn wĕḥāḵām) — "now let Pharaoh look for a discerning and wise man." Joseph moves from interpretation to policy recommendation without waiting to be asked — an audacious move for a prisoner. Nāḇôn (discerning, insightful — Niphal participle of bîn, to understand, perceive; the naḇôn is the one who has penetrating perception; cf. Prov 1:5; 10:13; the combination nāḇôn wĕḥāḵām appears in Deut 1:13 for the qualifications of tribal leaders and in 1 Sam 16:18 for David's qualities). The qualities Joseph recommends are exactly the ones Pharaoh will identify in Joseph himself (v. 39).
41.34: וִיפְקֵד פְּקִדֹת עַל-הָאָרֶץ (wîpaqēḏ pĕqiḏōt ʿal-hāʾāreṣ) — "let him appoint overseers over the land." The cognate accusative pāqaḏ pĕqiḏōt (appoint appointments/officials) — the pāqaḏ verb is the covenantal-visit word: YHWH "visits" his people in judgment and redemption (21:1; 50:24-25); here used for the administrative appointment of commissioners; the same root as pāqîd (official, overseer; cf. Esth 2:3). ʿAmmēš ʾet-ʾereṣ miṣrāyim (to take a fifth of the harvest) — the twenty-percent grain tax during the seven years of abundance; the taxation plan concentrates the surplus under Pharaoh's authority.
41.35: וְיִצְבְּרוּ-בָר תַּחַת יַד-פַּרְעֹה (wĕyiṣbĕrû-ḇār taḥat yaḏ-parʿōh) — "let them collect grain under Pharaoh's authority." Ṣāḇar (to heap up, accumulate — used in Exod 8:10 for the heaped frogs; Zech 9:3 for heaped silver; here for the systematic accumulation of grain surpluses). Bār (processed/clean grain — specifically grain that has been cleaned and separated from chaff; the higher-quality designation emphasizes that the stored food will be usable). Taḥat yaḏ-parʿōh (under the hand of Pharaoh) — under royal authority; the centralized storage system under Pharaoh's control is the key of the plan.
41.36: לְמִחְיָה לָאָרֶץ (lĕmiḥyāh lāʾāreṣ) — "for the sustaining of life for the land." Miḥyāh (sustenance, means of life — from ḥāyāh, to live; literally "that which gives life"; appears in Judg 6:4 for the grain left to the Israelites by Midian; the word for life-sustaining food). The goal of the storage plan is explicitly stated: wĕlōʾ-tiḵkaret hāʾāreṣ bārāʿāḇ (so that the land may not be cut off by the famine) — kārat (to be cut off — the covenant-curse word; to be "cut off" is the penalty for covenant violation [Exod 12:15, 19; 31:14; Lev 7:20-21]; here it is famine's threat to the land).
41.37: וַיִּיטַב הַדָּבָר בְּעֵינֵי פַרְעֹה (wayyîṭaḇ haddāḇār bĕʿênê parʿōh) — "the plan seemed good in Pharaoh's eyes." The standard formula of approval: ṭōḇ bĕʿênê (good in the eyes of) = to please, to seem right to; the idiom of subjective acceptance. Ûḇĕʿênê kol-ʿăḇāḏāyw (and in the eyes of all his servants) — unanimous approval; the plan was not just acceptable to Pharaoh but to his entire court. The universal approval legitimizes Joseph's appointment: it is not Pharaoh's whim but court consensus.
41.38: הֲנִמְצָא כָזֶה אִישׁ אֲשֶׁר רוּחַ אֱלֹהִים בּוֹ (hăniṣmāʾ ḵāzeh ʾîš ʾăšer rûaḥ ʾĔlōhîm bô) — "can we find a man like this, one in whom is the spirit of God?" Rûaḥ ʾĔlōhîm (spirit of God) — on the lips of Pharaoh, this phrase likely means "divine spirit" in the Egyptian sense (the spirit of the gods); in the Hebrew narrative it is presented as the Spirit of Israel's God. The same phrase as Gen 1:2 where the Spirit of God hovered over the waters at creation; and as Exod 31:3; 35:31 where Bezalel is filled with the Spirit of God for the tabernacle's artistry. Pharaoh inadvertently identifies the source of Joseph's gifts: the Spirit that creates and orders is the same Spirit that reveals and interprets.
41.39: אַחֲרֵי הוֹדִיעַ אֱלֹהִים אוֹתְךָ אֶת-כָּל-זֹאת (ʾaḥărê hôḏîaʿ ʾĔlōhîm ʾôtĕḵā ʾet-kol-zōʾt) — "since God has made all this known to you." Hôḏîaʿ (Hiphil of yāḏaʿ, to cause to know, to reveal — the Hiphil causative; God caused Joseph to know; the knowledge did not originate with Joseph). Nāḇôn wĕḥāḵām (discerning and wise) — the exact qualities Joseph had recommended in v. 33 are now applied to Joseph himself by Pharaoh: the recommender is identified as the best candidate. The narrative's wit: Joseph did not nominate himself but described himself.
41.40: עַל-בֵּיתִי יִשַּׁק כָּל-עַמִּי (ʿal-bêtî yiššaq kol-ʿammî) — "all my people shall be subject to your orders." Nāšaq (Qal: to kiss; but here in the context of the formula, likely a homophone meaning "to submit, to be ruled by"; some read this as "all my people will be at your beck and call" — the ruler-submission idiom); the idiom is debated but the sense is clear: Joseph has supreme authority over the Egyptian populace. Raq hakkissēʾ ʾeḡdal mimmeka (only the throne will I be greater than you) — the sole exception to Joseph's authority is Pharaoh himself; the throne is the only thing Joseph does not receive.
41.41: רְאֵה נָתַתִּי אֹתְךָ עַל כָּל-אֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם (rĕʾēh nāṯattî ʾōtĕḵā ʿal kol-ʾereṣ miṣrāyim) — "see, I have set you over all the land of Egypt." The perfect tense nāṯattî (I have given/set — a performative perfect: the declaration makes it so; the speech-act is the appointment); Pharaoh's word of installation is itself the legal act. Rĕʾēh (see! behold! — the imperative used for drawing attention to a decisive action or proclamation; cf. 41:41; Deut 1:8: "See, I have given you the land").
41.42: טַבַּעְתּוֹ מֵעַל יָדוֹ (ṭabbaʿtô mēʿal yāḏô) — "his signet ring from his hand." Ṭabbaʿat (signet ring — the seal-ring used for authenticating royal documents in wax; the transfer of the signet ring is the transfer of Pharaoh's authority for official acts; cf. Esth 3:10; 8:2 where the same ring-transfer signals authority). בִּגְדֵי-שֵׁשׁ (bigdê-šēš, garments of fine linen) — šēš (fine linen — an Egyptian word for high-quality bleached linen; the same word used for the tabernacle's linen hangings: Exod 26:1, 31, 36; 27:9, 16, 18; and for the priestly garments: Exod 28:5-6, 8, 15, 39). רְבִד הַזָּהָב (rĕḇîḏ hazzāhāḇ, the gold chain/necklace) — rĕḇîḏ (necklace, chain — hapax apart from Ezek 16:11; the gold neck-chain is an Egyptian royal honor; the three tokens of appointment: signet ring + fine linen + gold chain = the full Egyptian investiture ceremony).
41.43: אַבְרֵךְ (ʾaḇrēḵ) — the word called out before Joseph's chariot; a genuine hapax legomenon of uncertain etymology. Proposed readings: (1) Egyptian ib-r-k or similar = "bow the knee/head in homage"; (2) Hebrew root bāraḵ (to bless/kneel) with the Egyptian definite article or prefix; (3) Akkadian cognate; the LXX transliterates it without translation (abrek), suggesting it was already opaque to early translators. The form and function are clear — a cry of honor and submission as the second-chariot passes — even if the precise linguistic origin is disputed. Joseph rides in Pharaoh's second chariot (merkĕḇet hamišneh): the chariot of the second-in-command, the vice-royal chariot.
41.44: אֲנִי פַרְעֹה וּבִלְעָדֶיךָ לֹא-יָרִים אִישׁ אֶת-יָדוֹ וְאֶת-רַגְלוֹ — "I am Pharaoh, but without you no man will lift hand or foot in all Egypt." The formula "I am Pharaoh" (ʾănî parʿōh) echoes the covenant-declaration formula "I am YHWH" (ʾănî YHWH); Pharaoh uses the royal self-identification to validate the decree. Wĕḇilʿāḏeḵā (but without you — the same idiom Joseph used in v. 16: "apart from me, God will answer"; now Pharaoh uses it: "apart from you, no one acts"). The irony is complete: Joseph who said "apart from me" now has Pharaoh say "apart from you."
41.45: צָפְנַת פַּעְנֵחַ (ṣāpĕnat paʿnēaḥ, Zaphenath-Paneah) — an Egyptian throne-name given to Joseph; its exact etymology is disputed: proposed readings include Egyptian ḏd-p-ntr-iw.f-ʿnḫ ("the god speaks and he lives") or ṣnṯ-pꜥ-nḥ ("sustainer of life"); the form is authentically Egyptian despite uncertainty about the precise original. אָסְנַת בַּת-פּוֹטִי פֶרַע (Asenath daughter of Potiphera) — ʾĀsĕnat from Egyptian ns-nt ("she who belongs to Neith," the Egyptian goddess of wisdom and war); Pôṭî peraʿ (Potiphera — "gift of Re/the sun god"; cf. the priest Potiphar, possibly the same name-form; both are Egyptian theophoric names containing the sun-god Ra/Re). Joseph's wife is the daughter of the priest of ʾÔn (On = Heliopolis, the center of Egyptian sun-worship, the city of the great temple of Re); Joseph is integrated into Egypt's highest priestly aristocracy through marriage.
41.46: וְיוֹסֵף בֶּן-שְׁלֹשִׁים שָׁנָה בְּעָמְדוֹ לִפְנֵי פַרְעֹה (wĕyôsēp ben-šĕlōšîm šānāh bĕʿomĕdô lipnê parʿōh) — "Joseph was thirty years old when he stood before Pharaoh." The age datum: thirty is significant in the OT as the age of priestly service (Num 4:3, 23, 30, 35, 39, 43, 47 — Levites begin at thirty), of David's kingship (2 Sam 5:4), and of John the Baptist's and Jesus' public ministry (Luke 3:23: "about thirty years old"). Joseph was seventeen when sold (37:2) — thirteen years of servitude and imprisonment before his exaltation. The narrator supplies the chronological marker to anchor the subsequent events.
41.47: וַתַּעַשׂ הָאָרֶץ בְּשֶׁבַע שְׁנֵי הַשָּׂבָע לִקְמָצִים (wattaʿaś hāʾāreṣ bĕšeḇaʿ šĕnê haśśāḇāʿ liqmāṣîm) — "the land produced by handfuls in the seven years of abundance." Qāmāṣ (handful — the standard grain-measure of a handful or fistful; here used to express superabundance: the land produced by the handfuls; the idiom suggests that the grain was so plentiful it could be gathered by the handful and it was still abundant).
41.48: Joseph's collection system: wayyiṣbōr kol-ʾōkel šeḇaʿ šānîm (he stored all the food of the seven years) — the systematic collection; food from the fields surrounding each city is stored in that city (bĕtôḵāh, within it). The decentralized storage in multiple cities prevents a single point of failure and reduces transportation costs during distribution. The administrative efficiency of Joseph's plan reflects the sophisticated Egyptian granary system (attested in archaeological records of Egyptian grain storage).
41.49: וַיִּצְבֹּר יוֹסֵף בָּר כְּחוֹל הַיָּם (wayyiṣbōr yôsēp bār kĕḥôl hayyām) — "Joseph stored up grain like the sand of the sea." The sand-of-the-sea formula — the same comparison used for the promise to Abraham (22:17: "as numerous as the sand on the seashore"); now the covenant-promise of innumerable descendants finds an ironic parallel in the innumerable grain Joseph stores. ʿAḏ kî-ḥāḏal lisepōr kî-ʾên mispar (until he stopped counting because it was beyond number) — the bookkeeping stopped because the quantity exceeded recording capacity; the storage was literally uncountable.
41.50: וּלְיוֹסֵף יֻלַּד שְׁנֵי בָנִים (ûlĕyôsēp yullaḏ šĕnê ḇānîm) — "two sons were born to Joseph." The Niphal passive: were born to him; the children come before the famine. Liphê bôʾ šĕnat hārāʿāḇ (before the year of famine came) — the chronological marker places the birth of Joseph's sons in the abundance period; the children are born in Egypt, to an Egyptian wife, in the years of plenty — fully embedded in Egyptian life. The names Joseph gives them will explain his theological interpretation of his exile.
41.51: מְנַשֶּׁה כִּי-נַשַּׁנִי אֱלֹהִים אֶת-כָּל-עֲמָלִי (mĕnaššeh kî-naššanî ʾĔlōhîm ʾet-kol-ʿămālî) — "Manasseh, for God has made me forget all my trouble." The name-etymology is explicit: mĕnaššeh from naššanî (made me forget — Piel of nāšāh, to forget; the Piel causative: God caused the forgetting). ʿĀmāl (trouble, toil — the painful labor; cf. Eccl 2:11 where ʿāmāl is all the toilsome striving under the sun; Job 3:10 where ʿāmāl is life's suffering) + bêt ʾāḇî (my father's household) — Joseph attributes his forgetting of the pain of his father's house to God's providential mercy. The statement is psychologically complex: does forgetting his father's household mean healing, or is it a sign of Egypt's assimilation erasing his origins?
41.52: אֶפְרַיִם כִּי-הִפְרַנִי אֱלֹהִים בְּאֶרֶץ עָנְיִי (ʾeprayim kî-hiprānî ʾĔlōhîm bĕʾereṣ ʿonyî) — "Ephraim, for God has made me fruitful in the land of my affliction." ʾEprayim from hiprānî (Hiphil of pārāh, to be fruitful, to cause fruitfulness; the Hiphil causative: God made fruitful; cf. Gen 1:22, 28: pĕrû ûrĕḇû, "be fruitful and multiply" — the creation blessing now applied to Joseph's Egyptian sojourn). ʿOnî (affliction, poverty — the same word used for Israel's bondage in Egypt in Exod 3:7 ["I have seen the affliction of my people"]; and for Leah's affliction seen by YHWH in Gen 29:32). Joseph names his Egypt experience "the land of my affliction" — Egypt is simultaneously the place of his fruitfulness and his suffering.
41.53: The transition verse: "the seven years of abundance that existed in Egypt came to an end." Tāmmannāh (they were completed — Qal of tāmam, to be complete, finished; the years ran their full course exactly as predicted). The transition is stark and brief — one verse for seven years of abundance ending; the brevity mirrors the famine's erasure of the abundance.
41.54: וַתָּחֵל שֶׁבַע שְׁנֵי הָרָעָב לָבוֹא כַּאֲשֶׁר אָמַר יוֹסֵף — "the seven years of famine began, just as Joseph said." Kāʾăšer ʾāmar yôsēp (just as Joseph said) — the fulfillment-formula; the narrative credits Joseph's interpretation as having been accurate; the proof of prophetic interpretation is fulfillment (cf. Deut 18:22). Rāʿāḇ bĕḵol-hāʾărāṣôt (famine in all the lands) — the scope expands beyond Egypt: the famine is international, not local; this sets up the brothers' journey from Canaan.
41.55: וַתִּרְעַב כָּל-אֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם (wattiṛʿaḇ kol-ʾereṣ miṣrāyim) — "all the land of Egypt was hungry." Rāʿēḇ (to be hungry — the verb matching the noun rāʿāḇ, famine/hunger); Egypt itself, despite the stored grain, is hungry. לְכוּ אֶל-יוֹסֵף אֲשֶׁר-יֹאמַר לָכֶם תַּעֲשׂוּ (go to Joseph; what he says to you, do it) — Pharaoh's directive echoes the Egyptian idiom of total delegation; and it strikingly echoes the words that Mary will say at Cana: "whatever he says to you, do it" (John 2:5; drawing on the same structural pattern of absolute authority). Joseph is the one to whom all Egypt is directed.
41.56: וַיִּפְתַּח יוֹסֵף אֶת-כָּל-אֲשֶׁר בָּהֶם (wayyipttaḥ yôsēp ʾet-kol-ʾăšer bāhem) — "Joseph opened all that was in them." The verb pātaḥ (to open — the opening of the storehouses; the same word used for God "opening" the womb [29:31; 30:22] and "opening" the heavens [Gen 7:11]). Joseph "sells" (wayyišbōr, from šābar, to buy/sell grain — a grain-trade verb; Amos 8:5-6 uses the same root for the dishonest grain-sellers; the root is specifically for grain-commerce).
41.57: וְכָל-הָאָרֶץ בָּאוּ מִצְרַיְמָה לִשְׁבֹּר אֶל-יוֹסֵף (wĕḵol-hāʾāreṣ bāʾû miṣrāymāh lišbōr ʾel-yôsēp) — "all the earth came to Egypt to buy grain from Joseph." Kol-hāʾāreṣ (all the earth/land) — the scope is universal; Joseph has become the grain-provider for the known world. The phrase that will bring his brothers: the famine that was universal means Canaan too is affected; Jacob's sons will soon be among those who "came to Egypt to buy grain from Joseph." The chapter ends with Joseph at the center of the world's food supply — the dreamer of sheaves bowed to by his brothers has become the actual supplier of grain to all nations.

42.1: לָמָּה תִּתְרָאוּ (lāmmāh titrāʾû) — "why do you just keep looking at each other?" The Hitpael of rāʾāh (to see oneself, to look at one another — the reflexive-reciprocal form; Hitpael implies mutual, repeated looking: they are staring at each other without acting). The idiom expresses paralytic indecision in a crisis — looking around for someone else to take initiative. Jacob's blunt rhetorical question breaks the paralysis: the patriarch names the situation and commands action.
42.2: רְדוּ-שָׁמָּה וְשִׁבְרוּ-לָנוּ מִשָּׁם (rĕḏû-šāmmāh wĕšiḇrû-lānû miššām) — "go down there and buy for us from there." Rāḏaḏ (to go down — cf. the later "going down" to Egypt by Jacob [46:3-4]; the geographical descent from Canaan to Egypt is built into the Hebrew verb). Šāḇar (to buy grain — the grain-commerce verb of 41:56-57; Jacob uses the technical term of Joseph's operation). וְנִחְיֶה וְלֹא נָמוּת (that we may live and not die) — the stark life-or-death alternative; ḥāyāh (to live) versus mût (to die); the existential famine-demand reduced to its essence.
